Soon, she appeared in a dream to Zhou, saying, "I die without covering my guilt, still fall into the category of animals, end my life in a certain way to atone for my sins in this life. Thirty years later, you will be a scholar in Pingyang, and I will serve you as your maid." The matter is from another era, there is no evidence to verify, perhaps it is not false. Observing the result, one believes in the cause, the woman's belief in her karmic fate is also not unfounded.

In the Pudong district of Shanghai lived an old man named Zhang Laoda, whose ancestors were originally known as Old Mr. Dadao. Zhang Laoda was naturally frugal and stingy, never spending a single coin carelessly. When he fell gravely ill, he ordered his younger brother and son to divide the ancestral property into two parts, each receiving half. In his room was a chest of silver, accumulated by Zhang Laoda himself, securely sealed and marked. At this time, he commanded his wife to open it, intending to discuss the division, but Zhang Laoda had already passed away. Just as they were all shocked and terrified, the silver ingots inside the chest suddenly flew away with the wind. They all rushed to catch them, but the silver moved like fluttering butterflies, impossible to grasp. Some occasionally caught one or two, only to find them vanish from their hands instantly. In the blink of an eye, all the silver in the chest had vanished entirely. Thus they realized that even to be a money hoarder requires fortune: Zhang Laoda, though unable to use the silver, still managed to retain it; but his son and younger brother, who wished to become money hoarders, could not.

In the northern part of Cixi, there was a man surnamed Ye who, in the fifth month of the year Jia Xu, gave birth to a son with a human body but a tiger's face. Frightened by his appearance, they abandoned him in the wild. A neighbor went to see and found that he was still alive. The neighbor then said, "If you cry three times, I will take you back." The child indeed cried three times, and the neighbor took him back. The next day, the neighbors all gathered to see the child. Some felt pity for Ye, and Ye also regretted his decision, using one string of coins to redeem the child. The following night, the child died. He did not die in the wild or with the neighbor, but still could not be raised, for reasons unknown. In the mountains of Yixing, there was a man surnamed Zhao who, every evening, lit a fire in a metal container and added coal on top to prepare for the next day. One night, the coal fire suddenly went out, and this happened repeatedly over several nights. When they investigated, they saw a one-legged ghost crouching over the metal container, laughing and playing with the coal. The people gathered and attacked the ghost, but it leaped away in one bound. Someone said it was a mountain demon, and that it fears firecrackers. They waited for it to appear and threw firecrackers at it. The ghost, though black and ugly, was helpless and only knelt and begged for mercy. They gave it food, but it was terrified and dared not eat much. When they showed it the discarded hand drum, it became happy and laughed. They gave it the drum, and as soon as it took it, it laughed loudly and leaped away, disappearing in an instant. According to *Baopuzi: Ascending to the Heights*, "The mountain spirit appears as a small child with one leg facing backward, enjoys causing trouble at night, and is called a mountain demon. If you call its name, it cannot harm you." The ghost's appearance was undoubtedly that of a mountain demon, but the purpose of the hand drum it carried remained a mystery.

A maid from the Wu region who served the Qian family married a farmer's son from the countryside. In her area, there was a deity known as the "Madam of Qingming Mountain," who was said to be a black fish spirit from Taihu Lake. One day, the spirit possessed the maid and claimed to be her sister from a past life, coming to visit her. The spirit spoke with a northern accent, and her voice was clear and pleasant to hear. From then on, she would visit every few days, and this continued for over thirty years, with only the maid seeing her. Someone asked the maid, "What did the madam look like?" She replied, "She was about thirty years old, and over the years, she never aged. I am now increasingly feeble, but the madam still looks as she did when I first saw her." The old master of the Qian family sent the maid to offer incense in the mountain and ordered the servant Ah Qin to accompany her, hoping to observe any unusual occurrences. As soon as they left, the maid leaped onto the saddle, raised her hand as if to whip the horse, and moved very quickly. Ah Qin could not keep up with her. When they returned, they asked her about it, and she said, "The madam pitied me for not being able to endure the journey and brought my servant and his horse to welcome me." The maid ultimately had no other unusual experiences. At first, there was no temple in the mountain, only a table and a chair in an empty house. Later, the villagers made a male statue of her and called her the "Flying Tiger City God." It is said that Governor Gao Bozu of Wu County banned the worship of the spirit.

A certain person from Wu, traveling and buying goods, arrived in Shanghai and occasionally engaged in licentious activities, meeting a prostitute. After the wine had been consumed, he sent off the guests and detained a bald person, planning to sing together in a dream. By chance, he asked about her family background and residence, and she revealed that she was his lost daughter from the chaos, causing both their expressions to change. The prostitute asked him about his identity, and he revealed the truth. The prostitute cried loudly, and the father shook his hand to warn against making a sound: "Let's slowly plan to release you from your register." He then left silently. Thinking of the guest's meager bag, how could one obtain the money to redeem the daughter? And yet, unable to bear her degradation, he had no plan or strategy, and eventually died from taking opium. This person, having met his daughter, did not think to help her, but instead took his own life, which can truly be called an extreme case of helplessness.

A man from Guangdong, surnamed Jia, traveled to Shanghai and fell in love with a courtesan named Zijuan. She left the register, and he took her back to his home, intending to grow old together. Soon, however, he lost everything due to legal disputes, and Zijuan sold herself back into the brothels of Pukang to use her earnings to resolve the legal issues. The lawsuit finally ended, but Zijuan returned to the brothel, feeling depressed and discontented. Jia would often go to comfort and console her, planning to help her leave the register again, but he was short of funds. So he returned to Shanghai, planning to raise money from old friends, but after a long time, it was unsuccessful. Zijuan, in Guangdong, looked forward every day and night. Suddenly, there was news that he had died in Shanghai, and she took opium and died. Her companions noticed and saved her. The news spread to Shanghai, and people praised her, helping her return home, and they reunited. This woman, having been married, became a courtesan again, which is indeed a pity. However, she was originally a courtesan, so it cannot be held against her for being unfaithful. Moreover, her selling herself was to resolve her husband's legal issues; her returning to the brothel and not dying was to wait for her husband to redeem her. So her intention was never to forget her husband. Considering all these good deeds, it is appropriate to commend her as chaste and loyal.

Volume Two: The Two Filial Daughters of the Cao Family of Gushu The elder daughter, named Jingyi, was betrothed to the Yao family from a young age, but they were separated due to the turmoil of war. When the chaos settled, the son of the Yao family came to be a groomsman, and after living for a long time, they finally found out that he had already married and had children. The parents were angry and blamed the groomsman for his rudeness, but Jingyi tried to smooth things over. The son of the Yao family left in shame and anger, and thus they were separated. Jingyi, at home, served her parents with great care. And because of her mother's illness, she took over managing the household affairs, and everything was well-organized. Wanyi, the third sister, was very intelligent from a young age and could memorize what she read in one glance. Her parents loved her greatly and were strict in choosing a husband, so she was not yet betrothed. In the seventh year of Tongzhi, her father died, and Wanyi vowed to sacrifice herself. The family tried to stop her because her mother was still alive, so she and Jingyi took a long-term vegetarian diet and devoted themselves to Buddhism, reciting the *Diamond Sutra* together to pray for the deceased father's blessings in the afterlife. Not long after, her mother also died. The two sisters took advantage of someone's absence and both drank poison at the same time. Jingyi left a farewell letter to her brothers before dying: "Sister, it is unfortunate to be born a woman. From the time she gained knowledge, she immediately thought that women also receive boundless grace, but there is nothing to repay it with, deeply feeling a sense of inadequacy. As she grew older, she saw that her mother had a coughing illness, which occurred without a fixed schedule, and there must be someone who cares for her day and night to avoid unforeseen dangers. She truly feared that one day she would be married far away and would not be able to serve her mother's illness. And she was betrothed to the Yao family at a young age, it was hard to defy her parents' wishes, she had no way to divide herself, and she was in a state of extreme anxiety. After the groomsman arrived, she wanted to arrange for a concubine so that she could return to her parents' home in the future."

Heaven has already arranged for the person to be married and have children. At that time, many people met with misfortune, and all criticized men for being unrighteous, but the sister alone rejoices at the chance provided by heaven, fulfilling her wish to care for her parents. Therefore, she strives to resist the argument for proper naming, settling down at home to serve, fully hoping that both parents will live to a hundred years old and always enjoy the joy of family. Who would have thought that heaven would bring such misfortune, the kind father has passed away; the mourning period is not yet over, the old mother has contracted a serious illness, is likely to die. Alas! My father and mother, have both departed from the hall; may be reborn as a Buddha or ascend to heaven, swear to follow them in the afterlife. From now on, their smiles and laughter will always be close, there is no more regret. But remember that staying at home to care for one's parents, is not the duty of a woman from the Yao family; to serve in life and follow in death, can be called a daughter of the Cao family. The original betrothal gifts, two gold hairpins, hope to return them to the Yao family immediately, to permanently cut off entanglements, to clearly show a final break. The third sister on the day the father died, tried every way to die but could not, this time, it seems she cannot avoid it. Alas! Brothers repeatedly suffer great misfortunes, both branches broken together, the grief is unbearable to imagine. I have a word of consolation, in the afterlife, the family hearth, with the sisters serving, it is enough to put one's mind at ease. The brothers' duties as sons, not just in the words of grief and destruction, but to cultivate oneself and educate one's children, to accumulate virtue and honor one's parents, will be sufficient to comfort the spirits of the parents, and the sisters will also smile from the side. The second sister is now a wife of the Kong family, has her duties to fulfill, must grieve moderately and love oneself, do not violate proper conduct. Death is a brief separation, at the moment of writing, with deep sorrow!

An Elderly Scholar from Shaoxing, Wang Zhi Xu, recounts: At the end of the reign of Emperor Qianlong, there was a man named Jia Shengan, also an elderly student. He once dreamed of arriving at a place that resembled a high official's office, with all the doors closed and the place empty. As he was wandering around, suddenly several people brought a woman from afar to the door. They removed all her clothes. The woman was still young and slightly attractive, standing naked with a face showing extreme shame and embarrassment. Jia, who was always proud, went forward and scolded them, saying, "Who are you people, daring to act so impolitely?" They smiled and said, "What is there to be surprised about?" Before they finished speaking, the door suddenly opened. Several people carried out a large barrel, and an official carrying documents followed behind and left. The group then took the naked woman, and Jia followed inside. They passed through several doors and arrived at a large courtyard. They saw hundreds of men and women, some sitting, some standing, some lying down, all naked. On the dais sat an official, in front of whom was set up a large press. Several strong men held large iron forks and placed men and women at will into the slots. They pressed them with large stones, and blood and fat flowed everywhere. Below, a basin caught the liquid; when it was full, they poured it into the large barrel. This was done over ten times, and the large barrel was finally full. Several people carried it out. The official handed the documents to an official and left with him. Jia looked at the official and saw that it was his deceased neighbor Zhou Dafu. So he called out to him. Zhou was startled and said, "Why are you here? This is not a place to stay long. Come out with me quickly." Jia asked what was in the barrel. Zhou said, "Opium paste."

During the time when opium had not yet spread, merchants were unaware of this substance. They asked, "What is opium?" Zhou replied, "At present, peace has lasted for a long time, and the population has grown. There should be a great disaster to reduce the numbers. Historically, the greatest disasters have been those of water, fire, and weapons. Those who encounter such a disaster, whether wise or foolish, perish together, and the saying that good deeds bring good fortune and evil deeds bring misfortune often fails in such cases. Therefore, the Creator commanded the assembly of gods to create a special disaster in the form of opium, using the juice of the poppy flowers from this world to be refined into a paste for people to consume. Those who consume this opium are in the disaster, while those who do not consume it are not. Let people take what they deserve, and one cannot blame the Creator for being unkind. With this disaster to reduce the population, the disasters of water, fire, and weapons can be diminished by tenfold, with five or six of them being eliminated. However, the poppy is originally a grass flower, and it has existed since ancient times. Its juice is thin and cannot be refined into a paste."

Therefore, the ruler of the nine underworlds was again commanded to select from the Avici Hell those souls guilty of disloyalty, filial impiety, lack of propriety, righteousness, integrity, and shame, and to record and send them to this place. The marrow and blood were extracted from these souls and then delivered to the gods of mountains, hills, plains, lowlands, and mounds on earth. These gods were instructed to pour this marrow and blood into the roots of opium poppies, from the roots up to the flower buds. Then, its juice naturally became rich and thick, and once refined, it shone with a dark luster. You should recognize it; in several decades, this smoke will spread throughout the world. The merchant wanted to ask more questions, but suddenly, someone drove dozens of men and women to the place, whipping and driving them very harshly, all crying out together. The merchant was startled awake and told others, but no one believed him. By the middle of the Daoguang era, opium smoking indeed became widespread, but the merchant had already died before. However, his words still lingered in people's ears, so at that time, it was said that opium contained the marrow and blood of the dead, which was actually a misinterpretation of his words.

Lü Fengwu, a man from the state of Chu, traveled to Suzhou. On a boat, he saw a beautiful and charming woman. She came and went with swift oars, and in an instant, she was gone, but the memory of her was vivid in his mind. That night, as he lay down to sleep, he dreamed of someone telling him, "The woman on the boat is your wife." Lü had not yet married, and he could not remain unmoved, but there was no trace or lead, so he set the matter aside for now. The following year, he entered the Imperial Academy as a scholar-official and then went to the capital city. By chance, he saw a painting in the famous area of Beijing known for its antique shops and cultural artifacts. The painting depicted a woman who looked very similar to the one he had seen on the boat. On the painting, there was a poem that read: "Freshly made up, she comes out from behind the curtain. Together, we count how many flowers in the courtyard have bloomed. I am more understanding than you, but still not as wise. I did not lightly leave the painted terrace." Lü did not know about the story of "敬君," and he was confused and unable to understand, so he bought the painting for a thousand coins. That year, he was appointed as a county magistrate in Jiangxi Province and got along very well with his colleague Shen, who was also from Suzhou. One day, Shen visited Lü's study and saw the painting, greatly surprised, saying, "This is a portrait of my deceased wife, painted by me personally. In the past years in the capital city, I lost a box, and thus lost this painting."

Did you not obtain it at the city gate market?" Lü replied, "Then I have seen your wife." He then told him about the encounter in the boat in Wu Gate. Shen said, "No, no. My wife passed away a year ago." Lü asked, "If so, why is the resemblance so striking?" Shen said, "This must be my aunt. My father-in-law had two daughters who looked the same, even family members could not distinguish between them. The elder is my deceased wife, and the one you saw is her younger sister." Lü then recounted the dream to him. Shen said, "My aunt was indeed waiting to be married, and I will arrange the match for you." Finally, he arranged the marriage through intermediaries, and it became a tale of beauty. According to the *Shuo Yuan* (Book of Sayings), King Qi of Qi built the Nine-Tiered Terrace, recruited talented painters from the country, and rewarded them with money. There was a man named Jing Jun who often suffered from hunger and cold, and his wife was beautiful. Jing Jun was skilled in painting terraces and coveted the reward money, so he was away from home for a long time. He missed his wife and painted her likeness, smiling at it. The story of Jing Jun used by Shen is this. However, this story is found in the *Yiwen Leiju* (Classified Collection of Literature and Art) but not in the current edition of the *Shuo Yuan*.

A Man from Lai'an County, Chuzhou Prefecture One night, a man named Liu from Lai'an County, Chuzhou Prefecture, got up to go to the toilet. He heard someone speaking from outside the wall: "We have not eaten for three days, and the gods have promised us that we can be fully fed by the betrothed daughter of the Zhang family in the village tomorrow. You and the third son can also partake, please do not go elsewhere." Liu was surprised by the words and climbed the ladder to look. He saw two wolves sitting face to face outside the wall, and he was greatly frightened and descended. Thinking of the Zhang family's betrothed daughter, who was his clan's daughter, he went to inform her early in the morning. The girl was just thirteen years old, and that day she closed the door and did not leave. Her older sister-in-law laughed and told her, "You are just being fooled; how could there be wolves that speak like humans?" By evening, the older sister-in-law sent someone to fetch firewood for the evening meal, but the girl refused. The older sister-in-law then went herself to fetch it and looked around, saying, "Where is the wolf?" Before she finished speaking, one wolf emerged from the pile of firewood, charged at the older sister-in-law and knocked her down, entered the house, and carried the girl away. The older sister-in-law screamed, and neighbors gathered. They saw that the wolf was still nearby and chased after it. They were about to catch up, when suddenly two more wolves appeared from the side of the road, and they all fled together.

Wu Binxiang, a woman from a scholarly family in Hunan's沅陵 County, was orphaned at a young age and lived with her uncle in Wu Ling. In the fourth year of Xianfeng (1854), when the Taiping rebels arrived, her relatives all fled, but she stayed with her uncle in the besieged city. When the city fell, she urged her uncle to leave quickly. Her uncle replied, "Your mother entrusted you to me. How can I face your mother in the afterlife if I abandon you?" She responded, "I have been a burden to you for a long time. How could I bear to be a burden to you again in this critical moment?" She then went into the room and committed suicide by hanging. Several days later, the rebels entered her residence. It was a hot summer, and they were surprised that her body had not decomposed after so long. Upon examining her, they found that her clothes were still neatly sewn, and on her chest was a poem: "The city fell, and it was a difficult time for me. The journey was dangerous, but my resolve became even more steadfast. The reputation for integrity is valued for a thousand autumns. The words 'life' and 'death' are light. I have not yet repaid the kindness of my sovereign and parents. I am ashamed to harbor feelings of love and affection. I wish to end my life with a three-foot length of rope, to complete my hundred years of life." In Yuanjiawan, Hankou, there was an elderly carpenter who was sixty-three years old. His two sons had not yet married and both followed their father's profession, but they did not get along well. One day, due to a quarrel, the older brother raised an axe and struck his younger brother's head, killing him instantly. The neighbors wanted to tie up the older brother and take him to the authorities. The father wept and said, "I am an old man with only these two unworthy sons. Now the younger son has died an unnatural death, and the older son will also face legal consequences. What can I rely on in my old age?" Moved by his words, the people dispersed, and the matter ultimately ended. I have read in Ji Wen Da's notes about a similar incident, showing that there are situations in the world where both emotion and reason are at odds. The law allows the wife and children of a serious prisoner to enter the prison. Perhaps this can be adapted to such situations?

The Shan You Hou Family was immensely wealthy at the beginning of the dynasty, and it is said that the origin of their wealth was quite extraordinary. There was a couple who were poor and childless, yet they prayed to the gods every day, wishing for unexpected wealth, even if it meant dying without regret. For several decades, their prayers went unanswered, and by then, the couple had grown old. One night, as they were about to sleep, they suddenly heard a sound like a pot overflowing, and upon looking, they saw gold ingots spilling all over the ground. Overjoyed, they grabbed the ingots, and the more they took, the more there was, until they could no longer take any more, and both of them collapsed from exhaustion. There was a nephew, who was young, orphaned, and not yet married, living nearby and often helping them with cooking and fetching water. On that day, when he arrived, the door was not opened, and when he knocked, there was no response. He climbed over the wall and looked inside, only to find the old man crushed to death by the gold ingots, and the old woman barely alive, still able to speak, but she died shortly after. The nephew buried them, took possession of all their wealth, and became a wealthy man, but his fortune began to decline at the end of the Qianlong era.

The Kingdom of England had a fleet ship named *Tiaoxianni*, which set sail for Aden in the middle of the fourth month of the 13th year of the Tongzhi era. After more than sixty days of sailing, a fire broke out on the ship and could not be extinguished. The ship's crew then launched three small boats to allow people to escape for their lives, and a total of thirty-one people boarded the small boats. After several days, one boat lagged behind, and looking ahead, the other two boats were no longer visible. The supplies of food were all exhausted, and two people died, leaving six people alive. They only drank brackish water to survive. One person proposed an idea: "Dying together would be of no benefit; if one person dies so that five others can survive, this is also sacrificing oneself for righteousness." They then discussed using one person as food, cutting wood into sticks, and drawing the shortest stick to determine who would be killed and eaten. Soon after, the person who proposed the idea drew the shortest stick.

There was a sailor named Agas Smolits who, having drunk too much saltwater, feared he could not survive and offered to die in place of his companions. However, the one who drew the short straw refused. They then killed him, drank his blood, and ate his flesh, and all five survived. Several months later, another large British steamship caught fire off the coast of Africa. Two people escaped in a small boat, drifting with the wind for several days without reaching land. They were starving and, in desperation, ate their companion to prolong their lives. These two incidents also illustrate the dangers of sea travel. According to the *Wu Li Lun* by Yang Quan of the Jin dynasty, during the late Han period, there was a man named Guan Qiuyang who, along with his brother and a companion, fled during a time of chaos. Rained out and running out of food, he told his brother, "If we do not eat the companion, all three of us will die." They then killed their companion, obtained food, and reached safety. Later, they were pardoned and found not guilty. Kong Wenju argued that eating the companion was justified, but Liang Yuandi, in his *Jin Lou Zi*, criticized this view as contrary to proper conduct. Indeed, the weak being prey to the strong is a matter of animal behavior; if people resort to cannibalism when starving, what distinguishes them from animals?

A Man from Hua Ting Named Gu Bin Cao Gu Bin Cao, a man from Hua Ting, was young and intelligent, and both his parents loved him extraordinarily. In the eleventh year of the Xianfeng era, he accompanied his mother to Shanghai to avoid the war, and fell ill and died. On his deathbed, he pulled his mother's clothes and requested that his second brother's son, Li Chu, be adopted as his heir. His mother wept and agreed to it. Shortly after, the mother also died. When the chaos subsided and they returned home, the other brothers intended to follow the mother's command, but because Bin Cao had not married, he could not have an heir. Fortunately, there was a daughter of the Qian family from Jinshan who died before being betrothed, and she was about the same age as Bin Cao. They then arranged a marriage, following the wedding ceremony to welcome her coffin back, and buried her together with Bin Cao's tomb. Those who were fond of such things composed a poem about the wedding preparations. It should be noted that such things have existed since ancient times. To move a grave and marry a deceased person, the matchmaker's role is to enforce the clear prohibition, and Zhang Shi, the Agricultural Official, commented on the *Rites of Zhou* saying 'the current practice of marrying a deceased person' is correct. The Han Dynasty indeed had this custom. Emperor Wei Wu arranged for the marriage and burial of a deceased daughter of the Zhen family for the Duke of Deng, simply following the common practice.

A daughter of the Yuan family in Suzhou had been betrothed to a son of the Wu family, but before she could be married, she encountered the Gengshen calamity. Both families fled for safety. When the chaos subsided, the girl returned with her father’s brothers. Her father, who had previously been a merchant, resumed his old trade and accumulated considerable wealth. Meanwhile, the Wu family had suffered total destruction—its members perished, and only an elderly widow remained, who was the girl’s mother-in-law. The girl requested of her father that she be allowed to marry into the Wu family and serve her mother-in-law. Her father refused and planned to marry her off to another family, but the girl cut off her hair in a vow of determination, and so he did not force her. In the thirteenth year of the Tongzhi reign (1874), a relative of the Wu family returned from Shaanxi and said that he had been captured along with the Wu son, who was taken to Jiangxi, where the Wu son died on a certain date. Upon hearing this, the girl was deeply grieved and said to her father, “Today I shall follow my son’s wishes. If you do not allow it, I shall die.” Her father, unwilling to refuse, gave her in marriage to the Wu family. This woman did not change her heart in the face of poverty, wealth, or death—truly, she was a virtuous woman. During the Ming dynasty, Gu Xi Fu wrote an essay strongly criticizing the notion that an unmarried woman who remains chaste after her husband’s death is virtuous. More recently, Wang Rongfu also supported this view. I, however, do not agree. The *Biographies of Exemplary Women* says: “The widow of Wei was the daughter of the Duke of Qi, and she married into the state of Wei. When she reached the city gate, the lord of Wei had died. She performed the three-year mourning period and composed a poem saying: ‘My heart is not a stone, it cannot be turned. My heart is not a mat, it cannot be rolled up.’” If we were to follow the views of Gu and Wang, then why would the sages have included this poem of the Wei woman in their records? I recall that when I was in Shanghai, my colleague Ying Minzhi showed me his account of the story of Zhang the Chaste Girl. I wrote notes on it afterward, explaining this matter in detail. These notes are now included in my collected writings.

In the outskirts of Kai Ping County, Guangdong, there is a village named Zhangqiao, inhabited by the Zhang family, a prominent clan. There was a man named Zhang who had engaged a daughter of the Yang family, but he died before the marriage could take place. The Yang woman entered her husband's home in mourning attire, paid respects to her father-in-law and mother-in-law, built a house near her husband's tomb, and stayed for three days before returning. The Zhang family then chose a close relative to be the heir, and the Yang woman raised the child until he grew up and arranged for him to marry a daughter of the Su family. Just before the wedding, the son died. The Su woman requested to go to her husband's family and put on mourning clothes. The Yang woman said, "This is another instance like mine; this matter is not easy." She firmly refused, but the Su woman insisted, and ultimately, the Yang woman agreed to her wishes. Both the mother-in-law and daughter-in-law remained chaste widows, which is indeed a difficult feat. It is said that the Su woman's son has already made a name for himself in the schools, perhaps as a reward for the two chaste widows? Outside Suzhou City, there lived a girl from a small family who had been engaged to a man since her youth. When she grew up, her parents both died, and she lived alone with her brother. She heard that her fiancé was a wastrel and had fallen into disrepute, and she despised him, harboring thoughts of breaking off the engagement. She gave her brother ten silver dollars, which she had saved from her needlework, and instructed him to give it to the original matchmaker to repay the dowry and request the return of the marriage contract. Her brother, being a good-for-nothing, spent all the money on gambling and forged a marriage contract to deceive his sister. The girl did not know about this, believing that the engagement had truly been broken off. She indicated to a neighboring woman to act as a matchmaker and arranged to marry a man from the Wang family on Hulong Street in the city. Her former fiancé, learning that she had married the Wang family, plotted to kidnap her. However, the Wang family was prepared, and the plan failed. That same day, the matter was taken to court. The judge investigated the truth and, after beating her brother with a hundred lashes, ruled that the girl should return to her former fiancé.

According to law and propriety, it should indeed be so, yet if a woman cannot settle in her household, it can be predicted. In the Dashi village of Panyu, there lived a man named Mr. A, who had three sons and one daughter. His second son died young. In a certain year, he married his eldest son to a woman from the He family, and arranged for his daughter to marry a son of the Chen family, who was also a cousin of the He family. The woman and the girl got along very well. Once, when the girl was embroidering, the woman saw it and said happily, "Skillful to this extent, you may be called a needle god! What a pity if you have talent but no luck!" The girl found her words strange and asked her why she said that. The woman replied, "Your sister-in-law's husband is a distant relative of a poor family, although he is as beautiful as jade, he indulges in promiscuous behavior without restraint, has contracted a venereal disease, and damaged his body." Upon hearing this, the girl refused to eat for several days. Her mother asked her about it, and she told her. The mother said, "Perhaps the news was wrong." But the girl believed her sister-in-law's words and, one day, cut off her own hair. The mother was greatly shocked, called her wife over, blamed her for talking too much, and the woman was also greatly shocked, saying, "My mother-in-law is not being fair to me! I love my sister-in-law like a sister, so I hinted indirectly, but my sister-in-law did this extreme action immediately. Now that my husband's mother is blaming me, how can I bear it!" Mr. A and his wife then sent the matchmaker to the groom's family to request the termination of the engagement. The groom's family refused and instead wanted to sue Mr. A for breaking the engagement. Mr. A and his wife investigated the cause of the incident and scolded the woman daily. The woman could no longer endure it and also cut off her hair and threw it on the ground, saying, "I, the new wife, know I have sinned. May I observe a long-term vegetarian diet and sew Buddhist images with my younger sister to atone for my sin, is that acceptable?" Thus, the whole family was in a state of mourning. Mr. A went on a journey, urged them to return, gently persuaded his wife and sister-in-law, and still sent someone to humbly request the termination from the Chen family. The Chen family, unwillingly, agreed to it.

This woman did not understand the meaning of the poem "Yi" from the Han dynasty, and it was very difficult to correct her, and she also did not know what would happen to her in the end. Li Shengshi, known as Kenting, was from Shaoguan Prefecture in Guangdong Province. Originally a poor man's son, he became wealthy through hoarding goods, and in his old age, he unexpectedly became a wealthy man. Throughout his life, he encountered many things related to the number nine. His father was forty-nine years old when he gave birth to Reng Wu, and he died at sixty-nine. Reng Wu started learning trade at nineteen and returned home to live out his days at fifty-nine. He had nine sons and nine daughters. At sixty-nine, his second wife was thirty-nine and gave birth to another son. Soon after, his fourth son died, but he still had nine sons. Each son married one wife, and none took concubines, and the husbands of the daughters also did not take concubines. There were nine sons, nine wives, nine daughters, and nine sons-in-law, with nineteen grandchildren and twenty-nine grandnieces and grandnephews. On the ninth day of the first month of the first year of Guangxu's reign, it was his ninetieth birthday. This is also a rare blessing of the prosperous era. A woman in the north of the river had a bad relationship with her mother-in-law and often quarreled. One day, the woman swallowed a gold ring and tried to commit suicide, writhing in pain. Someone taught her to use burned and ground sheep leg bones mixed with food to take. The next day, the gold ring came out through her stool, and she was fine. Zhang Shaqu told me this, so I wrote it down to spread its fame.

A family named A lived in moderate prosperity, but they frequently encountered lawsuits that depleted their wealth, leading to a buildup of resentment that turned into illness, almost causing them to die. A beggar knocked on their door, claiming he could cure them. They invited him in to examine the patient, who had not eaten for three days and was on the verge of death, though there was still a faint movement in his abdomen. The beggar said, "There is something inside, which I cannot treat; I must ask my teacher to come." He set a period of twenty days. The family responded, "We cannot guarantee survival day or night; can we extend it to another ten days?" The beggar gave them a medicine, saying, "Take this, and you will be able to wait." After taking the medicine, the patient seemed to be in a deep sleep, and although he did not eat, he did not die. After twenty days, a monk came, saying, "The beggar recommended me." He examined the patient and took a pill from his bowl, tying it to an iron wire and inserting it into the patient's throat, then pulling it back and massaging his abdomen. After a long time, the patient's mouth began to move slightly, and the monk massaged his abdomen more vigorously while continuing to pull the iron wire. Suddenly, he pulled it out, and a small snake followed the medicine out, falling to the ground. The monk sprinkled a little more medicine on the snake, and it turned into water. The patient was completely cured. Someone asked the monk where the snake came from, and he replied, "It was the accumulation of toxic resentment. I not only treat the illness but also dissipate the resentment, so that the person involved in the lawsuit will not be entangled in karmic retribution in future lives." The family offered a generous reward, but the monk refused and left. Could this monk and beggar be divine or Buddhist incarnations?

A Monk from a Temple in Suzhou Every year, a monk from a temple in Suzhou held a Buddhist assembly, entrusting a neighbor, an old woman, to attract many devout believers. All who attended the assembly paid incense money, amounting to hundreds of coins, which the monk and the old woman divided equally. The old woman became increasingly wealthy over time, while the monk remained as poor as before. On New Year's Eve of one year, those demanding payment gathered at the temple's three gates, and the monk, unable to respond, eventually fled away. The following year, the locals appointed another monk to lead the assembly. On the evening of the Lantern Festival, they continued the tradition, still entrusting the old woman to act as an intermediary to attract attendees. After the event, the monk demanded payment from the old woman, but she procrastinated and refused for a long time. One day, the monk returned from outside, about to enter his room, when he heard a sound. Entering the room, he found someone on the bed, covered with a quilt and lying down. Calling out and asking, he discovered it was the old woman. He asked why she was there, but she did not respond. The monk, about to drag her out, just as he lifted the quilt, found the old woman naked, not wearing a single thread. Greatly shocked, he hastened out, went to the old woman's house, and called her son. At first, the son seemed not to believe, but the monk forcefully pulled him there. Knowing the matter was not going well, the old woman turned around and accused the monk.

A monk entered the kitchen, took a knife, and cut off one of his fingers. Enduring the pain, he showed it to them and said, "This is to prove my innocence!" The woman and her son staggered away. He then realized that the previous monk had fallen into the woman's trap. If one had the steadfastness of the latter monk, they would be a Buddha. There was a scholar-official who failed his examination and returned to the south. He fell ill at an inn, having brought no servants or attendants with him. Only a coachman accompanied him. After being ill for over a month, he recovered, but he owed the innkeeper and the coachman tens of strings of cash and had no way to pay. The coachman said, "Since you have no means to travel, and staying here is also not an option, there is a guesthouse nearby. I have arranged for you through someone, and it should work out. Why don't you go there, save some money, and then plan your return? What do you think?" The scholar-official was pleased and agreed. Soon after, the coachman came to tell him, "The arrangement has been made. However, it is still over a hundred miles away. Tomorrow, I will drive you there." At dawn, they set out on their journey. The path they took was far from the usual. At first, they passed through fields with intersecting paths, then through winding mountain trails, occasionally passing small villages that they did not recognize. After traveling for three days, they finally reached his home. The walls were carved, the buildings were grand, and the layout was very extensive. However, there were not many people, and no one spoke to him.

The coachman led him in, passing through more than ten layers of houses, until they reached a courtyard where flowers and trees were lush and dense, with bright windows and clean tables. He then told him, "This is where you will stay. Your duty is only to handle writing and calligraphy. The master has just gone out, and there are no writing materials either. You can just stay here, there's no need to worry. The money you owe has already been paid off for you, do not worry about it. Only there are not many servants here, it is difficult to find someone to serve. There is only a wheel plate on the wall to order food. If you need anything, knock on the plate and speak, and you will get it." After saying this, he left. The scholar-gentleman lived alone in this room, with a plentiful supply of food. However, there was no one around, and he felt very suspicious. Occasionally, he went out for a walk, and then all the rooms were locked, preventing him from entering. Only one room was not locked, and upon entering it, he found more than ten cabinets of books. He took one volume back to pass the time. The cover had a large 'water' character, and it contained many talismans and incantations, which were not understandable and very uninteresting.

Tomorrow, I planned to go and exchange it, but I lost its location and brought it back, temporarily placing it on the desk. After a few months, the north wind warned of the coming cold, and all the leaves fell off, making my longing for home very intense. Casually drinking tea, I opened the book with my hands and saw a talisman twisted like a snake, with a spell beside it. Playfully, I dipped my finger in the tea cup and drew the talisman, reciting the spell. Suddenly, I felt myself in a large body of water, with waves surging and boundless shores. Greatly afraid, I expected to die, so I temporarily closed my eyes and listened to where it took me. After a meal, I suddenly walked on flat ground, and my clothes and shoes were not wet. There were passersby on the road, and I asked them what place this was. They said, "It is Shaoxing Prefecture." I was very shocked. The scholar-official is originally from Wu, not far from home, and just happens to have a fellow student who is an official in Yue. I then went to visit him, helping him with money and returning home. After arriving home, my wife and children greeted and asked, "You were staying at a certain place, why suddenly say you're returning?" They asked how they knew this. I said, "A few months ago, a guest brought a hundred gold pieces and said that you were mending your skin, sending it home to provide for daily expenses, and because of busy affairs, there was no letter." The scholar-official finds it even more strange and tells the story. He also worries about being followed and moves the family to avoid it, later finding nothing else. Someone says that the talisman is a water遁 method of the magical arts, and the scholar-official cannot remember it anymore, and also dare not try it.

During the Jiaqing era, there was a candidate for office who attended the selection process and was assigned the position of magistrate in a certain county of Guizhou. He took his family with him to assume the post, accompanied by many attendants. Upon arriving at the provincial capital, he paid his respects to the provincial governor, after which a colleague told him, "This county is exceptionally poor and difficult; it would be absolutely impossible to bring your family there." He therefore left his family in the capital city and traveled alone to the prefectural city, where he met the prefectural governor. The governor said, "The place you are to govern is not a human habitat. Fortunately, the seal of office is here with me; you may receive it and need not go there. My staff currently lacks a recorder, and since you are a scholar, you must be skilled in writing and calligraphy. Would you be willing to serve temporarily in this role?" The magistrate thought to himself that although the post was meager, it was still better than being a guest in the governor’s office, and so he was determined to go. The governor then ordered the chief county official to prepare carriages and attendants, and also hired someone who had previously been to the county to guide the way. After traveling for several days, they lost their way, and everywhere they went, they encountered only Miao and other ethnic minorities, none of whom could be asked for directions. Estimating the distance, it should not have been more than five or six hundred li, yet they had traveled for ten or so days and still had not arrived. One day, the guide said, "The path ahead seems to be the right one." So they continued forward together. Upon arriving, they found themselves surrounded by high walls, as if it were a city. Inside, brambles and thorns blocked the path, making travel quite difficult.

For a long time, they saw several earthen houses, with damaged walls bearing inscriptions. Upon careful examination, they realized it was a certain county. In surprise, they exclaimed, "Could this be the government office?" They called out to the door, and someone emerged from the grass. When asked who he was, he identified himself. The man bowed his head and said, "I am the county official. There has been no county magistrate for over twenty years, and I did not expect to see you again today." They asked if there were any servants, and he replied, "There were indeed such things in the past, but now there has been no official for a long time. These people have all dispersed to various villages and have not been heard from for a long time; I do not know if they are still alive or dead." He then led them inside, where they found several feet of accumulated soil in the room, which he identified as the public desk. Inside, there were numerous abandoned graves. They asked who these people were, and the official replied, "They are all the previous county magistrates who died here and could not be returned to their hometowns for burial. We dug holes and buried them." The magistrate felt disheartened upon hearing this and decided to return immediately. Upon reaching the prefecture, the governor blamed him for having initially insisted on going, reproached him, and exiled him for several years before finally allowing him to be transferred to a less distant place. This story was heard from a friend named Wu Wenna in the capital, who could also name the county, but the details are now unclear. Such things may indeed occur in the remote and desolate frontier, or perhaps the storyteller is exaggerating?

In the southern region, the speaker addresses a specific moment, saying that a low-ranking official who travels far away has a solitary and precarious existence, with matters that are beyond inquiry. The state has a regulation that officials cannot hold positions within 500 miles of their hometown. The speaker suggests that even a low-ranking official serving far from home is pitiful and proposes imposing a stricter rule that officials should not be allowed to serve more than 1,000-2,000 miles away from their hometown. During the reigns of Emperors Qianlong and Jiaqing, there was a certain individual, a county magistrate, who traveled to Southern Yunnan. He died shortly after taking up his post. He had no children, and only one wife, one sister-in-law, one servant, and one old woman remained. After a while, the sister-in-law also died, and the servant and the old woman left, leaving the wife alone. She was still young and beautiful, living in a rented house. After a long time, she had no food and earned a living by washing clothes. Her hands bled, but she could not escape hunger and cold. One day, a neighbor, an old woman who was involved in matchmaking or social services related to officials, passed by and said to her, "Why are you suffering so much?"

There is a strategy that can temporarily alleviate hunger and cold—would you be willing to follow it?” The woman asked what the strategy was. The old woman replied: “The new county official is a young man who is frivolous and seeks fame for his learning. He enjoys flogging prostitutes, and always strips them of their clothes. The prostitutes are ashamed of this, and though they try to bribe their way out, they fail. They also offer large sums to find someone to take their place, but no one is found. If you can take their place before the magistrate, I will make an agreement with the other prostitutes: each flogging earns a thousand coins. The five hundred officials all accept bribes from the prostitutes, so the floggings are always light. Moreover, while the body is there, the name is not, and you won’t be ashamed in the afterlife.” The woman, impoverished and desperate, ultimately followed the plan. Afterwards, whenever a prostitute needed to appear before the magistrate, they all came to this woman to arrange it. The officials in the county all knew her, and they all laughed at her for her foolishness and lack of shame. But the woman eventually accumulated over two hundred gold pieces, and she used the money to return and bury her husband. I say that although she suffered great humiliation, she did not lose her virtue, and cannot be called unchaste. She did not spare her parents' body to return and bury her husband's remains, and cannot be called unjust. Gentlemen should pity her resolve and lament her fate, and not harshly criticize her.

In a rural area of Jinling, there lived an old man who had a son who was married to a woman from a neighboring village. During a period of military unrest, he lost his son. The old man, who was a trader by profession, traveled extensively around the four directions and adopted a little boy who was of similar age to his original son. He then adopted him as his own son and named him after his original son. When Jinling was recovered, the old man continued his trading activities outside, while the woman from the neighboring village had returned. As her daughter was growing up, she was to be married off. The old man was informed of this through a letter, and he thought that although he had lost his original son, he had a foster son who could serve as a substitute. He wrote a reply, agreeing to bring his son back during the summer or autumn of that year. When the time came, the son indeed arrived, but their old home was no longer there, so they rented a house to prepare for the wedding. Both families had already made arrangements, and the wedding was scheduled. Suddenly, the old man's original son returned, but he could not find his home and went to the neighboring village to seek out his father-in-law. The father-in-law was greatly surprised and personally escorted him to the old man's house. The son saw his parents, tore at their clothes, and cried bitterly. The old man was in a dilemma: if he did not recognize him, then he was truly his son; if he recognized him, he would be embarrassed by the father-in-law. So he said, "This is my brother's son, not my own son. He has been lost since childhood, and he doesn't remember accurately." The father-in-law returned home and became suspicious.

Soon after investigating, the truth was found, and he sent the matchmaker to inform him: "The one who originally betrothed my daughter is my son-in-law. How can someone else's son become my daughter's husband?" The old man was at a loss, but his adopted son knew the matter and it was not harmonious. After midnight, he carried a knife and went out, suddenly entering his mother-in-law's house. The father-in-law came out, and the adopted son grabbed his sleeve with his left hand, while holding a knife in his right hand and pointing it at him, saying: "If you do not marry me, I will first kill you and your daughter. Then I will return and kill the groom, and I will also commit suicide. All four of us will die on the same day!" Seeing the urgency, the father-in-law asked to proceed as scheduled. The adopted son replied, "It cannot be delayed, the matter is for tonight!" The father-in-law said, "Then you should let me go inside, and prepare my daughter for a bit. You may sit down for a while." After the father-in-law went inside, they planned to gather strong servants to bind and send him back. His wife said, "It is not a good plan."

If so, wouldn't he hate us even more? Our son-in-law and daughter will surely die at his hands." "What then should we do?" The wife replied, "He has a stepson; why can't we have a stepdaughter?" Then she secretly sent her daughter through the back door to her uncle's house and dressed a maid to stay in the blue tent, bringing the groom in to perform the wedding rituals. After the marriage was completed, she then told him, "You didn't tell anyone and got married; your father will surely be angry. Don't rush to say you're leaving. How about staying here for a full month?" He happily said, "Yes." The father-in-law sent a matchmaker to tell Mr. So-and-so, "Your son has already married my daughter. You still have your brother's son, and I also have my younger brother's daughter. Can we match them again?" The father-in-law understood his intention and made his son a son-in-law at his daughter's uncle's house. After a full month, the father-in-law chose a good day to send the two sons-in-law and two daughters back together. They each got along well with their spouses and lived peacefully thereafter.

A man from Guangchang, Jiangxi, named Qiu, read books as a child but struggled in the imperial examinations for children. One day, he suddenly dreamed of arriving in the underworld, where an official was seated at a desk adjudicating cases. Seeing Qiu, the official said, "This is a scholar who will pass as tenth place. Why are you here so soon?" He then beckoned Qiu out, and Qiu awoke, telling others about the dream. Someone said, "You will surely pass as the tenth candidate and enter the academy." Qiu thought to himself, "If I indeed pass as the tenth candidate, I can just call myself a scholar. Why would they call me a tenth-place scholar? This cannot be a good omen." He then abandoned his studies and became a merchant, serving as a clerk for a fellow villager who sold tea. He traveled to the capital in spring and returned in autumn, making this an annual routine, and he gave up on further advancement. One year, on his way home, he passed through the prefectural capital, where the prefectural examination was taking place. His classmates and relatives were all there, urging him to take the exam. Qiu refused, but they had already registered him in the county and submitted his papers to the prefecture. Reluctantly, he took the exam. When the prefecture issued the final list, they called for a re-examination, and Qiu was indeed ranked tenth. Seeing this, Qiu was filled with shock and unease, feeling unwell, and decided not to take the re-examination. Before he could return home, he died. Alas! The prefectural re-examination was such a major event, yet his name was already recorded in the records of the underworld.

Shen Baoyue of Tongxiang, courtesy name Maoting, was wealthy and fond of giving. Whenever he encountered good deeds, he would bravely undertake them, earning a reputation as a virtuous person in both far and near places. During the chaos of the Gengshen and Xinyou years, he fled to avoid bandits, and dozens of his relatives and in-laws followed him. Enduring hardship and cramped conditions, they never parted, and people especially praised him for this. He had no son at first, but after middle age, he took a concubine and had two sons. A neighbor who was careless with fire saw his house burn, but the wind changed direction, sparing him; everyone said it was a reward for doing good. However, in the winter of the first year of Guangxu, he was crushed to death by a collapsing wall. Those who previously called him virtuous could not help but doubt him by then. Yan Zhisheng, a scholar-official and his friend, asked me to write an article to dispel doubts. I based my argument on the *Yuan Shenqi* (The Book of Divine Contracts) theory of fate and Wang Chong's *Lunheng* (Critical Essays) discussion of the illusory nature of fortune and misfortune, and wrote an article to respond, elaborating in a thousand words. However, I do not agree with Wang Chong's notion of the illusory nature of fortune and misfortune.

The text of *Fu Yu* and *Yu Qing*, concerning calamity and blessing, is recorded in the *Zhouyi*; the principle of correspondence between heaven and humanity is fully detailed in the *Spring and Autumn Annals*. If blessings and misfortunes were all illusory, then there would be no basis for encouragement or punishment. At the same time, there happened to be the case of Zhang Shaoyu. Zhang Shaoyu's given name is Yuli, and in the first year of Guangxu, he was appointed by edict as a county magistrate to participate in the maritime transport mission. All those involved in maritime transport went to Shanghai and boarded steamships to proceed. There was a steamship named "Fuxing" that was scheduled to depart, and most of the officials from the Jiangsu Maritime Transport Bureau boarded this ship. Zhang Shaoyu had initially planned to join, but just as another steamship departed two days before "Fuxing," Zhang Shaoyu changed his plan and boarded the other ship. His fellow officials tried to persuade him, but he did not change his mind. Later, Zhang Shaoyu safely arrived at Dinggu, while the "Fuxing" sank at sea, and all those on board perished. Zhang Shaoyu was someone who usually enjoyed doing good deeds. In the second year of Guangxu, when Zhang Shaoyu was fifty years old, he asked me for advice.

I have completed the translation of the source text. Here is the final, polished translation: I then said that Shaoqu had done good deeds and received blessings, and should strive even harder while avoiding Wang Chong's views. I feared that future readers might have doubts about this, so I gathered and recorded these events in this compilation. There was an elderly scholar in Hangzhou who earned a living by teaching. He had a wife and a daughter, and his home had an extra room, which he rented to a man named "A." After living there for several years, A moved to another place, but they still communicated as usual, and their families continued to visit each other. One day, A sent two people to carry a sedan chair to welcome his daughter away. By evening, the elderly scholar returned from his teaching post, but his daughter had not yet come back. He sent someone to A's residence to fetch her, but found it empty with no one there. When he asked his neighbors, they said that A and his entire family had left before noon, and no one knew where they had gone. The elderly scholar could find no trace of them, and fearing that this would bring shame to his family name, he did not pursue the matter further. The daughter had already been betrothed, so he informed her fiancé's family that she had died suddenly, leaving her fate to be decided by them. A took the daughter to Wuxi, Jiangsu, and claimed her as his wife.

A Poor Man's Wife and Her Rescue There was a man named Mr. A, who was originally a poor and lazy person, unable to support himself, and thus relied on his wife for sustenance. His wife worked as a seamstress in the household of a bureaucrat in Suzhou, earning two silver dollars a month to give to her husband. However, Mr. A was still not satisfied with the amount, and he plotted to sell his wife into a brothel. The woman, upon hearing this, had no means to escape on her own. Suddenly, a man from Zhejiang came to visit her employer, and upon seeing his name card, she recognized him and said, "This is a prominent family from my hometown, Hangzhou, and my father used to teach in this household." She then found out where he lived, went to his house the next day to request an audience, and spoke about her family background and their close familial relationship, presenting her case in a very organized manner. He then arranged for her father to come, filed a lawsuit with the authorities, beat and expelled Mr. A, and ruled that the daughter be returned to her father. An elderly scholar looked gloomy and unhappy and said, "If this daughter returns, it will disgrace our family." Mr. Bu Mei, also from Hangzhou and serving in Suzhou, who was previously acquainted with the elderly scholar, then arranged a match for this daughter in Wu (Suzhou area) and married her to an official. I heard this story from Mr. Bu Mei, and because it is not a praiseworthy matter, I do not record their names.

The Tangxi family of the Lao surname was wealthy. They had a son who was about seven or eight years old but still could not speak or walk. Their family built a nursery hall. On the day the hall was completed, the child began to speak, and after two more years, he could walk. Now he is studying and visiting schools. The retribution is precise and without error in this case. My second wife, a daughter of the Yao family from Tangxi, returned from her parents' home this spring. She mentioned that last year her sister-in-law fell seriously ill. During her illness, she leaped from her bed, reaching the top of the bed, and a piece of iron bent in her hand as if it were dry bamboo. Her brother, Jinqing, knew that evil spirits had attached themselves to her and performed a long bow to her, placing several foreign coins before her and saying, "I will use these to buy paper money, burn it for you, and you can return." The sick woman said, "Yes. But I am from Hangzhou, so you must take me to a riverboat." Jinqing followed her instructions, burning paper money as they went, and when they reached the boat, he told her, "This is a riverboat from Hangzhou." Suddenly, a whirlwind arose beside them, causing her to tremble. When she returned home, her illness was gone. How strange! Do spirits also travel by boat and carriage like humans? Supervisor of the Zujing Academy, Shen Lanfang, is from Linping Town and resides in Hangzhou. His daughter-in-law's family had no children, so they raised another person's child as their own, who was from Shaoxing. One day, when his daughter-in-law returned to her parents' home, she suddenly fell ill and spoke in the dialect of Shaoxing, which was the voice of the grandfather of the adopted child. She urgently demanded the return of her grandson.

Suddenly, several voices were heard, all with the accent of Hangzhou, indicating that they were the spirits of her maternal family. They said, "This is unreasonable and disruptive. She has already become a wife of the Shen family. What business does she have with this matter? It is necessary to go to Linping to bring the Shen family people." The next day, the Shen family's spirits arrived in large numbers. Lanfang's grandmother, parents, and another concubine, all with the accent of Linping, appeared just as they had been in life. They said, "We are attached to the boat and have disembarked at Caisi Bridge and come here. With us here, the woman must be safe. However, we cannot stay here long, we should return home." The place was still several miles from where Lanfang was staying. They discussed how to return, and the spirits said, "Still, we will carry her in a sedan chair. Instruct the sedan chair bearers to walk slowly, and rest in the shade when encountered. Also, have someone frequently give her water to drink. There will be no harm." They followed this advice and returned home. The Shen family's spirits followed them, still attached to the sick woman, and engaged in conversation with the family. At that time, Lanfang happened to be in Wu. His family asked if they should summon him back, and the spirits said, "There is no great matter. The new wife's illness will not last more than three days, so there is no need to summon him." The woman's illness indeed recovered, and the spirits' words also fell silent. Lanfang told me this story in great detail, and this is a summary of the events. From these two incidents, it can be seen that spirits live among people and are not different from humans. Zhou Gong wrote in *Jin Teng* that "one can serve ghosts and spirits." Confucius commented on the *Yi Jing* that "one knows the nature of ghosts and spirits." The sages never said that there are no ghosts and spirits. The theory of the Jìn people that there are no ghosts, could it be true?

A woman from a certain family in a village near Hangzhou fell into the hands of bandits during the time of military turmoil and was transported to Shanghai, where she was obtained by a man named Mr. A and they became husband and wife. Mr. A was indeed a barber. When the chaos subsided, her parents found her and brought her back home. Because her husband practiced a lowly profession, they did not approve and planned to pretend she was waiting to be married, so they could choose another husband, but the woman refused. They then summoned her husband, but he had no other skills and relied on his wife for support. The entire family looked down on him, and the woman also treated him with disdain. Feeling depressed and aimless, the husband died after taking raw opium, and the woman eventually remarried. This woman initially seemed to understand the principle of fidelity, as if she understood great righteousness, but her actions were inconsistent throughout, making her a divided person, which is quite strange.

A scholar from Shaoxing, who had taken the imperial examination in Jingzhao but failed, stayed with his cousin's family. One day, he suddenly disappeared and did not return for ten days. When he finally came back, he was wearing only one shirt and one pair of pants, and his face was pale. When asked what he had been doing for the past ten days and why he looked so disheveled, he recounted his experiences in great detail, which were so absurd that they were laughable. It turned out that on that day, he had been wandering into the inner city and came to a narrow alley near the Xisi Pagoda. There, he saw a shop with a sign that read "Sale of Pouches." He knocked on the door to buy a pouch. A young woman came out and showed him one, which was already worn out. He asked if she had any new ones, and she replied, "If you want a new one, please come inside." He went in and sat down with her. She chatted aimlessly, without mentioning the pouch. When he asked about it, she handed him the old one again and said, "This is the only one we have." "Then why did you put a sign on the door to sell?" he asked. The woman smiled and said, "What we sell is not this pouch." He understood her meaning and tried to flirt with her.

The woman said, "My purse is expensive. Do you have this money?" The man immediately unzipped his bag and showed the woman a bill of exchange, which was worth several hundred thousand. At that time, one silver ingot was worth ten thousand coins, so it was also worth dozens of silver ingots. The woman said, "That’s enough." They went into the room together, where she relaxed her clothes and lay down. The man was confused and stopped there for the night. There was only one old woman in the house, who was the woman’s servant, and one young boy, who was her brother. Their daily meals were all dependent on the man. After a few days, the woman’s purse was empty. The woman said, "The purse is empty now." The man said, "This is a minor matter. I can handle it myself." A few days later, the woman said the same thing again, and the man answered in the same way. One day, while they were lying down together, suddenly a man appeared in their courtyard, his voice like a roaring tiger. The woman said, "We’re in trouble! My husband is back. If we don’t leave, he will kill us." She then led the man out through the north window, making him climb over the back wall and leave. The man was in such a hurry that he didn’t have time to dress, so he left with only one shirt and one pair of pants. The onlookers all laughed and said, "This is all a fake setup, but you shouldn’t go back again." The family’s relative then gave him some money and sent him back to the south.

In the alley of Li Jizong outside the Chuan Gate of Suzhou, there lived a man who made a living as a tailor (as recorded in the *Tang Six Codes*, the term "tailor" refers to the process of cutting and sewing to create garments, a more comprehensive term compared to the *Zhou Official*’s "sewing people"). He was over thirty years old and his family was relatively well-off. One day, he happened to see a woman from the north bank of the river, who was quite attractive, and he liked her. Soon after, someone mediated the matter, and they agreed on a dowry of one hundred foreign coins. Once the agreement was made, they chose a day to welcome her with a decorated carriage. Upon arrival, they saw the woman covered with a red scarf, carried by several people into the carriage, and accompanied by a package placed in the carriage. They welcomed her back with music and drums. When they were about to carry her out, she appeared weak and unable to stand, as if her beauty had faded. Upon closer inspection, it turned out to be a straw man. They opened the package and found it to be bricks and stones, likely to prevent suspicion due to the lightness of the carriage. The whole family was greatly surprised and hurriedly sought the original matchmaker, but he had disappeared like a yellow crane. The man stood there in a state of disappointment and disbelief, as if he were a wooden puppet.

A Man from Baoshan Village Named Zou Yubao In his childhood, Zou Yubao was betrothed to a girl from a certain family. Orphaned at a young age, he was raised by the family of the girl he was engaged to, and they treated each other as siblings. After some time, both were around sixteen or seventeen years old. One day, Yubao entered the inner room and saw the girl sitting alone, making sweets. He teased her, asking, "Sweet, isn't it?" She replied, "Yes, it's sweet." Yubao then said, "When it's made, give me one piece to make me sweet too." The girl smiled and nodded in agreement. When the sweets were ready, the parents and the girl ate them together, but the husband was not present. The girl felt disappointed, so she secretly gave one piece to a servant woman to deliver to Yubao. On another day, they met again, and the girl playfully asked, "Sweet, isn't it?" Yubao replied, "I haven't tasted it yet, how would I know if it's sweet!" The girl realized the servant woman had deceived her and scolded her, saying, "Old servant, are you still so greedy for food?" The servant woman retorted, "How about you, being young and secretly interacting with men?" The girl became angry, and they began to scold each other. The parents heard about it and lightly scolded the girl. The girl felt even more ashamed and hanged herself that night. When Yubao heard about her death, he also hanged himself.

During the years of Guangxu's reign, the years of Bingzi and Dingchou, there were two siblings, an elder brother and his sister-in-law from Zhili, who brought along their younger sister to Tianjin to seek work. They had walked to Zizhu Lin when the day was almost over, and they came across a man selling cakes from a small cart, who had just stopped by their side. The sister-in-law was hungry and wanted to eat, so the elder brother gave several dozen coins to buy the cakes, and the couple ate together but did not give any to the younger sister. The younger sister sat beside them and sobbed. The cake seller could not bear to see this, and he pushed his cart over to the girl and said, "My cakes are nearly finished, and what I have left is not much; I will give them all to you, and I will not charge you." After they had all finished eating, the elder brother and sister-in-law stood up and called for the younger sister to go with them. The girl said, "The road ahead is vast and unclear; where will we go? If we go without food, there will be no chance of survival. I have received this kindness of a full meal from this person; it would be better for me to follow him, so as not to be a burden to my elder brother and sister-in-law." The cake seller was happy and said, "I originally had no wife; to get this girl as my wife is how fortunate it is." He then asked the elder brother and sister-in-law, and they replied, "Since both sides wish it, what difference does it make to us?" The cake seller then took the girl in his cart and called for the elder brother and sister-in-law to come with him to his home. The next day, they performed the wedding ceremony, and he cleaned a nearby room for the elder brother and sister-in-law to live in. His family was not very poor; he had two mules, and he gave one to the elder brother to rent out and live off the rent. I think this girl had quite a bit of insight, and this cake seller was also a person of virtue. They met by chance and became husband and wife, which was not an accident. Who knows if their descendants will gradually become prosperous and flourish, forming a large family, and tracing back to its origin, it will become a beautiful story to be passed down.

In the first month of the third year of Guangxu, my fellow scholar-official Zhang Ziqing, governor of [a province], lost his mother, Lady Meng, the Grand Lady, in her residence in Suzhou. Prior to this, in the twelfth month of the second year, Li Meisheng, the Censor, dreamed of going to Zhang's residence. He entered the inner chamber and saw Lady Meng covered with a quilt lying on the bed, while Zhang Ziqing, the governor, sat facing west in front of the bed. There was a person kneeling in front of him. Li asked who he was, and someone told him, "He is the magistrate of Fengdu." Li then asked why he was kneeling, and the person replied, "He is a filial son of the governor, so he is kneeling to ask for something." Li woke up and told others, "Is the governor about to receive the news of a death?" It was not more than twenty days before Lady Meng's death. This was personally told to me by Li Meisheng, who also said, "The magistrate of Fengdu wore a seventh-rank hat but did not wear the official ceremonial robe, perhaps because the mourning attire had not yet been lifted. The person was humble and appeared like a loyal and kind elder. There were about ten followers, and their appearance was not very strange, unlike what is commonly depicted in the world, such as ox-heads and horse-faces, but the dark and gloomy atmosphere was oppressive, and it was extremely frightening." I noted that Fengdu has Pingdu Mountain, which has the Wu Yun Cave, one of the seventy-two blessed places mentioned in Taoist texts, and it should be a dwelling place for immortals. I then examined the *Baopuzi: Chapter on Dealing with the World* and it said, "The power can control Luo Feng." Therefore, the idea that Fengdu governs ghosts has existed since the Jin Dynasty. I thought about the distance from Sichuan to Suzhou, which is thousands of miles, and the magistrate of Fengdu traveled a long way. Is it not too much trouble, and why did he necessarily have to make Li Meisheng see him? Is it because Li Meisheng is from Sichuan and has a connection to his hometown?

There was a person named Jia, a son of a farmer's family. His parents loved him. Because he was weak and could not handle farming work, he had an uncle who opened a medicine shop in the market and made him learn the trade of commerce. His uncle loved drinking and would make him go to the butchery across the street to buy wine every day. At the age of twelve or thirteen, Jia had beautiful eyebrows and eyes. The butcher's wife loved him and often gave him extra wine. In this way, several years passed, and Jia was now sixteen or seventeen. The butcher's wife said to him, "Do you know I love you?" He said, "I know." "Then how can you repay me?" Jia said, "I don't know how to repay you." The woman laughed and said, "It's easy." She then took out wine and meat to share with him. After they had eaten, she invited him upstairs and had relations with him. After that, whenever the butcher was out, she would meet him. The matter was kept secret, and no one knew about it. One year, during the Mid-Autumn Festival, friends from the medicine shop all went out to enjoy the moon, and Jia also went with them. After a while, it started to rain, and all the friends returned, but Jia followed them later. When he reached the shop, the door was already closed.

Jia thought that knocking on the door and entering would surely result in being scolded by his uncle. While hesitating, the butcher's wife just opened the window of the upstairs room to look down. The room was located over the street, and she saw Jia below, gesturing to him with her hand. Jia asked, "Is the butcher here?" She replied, "He went to buy a pig." Then she opened the door to admit him, and he climbed the stairs and slept there. Later, the butcher also encountered rain and returned, calling out at the door. Jia was in a窘境 and said, "What should I do?" The wife said, "It's fine." She made him follow her from behind, and they hid Jia by the door. When the butcher entered, Jia came out, and the butcher didn't know. Jia thought the night had grown deeper, and he knocked on the door to enter again. His uncle scolded him even more severely, so Jia stood under the eaves, waiting for dawn. Soon, the butcher's wife opened the window again and saw Jia was still there. She asked, "Have you not returned yet?" He replied, "Yes. Where is the butcher?" She said, "He's drunk and asleep."
