Jia then left his hat on the floor and touched his own head, extending his hand to make a gesture of taking it. The wife said, "Okay."

Not long after, the door was opened to invite Jia. Jia entered and said, "The butcher is still alive; why did you call me?" The woman replied, "He has already been killed." Jia was shocked and asked, "Why kill him?" The woman said, "You showed me your hand, telling me to kill him, so why ask?" Jia went upstairs and saw the dead person lying plainly on the bed. He asked, "Which knife was used?" The woman replied, "The butcher's knife." "Where is the knife?" Jia asked. "It's under the bed," she said. Jia then took the knife from under the bed, killed the woman, took her hat, and left, returning directly to his home. He lied to his parents, saying, "Just now I went out with my friends to stroll under the moon. We walked a little further, and since we were close to home, I came back for a moment." His parents were pleased and kept him. There was a leather worker who lived near the pharmacy and had long been attracted to the butcher's wife but had not found an opportunity. Early the next morning, he shouldered his load and went out, passing by the butcher's shop. He saw the door slightly open and entered without being questioned. The leather worker knew the butcher had left the previous evening but did not know he had returned. He privately thought that the woman must be sleeping alone upstairs.

He then ascended his tower, where he found the butcher dead on the bed and the woman dead on the ground. Blood flowed and wet his shoes, and he was surprised and ran out, returning home and closing the door. He lay there for a long time. When it was broad daylight, all the shops were open. He saw the butcher shop was open but no one was there. He called out, but no one responded. He entered to investigate the situation and traced the shoe tracks, leading him to the cobbler's door. He then bound and sent the cobbler to the authorities. The cobbler could not bear the beating and falsely confessed. After several days, Mr. A returned, and his friends told him, "The couple from the butcher shop across the street were killed by their neighbor, the cobbler." Mr. A asked, "Is it true?" They replied, "By the time he reached the authorities, he had already confessed, and he will soon be executed in public." Mr. A said, "This was done by me. Why blame the cobbler?" His uncle covered his mouth. Mr. A refused, ran to the county, beat the drum to report, and described the entire incident. The official said, "He is a righteous man." The sentence was reduced, and he ultimately did not die. This event occurred previously in Xin'an, and I heard it from Mr. Cheng's words. It was once recorded, but after many years, the draft was lost, so I could no longer name his name and hometown. The *Biography of Feng Ma Yan* written by Tang's Shen Ya Zhi is quite similar to this. Are there similar events in ancient and modern times?

In Guangdong, there was a son of the Li family. As a child, he read books and took the children's examination but did not pass. He had a nature fond of gambling. His father repeatedly warned him, but he did not repent. His wife, Tien, often clashed with him over this. Soon, Tien gave birth to a son. The father named him Ah Kao. When Ah Kao turned one year old, the father said to Tien, "Your husband has often been with the likes of pig herders, not my son. I named my grandson Ah Kao, intending to abandon my son and rely on my grandson. For your sake, you should also abandon your husband and rely on your son." That day, the father prepared a feast to eat with his son and said, "You must eat heartily, you will be drunk and full, and I will send you to the clear stream." The son knelt down begging for mercy, but was not allowed. He cried and knelt before his wife, begging her to intercede, but Tien did not respond. The father then covered his head with a cloth bag and had a strong servant carry him and throw him into the river. He sank and floated, almost reaching a mile away. Someone rescued him, and it was an old friend from the gambling den. He was then left in the gambling den. After staying there for over a month, Li said to his friend, "Because of my love for gambling, my father no longer considers me his son, and my wife no longer considers me her husband. Now, every day I hear the sounds of dice and cards, which only intensify my hidden pain." He then left and begged for food in the market.

Con can still write, buying his own paper to write couplets, which he then widely distributed to the marketplaces. The people there would slightly compensate him for his writing materials, and although he was essentially begging, he received more than he would have otherwise. He traveled thousands of miles and arrived in a certain county in Shandong Province, where he stayed in a Taoist temple. He fell seriously ill and nearly died, but the old Taoist priest took pity on him, feeding and treating him with medicine. Fortunately, he recovered. The Taoist priest then told him, "Here, they highly regard scholars from the south. I will recruit several children and have you teach them the classics. There will certainly be people who come. This is a steady job for poor scholars, and it is not better than begging?" Li gladly followed the suggestion and was thus able to interact with scholars and officials in the county. There was a man named Zhao who ran an inn to accommodate guests from all directions. Zhao's family was fairly comfortable, but he had no son; he had only one daughter and wanted to find a husband for her, considering him as his own son. However, he had not found the right person for a long time. The Taoist priest then persuaded Zhao, saying, "If your daughter's husband is a local, he may temporarily be your son, but he will eventually take your daughter back home. Li, without a family, is alone and has wandered thousands of miles. If he becomes your son-in-law, he will certainly stay as your son." Zhao approved of this and married Li into his family. After a long time, Li told Zhao, "I see that the scholars and officials here are comparable to me in literary and artistic skills. If I am allowed to take the exam, obtaining a scholar's robe would not be difficult."

The old man was greatly pleased and immediately had his son take the examination under the surname Zhao, to be admitted as a student of the Imperial Academy. The following year, he was selected in the provincial examination and then passed the metropolitan examination to become a Jinshi (imperial scholar). Several years later, he was appointed as the magistrate of a county in Guangdong, taking his wife with him to the post. He served in many busy and demanding positions, and his official salary was abundant. Because he was serving in his hometown, he feared causing gossip and dared not inquire about his family. He served in office for over ten years but was dismissed from office due to official matters and was about to return to Shandong. He took a detour to visit him, but his father had been dead for a long time. His wife and son were still alive, but they were very poor and had no means of living. Li went to their house himself and lied, saying he was an old gambling friend of the Li family, and asked to see his wife. At that time, Li had a full beard, and his appearance was robust; his speech had a northern accent, and the wife did not recognize him. He called his son to look at him, and he was over twenty years old. He asked if he had a wife, and he told him it was because of poverty. Li said with a sigh, "I and Li have been friends since childhood in the gambling house. Now our old friend has passed away, and his wife and children are poor and cold, like the fine silk robe of the Western Han dynasty, which makes one sigh. Though I am a lowly person, I cannot bear to see it." He took out several thousand gold coins from his bag and gave them to him and also arranged a marriage. He married a woman from a neighboring village, and all the wedding expenses were paid by Li. On the day of the temple visit, Li also came.

R's wife personally came to the front hall to bow and thank, and she had her son and daughter-in-law ascend the steps of the memorial tablet to pay respects. In the middle of the hall, a seat was set up, and R was asked to sit there. At the time, all the guests were present, expecting R to show humility, but R sat in a dignified and authoritative manner, receiving their homage. After the bowing, R loudly called out, "Ah Kao! You were young and without a father, so you don't recognize me; why doesn't your mother recognize me either?" R's wife, who was standing by, heard this and was greatly shocked. R then said to her, "I am your husband." He recounted the events of the past, and the guests were all astonished and sighed. R said, "I have been an official here for many years, and now I cannot return to my hometown. I will soon leave you all to go north. The ties of husband and wife, father and son, come to an end here." Upon hearing this, R's wife cried out in despair. Some of the elder guests then spoke up, saying, "Since you have achieved such honor and distinction, why not bring your mother and son back to Shandong with you?" R laughed and said, "The matter of the past was indeed the intention of my father. However, the death of a spouse is the most tragic event in human life. They saw me covering my head with a cloth bag, yet they remained unmoved, showing the coldness of human nature! In Shandong, I already have a wife and children; where would I place these people? They should leave their husband and child behind and follow my father's command. Ah Kao, Ah Kao, treat your mother well." Having said this, R turned away and left without looking back.

When I lived in Linping Town, there was a man named Fan Guifang who lived to the west of me. He used to serve as a county official in Renhe. He once offended the grandson of Grand Secretary Sun Wenjing, named Gu Yun Xi Bo, and was publicly punished by wearing a wooden collar in the main street. However, after that, he remained as domineering and arrogant as before. I often saw him when I was young, and now he has been dead for a long time. In the fifth year of the Guangxu era, there was a woman from the Shen family in Linping who died of illness. Several days before she died, Fan Guifang's spirit attached to her body and conversed with the Shen family women. This was because Fan had a dispute with the Shen family over money matters while he was alive. Fan was originally from Hangzhou but had settled in Linping, so his speech still carried a Hangzhou accent, and so did his ghostly voice. It has been over forty years since Fan's death, and tracing back to his birth, he would be over a hundred years old. Fan was originally a small-minded person, so it is appropriate that, as Mr. Changli said, "his body is dead, but his ghost is not spiritless." How is it that his spirit has lasted so long without dissipating? The matter of ghosts and spirits truly is something that cannot be known. In ancient times, temples were built where people and ghosts could enjoy offerings, thus ghosts always resided in family temples. Confucius also said, "When the flesh and bones decay, they become the soil beneath the earth, and their energy rises above as a clear and bright spirit, and becomes the desolate and sorrowful grass." Thus, they also reside in graveyards. Where do ghosts truly reside? The Duke of Yanling said, "If the soul and energy are not limited to any place." Ghosts are no more than the leftover energy of humans. When people are alive, they cannot travel on water without a boat, nor on land without a carriage. But their leftover energy becomes ghosts, which can go anywhere. Thus, ghosts are even more powerful than people. Emperor Wen of Han asked about ghosts and spirits, but I do not know what Jia Yi's response was.

Confucius told Zilu, saying, "If one does not know life, how can one know death? If one cannot serve people, how can one serve spirits?" This suggests that even Confucius had his limits of knowledge. Zhu Xi said, "One must understand the origin of life to know how to live and the end of life to know how to die." This statement is something I find difficult to understand. By understanding the origin and the end, one knows the principles of life and death. This simply means that life is the beginning of human affairs, and death is the end of human affairs. However, I doubt that knowing life alone can lead to understanding death. The creation of a person begins with the union of the father's essence and the mother's blood, forming a fetus, a fact that is well-known.

《Huainanzi》states: "In the first month, it is like fat; in the second month, it is like flesh; in the third month, it is like a fetus; in the fourth month, it is like muscle; in the fifth month, it is like tendons; in the sixth month, it is like bones; in the seventh month, it is formed; in the eighth month, it moves; in the ninth month, it is lively; in the tenth month, it is born." This describes the condition before birth, and ancient people could describe it in detail. Now, what would the condition be after the soul ascends and the spirit descends? Can we infer it based on the same principles? Can we, or cannot we? Purple Yang also said: "The principles of the visible and invisible worlds are fundamentally the same, but learning must follow a proper order and cannot skip steps." This statement is incorrect. If this were true, then Zilu's learning would not have reached this level. Indeed, the levels of learning cannot be skipped! Yan Hui and Zengzi, whose learning was much higher than that of Confucius, did their diligent pursuits focus on human affairs, or on ghostly matters? On living matters, or on death matters? On a cold night with nothing to do, we discussed this due to the story of Fan Gui Fang. Without Jia Yi, who would be able to resolve this?

The old Daoist hermit resided as a guest in the northern region of Chu, claiming to be from Shu. When asked about his name, he replied, "I have no name." When asked about his age, he said, "I have forgotten it." He appeared to be in his sixties or seventies, but even those in their eighties or nineties claimed they had seen him in the same state since childhood, suggesting that his age was truly incalculable. He wore simple, unadorned linen shirts and trousers, with a hemp robe over them, which he did not change regardless of the season. In winter, he did not speak of feeling cold, and in summer, he did not speak of feeling hot. When given food, he ate without limit, yet he did not say he was full; when given drink, he drank without limit, yet he did not say he was drunk. Sometimes he would go without food or drink for ten days, yet he did not speak of hunger or thirst. People all regarded him as one who had attained the Dao. When someone asked about his methods of preserving life, the old Daoist said, "I do not know of any others; I simply let things take their natural course." All said, "The two characters 'nature' are nothing special; how could he be like this? The old Daoist must have some hidden techniques that he does not speak of." I say that Laozi said, "Heaven follows the Dao, and the Dao follows nature," and thus the two characters 'nature' are indeed the essence of the teachings of the Old Master.

If Heaven follows this principle, how much more should humans? In the human body, the ascending and descending forces naturally balance each other. Ancient sages were concerned that people could not follow this natural balance, so they developed alchemical practices and symbolic theories of lead, mercury, dragon, and tiger, to help people follow this natural balance, without any other methods. Extending this idea, the sage rulers of old governed the world in a similar manner. The relationships of ruler and subject, father and son, husband and wife, elder and younger siblings, and friends—all have their own natural order. Sage rulers established rituals and laws to help people follow this natural order. Furthermore, they created the six principles of Chinese character composition to align with the natural principles of writing, and the five tones and six pitches to align with the natural principles of music. Therefore, being overly concerned with the details of writing is not understanding the true essence of writing; observing a porter navigating a narrow path can provide insight into calligraphy. Being overly focused on the technical aspects of music is not understanding true music; listening to the waves of the sea can provide insight into the principles of music. There is nothing more to it than following natural principles. The words of the old Taoist sage deeply understand the essence of health and longevity. If one truly grasps this principle, even the *Zhenyuan Miaodao Yaoshi* (The Secret of the Mysteries of the Tao) can be transcended, let alone texts like the *Wuzhen Pian* (The Book of Awakening to Reality).

In Volume Three, my student Jiang Zeshan, who passed the imperial examinations, arrived at Chongming County to examine the examination papers. After the task was completed, he visited the Confucian temple and saw that the position of Tang Wenzheng Gong in the two wings of the temple was vacant. He asked the local school official, who replied, "There used to be the Five Tong gods causing mischief in this place. People who were troubled by the Five Tong gods would tell the school official and privately take away the position of Tang Wenzheng Gong to worship at home, and the trouble would disappear. This has been the case all along. We, who are in charge of the school, must come every ten or more days to inspect. If the position of Tang Wenzheng Gong remains unreturned for a long time, we just ask the school official to retrieve it. We cannot prevent this." In the Wu region, the Five Tong gods have been banned by Tang Wenzheng for a long time. In Chongming, a remote island, the ban might not have been fully enforced at that time. Even after his death, his spirit tablet still has the power to intimidate evil. His righteous spirit indeed continues to exist in the world.

A man from Su, Tao Anyi, had a fondness for gambling, but he often lost, and his bag was empty. With no other options, he walked alone, intending to borrow money from acquaintances. Suddenly, someone approached from behind and tapped his shoulder, asking, "Have you lost, Anyi?" Turning around, he saw his deceased friend Zhu Da. Tao was frightened. Zhu said, "I am not here to harm you. If you can bury my bones, I will help you gamble." Tao agreed. Zhu then produced a large sum of money, saying, "Take this to the gambling hall and bet on the White Tiger door; you will surely win. Use what you win to bet again on the White Tiger door, and you will win again. Do this four times, and then stop; do not be greedy." Tao followed his advice and indeed won greatly. By morning, he was surrounded by a large sum of money. Looking inside, he found a piece of paper money, which was the original note he had borrowed from his old friend. He searched in the wild, found Zhu's coffin, and sold land to bury him.

In the city of Tianjin, there were often unruly young men who would frequently visit gambling dens to demand regular payments, which the gamblers were happy to comply with. However, obtaining these payments initially was quite difficult. When I stayed in Tianjin, there was a rough laborer named Tian Sheng who would often go to the gambling dens. One day, he saw a drunkard enter the gambling den in a haughty manner, not wearing a top, nor pants, only covering his lower body with a small piece of cloth. As soon as the drunkard entered the gambling den, he immediately started cursing loudly. The gamblers gathered around, each holding a white wooden stick and beating him severely. However, the beaters beat, the cursers cursed, until his body was completely beaten up, his breath was barely left, and he still mumbled curses non-stop. Then the crowd exclaimed, "A true man, a true man!" They gave him a drink of urine and then washed his blood-stained body with warm water, carried him back to the home of the gambling den owner. From then on, a monthly fee was established. This person, is he not one of the strong ones of the north? In the county of She, there was a woman of the Wang family who was pregnant for sixteen months and gave birth to a boy. His body was very fat, his gums had teeth, his cheeks had whiskers, and his eyes were bright. Everyone was afraid and wanted to abandon him. His mother could not bear it and still wrapped him up. The child did not cry, nor did he eat. After six or seven days of breastfeeding, everyone believed he had no chance of survival. They placed him in a dark corner, planning to abandon him in the river when people were asleep. By evening, they suddenly heard a muffled sound. They looked at him and saw him eating soil with his hands. His body grew rapidly, so they let nature take its course. After more than a month, he died. People gathered to watch, and he looked like a statue of a local deity in a temple, two chi and eight cun long, with a beard about one cun long. When weighed, he weighed twenty-one catties.

In the capital city, there was a wealthy family. They employed a female servant from Changping Prefecture, who had served them for many years. She was very clever and could please the master, so the mistress trusted her greatly. All the valuables in the house were known to her. One night, after the usual time when people retire for the night, six thieves descended from the house, all masked and armed with knives. The other servants fled in fear. This woman heard the noise and rushed out, but was seized by the thieves. One of the thieves held a knife to her neck and asked, "Where is your master?" She replied, "My master is on duty and has not returned." The thief waved his companions and said, "Quickly bind the mistress and bring her here." The servant knelt and wept, saying, "The mistress has been kind to me. Please do not frighten her. I am willing to take her place." The thief said, "Since it is so, just tell me where the valuables are." The servant hesitated to speak, and the thief raised the knife to cut her. She was greatly frightened and then told everything. After searching thoroughly, the thieves were still not satisfied and again threatened her with the knife to make her tell everything. She said, "All the valuables are gone. There are still jewels and ornaments in a certain place."

Then the thief took everything, smiling as he said to the woman, “What you said may not be entirely true, but I do think you are quite loyal, so I will leave some room for you.” With that, he called out and departed. The mistress was deeply moved by this woman and went out to comfort and thank her. But the woman’s face was as pale as earth, she said nothing else, and only kept crying out, “Scared to death, scared to death.” By morning, the master returned home and learned of the incident, also calming and thanking her. Yet he pondered: though the woman had been robbed by the thief, why would she reveal everything so thoroughly when in a state of panic? And with so many servant women, why was this one alone targeted? The matter seemed suspicious. From then on, the woman claimed she had been so frightened that she fell ill, and after three days, she reported back to the master that she was too unwell to continue and returned home. The master gave her a generous reward and sent her away, secretly dispatching a capable servant to follow her journey. At first, the woman lay in the carriage, but as they exited Qihua Gate, she got up and sent the carriage back, hiring another one to continue her journey. She arrived at a certain village in Changping Prefecture and stopped there. A few people greeted her at the door with smiles, and the woman smiled and entered as well. The servant immediately ran to report to the authorities, who sent officers to accompany him. By then it was already midnight. They forced their way in through the door, only to find the woman in the act of dividing the stolen goods with others, gold, silk, and jewelry all present. She was bound and sent to the authorities, and was judged according to the law.

In the capital city, there was another household of officials that hired a young woman from the Three Rivers region. The woman had only just been married to her husband for a month when they parted. At the time of their separation, she made an agreement with her husband, saying, "By the end of the year, you should go to the house of a certain matchmaker in the capital city to find out where I am and see me there. I will surely have something to give you." By the end of the year, the woman waited daily at the door for her husband. One day, a villager lingered at the door, and the woman saw him, mistaking him for her husband. Because they had been together for a short time and had been apart for a month, their appearance and voice were not very clear. She immediately called out to him, saying, "Have you just arrived today? I've been waiting with bated breath. Wait a moment, I'll get some money for you." The man was puzzled and couldn't understand, so he waited. The woman indeed carried several pieces of paper with money and gave them to him. The man was very happy and took them away. The next day, her husband indeed came, and the gatekeeper informed the woman. The woman was surprised that he had returned and came out to ask him, saying, "The money I gave you yesterday, is it still not enough? I don't have any more."

The husband said, "I arrived in the city last night and stayed at the matchmaker's house you mentioned. Today, I asked her young son to guide me here. I didn't come here yesterday. Could you have been dreaming?" The wife then realized her mistake from the previous day, stood in a daze without speaking, and began to sob softly. The husband asked her again, and she cried out in distress. The host heard this and called her in to ask what had happened. Upon understanding the situation, he laughed heartily, pitied the young couple who had been separated by poverty, and gave them more than ten strings of cash, instructing her to give it to her husband. In Suzhou, at Feng Bridge, there was a person named Gao who made a living teaching children and enjoyed reciting poetry. One day, several refugees from the north of the river intruded into his school to beg for food. Gao was just then holding a brush and reciting a poem titled "Sentimental Reflections," coming up with a line that said, "Limited years hasten old age easily." He had not yet completed the couplet when one of the elderly refugees picked up a brush and continued it, saying, "Words without inspiration cannot easily send away poverty." Gao was very pleased, invited him to sit, but the old man did not sit; when Gao asked for his name, he did not tell; Gao gave him some money, which he accepted and left. Ah! There are poets among the wandering refugees in the rivers and lakes, which is also something to sigh about!

In the ninth month of the Dingchou year of the Guangxu era, within the military training grounds of Yangzhou city, a man from Shandong set up a cloth enclosure, within which there were five people with unusual physical forms. One man had an upper body like a normal person, but both legs were soft, as though there were sinews but no bones. Someone held his upper body and rotated it, like twisting a rope. Another man had an infant pressed against his chest, with skin and flesh fused into one body, possessing all five facial features and four limbs, and capable of movement and speech. Another man had a right arm only five or six inches long, with a right hand as small as a coin, while his left arm was longer than his knee, and his left hand as large as a palm-leaf fan. Another man had a navel larger than a cup, able to smoke tobacco; inserting a pipe into his navel caused the smoke to emerge from his mouth. Another woman had slender feet and prominent breasts, with a thick beard like a spear beneath her chin. Thus, many spectators gathered to watch. When the matter came to the attention of the authorities, they deemed these people to be members of the group involved in kidnapping and dismembering people, and ordered them to be driven out of the territory.

In the hillside outside the West Gate of Wulin in Hangzhou, there is a bridge called the New Bridge, which was rebuilt after a period of chaos and is quite sturdy. In the autumn of Ding Chou year, the villagers set up a stage by the bridge to perform plays, and many people gathered on the bridge to watch. A child shouted, "There is someone digging earth below the bridge (according to the *Shiji* *Fengchan Shu* *Siyin*: '掊' means 'to dig'), the bridge is about to collapse!" Everyone laughed and did not believe him. In a short while, the bridge indeed collapsed, and many were killed or injured. This child's identity is unknown, perhaps it was a warning sent by ghosts or spirits? It is also said that on that day, an old actor in the theater district knew beforehand that something unusual would happen and was afraid of harming his fellow performers. He secretly made a cut on each of the four pillars of the stage and lit two candles upside down in the room to ward off the evil. How could he have known that the actors were unharmed, while the audience was instead injured? Perhaps there was a predetermined fate. Jiang, a man from Lishui, was standing outside his door when suddenly a stranger who had never met him before approached, speaking in the Shaoxing dialect, and asked to borrow five silver dollars. Jiang said, "I just don't have any, please wait a moment, I will ask my neighbors." After borrowing money from a neighbor and returning, the stranger had already left, and he could not be traced. Jiang shouted, "I owe you!" and immediately took a knife and cut his throat, dying. Alas! Even if people try to do good, they do not go to such extremes, perhaps it was an old grudge? Whether this Shaoxing person was alive or a ghost, it is unknown.

In a certain county under the jurisdiction of Yongping Prefecture in Zhili (now Hebei Province), the standards of women's behavior are extremely strict. Whenever a woman marries for the first time, her mother's family must send someone to observe the wedding night. If the next day, the husband's family is filled with music and drums, and guests are bustling and crowded, it is a great joy; if the day is quiet and empty, the bride's family feels embarrassed. Whether the bride stays or leaves, only the husband's family has the authority, and they dare not dispute it. This custom has been passed down for generations, and although its intention is good, it is also a backward custom. There was a woman named Wang who married into the Li family. On the wedding night, Li rejected the new bride because he found her appearance unattractive. The next day, he claimed she was not a virgin, did not hold a celebration, and still called the matchmaker to send the bride back to her mother's home. The bride had lost her mother at a young age and lived with her sister-in-law. The sister-in-law knew the young bride had no other issues and asked about the events of the previous night, which confirmed that they had not consummated the marriage. The sister-in-law then strongly argued with the father-in-law, making him sue in court. The court ordered an investigation, and it was found that the bride was indeed a chaste and disciplined maiden. The court then ruled that the Li family must still welcome the bride with music and drums. I have studied the *Mao Shi* (Mao's Book of Songs) and found the line: "Once we met, my heart was at ease." Zheng Xuan interpreted "once we met" as referring to men and women meeting and having sexual intercourse, and "my heart was at ease" as meaning that from then on, one can bring peace to one's parents, so the heart becomes calm. Scholars all laugh at Zheng Xuan's interpretation as being crude. Now, observing the custom in Yongping, it is believed that only after seeing each other can one bring peace to one's parents. Is this truly the poet's intention?

A man from Yin named Li, married his younger brother and rented a house from his neighbor Chen for a new home. Every night at midnight, strange sounds would come from the room, causing much suspicion and fear. One night, the sounds were particularly loud, and the whole family gathered. They noticed that the soil under the bed was loose and dug it up, finding four copper bowls, one iron如意 (a ceremonial object), and one iron bowl. Afterward, the sounds ceased, and there were no further incidents. Han Yu said, "Accidentally writing it as a wooden Bodhisattva, there are countless people seeking blessings." The *Fengsu Tong* records stories like those of Bao Jun, Li Jun, and Shi Xiansheng, which are largely similar to this, and have been this way since ancient times. As for the story of the snow Maitreya in Tianjin, it is especially laughable. In a certain year, there was a heavy snowfall in Tianjin, and some playful people gathered snow to make a Maitreya. With lowered eyebrows and eyes, a smiling face that is endearing, sitting cross-legged with one arm bare, a large belly protruding, holding a mala (prayer beads) in the right hand and a cloth bag in the left hand. They also made two attendants, both lively and expressive. Foolish men and women saw it and bowed in worship, some even offering incense and candles. Various rascals then used it to collect money, extravagantly talking about miraculous things. Many people came to worship, so they built a pavilion to cover it, hanging two red lanterns in front of the eaves, making it look like a Buddhist temple. The people were numerous and their spirits were high, and with the added fragrance of incense and the light of candles, they were steamed all day, not even reaching noon, and the snow mountain melted. All the believers returned disappointed.

Yangzhou's Xingjiao Temple has a Wanfo Tower, said to have been built during the early Ming Dynasty. The tower houses an actual number of Buddha statues, intricately carved with great skill. After the devastation of war, this tower remained undamaged. In the first month of the year in the sexagenary cycle of Ding Chou, an elderly man with white hair ascended the tower to burn incense and told the temple monk, "There is a long-life lamp hanging inside; one should be cautious of fire." The monk agreed to his suggestion. Ten days later, indeed, the fire rose from the lamps, spreading from the tower to the main hall and the abbot's quarters, ultimately suffering the wrath of the Fire God. The old man's words were unfortunately proven true; could it be that the gods had warned him in advance? Three days before the disaster, more than a dozen Buddha statues in the tower fell to the ground by themselves, which is also quite strange.

In Tianjin, there was a man named Wu San. His father had made his fortune through sand ships and had been dead for a long time. Wu San was addicted to gambling and thus squandered his wealth. His farmland and gardens were all taken by others, but only the house remained. He lived with his mother in it. Soon after his mother died, the funeral rites in Tianjin were extravagant, and the funeral procession was even more so. Wu San sold the house to pay for the funeral and thus had nowhere to stand, living with gamblers. He had an aunt who married into the Xi family, who was quite wealthy, but because she considered her nephew unworthy, she had not communicated with him for a long time. Wu San was in his twenties and had not yet married. One year, as the New Year approached, he was in dire straits and had no means of living. A gambler made a plan with him, lent him formal attire, and sent him to his aunt's house to ask for an audience. The aunt declined, and he told the gatekeeper, "I am not here to make any requests, but because I am about to be married, I dare not fail to inform the elder." The aunt heard this and allowed him to enter. He was neatly dressed and did not look shabby. She asked where he had been for many years, and he replied that he had been engaged in trade. She asked when the wedding would be, and he said, "It will be the day after tomorrow." The aunt was very pleased and gave him ten taels of silver as a wedding gift, and said, "I will come to congratulate you on the day." The aunt had two married daughters, each gave him ten thousand cash. Wu San took the silver and cash and returned to consult with the gamblers. The gamblers were delighted and said, "So there will still be future benefits." They then decorated his house with the workers, found a young prostitute, dressed her in plain clothes, and had her pretend to be the bride. On the day, the aunt indeed came, saw the woman, and was pleased with her. The woman was also good at serving and welcoming, entered the kitchen to cook soup, and knelt to serve it.

The woman, pleased, finished her meal and said, "This house is cramped, and I cannot stay here. I will come back tomorrow with some assistance." The next day, she returned and handed over a deed to the house, saying, "I give this house to you and your wife, and you may move in." She then presented a land deed, stating, "A hundred acres of land, which can roughly provide for your basic needs." Wu was overjoyed beyond expectation. The father of the prostitute was also a gambler who had lost a large sum of money and temporarily used his daughter as a means to earn money. Now that he learned Wu had land and a house, he married his daughter to Wu. Thus, what began as a pretense became reality. Is this not what happened to Wu? Teng, a native of Hangzhou, was naturally cautious and reserved. He owed a hundred thousand coins and could not repay it, which caused him great distress and led to a mental illness. One day, as he passed by a shop selling ritual objects, he saw paper-made gold ingots being dried in the sun and exclaimed, "This is a gift from heaven!"

"I don't have many demands; just two will suffice." He then took two gold ingots and left. The people in the shop knew he was [unusual/crazy], and they temporarily let him go. Chen returned home and told his wife, "Today, heaven has given me two gold ingots, which can be used to repay my old debts." His wife took them and looked at them, smiling and saying, "These are treasures from the netherworld." (See *Qing Yi Lu*.) Chen said, "When I took them back, they were clearly two gold ingots, but now they have turned into paper; heaven has forsaken me!" He then wept bitterly and sought death. His wife tried to comfort him, but he would not be consoled, and his illness worsened. Among his relatives, there was a man surnamed Gao, who was wealthy and brave in doing good deeds. He took two genuine gold ingots and gave them to Chen's wife, instructing her to tell her husband, "The treasures given by heaven have turned into silver again." Chen was greatly pleased and used them to exchange for money to repay his debts, using the rest as resources for the rest of the year, and his mental illness suddenly healed.

In the outskirts of Suzhou, outside the Xumen Gate in Meiwang Lane, there lived an elderly widow named Old Lady Yao. She had raised an orphaned son until he reached the age of twenty, at which point she intended to arrange his marriage. Over the years, she had saved more than forty foreign silver dollars to cover the wedding expenses. Additionally, she had prepared several pieces of silver jewelry and the bride's birth chart, all of which were placed in a small box. One night, these items suddenly went missing. Thinking of the years of savings she had accumulated, Old Lady Yao realized that everything she had was now lost. She knew she could not replace the items, and with the wedding date approaching, she felt utterly at a loss. Day and night, she wept in despair. Three nights later, after everyone had gone to bed, she heard someone call out from above the house: "Old Lady Yao! Last night, I acted rashly and nearly ruined someone's marriage. Today, I am returning the items. Please come to the eaves to retrieve them. I am leaving now." Old Lady Yao called her son out to see, and indeed, the original items were found under the eaves. Such a person, one might say, is the so-called "gentleman on the beam."

Thirty li away from Dingzihu, there is a place called Yangliuqing, where many brothels can be found. There was a man named Mr. A who was wandering around and came to this place. He saw a sign on a door that read "For Rent," with a note beside it that said, "Those who wish to rent, please enter to discuss in person." Mr. A had no intention of seeking shelter, but he decided to go in, thinking he might find some pleasure. An old servant led him upstairs, and a young woman emerged from a room. She had bright eyes and a radiant smile, and she appeared to be about twenty years old. He asked the servant to show him the house, and the woman invited him into her room. The room was adorned with a red silk double-layered canopy, a horn pillow, and a brocade quilt, clearly her bedroom. Mr. A wanted to leave, but the woman said, "My husband is a distant guest, and we have no means to live on. We cannot sit idly and live in poverty, nor do we wish to sell ourselves for money. We borrow the 'for rent' sign to serve as a substitute for a 'selling wine' sign. Since you have kindly come, how can we not offer something in return?" Mr. A tried to refuse, saying he had no money, but the woman laughed and said, "How modest of you to say that." She immediately searched through his belongings and found sixty thousand coins in his boot pocket. She thanked him and said, "With this, we can have enough for entertainment." She then handed the money to two men outside. Mr. A saw that although the woman seemed weak, the two men were strong, and he realized he had fallen into their trap. He did not dare to argue and left in a hurry. This situation was similar to what happened when he tried to buy a bag. Although Mr. A acted quickly and avoided a false alarm, he still spent a lot of money, which serves as a warning to those who indulge in pleasure-seeking.

In the western part of Guangdong, there was a man named Yao San. As a child, he was no different from others. At the age of eighteen, he once fished in a pond and caught a fish without scales, which he cooked and ate. Suddenly, he fell gravely ill and remained sick for over a month. When he recovered, his body had suddenly grown by about a foot. After that, he suffered many illnesses and recovered many times, and each time he recovered, his body would always grow longer. Over the course of several years, he grew to a height of one zhang (about 3.3 meters). However, his head remained the same as that of ordinary people. When asked about the reason, it was explained that when he ate the fish, he left the fish head uneaten, and a dog ate it. Soon, the dog's head also became much larger than before. Unfortunately, the dog was soon killed by people; otherwise, it would have been a remarkable sight. In a rural area of Fengcheng, Jiangxi, during a certain spring and summer season, an elderly woman died on the road. The people felt pity for her and collected money to buy a coffin for burial, carrying her to a public cemetery. Before the burial was completed, a great thunderstorm and violent wind and rain came, and everyone fled. Soon there was a loud clap of thunder, and the coffin was split into two pieces. The elderly woman stood up in the coffin, and when looked at, she was already alive. I believe the elderly woman had a sudden case of asphyxia, not that she was truly dead. The thunder shook her, and her breath returned to circulation, so she remained alive. However, her coffin being split by a sudden clap of thunder was not accidental; it was likely that her fate was not to die, like Duke Guo's son being revived by Bian Que.

In Yin there was a Mr. Wang, who was wealthy in wealth. He was widowed with no son and took a concubine, who had a son who died young. When the old man died, clan members coveted his wealth and forced to marry off his concubine. On the twenty-first day after the old man's death, his family performed Buddhist rites, and all relatives gathered. The concubine went out to greet them all and wept and said, "I, though a woman from a small family, also understand the principle of fidelity to one's husband until death, swore to never leave this door, wished to observe a long fast and worship Buddha, to end my remaining life. Only asked the respected elders for mercy, to give me a room, I will then shave my head and practice Buddhism, the family's wealth, I will not inquire about." After speaking, she took a scissors from her sleeve, shaved her head and threw it to the ground, wept and went inside, at the time she was just twenty. Some in the crowd were moved to tears, they then cleaned a small tower in the western part of the house, and made her live there. Alas! This is also a Yanzi Tower.

A beggar was begging for food in Wu city when a low-ranking military officer suddenly encountered him. The officer asked, "Are you not the son of a certain family from a certain place?" The beggar replied, "Yes." The officer said, "I once received your favor, but how have you fallen into such poverty?" He then took the beggar to a tea house, had him drink tea, and sat with him for a short while. Shortly after, he brought clothes, a hat, and shoes, and exchanged them all with the beggar. The officer then took out more than ten taels of silver from his pocket and gave it to him, saying, "With this, you can make plans to return home. Do not stay here begging for long." The beggar was completely puzzled and unable to guess where it came from, so he asked the officer why he had done this. The officer replied, "Though the matter is long past, you have forgotten it, but I have not." He then left. This officer showed great gratitude and a sense of righteousness, and if he had succeeded, he would have easily repaid a favor as Han Xin did with the "thousand gold pieces for a single meal."

A woman from the Li family in Panyu was engaged to marry a son of the Zhao family. Because of poverty, the Zhao son went overseas to make a living and did not return for many years, and news ceased entirely. The woman waited until she was twenty-eight years old, and her parents wanted to arrange another marriage for her, but she refused. They then sent a matchmaker to speak to the Zhao family, and a proxy wife was married into the household to wait for the Zhao son's return, which was also a custom of the Guangdong region. The woman thus sometimes lived with her husband's family and sometimes with her mother's family, and this continued for over a decade. The Zhao son eventually returned by steamship, but on the way, the ship drifted away and was lost, and all those on the same ship died at sea. Only the Zhao held onto a piece of wood and drifted for dozens of miles, where he was rescued by another ship and survived to return home. Later, he renewed the wedding ceremony with the woman, and both the couple were approaching fifty years old. Perhaps the gods or spirits pitied the chaste woman and helped her in secret, allowing them to reunite completely.

A Person from Shandong Surnamed Cheng A person from Shandong surnamed Cheng temporarily resided in Suzhou. He had a servant girl who married a man from a farming family surnamed Ye. During the tumultuous period of 1640, the Cheng family took refuge with the Ye family, entrusting all their household goods and wealth to them. Within a few years, the Cheng family members died one after another, leaving behind only a young child in swaddling clothes. The servant girl raised the child as if he were her own son, making him grow up with her other sons. Soon after, her husband also died. The servant girl upheld her virtue and did not remarry, raising both the Cheng family's son and her own son to adulthood. She arranged a marriage for the Cheng family's son to a girl from a nearby village. On the wedding day, she asked her aunt to come out to introduce the new bride. However, the servant girl hurriedly came out from her room, climbed up to a high place, and bowed first. The Cheng family's son was greatly surprised. The servant girl explained to the crowd, "I am not the bridegroom's mother; I am a servant of the Cheng family. Our master unfortunately encountered turmoil, and almost all of us died. I raised the young master because he was too young and had no one to take care of him. So, for over ten years, I crossed the boundaries of master and servant, pretending to be mother and son. Now, dare I not reveal the truth?" She then fully described the events, contributed her money and property, and returned everything to the Cheng family's son. The Cheng family's son wanted to give her half of what she had contributed, but she refused. Instead, he made his wife treat her as an aunt, and he continued to call her mother.

Brothers 甲 and 乙 lived under the same roof but had separate hearths. 甲 was a widower with no children, while 乙 had a son who was already over ten years old. 甲 wished to adopt 乙's son as his own, but 乙 was bewitched by the words of his concubine and insisted on refusing. One day, 乙's son went to the riverbank to wash rice, lost his footing, and fell into the river. The concubine saw him, shouted for help, but sat still and did not move. 甲, who just had a minor illness and was lying in bed, heard the sound, rushed out, leaped into the river, and rescued the boy, lifting him up. The son was saved, but 甲's illness worsened. When 乙 returned and learned the situation, he was deeply moved. He invited a doctor to treat 甲's illness and adopted his son as his own. Alas! Initially, 乙 was bewitched by the words of his concubine, thinking that she loved his son and that 甲 did not. Without this incident, how could the truth and falsehood have been revealed!

“If a disease is not treated, one often ends up with a mediocre cure.” Jia Gongyan included this saying in his commentary on the *Rites of Zhou*, noting that it is not only an ancient proverb but also a principle of the classics. Pan Yuquan, a high-ranking official, once told me about a case where a sick person invited a doctor to treat him. The doctor prescribed a small amount of ephedra to induce sweating. The patient carried the prescription to the pharmacy, but it just happened to be out of ephedra, so they gave him a counterfeit version. Taking it had no effect. The next day, the doctor arrived, surprised and said, “Is it because too little ephedra was used, so it wasn’t enough to induce sweating?” He then doubled the amount. By then, the pharmacy had already purchased genuine ephedra. The patient took it according to the prescription and sweated profusely, dying as a result. However, this was the mistake of the medicine. There were also two brothers who, during the years 1660 and 1661, fled to Shanghai to escape the chaos and fell ill at the same time. The doctor gave each of them a different prescription and warned them, saying, “Since the illnesses are different, the medicines are different; do not mix them up.” But their household had only one kitchen, and the maid was preparing the medicine. She accidentally mixed them up, and the next day, they both recovered. If she hadn’t made a mistake, wouldn’t they both have died? The unreliability of doctors is shown in this way. I say that doctors rely on pulse diagnosis, but the art of pulse diagnosis has been lost for a long time. The *Biography of Bian Que* in the *Records of the Grand Historian* states that Bian Que drank the medicine given to him by Chang Sang Jun, “and could see people on the other side of a wall. Using this ability, he could see all the internal organs and their ailments. He was known only for pulse diagnosis.” It also says, “Until today, those who talk about pulse diagnosis are all followers of Bian Que.” Bian Que was known only for pulse diagnosis, but even today, those who talk about pulse diagnosis follow him. This shows that Bian Que was known only for pulse diagnosis, while later generations truly used it to treat diseases. From this, we can see that it is unreliable.

The *Suwen* has discussions on the Three Sections and Nine Pulses. Are the Three Sections referred to today the same as those of old? Are the Nine Pulses referred to today the same as those of old? Since the methods of pulse diagnosis have been lost, one might argue that the study of medicine could be neglected. Yet doctors, symbolized by the gourds they hang, are everywhere in the streets, and titles such as "衙推" (a low-ranking official) are even bestowed upon butchers and wine sellers. Moreover, the *Suwen* is said to have been passed down from the Yellow Emperor, and its role is listed in the *Zhouli* (Rites of Zhou), a tradition that has been maintained since ancient times, and no one dares to abandon it. Parents owe their children care, and descendants owe their ancestors respect; if medicine is lacking, then filial piety and benevolence are compromised, and many people fail to reach the end of their natural lives. Both doctors and diviners are listed in the *Zhouli*, and divination was also considered important by the sages. However, during the Tang Dynasty, Li Hua proposed the idea of abandoning the use of tortoise shells for divination.

Divination can be abandoned, and why not medicine as well? I once wrote *The Discourse on Abandoning Medicine*, which was included in *Yulou Miscellaneous Compilation*. Though it may shock the common ear, I remain unconcerned. The northern lands are severely cold, and in winter, the water freezes solid in the middle, making it impossible for boats to pass. Even at the open ports, one cannot help but stop and wait, a phenomenon known as river freezing. In contrast, the southern regions have never experienced such conditions. During the twelfth month of the Dingchou year in the Guangxu era, after a heavy snowfall, the climate became extremely cold, and the ice on the river grew to be about a foot thick. Boats coming and going were delayed for more than ten days. A man from a certain family in Suzhou had engaged a woman from a certain family outside Xumen Gate as his wife, planning to marry on the eighth day of that month. However, snow and ice blocked the way. If they took the land route, the snow would be deep up to the knees, and carriages could not move. If they took the water route, the ice would be as hard as stone, and boats could not proceed. The parents of both families made the bridegroom and bride perform a ceremony of mutual bowing in the air to meet the auspicious time. After seven days, the Cowherd and the Weaver Girl were finally able to meet. This is also a form of wedding ritual that has been lost.

In Yinzhou, there was a diviner who set up a stall in the market. One day, a man in the stall began to shout loudly, and it seemed he was about to use violence. The crowd gathered around to ask what was happening. The man said, "During the summer, because my family was not well, I went to him for divination. He asked which direction the stove was facing, and I said, 'South.' He said, 'It should be changed to southwest.' I followed his advice. But by autumn, my family was still sick, so I went to him again for divination. He asked again which direction the stove was facing, and I said, 'Southwest.' He said, 'It should be changed to west.' I followed his advice again. Now that winter has arrived, the sick have not recovered, and my business has suffered losses, leaving me very bored. So I decided to consult him again. He asked the same question, and when I told him, he said, 'It should be changed back to south.' This brings me back to where I started. From summer to winter, I have treated him as a diviner, and his advice has been so inconsistent! The crowd laughed and tried to calm me down before I left. I remembered that among my relatives, there was someone who had consulted a house surveyor, and in one year, the stove's orientation was changed three times and returned to its original position, similar to this case. However, in that case, there were three people giving three different opinions, whereas in this case, it was one person giving inconsistent advice, which is even more laughable. The sayings of itinerant diviners are indeed not worth relying on."

There was an old man named Su, who lived through the Geng Shen disaster. During this chaos, he lost his son. Soon after, he found and adopted another child. When the chaos subsided and he returned home, he was fortunate that his house and property remained undamaged. He then took the adopted child as his own and arranged for him to marry. Not long after, the adopted son died. Just as they were preparing to bury him, the son suddenly sat up, stretched, and bowed to his parents, saying, "I have been away from you for a long time. Are you both well?" He then pointed to the woman he had married and asked, "Who is this?" Surprised, the parents listened carefully to his voice and realized it was their own son. They asked, "Are you really our son? Where have you been?" The son replied, "After we were separated, I wandered to various places and finally boarded a ship with someone to return home." Before he finished speaking, the person who had brought him home knocked on the door and said, "I brought your son back, but he suddenly fell ill and died on the way. His body is still on the boat." The parents rushed outside to see the body and realized that it was indeed their son's body. They then understood that their original son had been presumed dead, and the body of the adopted son had been used as a substitute. They placed the body in the coffin and were relieved that their true son had returned. However, the woman he had married was ashamed of her husband's changed voice and mannerisms and refused to live with him. The old man's in-laws could not do anything to resolve the situation, so they repeatedly asked the parents to come and persuade her. Finally, she agreed to return to her husband, and they resumed their life together as before.

During the summer and autumn of the Guangxu Bingzi year, there was heavy rain in Zhejiang, and in Lin'an County, twenty-seven dragons emerged. In the northern part of the county, two mountains suddenly merged into one. Mr. Xu Huannong, a juren, wrote to describe this, stating that it was what people from his county who came to take the provincial exams had said, and it was likely true. I refer to the "Wuxing Zhi" section of the Ming History, which records that in Qin Prefecture, there were two mountains very far apart, with several million households living between them. One day, an earthquake occurred, and the two mountains merged, with the residents all being swallowed up. Thus, this event also has precedents in historical records and is not unusual. A certain elderly man from Shaoxing had one son and his wife died; he then remarried and had another son. Both sons grew up and got married, but the elderly man died. He left behind an inheritance of a thousand gold pieces, which the clan divided equally, allowing the brothers to live together but cook separately. However, the younger brother was lazy and wasted his share of the inheritance. He then plotted with his mother to expel the elder brother and take everything. As a result, mother, son, brothers, and sisters-in-law quarreled all day long. The elder brother, unable to bear the pressure, one night told his wife, "There is a certain amount of money at a certain place. You can raise the orphaned child and make a living. I have already bought opium and am willing to die to resolve the grudge." The wife took the opium and said, "If you die, what will I rely on? It would be better if I died." The husband initially thought it was just words of comfort and did not pay attention. Soon after, he heard strange sounds from the room. He went to see and found her writhing on the bed. Rescue efforts were too late, and she died. The wife was pregnant for several months, and after her death, there was a large movement in her belly, which stopped after a while. Those who saw it felt pity.

In Hangzhou, there was a man surnamed Zheng who was in his forties. His wife was pregnant, and he took great care of her. He hired an old woman to work in his home, and all cooking, fetching water, washing, and cleaning tasks were done by her. He told his wife to sit comfortably and not overwork herself. One day, she was hanging clothes in the courtyard when it suddenly rained heavily. The old woman happened to be out, so she got up herself to collect the clothes. She returned to the room and heard the sound of a baby crying. She thought it was a neighbor's child, but upon checking, she realized it was her own. She was greatly surprised and lay down. Her husband came back and told her, and they called a midwife to treat her, but it was ineffective. At the time, she was seven months pregnant, and the baby cried in her womb for over a month before stopping. When the time came, she gave birth, and there were no other unusual events. In Shaoxing, there was a man named Zhang who had been engaged to a woman from the Chen family since childhood. Soon after, he lost both his parents and suffered a fire disaster, leaving him with no means of living, so he went to Tian台 to become a monk. Meanwhile, the Chen family waited for their son-in-law, but when they learned that he had taken refuge in the monastic life, they arranged for their daughter to marry the Shen family instead. On the day of the wedding, Shen was already gravely ill, and the ceremony was carried out with great difficulty. Shortly after, Shen passed away, and the daughter remained a virgin. Her mother-in-law felt sorry for her and planned to find her a new husband. By then, Zhang had been in Tian台 for three years. An old friend of his encountered him in the mountains and persuaded him to return to secular life. Zhang followed his friend's advice and returned home. Upon inquiring about his former wife, he learned that she had already remarried. His friend, who was a high-ranking official, married the woman from the Shen family, and it turned out to be the same woman Zhang had originally been engaged to. It was as if they had found their lost sword again, which seemed quite extraordinary. However, with all these twists and turns, it is unclear whether fate truly wanted to bring them back together or was merely playing with them.

A certain scholar-official, impoverished and destitute, had no means of making a living. He borrowed money from relatives and friends, but none responded to his pleas. There was a gambler who alone treated him kindly, occasionally giving him gifts to support his daily needs. When the scholar-official set out for the imperial examination in the capital, the gambler helped him prepare his travel arrangements and even provided for his family. Shortly after, the scholar-official succeeded in the imperial examination and was appointed as a county magistrate. Grateful for the gambler's past kindness, he sent someone to invite him. The gambler refused to come, saying, "We who play dice and indulge in a carefree life have long been unrestrained. Once I enter the red doors of the wealthy and powerful, I will feel constrained to the point of death, which is not what I desire. If I were to stay in your household and still go out every day, associating with lowly people, would that not harm your reputation? And that is not how I could truly show my affection for you." The scholar-official then sent him a thousand gold pieces as a gift, but the gambler refused to accept it, saying, "Even if you give me a thousand gold pieces every day, it would only be enough to fund one round of gambling. It would only hurt your generosity and not alleviate my poverty. It would be better to leave it alone." This gambler had a very high level of insight. If Han Xin (淮阴侯) had been able to see things this way, he would not have lamented the fate of "when the birds are caught, the bow is put away." Who would say that there are no heroes among the common people!

The Sun Family of Ningbo had a house that suddenly caught fire at irregular intervals during the spring of the year of the Tiger (戊寅). Although the fires started and stopped quickly, the beams, columns, and window frames were all marked with burn scars. The Sun family's son also suddenly went mad, experiencing episodes of madness and recovery. When he recovered, he was asked what he had seen, and he claimed to have seen five men in red robes and official hats, who identified themselves as five brothers, all late Ming dynasty scholars of the Hanlin Academy, and requested that the Sun family build a shrine for them. Additionally, there were times when written papers would fall from the ceiling, some of which contained poems. One day, a seven-character poem was found, using the "Four Heroes" rhyme scheme, but with a missing word. Some thought it was out of rhyme, while others believed it was a writing error. Shortly after, a paper fell from the ceiling, stating, “According to the *Jiyun*: '跳' and '逃' are interchangeable.” They then asked if this usage had been used by ancient scholars. The next day, before going to sleep, the person who had been asking received a paper from the bed saying, “Yuan Zhen's poem: 'The commander was so startled that he jumped and abandoned his flag and battle-axe.'” Everyone was amazed and impressed. I checked the *Book of Han* (漢書), specifically the biography of Emperor Gaozu (高帝), and found the commentary by Rú Chún (如淳): “The pronunciation is '逃'.” These ghosts, despite not reading ancient books, used Yuan Zhen's poem as evidence, confirming their identity as late Ming dynasty scholars.

Hong Lan, a courtesan from Suzhou, had promised to marry a certain scholar, but the scholar was unable to free her from her status as a courtesan, and Hong Lan became ill due to her emotional distress. There was an old woman named Fei who worked as a servant in the courtesan's household. She said to her, "Your beauty is unmatched in the city. Why worry about not having a golden house to keep a beauty? Why are you so attached to a poor scholar?" Hong Lan replied, "Those who come and go in the brothels of Qin and Chu are mostly idle rich and fat merchants. Who is like this scholar who understands and cares for me through thick and thin? He is weak in strength, and I am weak in fate. The vast sea of karma, it is unknown where it will end." The old woman said, "If that is so, I will help you achieve your marriage." One night, while the fake mother was out, she carried Hong Lan to the scholar's place. The scholar was afraid and did not dare to accept. The old woman gave Hong Lan's contract to the scholar and said, "I have already stolen this paper, so she cannot do anything about it." The old woman returned and then found the fake mother's location, and told her that Hong Lan had escaped. After searching for several days, they finally went to the scholar's place to see her. The fake mother urged Hong Lan to return, but she swore to die rather than comply. The old woman said, "This woman's heart has changed. Quickly go back and get the contract, sue in court, and you will definitely win. I will be the witness." When the fake mother returned to look for the contract, it was gone. She could not sue. The old woman then mediated, making the scholar pay the fake mother a hundred gold pieces, and Hong Lan finally married the scholar. This old woman must be the ancient Xu Jun or Kunlun Slave.

A rascal and wicked young man from Suzhou, named Su, was known for his persistent bad behavior. He had been beaten many times before but never repented or changed. People called him "Stone Mortar" because he could withstand physical punishment. One day, he drank at an inn and, after finishing his drink, left without paying. The bartender demanded payment, and Su said, "Your father is short of money for the stick, wait for another day." The patrons in the tavern were furious and began to insult and beat him, pounding and dragging him until his blood flowed like water. When they saw that he was nearly dead, they let him go. A few days later, he returned, and his injuries had already healed. He continued to act as he had before. The patrons marveled, saying, "He is truly a stone mortar." I gave him a thousand coins, and he left.

阿保，是一个出身卑微的女子。她的父母早逝，由邻居家的金氏老妇抚养长大。待到她长大成人，容貌颇为美丽，金氏老妇想把她当作摇钱树来利用。阿保哭着说：“您养育我的恩情，我终身难忘，即使把我卖作妾或婢女，我也不会抱怨。但如果让我沦落风尘，刀锯鼎镬，我也不能改变我的志向。”金氏老妇无可奈何，将她卖给了某户人家作妾。那家的正妻非常嫉妒，每天对她鞭打，打得她体无完肤，阿保只能默默流泪。有一天，正妻趁丈夫外出，把她送回了金氏老妇家，并告诉丈夫说她已经逃跑了。阿保等丈夫回来后，写信为自己辩解，但没有收到任何回信，也不知道信是否送达。金氏老妇于是伪造了丈夫的信，命令她另嫁他人。阿保收到信后，当天就服毒自尽了。唉！这位女子的贞烈行为如此，却默默无闻，连她的姓名和籍贯都被人遗忘，真是令人叹息。

A Certain Official in Shandong, in Charge of the Treasury, Had a Daughter Who Was Very Beautiful. From a Young Age, She Was Betrothed to Her Mother's Younger Brother. Before She Could Marry, Her Fiancé Died, and Those Asking for Her Hand in Marriage Came One After Another, but She Vowed Not to Marry Anyone Else. Her Parents Tried to Persuade Her, but She Did Not Comply, So They Built a Separate Quiet Room for Her to Live In. She Observed Long-Term Vegetarianism and Devoted Herself to Buddhism for Several Years, and Her Heart Became as Still as an Ancient Well. Soon, Her Father Was Caught Using Official Money, and He Was Imprisoned and Sentenced to Death, with No Way Out. A Wealthy Young Man Told Him, "I Have Lost My Wife and Have Not Remarried. If You Can Give Your Daughter to Me as My Wife, I Will Definitely Pay Back the Official Money on Your Behalf." Her Parents Discussed This with Her, but She Firmly Refused. Her Mother Wept and Said, "Your Father Has No Hope of Survival Anymore."

When my father passed away, I was determined to follow him in death, asking you to remain loyal to your betrothed husband without considering the death of our parents. Now, if there is no other way but you insist on not complying, I will take my own life before you!" The woman wept for several days and eventually agreed. After she married, the matter was indeed resolved. However, because she went against her original wishes, she lived in constant sadness and passed away within a few years. Traditionally, virtuous women are praised with the titles of "chastity" and "filial piety." It is said that a woman, following her parents' command, dedicates herself to another, remaining faithful to her husband is equivalent to being filial to her parents, thus chastity is filial piety. Yet, in this case, the woman could not fulfill both her duty of chastity and filial piety, which is an exception to the usual human relationships. Nevertheless, a woman maintaining her loyalty to an未婚夫 (betrothed husband) has been a subject of varied opinions throughout history. This woman remarried another family, which is not against the rules, but if she had insisted on not complying and her mother also died, the sorrow would be immeasurable. She complied with her parents' wishes to fulfill her filial duty, and yet her subsequent depression and death were a way of maintaining her chastity. A gentleman would not criticize this woman.

Prostitutes in the city of Jinmen mostly come from places such as Yangliuqing and Duliú, where licentious customs flourish even more than by the rivers Zhēn and Wěi. There was a woman from the Liu family, also of Yangliuqing, whose mother-in-law was employed by a certain family. The son of that family, upon learning of his wife’s beauty, bribed her mother-in-law with a large sum of money, persuading her to bring his wife to serve as a seamstress. At first, the wife was unwilling, but her mother-in-law forced her to go. When she first arrived, there was little to do—sewing tasks were rare—and yet the food and drink were plentiful and rich, so she was somewhat at ease. After several days, the son of the family entered her room at dusk, making advances with words, but she refused. He then forced himself upon her, and she fled through the door, rushing back to her home. The son then sent his mother to persuade her, and she told her son, who also thought it was acceptable. The mother and son pressed her day and night, and in the end, the woman committed suicide by drowning. Alas! A woman of humble birth, living in a place where the giving of orchids and peonies signifies romantic entanglement, yet she remained so firm and pure in her virtue. Is chastity or licentiousness truly a matter of innate nature?

In the western suburb of Suzhou, outside the Chuan Gate, there was a wealthy man who had a son in his old age. The child was only three years old when he suddenly fell ill with a severe condition known as "jingfeng" (a type of convulsion or epilepsy), and after one fainting spell, he did not regain consciousness. The old man was deeply grieved, and the entire family was in a state of panic, searching far and wide for a doctor. One day, a Taoist monk knocked on the door, claiming he could heal the child. When asked what he needed, the monk instructed that a shrine be set up in a quiet room, and that one peck of white rice, ten taels of gold, and ten taels of silver be used to anchor the altar. The monk entered the room, recited incantations for a long time, and then ordered the child to be brought out. The monk stroked the child's body several times and then firmly pressed his middle finger against the child's nose. Suddenly, the child sneezed. The monk exclaimed, "He is alive! He is alive!" The old man was greatly relieved and invited the monk to stay for a meal, but the monk refused and hurriedly departed. When they looked at the gold and silver used to anchor the altar, they found that the wrapping was still intact, but the precious metals had already been replaced with tiles and gravel. Just as they were astonished and suspicious, the child suddenly suffered another convulsion and died on that very day.

Mr. Su, a man from Suzhou, was eccentric and lacking in wisdom. Shortly after his mother's death, he immediately wanted to take a concubine and sought out beautiful women everywhere. His relative, Mr. Yi, learned of this and showed him a photograph of a woman taken by Western methods, saying, "Do you think she is beautiful?" Mr. Su replied, "Very beautiful." Mr. Yi said, "This is the daughter of a certain family; she can be arranged for you. However, a substantial dowry is required." Mr. Su then entrusted Mr. Yi to negotiate, and after several back-and-forth exchanges, Mr. Yi reported to Mr. Su, "The matter is settled." They agreed on a dowry of 500 taels of silver, with an initial payment of 200 taels to prepare the concubine's dowry. Mr. Su paid the amount as agreed. After several days with no news, he sent someone to inquire, and found that Mr. Yi had already left far away. Mr. Yi left a letter for Mr. Su, saying, "You have just suffered a great loss and are immediately taking a concubine; not only is there the fear of public opinion, but it is also forbidden by national law. This matter should be handled gradually. There are many beautiful women in the world; wait until you have completed the mourning period, and we will consider it again."

I have just the one head and two arms. I am just about to go on a long journey, so I need to borrow some money in advance for use. Upon receiving this letter, Jia felt disoriented for several days. Someone comforted him, saying, "Spending this two hundred taels of silver saves so much trouble, it's worth it." Another person, during the provincial examiner's examination period, falsely claimed to be a distant relative of the examiner and could facilitate connections. A student was deceived by this claim and agreed to pay two hundred taels of silver, giving one-fourth upfront. After the exam, it proved ineffective, and when he tried to find the person again, they were nowhere to be found. Suddenly, he received a letter stating, "The provincial examiner is a high-ranking official of the court; how could one influence him with private matters? Students should understand their duty and fate; there is no way to advance through connections. Thank you for the silver, which I will temporarily use for casual expenses. I hope you will strive to study diligently to advance, do not seek shortcuts, and avoid breaking the law." These words can be considered as advice that is like medicine. Spending fifty taels of silver to get it, it cannot be called not worth it.

Mr. Jia was a copper craftsman who lived alone outside the阊gate of Suzhou. He had some savings but was thrifty, and his clothes and shoes were incomplete. He lived in two dilapidated rooms, inside which there were only straw mats and old rags. One night, he sat alone without sleeping, as a storm with wind and rain raged outside. Suddenly, he heard a sobbing sound at the door. Upon opening it, he saw a young woman, quite beautiful. He asked where she came from, and the woman, speaking with a northern Jiangsu accent, said, "My parents are both dead, and I was lured here by relatives, who were going to sell me to a brothel. Fearing that I would fall into a fire pit, I ran away in the rain, but now I have no place to go." After speaking, she wept again and handed a gold hairpin to Mr. Jia, asking him to help her. Mr. Jia was attracted by her beauty and also lured by her wealth, so he invited her into the room and said, "The room only has one bed, what should we do?" The woman replied, "Since I am here, I will do whatever you say." Mr. Jia was pleased and lay down with her. When it was morning, the woman got up to cook, as if they were very comfortable. Soon, several people from northern Jiangsu forced their way in, saw the woman, and said, "Here she is!" They then also seized Mr. Jia and said, "You lured and hid a virtuous woman; you should be bound and sent to the authorities." The woman lowered her head and said nothing, and Mr. Jia was also unable to argue. The group searched the room thoroughly and found a gold hairpin, sixteen gold rings, and several dozen foreign coins. They asked the woman, "Did you bring these items with you?" She nodded, and they took everything, taking the woman out. They also wanted to take Mr. Jia away, but one person tried to mediate and persuade them. They then let him go and dispersed.

A man from Shandong named Zhu, who traded in Suzhou, was by nature fond of giving. If someone came to him in urgent need, he would lend them money without demanding repayment. When he was about to die, his son asked him, "Were the loans you gave out recorded with contracts or bonds?" Zhu replied, "You need not ask about that. In my pillow box, there is a small chest. After my death, you may open it and see; it will provide you with a lifetime of sustenance." Soon after, Zhu died, and his son opened the chest. Inside, there was nothing but a single copy of the *Zhu Zi Jia Xun* (Instructions for the Family of Zhu). This man had the same intention as Yanzi, who carved a message into the lintel of his house, and his wisdom surpassed that of ordinary scholars and officials.

At dusk, I saw an older woman crying on the road. I asked her, and she said, "I am from a certain place and boarded a ferryboat here to visit a relative, but their family has already moved to another location. I want to return home, but the night ferry has already departed. I am at a loss, not knowing what to do, and that is why I am sad."
