Moved with pity, I invited her to my home, fed her coarse millet, and let her stay overnight. At dawn, she left without saying goodbye, and I realized that she had lost countless clothes and belongings. It dawned on me that she was responsible for the theft: women can also be thieves. There was a household where something was lost every day, though the value was not much, yet it happened every day. There were no outsiders coming and going in the house, only a young neighbor boy who came several times a day, played for a long time before leaving. He was not yet ten years old, so it was not doubted. One day, when this boy was about to leave, he tripped over the threshold and fell. I quickly helped him up, and several items fell out of his pocket, all of which were from his home. I realized that this boy was responsible for the theft: children can also be thieves. In Jinling, there was a man who had taken the examination for young scholars for many years and studied at Fengchi Academy, unexpectedly ranking high. However, by nature, he was frivolous and unrestrained, and his mother spoiled him, not taking precautions. He had a secret affair with a neighbor girl, and the girl's mother demanded ten taels of silver, but he could not pay. Just then, a widowed old woman named Zhu who lived nearby and was quite wealthy, the man then sneaked into her room at night, stealing several dozen taels of silver, thirty taels of gold, and more than ten pearls. At dawn, the woman reported it to the authorities, and the officials went to investigate, concluding, "This was not done by an outsider."

A thorough search of his home revealed gold and jewels in the study of a certain family's son, but no foreign coins were found. The person was then bound and taken away: this scholar could be a thief. On the front street of Suzhou, there was a guesthouse with many buildings, each room divided by wooden boards, creating front and back sections, with guests occupying separate rooms. A guest from the front room left, locked the outer door, and upon returning, opened the lock and entered to find the guest from the back room in his room, having already gone through his boxes, bags, and chests. The wooden boards were not very high, so he stacked chairs and leaned on them to climb over, easily jumping over them. The guest who lived in the back room had been there for a month, usually wearing a fifth-rank official's cap, riding out in a sedan chair, with one servant answering the door and another following him—clearly an official: this official could also be a thief. Alas! The custom of leaving outer doors unlocked is indeed difficult to expect from the middle ages and beyond. Yang Lian'an of Jinling was a doctor.

It was already the second watch of the night when someone pounded on the door with great urgency. Upon asking, the person said, "I seek a doctor." The owner then sent a hired woman to open the door, only to find that it was a thief. The thief, masked and holding a knife, directly entered Yang's room. Upon seeing the thief, Yang felt panic and at a loss, and turned to say, "Why do you need to wear a mask to come here?" His words implied that he had nothing valuable to offer the thief, making the thief's visit pointless. The thief, hearing these words, mistakenly believed that Yang recognized his face, and thus did not dare to rob and left. Another doctor surnamed Zhang from Chuzhou also encountered a similar situation one night. A thief, also masked and holding a knife, entered his room. Zhang, who recognized the thief, said to him, "Why do you need to wear a mask to come here?" The thief, realizing that the owner recognized him, feared that he would report to the authorities the next day, and eventually killed Zhang and left. Both thieves were masked, and both were asked the same rhetorical question, yet one escaped unharmed while the other was killed. Can all things in the world be judged by the same standard?

In the county of Jixi, there was an old man named Yin, who was friends with Old Man Liu. Before Old Man Liu died, he entrusted his son to Old Man Yin. As the Liu son grew up, he became a vagrant and unworthy, making drinking and gambling his daily affairs. Old Man Yin recounted his father's last words, earnestly advising and warning him, weeping and crying bitterly, but the Liu son never repented. Old Man Yin knew that he could not teach him, so he made his guests follow him in gambling every day. When the Liu son lost a lot, Old Man Yin then taught him to sell his land, and he paid his own money, under someone else's name, to buy it at a low price. The income from the land was set aside in a separate place, and he did not touch even a grain of it. All of the Liu son's sales of land, property, gold and jade, and fine objects, Old Man Yin bought with the money from the land. Within a few years, all the good land and fine property belonged to Old Man Yin. Except for buying items from the Liu family, he had no other use for the money, and his savings became even more abundant, which the Liu son did not know. After a long time, even the house where he lived also belonged to Yin, and the Liu son had no place to stand. At first, he stayed in the homes of relatives, suffering great humiliation by receiving cold food and wine. He then moved to temples and monasteries, but was not accepted. He ended up begging in the market, and at night, he slept on the ground with the sky as his canopy, joining the ranks of beggars.

Mr. Yin then invited Li Zi to his home, bathed and fed him, and said, "Do you also remember what I said before?" Li Zi burst into tears and blamed himself. Mr. Yin replied, "The lost thing cannot be returned. Just try to study hard, and you may hope for success in the future." From then on, Li Zi indeed became determined and studied diligently. After a year, he was enrolled as a student. Mr. Yin then returned all the land and houses he had bought, saying, "Before, I saw you lost and not understanding. It cannot be resolved by words alone. Unless one reaches the end of their resources, they do not know to change their plan. I had no choice but to use this risky strategy, placing you in a desperate situation to survive. Before, I played drinking and gambling games with someone named 某甲某乙, and those were my agents; the people who bought your land and houses were my aliases. Now this plan has succeeded, your son's future is limitless. I am old and will soon die. When I die, I will have no shame in facing your father in the underworld." Li Zi was so moved that he knelt and bowed deeply, unable to speak. The villagers all praised Mr. Yin for his noble character and foresight, noting that not even ordinary people could achieve such things. I have read a similar story in a Yuan dynasty play, and I suspect it is an ancient story that was mistakenly passed down as a modern one. However, the storytellers are very convincing, and they believe it is not a lie, so I will write it down. As a minor official and a novelist, I do not need to be overly concerned with the truthfulness of the story, but I will only take what is sufficient to influence the world.

A traveler from Ningbo reported an incident of a girl child being burned to death. In an open field, they piled up firewood and placed the infant on top, then lit the fire and burned her. At first, she cried out pitifully, then moved weakly, and after a long time, her skin and bones were completely charred, and she no longer resembled a human form. They then wrapped her remains in cloth, tied them with stones, and threw them into the river. Hundreds of spectators were moved to sigh. When asked why this was done, it was explained that the family had already drowned two daughters. Now, with the birth of another daughter, they chose not to drown her but to burn her, hoping her spirit would be afraid and not come back. Alas! Drowning girls has already become a shameful custom, yet they resort to burning her with a single flame. What crime has this innocent child committed, to suffer such a cruel fate akin to the punishments of Wang Mang? How could the people in charge not impose a strict ban on such practices? The respected lady, the grandmother of Minister Jin Shao, lived to an advanced age and passed away just short of her hundredth year. In her later years, her mental faculties declined, and she would call her daughter-in-law and granddaughter-in-law "big sister" and "second sister," thinking they were her relatives. On the day of family rituals, when the daughter-in-law prepared the food, she would say she had already eaten. At first, it was thought to be the ramblings of old age, but this happened every time the rituals were performed, which was strange. Upon asking what she had eaten, it turned out to be the sacrificial offerings. They realized that her physical and spiritual essence had already departed, and on the day of the family rituals, she was already sharing in the enjoyment of the ancestors. This led them to understand that "preparing the hall with purity" indeed means that the spirits do come to partake. The sages understood the nature of the spirits and established the rituals of sacrifice. As the *Book of Songs* says, "The fragrant offerings of filial piety are enjoyed by the spirits," which is not an empty statement.

Qin Niang was a courtesan from the entertainment district in Yangzhou. Her father was an elderly scholar, and no one knows his surname. She was born with extraordinary beauty, but she lost her parents at a young age and lived with her uncle. Her uncle was in debt to the government, and out of necessity, he considered selling his niece. A matchmaker deceived him, and Qin Niang was taken into a brothel. Qin Niang remained chaste and refused to compromise her virtue. Her foster mother tried to persuade her with kind words, but she refused. The foster mother then resorted to intimidation and severe beating, but Qin Niang only swore to die rather than yield. The foster mother, at her wit's end, considered selling Qin Niang to another place, but she hesitated because of Qin Niang's beauty. Someone suggested to the foster mother, "All women have desires. It would be better to find a wealthy young man and not demand payment for her services. If he finds her pleasing, allow him to stay with her for a night or two, and then it will be easier to arrange a marriage." The foster mother agreed and entrusted her contacts to find a suitable match. Soon, wealthy young men began visiting the brothel daily. Qin Niang would cry whenever she saw them, and she would angrily curse them if they approached her. The foster mother could no longer bear it and began to punish her daily with beatings. Qin Niang resolved to die. One night, she dreamed of an elderly man who said, "I am your father. Do not die. I have already found a good husband for you, and tomorrow you will be married." There was a young man named Jiang from Wu who was traveling to take the imperial examination in the capital. He had no intention of visiting the brothel. Jiang had a friend who had previously been entrusted by the foster mother to find a match for Qin Niang. The friend, seeing Jiang's beauty, persuaded him to visit the brothel. Jiang initially refused, but his friend persisted. When Jiang arrived, he found Qin Niang crying into her pillow, as she had been doing before.

Jiang Diao Zhi said, "I have heard of your name, Qin Niang. I am known by the nickname Jinlang. A match between Qin and Jin is naturally a good alliance. Why do you reject me so deeply?" Upon hearing this, the woman recalled her father's words in a dream. She cast a sidelong glance, noticing Jiang's extraordinary demeanor, and without realizing, her crying suddenly stopped. The woman's foster mother was delighted and said, "Very good, very good! Today, the immortal woman has thoughts of the mortal world. I will go prepare the wine and food." The woman and Jiang sat together in the room, though they exchanged no words, there was no sign of resentment. Soon, the wine and food arrived, and the foster mother invited the woman to sit with them. The woman gracefully joined them, though tears still streaked her face. Jiang, seeing that no one else was present, asked her, "Observing your emotional state, you must have hidden feelings. Though our acquaintance is shallow, what prevents us from speaking deeply?"

The woman spoke at length about her own goals and shared her dream with him, then spoke tearfully, saying, "If you can commit to a lifelong partnership, I will obey the command of my father in my dream. But if you consider me a woman of low social standing, merely a fleeting whim, then even death would not make me follow you." Jiang sighed and said, "What a determined woman you are! I have not yet married, but I am poor and cannot provide a luxurious home. What can I do?" The woman replied, "If you consent to be with me, I will not resent a simple life. I only ask that we first swear by the sky and the sun, and then share meals together." Jiang agreed, and they made a vow before the gods. That night, they shared a feast. The foster mother was delighted with the daughter's new resolve and decided to keep her for a few more days, so they spent three days together. The woman said to Jiang, "After you leave, the foster mother will not allow me to live alone. You should make plans early. Who lives with you in your household? Where do you reside? Please tell me everything." Jiang replied, "There is no one at home except for my widowed sister, who relies on me. We live in a certain alley in Suzhou."

The concubine was delighted and said, "I have a plan now. You should write a letter to your elder sister, detailing my situation, and I will have a way to escape this dangerous situation." Jiang followed her advice. Three days after Jiang left, the stepmother indeed invited another guest, and the concubine forced a smile to welcome him, getting him drunk with wine. She then put on the guest's clothes and shoes, pretending to be the guest, and opened the door, walking out directly. She shouted loudly, "What kind of maid are you, so stubborn, making me furious!" The stepmother suspected that the concubine had changed again and offended the guest, chasing her out to apologize, but the concubine walked away proudly and left. When they entered the room to inspect, the guest was indeed drunk and asleep, and the concubine had already escaped. They then began to chase her. Just as she was about to leave the door, a strong wind suddenly arose, and all the lamps and candles went out. Because the concubine had silently prayed to her father, there was someone secretly helping her. The pursuers were all shocked and returned. The concubine walked alone in the darkness, as if someone was guiding her, and she boarded a boat to Suzhou, finally arriving at Jiang's house. She sent a letter to her sister. After her sister examined the letter and found it true, she kept her, but the concubine was already pregnant. At the due time, she gave birth to a boy. Her sister was initially doubtful, but when she saw that the boy looked very much like her brother, she was greatly pleased. Jiang, after parting from the concubine and going to the capital, took the examination in the capital but failed. Someone recommended him to join the education office in Sichuan, but just as he arrived, the education commissioner died, and Jiang stayed in Sichuan and could not return. Soon, he encountered the uprising of religious bandits in Sichuan and Hubei, which made him even more difficult. Just then, a general wanted to invite a friend who was a secretary, and Jiang entered his office. The host and guest got along very well. At first, he only managed writing and correspondence, but after a long time, he participated in secret plans and discussions, and was recommended for the position of tutor with military merit.

At that time, the roads were blocked, and it was rare to encounter fish and geese, indicating the difficulty of communication. Jiang was also engaged in military service, neglecting family affairs, and thus had been out of touch with his family for a long time. After the pacification of Sichuan and Hubei, he was awarded for his service and selected for the position of magistrate, and he finally asked for leave and returned home. He had left home and traveled north to this place, nearly twenty years ago. As he looked far into the distance at his hometown, he felt a sense of apprehension as he approached. Upon reaching his neighborhood, he found that the house was as it was before, with red lanterns hanging and colorful curtains hanging high, drums and music filling the air, spilling out into the streets, but he did not know what was happening. Upon entering, he found the house full of guests, most of whom were strangers. A young man approached and asked where the guest came from. Jiang was surprised and said, "I am the former Mr. Jiang, and this is my home." The young man was greatly startled and entered. Soon, a middle-aged woman came out, and she was his sister, who was surprised and delighted and said, "My younger brother has returned?" She led the young man to Jiang and said, "This is my younger brother's son." It turned out that Jiang's son was already twenty years old, and on this day, he was just getting married. The guests were all greatly amazed, considering it a coincidence. His sister said, "There is one matter that is difficult. My sister-in-law is about to become a grandmother, but she is still wearing her hair down and dressed as a young woman. Making her change her appearance is not possible. Now that my younger brother has fortunately returned home, what should we do?" One guest suggested, "Why not take advantage of this auspicious day and let the parents and the son's wife complete their marriages on the same day? It would also be a good story." Everyone in the hall exclaimed, "Yes." Inside the blue tent, the candles were lit brightly, and the father-in-law and mother-in-law bowed first, followed by the son and his wife. The spectators all murmured, considering it an unprecedented grand event. Those who enjoy telling stories wrote a legend called "The Legend of the Qin and Jin Pairing."

In a rural area of Suzhou, there was a man named Jia who was wealthy but had no children. At the age of forty, he was widowed and decided to seek a concubine. One day, he chanced upon the city gate of Changmen and encountered an old woman whom he seemed to recognize. She invited him to her home, where they exchanged polite greetings. She then asked, "Since you have lost your wife, are you considering remarrying or buying a concubine?" Jia replied, "I am also thinking of buying a concubine." The old woman volunteered to act as a matchmaker. As they spoke, a young woman brought tea. The old woman said, "This is my young daughter. Do you think you could take her as a concubine?" Jia glanced at the girl and found her quite beautiful and graceful. He then asked about the dowry and who the matchmaker would be. The old woman explained, "I am childless and have been a widow for a long time, and I have been thinking of becoming a nun. However, I am troubled by this daughter. If she finds a suitable home, I will be greatly relieved. I do not dare to ask for much. I only hope for one hundred taels of silver, which will be used for my alms and begging bowl. If you agree, we can make a face-to-face agreement, exchange the person and money, and there is no need for a matchmaker." Jia was delighted and found that he had the exact amount in his bag. He immediately gave the money to the old woman, took the girl with him, and returned on the same boat. Just as they left the city, several people arrived in boats, among them an old man who cried out for his daughter. The girl also cried out for her father. The two boats were very close, and the old man rushed onto the boat, taking his daughter away. The crowd became noisy, and the situation seemed to turn violent. Jia had no marriage contract and could not prove himself. When he asked the girl, she remained silent. He realized he had been deceived by the old woman and cried out in frustration, but the crowd had already scattered in a hurry. This is what the Suzhou saying refers to as "releasing a white pigeon."

Chapter Four: A Woman from Songjiang A man from Songjiang, named Zou, married a woman named Qiao, and they had a son named Ajiu. When Ajiu was just one year old, Zou died. Qiao maintained her virtue and raised the orphan. Her household was modestly comfortable, sufficient for their needs. At this time, the Taiping rebels had already occupied Suzhou and Hangzhou, and Songjiang had also fallen to the rebels. Qiao feared she could not escape, and considered ending her life to preserve her virtue. However, thinking of her crying infant, she realized that the child could not survive without her, and her mind remained undecided. That night, she suddenly dreamed that her husband told her, "Our family has had only one child per generation for three generations. Now we have only this one child. I have already asked our deceased ancestors, and you should rather lose your chastity than abandon the orphan." Upon waking, Qiao thought about her husband's words, which made sense, but she believed that a woman's virtue was paramount and could not be compromised. Her mind was still undecided. That night, she dreamed again that her husband came with two elderly people, an old man and an old woman, who said, "We are your father-in-law and mother-in-law. Your intention is good, but for your own sake, it is more important to maintain your virtue; for the sake of our family, it is more important to preserve the orphan. We wish you to consider the family's welfare rather than just your own." Upon waking, Qiao set up an offering and paid respects to her in-laws and husband, saying, "I have understood your wishes." Later, both mother and child were captured by the rebels and taken to Suzhou.

Qiao was exceptionally beautiful and was favored by the bandit leader. While holding her child, Ajiu, she never left his side. She told the bandit, "If you love me, please also love my child. If this child dies, I will die too." The bandit was so infatuated with her beauty that he did not take away Ajiu. After a long time, Qiao was regarded as a woman of high moral integrity, and Ajiu was treated as a nobleman's son. A "贞人" is a woman among the bandits who holds a notable position. At that time, the bandits had occupied Suzhou and Hangzhou for a long time, burning and looting the villages outside the city walls almost completely, and even chickens and pigs had been exterminated. The bandits bought all their daily necessities at high prices from the north of the river. Therefore, the poor people in the north of the river often used small boats to carry various goods across the river and sell them secretly to the bandits. There was a man named Zhang Tuzi, whose wife and he had been in this business for the longest time, and the bandits trusted them the most. They were given a small flag, and they could go anywhere within the bandits' territory. Qiao heard about this and sent a message to the "贞人," summoning Zhang's wife to speak with her and ask her to buy various goods from the north of the river. After frequent meetings, she secretly told her the plan and plotted to escape together. They pretended to celebrate Qiao's birthday while the bandit leader was traveling to Huzhou, got the servants drunk with wine, and at night, they took Ajiu and boarded Zhang Tuzi's boat to flee. The boat had a bandit flag, so no one questioned them, and they safely reached the north of the river.

While Zhang's wife believed that Qiao, having lived among the bandits for so long, must have brought something with her; they investigated and found nothing, quite disappointed, they then took him to Yangzhou and sold Qiao to a brothel, unaware of what was happening. The brothel owner, along with many others, took him away. Qiao still held Ajiu tightly and said to the brothel owner, "The one who bought me from your family treats me as a money tree. If this child dies, I will die too, and your family will lose both person and wealth. If you let me raise this child, then I, a woman who has lost her virtue, how can I still be concerned with reputation and morality?" The brothel owner agreed to it. Qiao lived in the brothel for several years, and Ajiu also grew up. Qiao used the money she earned as a prostitute to pay for her own education and made Ajiu study under a private teacher. Soon after the bandits were defeated, Qiao saved up money to repay the brothel and buy her freedom, and took Ajiu back to Songjiang, where she lived with her brothers. When Ajiu grew up, Qiao arranged for him to marry, and she then set up an altar to worship her in-laws and husband, saying, "I was once entrusted with the task of caring for an orphan, fortunately, I did not fail my mission. But for women, chastity is the most important thing. I, a woman, first was a chaste person among the bandits, then became a prostitute, how can I still face the world?" She hanged herself and died. This woman, by not dying but caring for the orphan, and still by dying to uphold her chastity, did not lose her integrity. Chengzi said, "Dying of hunger is a minor matter, losing one's chastity is a grave matter."

However, dying of hunger and losing one's moral integrity, both affect the individual alone. If one loses personal reputation and moral integrity, but preserves the family honor, it seems that family honor is more important than personal reputation. Guan Zhong had great achievements in stabilizing the state of Qi, so he did not need to follow minor moral lapses. This comparison is not applicable to officials who prioritize comfort and longevity. In the third year of the Xianfeng era, bandits rose in Shandong and raided the village of Zhongcun in Feixian County. One bandit rode a horse in the wilderness and encountered a woman walking alone. He quickly dismounted and pushed the woman to the ground, intending to rape her. Unaware that this woman was a prostitute, she was not at all ashamed or fearful, instead laughing and saying, "Your horse will escape, what then?" The bandit thought her words were true, but looked around and found no place to tether the horse. The woman then laughed and said, "What a fool, you bandit! Why don't you tie it to your own foot?" The bandit also laughed and said, "Okay." He then pulled the horse's reins and tied them to his own foot, undressed, and approached the woman. The woman suddenly picked up the bandit's knife from the ground and struck the horse's tail with force. The horse was startled and in pain, racing wildly for more than ten miles without stopping. The bandit was dragged away, crushed skull and broken ribs, whether alive or dead is unknown. The woman watched, clapped and laughed loudly, searched the bandit's clothes, and found several large pieces of gold. She carried it back home.

A wealthy man from Huizhou named Xu, intending to bury his parents, extensively sought geomancers, traveling to countless mountains, yet failed to find an auspicious burial site. By chance, he passed by a woodcutter's hut, where a geomancer remarked, "This land is surrounded by mountains, with wind concealed and energy gathered—truly an auspicious sign." Xu asked, "Then why is his family so poor?" The geomancer replied, "It is suitable for a burial site, not a residential one." Xu sent someone to consult the woodcutter, who said, "My ancestors' old hut is invaluable, worth a hundred gold pieces." Xu's follower, a man surnamed Zhou, skilled in mental calculation and eloquent speech, was sent again to persuade him. The woodcutter finally could not be persuaded. As Zhou was about to leave, he saw a young girl with braided hair entering, carrying firewood, her face graceful and beautiful. Zhou remarked, "Is this the woodcutter's cherished daughter?" The woodcutter replied, "Indeed. We have no son, only this daughter."

"Is she engaged to be married?" asked Xu. "No," replied the man. The woman from Zhou informed Xu, "I have found it. Chan has a daughter whose age is similar to your youngest son. Why don't you consider marrying her as your daughter-in-law? This place can definitely be obtained." Xu's father said, "He is just a woodcutter. How could he be married into our family?" Zhou replied, "How narrow-minded you are! After obtaining this land, whether this girl stays or leaves is entirely up to you." So she asked Zhou to act as a matchmaker. The woodcutter initially refused, saying they were not a suitable match, but after several back-and-forth discussions, he finally agreed. A year later, they hastily completed the wedding. Xu's father told the woodcutter, "We have quite a spacious house. Why don't you move your family here so that the bride and groom can see each other every day, and they won't feel lonely." The woodcutter agreed and moved with his wife. When they first arrived, they got along very well. Xu then asked the woodcutter again about the land. The woodcutter said, "Now that we are close relatives, why should we cherish our humble abode? You may accept it as a gift."

Mou then chose an auspicious day, hired a geomancer to determine the grave site and dig the tomb. After digging several feet deep, they found a broken stele that said, "This place is unsuitable for living, but it is auspicious for burial." Then he realized that the geomancer's statement that it is suitable for a tomb but not for a house was not wrong. After the burial for several years, his family became richer and more prosperous. His eldest son entered public service through the grain donation system and became a county magistrate in the Qin region. His second son also passed the imperial examinations and became a scholar-official. But Mou's youngest son greatly enjoyed idling and playing. Soon, he married a courtesan as a concubine. The woodcutter's daughter had slight disagreements, and they would immediately quarrel. The woodcutter's daughter complained to her in-laws, but they did not think she was right. They said, "You are a girl from a poor family, with shallow eyes. But my husband is a son of a wealthy family, sleeping east and west is a common thing, how can it be like a farmer's son who has only one wife!" The concubine heard this and became even more arrogant. She daily spread slander against her husband and falsely accused him of private matters. The woodcutter's daughter had no way to prove her innocence and hanged herself. The old woman, grieving for her daughter, also died. Then they drove away the woodcutter.

The elderly man returned, lingering at the newly constructed tomb of the Xu family, weeping bitterly at its foot, and died upon touching the tombstone. Not long after, a great thunderstorm struck the mounds of the Xu family, uncovering the burial site and breaking open the coffin. The Xu elder, having no choice, replaced the coffin and re-buried the remains, and in the process, found a broken stone tablet inscribed with the words: "Righteousness brings good fortune, unrighteousness leads to ruin." This inscription was in harmony with the previous one, indicating that they were part of the same stone. On the day of the reburial, the youngest son suddenly died. The following year, the second son, who was taking the imperial examination in the capital, died from a fall from a carriage. The eldest son, who was serving as an official in the Qin region, was executed for embezzling military funds. The Xu elder lost his home in his old age and died of grief. Meanwhile, a guest of the Xu family, surnamed Zhou, happened to pass by the Xu family's tomb and saw the woodcutter bowing to someone. It was still the same place where the woodcutter used to stay. Zhou woke up in a state of fear, feeling as if he were still in a dream. He rushed back home and told his family about what he had seen, but before he could finish, he suddenly said, "The woodcutter has come again!" He then coughed up blood and died.

A witch named Jiudi Gun, known for his ability to summon the spirits of the deceased with a spell, performed his ritual by first kneeling down and muttering incantations softly. After finishing the incantation, he rolled on the ground once, and the spirit of the deceased would then attach itself to his body, allowing him to converse with the family as if the deceased were still alive. His method was very effective, which is why he gained this name, though his real name is less well-known now. One day, a scholar asked him to summon his father's spirit. After a long time, the spirit did not come. The witch felt ashamed and went to see his master to ask for an explanation. The master said, "The scholar's father must have been a very wicked person." The witch replied, "He is also a Confucian scholar, and I have not heard of any major sins." The master then said, "Then the scholar must have been reborn in heaven." The witch asked for an explanation. The master replied, "You can only perform the ritual to summon the dead, but you do not understand the circumstances of the deceased. When a person is alive, they are a body of flesh and blood, and their substance is heavy and turbid. Even sages like Confucius and Mencius, who have knowledge that encompasses heaven and earth, and heroes like Bi Gan and Yu Hui, who have the strength to tear apart buffaloes and drag oxen, cannot move if they leave the ground by even a step. When they die, this solid mass is buried underground, but their residual energy still exists, and it is light and clear, rising upwards. Generally, the clearer the energy, the higher it rises. So Confucius, Mencius, Yan Hui, and Zengzi are revered for thousands of years, and in the world, there is no trace of them. Their energy has risen to a very high place, far from people."

If there is even a trace of impure qi remaining, one cannot be in harmony with the Great Void. Thus, there are those who stand out clearly and in an orderly array as bright deities. The lower their rank, the more impure qi they have, and the closer they are to humans. As for ordinary people, they are born ordinary and die without significance, relying on their descendants to reside. Those whom you summon and immediately appear are such spirits. For extremely evil people, having lost their pure qi for a long time, they are purely impure qi. In life, they have a form and substance, which allows them to mingle with the human world; but after death, their form and substance separate, and they are no longer borne by the earth. Their qi becomes heavier and sinks deeper, falling into the Nine Lower Regions, and they are far from the human realm. I initially suspected that the father of this person was a great evil-doer, fearing that he had fallen into the Nine Lower Regions, which is beyond the reach of my art to summon. If not such a person, then it must be that this person has more pure qi than impure qi, having transcended the realm of the mundane, so I know that he has been born into heaven. This argument is extremely precise, something that other shamans cannot comprehend. The Buddhist concepts of heaven and hell do not go beyond this. I have often wondered why ordinary people still have spirits after death, while scholars and officials, upon their deathbed, become silent, which is hard to understand. With this shaman's argument, I now understand. If one repays kindness with kindness and resentment with resentment, and their hearts are attached to something, then regardless of the purity of their qi, they cannot ascend, and they often manifest to bring about good or evil. This is something the shaman did not mention, but it can be inferred.

Hu, a native of Suzhou, serves in the underworld. Whenever he goes on duty, he is never aware of it; sometimes he walks down a busy street and suddenly falls to the ground; at other times, he may fall from a staircase, breaking his face, which causes him great suffering. He laments to the master of the underworld, asking to be forewarned of the dates when he will be called. The master says, "Others do not experience this, but you do, which indicates a predestined punishment. You should make a vow of repentance." Hu vows never to take another life from then on. Afterward, he begins to know in advance when he will be called, and he prepares by lying down on his bed, falling into a deep and death-like sleep, sometimes waking up after one or two days. As for what he does in the underworld, he does not speak of it to others, only saying that the cities and markets there are no different from those in the human world. However, the underworld is dark and without sunlight, and the people along the way rub shoulders with one another, yet they never speak a word to each other. He also says that the underworld has several levels, and the place where he serves is the lowest level; looking from the lowest level to the next higher level is like looking from a small town to the provincial capital; looking from the next higher level to the one above it is like looking from the provincial capital to the imperial city; if one goes even higher, it cannot be seen. Those on the lowest level can temporarily reach the next higher level but cannot reach the one above that; those on the higher levels can reach the lowest level, but few actually do. What he says can be corroborated with the words of the witch.

In the countryside of Nanchang, Jiangxi, there was a young boy. He released ducks into the river. One duck secretly entered a field ridge, and the field owner, a certain scholar, was walking along the edge of the field and captured it to take back. The boy came to claim the duck, but the scholar angrily waved his arm at him, causing the boy to fall into a pond in front of the door. The pond was wide and deep, and the boy drowned. His mother arrived crying, and the scholar loudly declared, "Your son fell into the water and died; what do I have to do with it?" The mother saw his stern expression and words, and being afraid to argue, and also grieving over her son's death, she also drowned herself in the pond. The matter was reported to the authorities, and the scholar was sentenced to death. On the day of his execution, his wife and three sons all came to cry and bid him farewell, but the scholar remained silent, weeping alone. There were many onlookers who saw that he was around forty years old, calm and elegant in appearance, with no sign of being fierce or violent. Because of one duck, he suffered the death penalty; was this karmic retribution from a past life? It can also serve as a warning to those who lack patience.

In a money shop in Hankou, a woman placed one thousand coins on a money board. In an instant, both the coins and the board disappeared. At that time, there were no other people around, only one woman stood briefly in front of the counter and then left. So several people were sent to search east and west. Indeed, they found her in a narrow alley. She was just pouring the coins into her sleeves, but not all of them were in yet. They immediately pulled her back and took all her money. They were about to let her go. A young man said, "A man committing theft is already despicable, let alone a woman. She should be severely punished." So they completely stripped her of her upper clothing, tied her to a pillar, and beat her with rods. The young man's intention was not to hate her for stealing; it was because she was only in her twenties and had a beautiful appearance. They wanted to see her exposed. As they tied her, the young man deliberately touched her breast. After beating her for a long time, he said, "This woman is stubborn and shameless; she should also be stripped of her lower clothing." Everyone present said, "No, we cannot do that."

The youth then removed his shoes by hand, unfastened his double bindings, and threw them into the main road, saying, "Just to show humiliation." Everyone tried to dissuade him, and he finally let go. The woman put on her clothes and picked up her shoes, cried out, and left. She was from a respectable family, always had a good reputation, but due to poverty, she lost her resolve and suffered this great humiliation. After returning home, she was beaten by her husband again, feeling both shame and anger, and at night, she ultimately hanged herself. The next day, the youth was in the market when he suddenly said, "This woman has come again to steal money!" Everyone thought it was a joke, but as he repeated it, they began to be alarmed. Suddenly, the youth opened his eyes and spoke in the woman's voice, "I didn't come here to steal your money! I came to seek retribution! I truly did not intend to steal your money, but why did you humiliate me so much? If I die, how can you live?" Having said this, he used the grass rope used for threading coins and made a gesture of hanging himself. Everyone was shocked and turned to look, and the youth had already lost consciousness.

In a certain village in Gaoyou County, there lived two peasant sisters. The elder sister married and died. Her husband asked to take her younger sister as his second wife, and both parents were pleased with the idea, but the younger sister refused. The parents insisted, but she firmly refused. The mother privately asked her why, and the sister replied, "The private relationship between husband and wife is the greatest shame in the world, fortunately no one else knows about it. If both sisters were to serve the same husband, then my elder sister's private affairs would be known to me, that would be adding to my elder sister's shame." The parents could not force her, so they gave up. According to the "Biographies of Exemplary Women" in the *History of Ming*, there was a woman named Yu Fengniang from Yiwu. Her elder sister married Xu Minghui and died. Xu Minghui heard of Yu Fengniang's virtue and wanted to marry her as his second wife. Upon hearing this, Yu Fengniang wept and told her parents, "Brothers have never shared a wife, so it is the same for sisters." The father insisted, and Yu Fengniang hanged herself. I believe Yu Fengniang's actions are not worthy of emulation, and her words are also not fully precise in terms of propriety. It is fundamentally different to share a husband versus sharing a wife. A wife cannot be shared, not only for brothers, but even for non-brothers, there is no reason to share a wife. A husband, however, can be shared. The *Gongyang Commentary on the Spring and Autumn Annals* states, "When a feudal lord marries a princess from one state, two other states send brides to accompany her, with her niece and younger sister following. What is a niece? She is the son of one's elder brother; what is a younger sister? She is one's younger sister." Thus, sisters sharing a husband is an ancient custom. If we say that sisters cannot share a husband, then Yao should not have married two of his daughters to Shun. Today this girl's view aligns with Yu Fengniang's. There are indeed similar cases in ancient and modern times, but they are all one-sided actions, not the middle way.

Xi Dāizi was a man from E who made his living by cutting and selling firewood. He was poor and had no wife. However, he had a strong inclination towards sexual desires. Every time he carried firewood into the market, when he met women asking for the price, he would always sell them at a low price without discussing the actual value. Therefore, the people in the market called him "Xi Dāizi" (foolish Xi). In the market, there was an old man who had a daughter who had reached marriageable age and was beautiful. Xi saw her and found her attractive. Every day, he would bundle the firewood and sell it at her house. Soon after, the old man's daughter died. Xi knew where she was buried and took advantage of the night to dig up the grave and carry the body away. He returned home and had intercourse with her. The next day, he locked the door and went out to cut firewood, leaving a fire in the room. Smoke came out from the chimney. Neighbors forced their way in through the door and extinguished the fire. They saw someone lying on the bed and laughed, saying, "So noisy, and still not awake! Who is this person?" They lifted the blanket and closely examined it. It was a dead person. They were greatly surprised. Someone recognized and said, "This is the daughter of a certain old man." The old man heard about it and rushed to the scene. Upon verification, it was confirmed, and it was reported to the authorities. Punished according to the law. How strange! There is someone so obsessed with sex. Thus, one can only sigh that Emperor Song Wu of the Southern Dynasties made a coffin with interchangeable lids for his favorite concubine, Yin Shuyi, so that when he wanted to see her, he could pull out the lid to look at her body. It was still not an unusual thing.

There was a couple from Guangdong who lived in Suzhou. The wife, every year at the end of the year, would save her earnings from needlework and request a few pieces of foreign currency from her husband to send to her mother in Guangdong. One year, when she made the request, her husband refused to give her the money. The wife was furious and wept for several days before committing suicide by hanging. She had a son who was just two years old. The husband, lacking a wet nurse or caregiver, sold the child to the Liu family. The Liu family took the child away. Two days later, the wife's ghost suddenly possessed her son and spoke to Liu, saying, "Quickly summon my husband." Liu was greatly alarmed and summoned the husband. The ghost then said, "You do not care for my mother, and you do not care for your own son. How wicked you are! I have come to take the child away, so that he will not be called someone else's father." After saying this, the child died. This woman can truly be called a spirit. Yet, her resentment toward her husband led her to kill her son. She was indeed a fierce woman, and it is appropriate that she chose to harm herself.

In the first month of Ding Chou in the Guangxu era (January 1897), a fire broke out in the city of Yuyao, spreading to burn over four hundred rooms. The hall of a certain family was destroyed, but the main gate remained standing alone and unscathed, with the plaque above the gate inscribed by Wang Yangming of the Ming dynasty. According to the old people, this gate had encountered fires three times before and remained unharmed each time. Could it be that the writings of great scholars are indeed protected by divine beings? A widow from the Huang family, living outside the Qi Gate of Suzhou, went to draw water from the river. Her five-year-old son followed her. The woman slipped and fell into the water, and the child cried loudly, also crawling into the water. Just then, a boat passed by, and they pulled the woman out, but the child had already been swept away by the current. They reached the other side of the river and managed to rescue the child, who was found with a swollen abdomen and no breath. Following the method, they placed the child on a large bamboo basket, ground soapberry into a fine powder, and blew it into the child’s nose. Soon, the child made a loud cry, vomited out a large amount of water, and finally revived. A young child, following his mother into the water, acted out of his true nature. To die and then revive, could it be that the ghosts and spirits were secretly helping him?

Xu Zhongwan, a virtuous woman from Pinghu, married Zhao Baoshan, a man from the same village. Zhao Baoshan's brother, Zhao Baoyuan, also married into the Xu family, making Xu Zhongwan and Zhao Baoyuan cousins. They were very close. Zhao Baoshan's father-in-law served as an official in Shu, and his mother-in-law followed him. Xu Zhongwan often wrote letters to inquire about Zhao Baoshan's well-being, and her elegant handwriting impressed her in-laws. Zhao Baoshan was skilled in painting flowers and birds, and Xu Zhongwan would copy his sketches and embroider them onto silk. The embroidery was vivid and well-executed, and those who saw it all praised it. She gave birth to one son and one daughter. When Zhao Baoshan died, Xu Zhongwan said, "My sister-in-law is my sister. My children will not lack a mother, but the deceased has no companion. I will follow him into the afterlife." The family guarded her, preventing her from dying. After a long time, the guard became lax, and she eventually hanged herself. Opening her box, they found several sheets of paper left for her parents and in-laws. She also left poetic instructions for her orphaned children and arranged matters after her death, warning against mixing Buddhist rituals with funeral rites. Women who follow their husbands into the afterlife are common in the world, but Xu Zhongwan, who was both virtuous and talented, is especially rare.

Gu Xiaonü, named Siwei and courtesy name Yafen, was from Jinkui. At sixteen, she followed her parents to the countryside to escape bandits. Her father sometimes taught the children with poetry and prose, and Gu Xiaonü would imitate and write them. Every word she wrote was elegant and pleasing, and in times of hardship, she used this to win her parents' affection. When the chaos subsided and they returned home, the parents fell ill one after another. Gu Xiaonü cut off her arm's flesh and mixed it with medicine to give to them, but it was ultimately ineffective. When the parents died, Gu Xiaonü wanted to sacrifice herself. Someone stopped her and said, "Your mother is ill and has entrusted her weak younger brother to you. Now, in the family, there is strife and chaos, and the situation is very urgent. If you die, what will happen to your younger brother?" She then no longer spoke of dying. After the war, the houses and buildings were all destroyed, and the room she lived in was as dark as ink. Gu Xiaonü also disliked being disturbed by people, so she closed the door and blocked the windows, and her feet did not cross the threshold. She sat there every day, burning oil to read, wearing plain clothes, and eating simple food. Someone advised her to worship the Buddha, but she said, "It is not what I wish." When her younger brother returned from school, she would call him and ask about his studies. She took great care of his clothing and food. If the younger brother needed something, and the head of the household was stingy and did not give it to him, she would then pull out her hairpins and search her box to give to him. When she arranged the marriage for her younger brother, she then sighed and said, "Now, I can finally see my parents in the netherworld." She drank poison to die. She wrote a collection of poems called *Green Fragrance Anthology*, and at the end of her life, she threw it into the fire. This and the case of Xu's chaste wife are both virtuous and talented, so they are recorded together. Although it is a *Yuchu* novel, it has always been about promoting filial piety and righteousness.

In Hankou Town, there was a girl from the Liu family who was betrothed to a son of the Zhang family. They interacted and played together without any taboo, as this was also the custom of their village. There was a wealthy son who found the girl attractive and planned with his father to buy her as a concubine for a thousand gold pieces. The father casually agreed. The girl thought it was true, so that night, she secretly left her home to go to Zhang’s house to inform him, and they immediately performed the wedding ceremony that night. At dawn, the father discovered his daughter was missing and rushed to tell Zhang, and then the girl and her husband came out together to greet him. A virtuous woman would not proceed without the six rites, and this girl’s actions are particularly contrary to proper etiquette. However, since she was already engaged, it is not considered elopement. The Song Confucian scholars say, “to act with moderation and find the middle way is the essence of propriety,” perhaps this can be used to excuse the girl.

In the three villages of Suzhou, there was a monk named Jietong, who was sixty years old. On New Year's Eve, which was also his birthday, he had a grand assembly of monks and laypeople a month in advance, lavishly setting up wine and food to celebrate his sixtieth birthday. On the same day, he prepared for his afterlife, personally measuring and preparing bedding and shrouds. When guests asked him why, he said, "I won't make it through the winter." Nobody believed him. However, on the second day of the twelfth month, he finally sat in meditation and passed away. This monk usually drank alcohol and ate meat, no different from ordinary people, but his departure was so clear and definite, which is indeed strange. There was also a foolish Taoist priest who sold medicine at the Du Seng Bridge outside the city gate of Suzhou. No one knew where he came from, and his speech was incoherent, so people all thought he was foolish. Every night, he lived in a thatched hut. In the winter of Ding Chou, heavy snowfall covered the ground up to a foot deep, but there was no snow on top of his hut. When someone asked him why, the priest said, "Tomorrow, drink my wine, and I will tell you." The next day, a curious person carried wine to visit, but the priest was nowhere to be found. Could he be an extraordinary person blending in among the common people?

A man from Shanxi, whose name is unknown, entered public service through financial means and was appointed as a mid-level official in the Ministry of Agriculture. He repeatedly failed to advance in his career despite his efforts. In the spring of the year Ding-Chou, he suddenly dreamed of someone reciting a poem to him: "Ten years of a dream in the spring night of flowers and birds, eight years of joy like fish and water in the mirror." Upon waking, he thought the words seemed ominous, and he felt discontented. Soon after, he fell ill and died. From Ding-Mao to Ding-Chou, it was ten years, and his second wife had been with him for exactly eight years. The numbers in the dream matched his life events, which was indeed remarkable. Li Yugu was a courtesan. Originally from Sichuan, she did not know her own surname, and after being taken in by a foster mother, she adopted her surname, hence the name Li. She was quite popular among the courtesans. There was a scholar from Changsha named Li, who was traveling north and passed through Han-Gao. His friend took him to visit a brothel, and he met Li Yugu there. She often looked at him. His friend said, "Do you like Li Lang? He is indeed planning to buy a concubine but has not yet found the right person." Li Yugu privately told Li, "Is it true? If it is true, I have some private funds, and I will give them to you to help you pay the fee to free me from my status." Li was moved by her words and promised to fulfill her request after passing the imperial examinations. However, he failed to pass the examinations and ended up living in poverty in the capital, thus breaking his promise. Li Yugu lived a difficult life in the brothel but never forgot Li. A wealthy merchant, captivated by her beauty, forced her foster mother to give him a thousand gold coins and took her away. Li Yugu refused to eat for seven days, but she did not die. She eventually committed suicide by drinking poison. Alas! Her chastity and loyalty were so great; she was no longer just a courtesan.

People in the countryside call the county magistrate's assistant "Fourth Office," because under the county magistrate, there are the deputy, the clerk, and the assistant, so the assistant is fourth in rank. There was a man named Ma in the town of De Xing, Tongzhou, who was a bully in the countryside, and people called him "Local Fourth Office." He kept a dog, loved it very much, and named it Ah Sheng. He shared his food, drink, sleep, and living space with the dog. Every year on the sixth day of the sixth month, it is said to be the dog's birthday, and he would prepare wine and noodles to celebrate. One year, the dog was killed by a neighbor named Wang Changlin. Ma became very angry and forced the preparation of a coffin to bury the dog, dressed in mourning clothes to send it off. He also had the death notice handwritten and posted in public places. The notice reads: "Unfilial dog son Wang Changlin, burdened with heavy sins, did not die naturally, but brought disaster to his dog father Ah Sheng, who passed away on a certain date. Funeral arrangements will be made immediately. This notice is respectfully given." This is truly an unprecedented strange text. In the past, when King Zhuang of Chu's beloved horse died, he ordered the ministers to mourn it and bury it with the rites of a high-ranking official, but this was stopped by the advice of You Meng. Ah Sheng was ultimately buried with proper rites, and the arrogance of the local "Fourth Office" can be imagined.

In one county of Henan, the name of the county has been forgotten by those who speak of it. The local custom is to marry a young son to an older wife, in order to manage the household and domestic affairs. There was a farmer’s son, barely thirteen or fourteen years old, while the wife he had married was more than twice his age. On the day after their wedding, all the guests had gathered to congratulate them, yet the bedroom door remained unopened, and the day was already late. The father-in-law and mother-in-law called out from outside, hearing their son respond but not seeing him come out. Looking through the window, they saw him bound tightly at the foot of the bed. Startled, they asked him what had happened, and he said, “Last night, after the hour of human settlement, a man emerged from beneath the bed, tied me up here, and lay with my new wife.” When they asked why he had not spoken up, he replied, “If I speak, they will kill me.” Before he had finished speaking, both the man and woman emerged from the bed. The man raised his voice and said, “I have known your new wife since childhood. I took advantage of the confusion to enter this room. I should be allowed to enjoy myself and then leave.”

"If anyone dares to break the door and enter," he said, drawing a white blade from his sleeve and pointing it at his son, "I shall cut open your son's belly!" The whole family was shocked, not knowing what to do. Meanwhile, the man, inside the room, demanded wine and meat, demanded food, demanded soup and steamed cakes, saying, "If you do not give it to me, I will kill your son. If you give it to me but it is not rich or delicious, I will also kill your son." The family, having no choice, gave him everything one by one. The man then had him place the food on the table from the window, and tied his son with a long rope, sending him to the window to retrieve it. First, he had his son taste it, and only after his son had eaten did he himself eat, consuming the remainder in front of his son. After finishing, he placed the empty dishes and ordered them to be removed. Thus, the onlookers gathered in crowds, all fearing that he might harm his son, but having no idea what to do. They held out for three days until the matter was reported to the authorities. The officials were also astonished and personally visited the scene to see it, believing it to be true. The official asked if the bride had parents, and they replied, "Yes." The officials then summoned them, ordering them to call their daughter, but the daughter did not respond. The official commanded the bailiff to beat the father's buttocks and slap the mother's cheek. The parents cried out, their wails very pitiful. Again, they were ordered to call their daughter, but she still did not respond.

Three times, the mother slapped her cheek a hundred times, and the father beat her buttocks two hundred times, causing blood to flow continuously. The parents knelt outside the window, pleading with their daughter to open the door, but she seemed indifferent. The officials had no choice but to order someone to guard her and bind her parents and take them away. At that time, there was a thief in the jail who was skilled at digging through walls. The officials sent him to the house, and he waited outside the door while the thief waited for both the man and woman to fall asleep. The thief then entered from behind the house, secretly used a knife to cut the rope binding the son, and dragged him away. Meanwhile, the person outside the door broke the door and rushed in, capturing both the man and woman. The incident caused a great stir in the entire district, and the crowd filled from the main gate to the hall. The official sat in the main hall and interrogated them. The man was a butcher, with a pockmarked face and a beard, appearing black and ugly. The woman was graceful and fair-skinned, with a slender waist and arched feet, and had a certain charm.

The official called the woman forward and, raising his hand in anger, loudly scolded her, saying: “I have seen many people, but never one as shameless or as unfilial as you! You are worse than a dog or a pig—no longer human at all. The law states that women who commit adultery must be stripped and whipped; how then can you be treated as a human being?” He ordered his attendants to remove all of her clothes, leaving not a single thread on her body. He first struck her cheeks the same number of times as her mother had been struck, then lashed her buttocks the same number of times as her father had been lashed. He then charged her with the crime of adultery and sentenced her to forty lashes with a heavy stick. He ordered her parents to take her away and return the betrothal gifts to the husband’s family. Her parents supported the naked woman as she was led out of the county gate. Each of them removed their own clothes to cover her, but the onlookers numbered no fewer than several thousand, and they rushed forward to tear the clothes from her body. In the end, she returned home without a single piece of clothing. The executioner was whipped two thousand times, but did not die. The next day, he was whipped another two thousand times, but before the punishment was completed, he died. The woman’s parents, shamed and furious, died one after the other. The girl’s wounds soon healed, and she was unharmed. Several years later, someone saw her in an inn, serving wine to patrons. Her grace and manner were unchanged, but there were visible scars on her cheeks. When someone pointed her out and asked her about it, she recounted the events in full detail, without concealing anything. Especially when she described the humiliating experience in the public hall, she spoke with vivid clarity, as if painting the scene with her words. Many people enjoyed listening to her story, using it to earn money to buy food.

In Jiangning, there was a man named Xu who was a son-in-law to the Wang family and had a son. The Wang family, due to Xu's poverty, treated him like a slave. Unable to bear the mistreatment, Xu returned home. His father-in-law was engaged in business in Anhui and planned to move the family there. Upon hearing this, Xu went to try to persuade his wife to come back, but she refused. Realizing that his wife would leave with her mother, Xu discussed the matter with his family members and decided to take the child back. The family agreed. Xu then took seven or eight people with him, first visiting all the neighbors and explaining the situation, and then directly entering his wife's room to seize the child. Xu's mother-in-law shouted that there were thieves, but the neighbors did not respond. When questioned about their inaction, the neighbors said, "Xu's son is being taken by Xu's father and brothers; this is the right thing to do, what business is it of ours?" Xu's mother-in-law then went to Xu's house to retrieve the child, but Xu and his family members all shouted at her, and she returned without success. This act of Xu and his family had the same intention as Zhang Fei and Zhao Yun leading troops to intercept and reclaim Liu Bei's son, Liu Shan (A Dou), from Sun Quan, likely inspired by reading the Romance of the Three Kingdoms. In Yangzhou, there was a woman from the Wang family who was six months pregnant. One day, a neighbor's cat tried to snatch a bird from her cage. The woman got up to chase the cat and fell to the ground, causing a disturbance in the fetus. The situation was very dangerous. A neighbor advised her to quickly find one or two expired and unredeemed pawn tickets, burn them into ash, and mix them with boiling water to drink. If she followed this advice, the fetus would be safe. Indeed, she followed the advice and had no further issues. An expired pawn ticket, once considered useless, had this unexpected use in ensuring the safety of the fetus, a mystery that also illustrates that nothing in the world is truly useless.

In Yangzhou, it is customary for newborn infants to be given 化毒丹 to detoxify the body and eliminate 胎毒. A woman named He gave birth to a son and sent an elderly woman to a pharmacy to buy 化毒丹. This old woman was from Nanjing, and her accent differed from that of Yangzhou. The pharmacy staff misheard her and mistakenly gave her 活络丹 instead. After the infant took the medicine, he suddenly fell severely ill, refusing to nurse, and died within a few days. Upon inspection, a wax pill was found, inscribed with the words "活络丹." After a thorough investigation, the cause of the tragedy was discovered, but it was too late to change the outcome. Thus, one must be extremely cautious when purchasing medicine. There was an elderly woman who sold firewood and brought her son into the city to a wealthy household. Her son was in his late teens and had not yet married. The wealthy family had a bowl of leftover cold porridge from the previous day, in which a dead centipede was found. They considered discarding it into the river, but the son, who was starving, said, "This is not a problem." He then picked up the centipede with his finger and ate the porridge immediately. The wealthy daughter saw him and laughed. The son thought she was attracted to him and could not stop thinking about her, eventually falling ill and becoming increasingly weak, nearly unable to recover. The old woman learned of his feelings and told the servant girl, who relayed the information to the daughter. The daughter said, "Then quickly summon this boy." The son was very happy and hurried to the house despite his illness. The daughter saw him and scolded, "Is there such a foolish person in the world? I saw you eating without discrimination, so I laughed at you. How dare you suddenly develop such foolish thoughts?" She ordered the servants to slap him across the face many times. The son bowed deeply in submission, acknowledging his guilt, and was then ordered to leave. Upon returning home, his illness seemed to vanish as if it had never existed.

Chen Guangfu and Chen Guangbi were brothers from Chao County, Anhui Province. One day, having heard the widespread rumor that a dragon had appeared in the sky, they climbed to the city wall to see it. There was already a man on the wall when it began to rain, making the ground muddy and slippery. He fell to the ground and pressed down on Chen Guangfu’s foot. Guangfu lifted his foot in an attempt to pull the man up, but unintentionally, when he lifted his foot, the man rolled down from the city wall, sustaining severe injuries to his head and face, and died suddenly. As a result, the soldiers and officials at the city gate gathered, seized the two men, and prepared to take them to the authorities. Guangfu said, “This is truly my fault; what does it have to do with my brother?” Guangbi replied, “No, this was my doing, not my brother’s. You should arrest me.” The brothers continued to argue without end. Guangfu said, “I was reckless at the time, which led to this. How could I accuse my brother?” Guangbi replied, “My brother has only just married. If he were to die, my sister-in-law would have to remarry; but I have no means to marry, and then the ancestral rites would be cut off. It would be better for me to die.” At this, the crowd all sighed and said, “Righteous men, righteous men. Though that man is dead, perhaps there is still a way to save him. Let us try to save him.”

After some time, the man indeed revived, and his mother also rushed to him. Seeing that her son had come to life, the crowd informed her of the words of the doctors, and the mother wept, saying, "I am fortunate that my son has survived. Even if he had not, I would not blame you for the debt." The doctors bowed and thanked her. People on the road sighed and praised it as three acts of righteousness. In Gao You, there was a farming family whose basic needs were met. They had a daughter who was extremely beautiful, and her parents loved her dearly, setting very high standards for her future husband. Later, their home was near a large river, and a son of an official anchored his boat by the riverbank. He saw the girl and was captivated by her beauty, so he stayed and did not leave. One day, he noticed a nun leaving their house and followed her to a temple, where he explained his intentions and offered a large bribe. The nun agreed. A few days later, he drugged the girl with wine and sent her to his boat, then sailed away. When the girl woke up, she cried loudly, and the official's son comforted her gently and swore to live with her until old age. By the time they arrived home, there was an old man waiting for them. The girl cried to her father, who said, "It is my son's fault. But since it has happened, do not be sad; I will make this happen successfully." He then hired matchmakers and prepared gifts, going to the girl's parents' home to formally betroth her to his son. The farming family, having lost their daughter, was in deep distress and had no solution, but they were overjoyed when this happened, and thus they formed a marital alliance. Those who heard about it composed a legend titled *The Legend of Unexpected Fate*.

Wang Shih Ch'uan, a native of Anhui, settled in Yangzhou. His wife was from Xian Nu Town in Yangzhou. They had one son and one daughter, but his wife passed away. The son was just about to come of age and had betrothed a daughter of a certain family as his wife. He was learning trade in a pawnshop, so he was often away from home, leaving only the daughter to live with her father. One day, his stepmother came to the house and said to the daughter, "I am going to visit my hometown in Xian Nu Town. Why don't you come with me to your mother's family?" Wang Shih Ch'uan was away on other business, so the stepmother told the neighbor's old woman and took the daughter away. When Wang Shih Ch'uan returned home, he found that his daughter was missing. The neighbor's old woman was also out, so he had no one to ask. In the area south of the Yangtze River, after the chaos of war, there were very few women. Those who wanted to marry had to look north of the river. Unscrupulous people, pursuing profit, often kidnapped and sold women. Wang Shih Ch'uan suspected that his daughter had been kidnapped, so he crossed the river to the south and traced her. He reached a small village near DanYang County and heard the villagers talking, "It's a pity that such a good woman is married to this old man." Wang was shocked and asked the villagers why. They told him that there was an old man named Li in the village, nearly sixty years old, who had recently bought a concubine.

The man then visited her home, pretending to be a celebrator. Upon hearing crying inside the room, he rushed out to the city and appealed to the official. The official sent men with him, who broke into the room and shouted loudly to find the girl. The girl emerged, but she was not his own daughter. The man was shocked and blamed himself for his carelessness. The girl wept and said, "I am a daughter of a family from Yangzhou, betrothed to the Wang family. Wang Shihuai is my father-in-law." The man was both surprised and joyful, saying, "Are you my daughter-in-law? I am Wang Shihuai." He took Mr. Li to the public court. Mr. Li agreed to return the girl and offered two hundred taels of silver as compensation, thus ending the lawsuit. The girl was left at a nunnery, and a messenger was sent to inform her father. The girl's family, having lost her, was in a state of panic. Upon hearing the news, they rushed to the nunnery, found the girl, and were overjoyed. They told the man, "Yesterday we came to your house and heard from a neighbor that your daughter was safe, having gone to her aunt's house. Had you heard the neighbor earlier, you would not have crossed the river to search for her, and if you had not crossed the river, we would not have found her. This is a great fortune for both our families." The man also said joyfully, "This is truly a blessing from heaven. Having benefited from heaven's blessing, should I defraud someone's wealth?" He returned the silver to Mr. Li and, accompanied by the girl's father, took the girl back home.

Mr. A, a salt official, was waiting for a position in Yangzhou. He had a large pearl, weighing slightly more than two qian, which emitted a faint glow when placed in the dark. It was the anciently described night pearl. One day, several colleagues came to his door to see it, all praising it highly before leaving, except for one man surnamed Zhuang, who stayed behind. After Mr. A sent the guests away and returned, he found that Zhuang was still sitting on a cushion, smoking opium, and the pearl was missing from the tray. Mr. A was greatly surprised and asked Zhuang, who also looked shocked and said, "There were no other people in the room, could it have been me who took it?" Zhuang then undressed, leaving not a single thread of clothing, and asked Mr. A to search him thoroughly. He added, "If you suspect I hid the pearl in my clothes, please keep this garment, and you can lend me your clothes to go back." Mr. A was very upset at losing the pearl but did not initially suspect Zhuang. A month later, Zhuang's servant returned the pearl. It turned out that Zhuang, having been a rascal in his youth, had learned various magic tricks like swallowing swords and spitting fire. On that day, he took advantage of Mr. A leaving to send the guests away and swallowed the pearl. Upon returning, he vomited it up. Later, due to mistreating his servant, the truth came out. The ancients said that hiding a pearl in one's belly was extremely foolish, but this case was truly beyond comprehension.

In the inner part of the Fuzhong Gate in Suzhou, there was a man named Wáng Qī, who had a familial or social connection with a certain family in the Furen alley. His father had called the woman of that family "Dry Aunt" (a term of respect). After his father's death, the family took care of Wáng Qī, treating him like a son. The woman had a daughter who was about the same age as Wáng Qī, and initially, it was intended that she would marry him. However, when Wáng Qī grew up, he became a reckless young man. The woman then regretted her previous arrangement and agreed to marry her daughter to a man named Mǒu Shēng outside the Xuhu Gate. The wedding was set to take place soon. Wáng Qī heard about this and gathered a group of about ten young men who were known for their misbehavior. They forcibly took the woman and brought her back to Wáng Qī's house. When the woman arrived at Wáng Qī's house, she locked herself in a room and cried loudly. After a while, there was no more sound. Someone peeped through the door and saw that she had hanged herself. They broke down the door, rescued her, and she came back to life. She then refused to eat, seeking death. The incident was reported to the authorities, who ruled that Wáng Qī's act of forcibly taking the woman for marriage was improper. He was whipped one hundred times and the officials told him, "You thought that the family had previously agreed to marry you, but there is no solid evidence for this. Since the woman has vowed to die rather than marry you, what do you care? Men are not lacking in wives!" The officials then ordered the woman's family to give Wáng Qī fifty units of currency (洋泉) as support for his future marriage, acknowledging the past kindness and nurturing provided by the family. The woman was then returned to her original fiancé, Mǒu Shēng.

He Aishuang, a man from Jinling, was engaged in the business of carrying goods for sale. He kept a dog whose entire body was covered in white fur like snow, bright and clean, lovable. Whenever he went out or came in, ate or slept, he was always with it. There was no one else in the room, so he took the dog as his companion. One night, he brought the dog back to sleep. By the next day, the door remained closed, and there was no sound from either the man or the dog. The neighbors became suspicious. Another night passed, the door remained closed as before, and no one responded when they called. They then forced their way in and found blood everywhere on the bed. It turned out that He Aishuang had been bitten in the throat by the dog and died. The dog was still sitting on his belly, eating voraciously. They then collected money to bury him and immediately killed the dog to offer it as a sacrifice. Sages say that one should not live among birds and beasts. Indeed. Zhang Aifu, a native of Shaoxing, lived in Hangzhou. From a young age, he had been betrothed to a woman from the Wang family, but at thirty years old, he was too poor to marry her. She was also twenty-seven years old. His mother repeatedly asked the matchmaker to urge Zhang Aifu to marry, but the matchmaker said, "What can he do if he is poor?" The mother replied, "He has no marriage expenses, and I have no dowry. If nothing else works, perhaps we should resort to eloping?" The matchmaker told Zhang Aifu, who was greatly pleased. He then planned to gather a group of people to kidnap the woman on a certain day. The mother deliberately gathered neighbors to Zhang Aifu's house to take the woman, but by the time they arrived, the wedding had already taken place, and the house was filled with guests. The matchmaker advised, "The situation is already this way; what more can be said? Tomorrow, we should make him come to apologize." The mother reluctantly returned home. Kidnapping for marriage is a vile custom, explicitly forbidden by law. Yet in this case, it was the woman's family who invited the kidnapping, which is a novel and unprecedented occurrence.

The legal code prescribes that those who hire out their wives and concubines to others as wives and concubines shall be sentenced to eighty strokes of the rod. Yet, in the countryside of Ningbo, such incidents often occur, making it a bad custom. There was a man named Tang who made a living by gathering firewood. With his mother and one wife, he supplemented their income by binding sandals and weaving mats, but they often suffered from not having enough. During a year of famine, their meals were scarce. He heard that a man named Wang in the neighboring village, who had no children, wanted to hire a wife to bear a child. Tang consulted his mother, planning to hire out his wife. The wife refused, but Tang said, "A woman losing her virtue is indeed a serious matter, but if my mother were to starve to death, that would be an even greater matter." The wife then agreed, and was hired out to Wang for a period of one year. However, the woman was beautiful, and Wang favored her. When the term was up, Tang went to redeem her, but Wang changed one character in the contract to ten characters, and Tang could not argue.
