The woman told everyone, "I endured this because I thought that with a short time left, I could save my mother-in-law and my husband's lives. If it were delayed for ten years, I would soon be dead, so what would there be to redeem?" She then drowned herself. This woman lost her virtue to support her mother-in-law, ultimately ending up in a situation where the broken mirror could not be mended, carrying her grievances to the netherworld, which is truly pitiful.

In the city of Jinling, there was a corpse covered with reed mats. The upper half of the body was not visible, while the lower half was only partially covered, with the pants reaching just below the knees. Both feet were bare and without socks, and the body was stiffly motionless. Beside the corpse stood a beggar who wept before the crowd, raising money for the burial, claiming that the deceased was his father. At that time, there were many onlookers, and some, moved by pity, gave money, ranging from a hundred to dozens of coins, accumulating to over a thousand in total. The beggar still felt it was insufficient and pleaded for more. An old man, holding a tube and smoking tobacco, accidentally dropped the embers onto the corpse's feet, causing them to twitch. The crowd was greatly surprised, but soon realized it was a deception and burst into laughter. The "corpse" then lifted the reed mats and stood up, saying, "I am better, I am better!" He rolled up the reed mats, bowed to the crowd in gratitude, and carried the money away. In Suzhou, there was a merchant named Wang who traded in Changzhou. He entrusted his friend, surnamed Jin, to deliver a letter to his mother. Jin was a habitual heavy drinker. After arriving in Suzhou, he first bought and drank heavily, then went to Wang's residence. Wang lived behind Tiger Hill. Jin, while walking to the bridge named Wàng Shān Bridge, slipped and fell into the river. He felt someone holding his hand tightly and preventing him from rising. Jin was greatly alarmed, and as he struggled, someone suddenly shouted, "This person is delivering a letter for Mrs. Wang, how could you keep him!" Jin then managed to climb up to the shore, hurriedly made his way to Wang's residence, his clothes soaked, and his wrists bruised and black. Wang's mother asked him about the incident, and he explained everything. She said, "I never expected a woman's virtue to be so great, it has moved the spirits."

A young man from Zhejiang, handsome and graceful, was well-known in the local schools. Having lost his parents at an early age, he remained unmarried at the age of twenty. In a certain year, he traveled to the provincial capital for the regional examination and stayed at the home of a relative. The relative's daughter was beautiful and talented, and the two fell deeply in love, making a blood oath to each other. The girl's father had already passed away, and her mother, upon learning of their relationship, did not forbid it. When the young man sent a matchmaker to propose marriage, the mother asked about his age and discovered that he was six years older than her daughter. Due to the traditional belief that a marriage in the sixth year is inauspicious, she declined the proposal. The girl, realizing that their union was doomed, wept endlessly. Her mother initially tried to comfort her but then scolded her, leading the girl to take her own life by hanging. Upon hearing of her death, the young man also took poison and ended his life. Bound by superstitious taboos, both the man and the woman died out of their love for each other, though their actions were not sanctioned by propriety, their story is indeed pitiable. As for Gong, whose origins are unknown, he came to the capital to take the provincial examination. Before the examination, he purchased a concubine and a maid from a broker, but their names and origins are unknown. He rented a house near the front gate of the city, divided into front and back courtyards. As he was about to enter the examination hall, he was concerned that his concubine, left alone, might be in danger, so he asked a friend, a certain Departmental Officer, to stay in the front courtyard. The Departmental Officer, known for his cautious and conservative nature, accepted the request and stayed in the front courtyard, reading books all day. After the examination, Gong returned home, and the Departmental Officer informed him that he had carefully guarded the premises, fulfilling his duty. Gong thanked him repeatedly and then went to the back courtyard, where he found it empty, with all the belongings gone. The windows were intact, but the wall at the back had collapsed. He then realized that his concubine and maid had escaped through the back wall. He had guarded the front but failed to protect the back.

In the ten-mile fields east of Yangzhou, there was a man who watched over the young crops, sleeping in a tent in the open fields. From a distance, he saw firelight at the land temple by the river, two miles away, flickering in size and brightness, sometimes bright, sometimes dim. Suspicious, he gathered several strong men and went with weapons. Upon arriving, they found a monk sitting on the ground, with a primitive mortar beside him, supported by stones, and firewood gathered and burning. They asked him what he was doing, and the monk replied, “I returned from the South Sea this morning, and passed by here when I was hungry, so I am merely cooking.” The group was about to return when one of them laughed and said, “Monk, are you not eating meat?” Playfully lifting the lid, they found that what he was cooking were two infant fetuses. The crowd was greatly shocked and rushed to strike the monk with their weapons. The monk fell to the ground and sighed, “My fate is exhausted! For all of us who practice the Diamond Chan, we must consume thirty-six male fetuses in order to attain the Great Dao. I have only consumed seven of them, and now I have encountered you all — this is due to my thin fortune. Kill me quickly, and do not speak further.” The crowd was furious and killed him, then cast his body into the river.

In Wuxi, there was a mute beggar who had been begging in the market for many years, and everyone knew him. One day, he went to a wealthy family in the southern part of the city to beg for food. The gatekeeper rejected him, saying, "Our master has been busy since morning until noon because his young son is gravely ill. He hasn't had time to eat, so how can we feed you?" Suddenly, the mute beggar spoke and said, "Why not consult me? I can treat him." The gatekeeper was astonished that the mute beggar could speak and reported this to the master. The master invited the beggar in to try his skills. The beggar took the pulse, prescribed medicine, and his treatment was well-organized. After one dose, the patient showed signs of improvement, and after three doses, he recovered completely. The master was overjoyed and asked about the beggar's background. The beggar said, "My father used to run a pharmacy. Although I was born mute, I learned to read since childhood and enjoyed reading medical books, so I know a lot about medicine. After my father's death, our family became poor, and because I couldn't speak, there was no way to apply my skills, so I became a beggar." When asked why he could speak now, he said he didn't know. As a result, his medical reputation became very famous, and he set up a clinic at the Temple of the City God. Thousands of patients came to see him every day. However, in that winter, a man named Huang Zhiwen from Hangzhou passed through Wuxi and visited the beggar. He saw that the prescriptions the beggar wrote were not coherent, and the names of the medicines were often misspelled. He feared that the beggar's skills were just a lucky coincidence and yet he was considered a great doctor. He did not believe it. Someone said, "A mute speaking is also a great wonder." But how can we know if he was pretending to be mute to beg?

In the year of the provincial examination during the Ding You year of the Daoguang era, Shen Tao of Shuanglin Town in Huzhou was napping during the day when he had a dream. Several people suddenly entered, shouting, "Shen Tao has passed the examination!" Just as he was filled with surprise and joy, another person suddenly ran in and said, "Mistaken! Mistaken! This is not Shen Tao." They then suddenly left all together. The sound of their footsteps was first from outside to inside, then from inside to outside, clattering on stone and echoing on wood, all clearly distinguishable. That year, Shen Xiangquan, who passed the examination, was the one who actually passed. Xiangquan's name is also Tao, and he is from the city of Huzhou, which is the person referred to, not this Shen Tao. Perhaps the ghost or deity played a trick on him due to the similarity in names?

The northern frontier of Yangzhou, in Leitang Village, is where Emperor Yang of Sui was buried. Recently, a creature known as a "thing-woman" (物女) appeared there. ("Thing-woman" is a term found in the *Spring and Autumn Annals* (*春秋繁露*), which states, "In the dry creek, there is a thing-woman; when the water dries up, the woman appears.") She engaged with the young and strong men of the village. Those who engaged with her fell ill, and those who became severely ill died. On the twenty-fourth day of the sixth month in the year of Guangxu (光绪二年), at midday, the sky suddenly grew dark as if it were night, and there was a heavy thunderstorm. In the lightning, a woman was seen, dressed in white with a red headband, holding a pair of forks, fighting against the lightning. The thunder could not strike her. They fought for a long time, and then a loud sound suddenly erupted, and a bolt of lightning shot from the ground, injuring one of her feet. The woman hesitated for a moment, and then a swift bolt of lightning struck down from the sky, its sound being exceptionally loud, causing all the spectators to faint. Soon after the rain stopped, a creature was found dead on the ground. It was like a pig without a tail, like a cow without horns, with white hair all over its body, and black hair forming a wish-fulfilling pattern on its back and sides. It had a fleshy strip on its belly, two feet long, and its smell was pungent and unpleasant to be near. It weighed over two hundred pounds, and no one could identify it. There is an观音庵 (Guanyin Temple) in that area, and an old monk came out to look and said, "This is a chīhǔ (螭虎)."

A local resident dismembered and burned the body, and the stench spread throughout the neighborhood. According to the *Shuowen Jiezi*, a *chi* resembles a dragon but is yellow. The *Han Shu* (History of the Han Dynasty), specifically the biography of Yang Xiong, with annotations by Wei Zhao, states that a *chi* resembles a tiger with scales. However, this creature had white fur and black markings but no scales, so it may not have been a *chi*. In the area of Ten Temples Sea and Yandaixie Street in the capital, there lived a girl named Xiangzhu from Shanxi. She was betrothed to a local man named Huang from a young age. Huang went to learn trade elsewhere and lost contact. The Liu family fell on hard times, and the girl became involved in the entertainment industry. Later, Huang accumulated several hundred gold coins and returned to the capital with them, intending to find his former betrothed. He stayed at an inn near Xihéyán. The innkeeper coveted his wealth and lured him into a life of debauchery. Being young, Huang fell for the innkeeper's scheme. There was a courtesan named Xiulan who became very close to Huang. Soon after, Huang spent all his money, and the courtesan's household planned to expel him. Xiulan pretended to go to a temple fair and escaped with Huang to Liangxiang, where they sought refuge in the home of an elderly woman. The elderly woman looked at them closely and asked, "Are you not Huang from Shanxi? Is this girl Xiangzhu from the Liu family?" When they asked her name, she revealed that she was Huang's aunt and was called "gàn ānai" (a kind of godmother) by Xiangzhu when she was young. Thus, they realized that Xiulan was indeed Xiangzhu. The elderly woman arranged a wedding ceremony with wedding candles and music, and they became husband and wife.

During a period of chaos, a large mansion on Left Guard Street in Yangzhou was occupied by a prominent bandit leader as a counterfeit government office. After the chaos subsided, a family moved into the house. In the courtyard in front of the main hall, a woman's image would appear every time it rained, and it could not be washed away; it would disappear when the sky cleared. The house was then sealed and not used. One day, a distant relative came to visit, accompanied by many servants, and they moved into the house. At midnight, they suddenly saw bricks moving incessantly, and in a moment, a mound rose up. One servant stepped on it and fainted, falling to the ground. The next morning, they reported this to the owner, who dug it up and found a female corpse. The dress was intact, her face looked as if she were alive, and her entire body was covered with green hair about an inch long, which seemed to be about to move. The authorities were informed, and they ordered the body to be burned. A neighbor, an old leatherworker, said, "I know about this incident. I was captured by the bandits and served under this leader. The bandit leader captured this woman, wanted to defile her, and she refused and cursed him. She was beaten hundreds of times, cursed even more fiercely, and was then buried alive. I did not expect her spirit to cause trouble now." I believe that this woman, having died a heroic death, her brave spirit and pure soul have long returned to the void; this is merely her remains. Over time, she has become a ghost, perhaps due to the local climate, and this has nothing to do with her being a virtuous woman. It is regrettable that the old man knew the truth but could not provide her name, so she could not be honored.

After the war and destruction, Nanjing often saw coffins being dug up from the houses within the city, all hastily buried in the chaos. Beside the Prince's garden, there was a ruined land that was acquired by a high-ranking official. He piled up tiles and rubble into a mountain and ordered soldiers to level it. Over twenty coffins were dug out, and upon opening them, one contained a female corpse whose clothes were as bright and new as if freshly made, but they were all turned to ash and crumbled at the touch. Her body was not decayed, her skin white and moist, with traces of makeup still on her cheeks. Another coffin contained a male corpse, sitting upright rather than lying down. Yet another coffin contained a zombie, with animal fur on the upper body and bird feathers on the lower body, which is particularly strange. The high-ranking official heard about it and ordered the coffins to be moved to another place, forbidding any further examination. In Guizhou, there was a man named Ren who remarried Hu, a woman who had been widowed and remarried. She was very virtuous and treated her husband's children as her own. After several years, Ren fell seriously ill, and Hu served him medicine and supported the household, tirelessly for months. Seeing her husband's condition worsen and knowing he could not be saved, she first took poison, wanting to die with him. She hugged him and cried, and when he died, she did too. Friends and relatives came to mourn, all praising her loyalty. Some also thought it was a pity that she did not die with her first husband. I consulted the *Book of Sui: Biographies of Exemplary Women*, which first records the story of Princess Lanling, who also died to honor her second husband. The historian praised her character as surpassing that of a cold pine. This woman is also of a similar caliber.

There is a large house in Xidan Plaza in the capital city, which is inhabited by foxes and is not rented out. The owner, having been unemployed for a long time, was very angry and went to the house to curse the foxes. On that night, he suddenly lost his son, who was found the next day in the same house. When asked how he got there, the son did not know. However, the son became increasingly weak and close to death. It is said that there is a fox official (总管) at the Dongbianmen Gate Tower, who actually manages fox affairs in the capital. The owner prepared food and wine, wrote a petition, and went to complain. After several days, he went to check the empty house and found a creature hanging from a tree, resembling a large cat with a longer beak. It had been killed by the fox official. Even foxes and rats have their own ways, it seems.

Ten miles from Tongzhou, there is the Puji Lock, and to the southwest of the lock, there is a cluster of graves, which is said to be the monk's grave. No one knows the year of burial or the identity of the monk. Every evening after dusk, it appears as a ghost market, bewitching travelers. Travelers are troubled by it and take detours to avoid it. In the summer of the year of the Earth Pig, a small boat carrying grain to the Puji Lock passed through the area in the evening. The boatman called the poleman to change to the opposite bank. There were two polemen; one retracted the pole and boarded the boat, while the other, relying on his strength, laughed and did not believe, raising his arm and going alone. Soon he saw flickering lights and high-hanging blue curtains, which seemed to be a tavern. Knowing it was an illusion, he immediately broke off a large willow tree from the roadside and went with it. Getting close to the ghost market, he then raised the willow and hit it randomly, seeing countless monks fall under his blows. Upon closer inspection, they were in the cluster of graves. The illusory scene was no longer visible. He then found a way back to the boat to report, but the people on the boat did not believe him. However, after this, the soil of the monk's grave collapsed completely, and there were no more strange occurrences. It was all due to his single strike.

During the mid-Xianfeng period, troops were stationed in He Kou Town, Jiangxi, to set up defenses. They ordered laborers to go into the mountains to gather firewood. The laborers saw an ancient elm tree, large enough to shelter a cow. They lifted their axes to chop it down, and blood and sap spurted out. A local person warned them, saying, "This tree is over four hundred years old and has very spiritual and supernatural qualities; cutting it down is inauspicious." The laborers then left the tree and returned. The soldiers in the camp heard about the incident and said, "We, as a group, fear no ghosts or deities. To obtain such a large piece of wood and not take it, how can we use it for cooking?" The next day, they again led dozens of laborers to cut down the tree, and the blood flowed as before. Soon the tree broke and fell to the ground, with the sound echoing through the mountains and valleys. The tree was over ten zhang long, hollow like a bamboo, and inside there were bones scattered everywhere, including a skull, and countless broken bowls and bamboo chopsticks. All who saw it were shocked and amazed, and no one could guess where it came from.

A Son of a Prominent Family in Jiangxi A son of a prominent family in Jiangxi relocated the burial of his ancestor. When the tomb was opened, the coffin was already decayed and incomplete, and inside it were filled with porcelain bowls and bamboo chopsticks, yet the location of the corpse was unknown. A geomancer remarked, "This must be what is called a 'transferred body site,' perhaps?" He ordered the empty coffin to be removed, and then entered the tomb to inspect it. There, he found only a small hole in the northwest corner, round as the mouth of a basin, and just large enough to fit a fist. Tentatively, he ordered digging from that spot, and after excavating more than two zhang, the body was found, lying stiff but not yet decayed. Beside it were several porcelain jars, some large and some small, of very ancient design. When displayed to others, none could identify them. The body had disappeared from the coffin, replaced instead by the porcelain bowls and bamboo chopsticks, and no one could understand the reason for this. In the west of Jiading City stood a temple dedicated to the Xuantan deity, known for its spiritual phenomena. In the year of Gengshen, when the city fell, the temple was soon destroyed and left to be overgrown with grass. It is said that originally there was a certain scholar who served as a guest at the residence of Zhang the True Person in the Longhu Mountains of Jiangxi. One day, he received a letter from home and learned that his father was ill. He urgently wished to return and consulted the True Person. The True Person gave him a talisman and warned, "Burn it as soon as you arrive home." The scholar boarded his boat and loosened the mooring ropes. He heard only the sound of crashing waves, and the boat moved as if flying. He arrived in one day and night, but his father's illness was already critical, and his mind was in turmoil. He had forgotten to burn the talisman. The spirit of the talisman appeared repeatedly in visible form, asking to be taken away. Only then did he recall the True Person’s words, and he prepared incense and candles to burn it. That night, the spirit appeared to him in a dream and said, "You sent me too late; the deadline has passed, and I cannot return." The scholar then raised funds to build a temple to honor the spirit. At first, he did not know the spirit’s name, but because the image resembled the Xuantan deity commonly depicted in the world, he called the temple the Xuantan Temple.

In the town of Diakou in Zhuji County, Shaoxing, there was a house belonging to the Chen family. Whenever a fire broke out, the house remained unscathed. It is said that at the beginning of the nation, there was a man named Chen Zi Yi who intended to build this house. He personally went to Shaoxing city to consult with a person surnamed Xia for selecting an auspicious day. Xia looked at him and said, "Please wait a moment, and I will choose a day for you." Chen immediately offered ten silver dollars as a gift. Xia replied, "If that is the case, please come back in three days." Chen realized that Xia's decision-making was influenced by the amount of compensation, so he offered 100 taels of silver and said, "I have worked hard all my life, and now I am undertaking this project. Please pay attention, sir." Xia then said, "If that is the case, please come back in a month." When Chen returned after a month, Xia told him, "The day has already been selected. Please do not make any changes." Chen followed Xia's advice. After the house was completed, a major fire broke out in the town, and everything around it turned to ashes. Only Chen's new house remained standing. Since then, the house has survived over 30 fires. To this day, the Chen family still lives in the house, and Xia's descendants continue to practice the profession of selecting auspicious days.

In Xiangxiang County, Hunan Province, there were five brothers of the Tru family, all of whom lived to a very old age. In the fourth year of the Guangxu era (1878), the eldest brother, Tru Chao Yu, was eighty-nine years old, the second brother, Tru Chao Rui, was eighty-seven years old, the third brother, Tru Chao Gai, was eighty-five years old, the fourth brother, Tru Chao Yao, was eighty-three years old, and the fifth brother, Tru Chao Huan, was eighty-one years old. The governor of Hunan reported this to the central government, which indeed was a sign of good fortune. The story is recorded in official documents, and I am recording it here. The five Tru brothers are as remarkable as the eight virtuous men of the Zhou dynasty. He Ajin, from Linping Town, Renhe County, was forced to join the bandits during the Taiping Rebellion. He was coerced into following them to a village where they searched for and seized valuables. In a jar of pickles, they found eighty pieces of foreign currency, which he took and left. After escaping, he used the money to open a dumpling shop, which was very profitable. However, the farmer who owned the jar of pickles knew about it and demanded the money. He Ajin firmly denied the accusation and explained, "The bandits took countless people's money and valuables; are they all going to demand payment from me?" The farmer replied, "You took my money, and everyone knows about it. I am not asking for repayment, but I am poor and cannot make a living. If you can give me something in return, even a little, I will not resent you and will be grateful to you." He Ajin refused to comply. The farmer could do nothing about it and left. Later, the farmer could no longer bear his poverty and drowned himself. One day, while He Ajin was at the dumpling shop, he suddenly said to his wife, "Today I will die." When asked why, he did not answer. He hurriedly left the shop, and someone quickly followed him. It turned out that he had jumped into the water and died. Everyone knew that the farmer had come to demand payment.

In the village of Linping, there were three brothers who were all disrespectful to their mother, with the eldest and second brothers being particularly so. During the rebellion in Guangdong, all three brothers joined the rebels in looting and gained some wealth. After the rebellion was quelled, they still had some remaining wealth, and they expelled their mother, refusing to live with her. Soon after, the eldest brother married, and both younger brothers were present to celebrate with a feast. The mother heard about it and came to visit, but the eldest brother angrily said, “Today is my wedding, you poor old woman, how dare you come to disrupt my celebration!” He took a stick and chased her away. The second brother also came out to curse and scold her, and his manner was very aggressive. The mother fled in panic, crying and shouting in the marketplace, “Heaven! I have such sons!” At that moment, the sky was clear and sunny, but suddenly dark clouds gathered, and thunder and rain raged. A loud clap of thunder struck, and the three brothers were all thrown out of the house, kneeling before their mother and dying. The mother cried again, “Heaven! My youngest son was not so harsh to me, why wasn’t he spared?” Another clap of thunder struck, and the youngest son was miraculously saved. In the year of Tongzhi, the fifth year of the reign, there were many tiger attacks in the rural areas of Xian Ning, Hubei. A boy from the Sheng family was herding cattle in the outskirts when he suddenly encountered a tiger. The boy fell from the cow’s back, and the cow protected him with its body, charging at the tiger with its horns, but it was no match. Another cow came to help, and the tiger fled. The boy from the Sheng family was saved, but the cow he was herding died from its injuries. The elders of the Sheng family gathered and said, “This was a righteous cow.” They bought a coffin, buried it in a grave, and performed Buddhist rituals for it. They also dressed the boy in mourning attire to conduct the funeral, treating it as if he had lost a close relative, and called him the “Cattle Filial Son.”

Suzhou, after the chaos caused by the Taiping rebels, saw many houses and dwellings destroyed, though some still remained between Fong Gate and Qi Gate. After the disorder was quelled, many people bought these houses and relocated and rebuilt them between Chang Gate and Xu Gate. An elderly scholar borrowed a dilapidated house by the Hongqiao Bridge and gathered several young students to teach them. Suddenly, he heard sounds coming from all four walls, like a hen calling her chicks. At night, again, he heard as if dozens of women were weeping softly. This continued for several days, and by then the carpenters had already arrived with their tools, for the owner had already sold the house to someone else, who intended to relocate and rebuild it elsewhere. My uncle, Mr. Yao Songtian, told me about this and said, "His ancestors and father worked hard to build this house, yet their descendants could not keep it; it is appropriate that there is deep sorrow in the unseen realm." Dai Zigao, however, remarked, "This ghost is very unenlightened. Those who build this house will also be those who tear it down. All things that are created will eventually be destroyed; even heaven and earth cannot last forever, how much more so a small house!" I agree that Dai Zigao's argument is quite insightful, but I do not believe these sounds were made by his ancestors or father. For the essence and energy of heaven and earth are everywhere.

In places that are empty and devoid of substance, the energy is scattered and flowing, unable to coalesce; it must have something upon which to rely before it can gather together. In the case of great buildings, their outer walls are high and lofty, their inner doors and gates are layered upon one another. If such a structure stands for a hundred years, or even several hundred years, its energy has long since become condensed. Once condensed for a long time, it then acquires form and substance; once form and substance are established, it then contains spirit and intelligence. Whether great as mountains and rivers, small as cities and communities, or even smaller as houses, their spirit is one and the same. But if such a structure is suddenly destroyed and dismantled, it loses its place of reliance, and its energy is no longer able to gather. Its spirit truly knows of this beforehand, hence the sound of sorrow and lamentation arises—this is the spirit of the house, not the ghost of the one who built it. My maternal family, the Yao clan, lived in Linping Zao Mountain Port. The house across the door, belonging to the Shen family, is said to have been built during the Yuan dynasty, and its structure and design differ from those of today. After the chaos, it was also dismantled by people. At the beginning of the demolition, a loud sound suddenly occurred, and one craftsman died. Because its energy had been condensed for several centuries, it was strong and could cause harm. As for thatched huts with minimal coverage, which occupy little land and gather little energy, they are built and destroyed immediately, with no spiritual or miraculous phenomena—its principle can also be imagined. If one were to say that it is the work of their ancestors or fathers, would the spirits of those in humble dwellings with narrow doors and small windows be more open-minded and free-spirited than those of the noble families?

A person from Tangxi Town in Renhe named Yao Jinqing is the younger brother of my second son's wife. When he was young, he invited a teacher named Jiang to teach him to read. Jiang was also young and very diligent, sitting in the study all day. One day, he suddenly felt pain in his body, which quickly worsened. He writhed in his bedding, calling out to his parents, and did not stop until dawn. The Yao family sent him back home by boat. After returning home, Jiang lay in bed and did not get up. Every night, when the lamp was extinguished and it was pitch black, he could still get out of bed and walk around the room, but if there was any light, he could not move. If it was daytime, even if the room was surrounded by curtains, he could only lie stiffly in bed and could not even turn over slightly. For several years, he had to use the bed for urination and defecation, but his diet remained normal, and his body remained plump, as if he were healthy. His father, Jiang Lanpu, who was a skilled physician, tried every possible method to treat him, but he did not recover. Later, he exhausted all his methods and became tired of it, and no longer prescribed medicine. After taking medicine for a long time, if he did not take it one day, it was like hunger thinking of food or thirst thinking of drink. So he allowed him to manage himself, buying a few herbs each day to take, even though it was ineffective, it did no harm. One day, he suddenly opened the door and went out, moving as usual. Calculating from when he got sick until now, it had been ten years. Suddenly, he recovered completely, not knowing whether it was because the medicine he took that day happened to cure the illness, or if it was because the karmic entanglements were finally resolved. If Duan Chengshi saw this, he would certainly include it in his *Record of Strange Diseases*.

Jiang Lanpu had a daughter-in-law who, after becoming pregnant, suddenly saw a woman lying in bed with her, engaged in earnest private conversation. Initially, she was greatly alarmed, but soon became accustomed to seeing her. This woman was wearing a red skirt, and her appearance was clear, though what she said was incoherent and trivial, so it could not be understood. The Jiang family's daughter-in-law was greatly distressed, knowing that she would inevitably die upon giving birth. Whenever she spoke about it, she would cry bitterly. Soon enough, she died in childbirth. It is said that the Jiang family's daughter-in-law was quite gentle and was still young, with no evil deeds. It must be a retribution from a past life.

A Woman from the Western Branch of the Yao Family in the Tang Dynasty A woman from the western branch of the Yao family in the Tang dynasty suddenly fell ill with a strange and terrifying disease. Her speech was incoherent, and her appearance was bizarre and eerie. She could bend her foot backward to her head, and at times she would protrude her tongue, extending it over a foot in length—an extremely horrifying sight. The Yao family had long worshipped the Ji spirit, so they summoned the spirit to inquire about her condition. The spirit replied: "This woman in her previous life was a son of the Shi family from Hangzhou. His wife had been sold by him, and he composed a marriage contract for her. But the wife refused to remarry, and committed suicide by hanging. Her husband later regretted his actions and also hanged himself. Now they come to claim their lives, and this cannot be prevented." The members of the Yao family gathered around and pleaded with the spirit, saying: "The matter of the previous life involving the Shi family's son has no connection to her present life. In this life, she has been a wife in the Yao family and is quite virtuous. Since her husband's death over a decade ago, she has remained faithful and not remarried, displaying a cold and pure virtue worthy of great praise. If you allow her goodness in this life to atone for her sins from the past, we wish to perform many Buddhist rituals to assist in her karmic reckoning and repentance." The spirit agreed, and spoke through the Ji spirit, saying: "The previous sins cannot be completely absolved, but since she has now become a chaste widow, we will for now let it go. Wait until the next life to take her life again." From that day on, the illness of the Yao family's wife indeed recovered, and she lived to be over sixty years old before passing away.

A woman from the Yao family in Tangxi had a frail constitution and could see ghosts in her eyes. Her maternal family had a woman who was ill, so the Yao family woman went to visit her. She saw a dark figure standing by the sick woman's bed, whose face was not very distinguishable; there was also a person whose complexion was no different from that of an ordinary person, who bowed to the dark figure. Every time she saw them, it was the same, and she found it very strange. The sick woman's illness soon healed. It is likely that the dark figure was a malevolent spirit seeking to take life, and the person who bowed to the dark figure was a benevolent spirit of gratitude.

In the city of Suzhou, there was a man named Jia who initially made his living by working on night boats. At the age of forty, he still had not married. Later, he traveled and traded in the rivers and lakes, accumulating a small amount of wealth, and then planned to marry. He was attracted to a woman from a neighboring village and entrusted an old woman to mediate the marriage. The old woman negotiated with the girl's parents, but they refused. The old woman returned and teasingly told Jia, "I will act as your matchmaker, and her parents were not opposed, but this girl refuses to be your wife because of the wart on your face. What shall we do?" It was because Jia had a small wart on his face that the old woman used this as a joke. Upon hearing this, Jia became very angry and that night, he used a sharp knife to cut off the wart, and blood stained his bed and mat. The next day, his head swelled like a basin, and he writhed in pain, crying out. After several days, he died. The neighbors all blamed the old woman. An old man sighed and said, "It is not the old woman's fault; this is a case of retribution." At the time when Suzhou was occupied by rebels, Jia served as a local official for the rebels and took possession of his friend's wife. His friend took the opportunity to take his wife and flee, but Jia caught up with them. The friend had a wart on his neck, and Jia first cut the wart, causing blood to gush out like a stream, and then killed him. The friend's death was very gruesome. Considering this, could it not be a case of retribution? How fearsome it is!

In the town of Linping, there lived a man from a certain family who was cutting grass in the suburbs. He chanced upon a skull, pitied its exposure, and dug up the ground to bury it. On returning, he told his family, saying, "Today I did a good deed." Soon after, he developed a severe fever and chills, and a ghost possessed him, saying, "I was very happy in the wilderness. You buried me in the ground, and I was suffocated and could not bear it. I will surely kill you!" His family was greatly frightened and prepared wine and food to offer as a sacrifice, burning countless paper money. After several days, the ghost finally left, and the man from the certain family fell ill for ten days and then recovered. I say that the skull's happiness exceeded that of the king who sits facing south; this is just a parable from the *Zhuangzi*. To cover the bones and bury the flesh was an ancient benevolent policy. What the man from the certain family did was indeed a good deed, but this caused him to offend the ghost. Why is that? Perhaps there was another evil ghost using this matter to cause trouble, seeking wine and food, rather than the ghost being resentful of the good deed.

A respected elder, a high-ranking official, had a second wife who fell ill and was possessed by a ghost. After returning home from his duties, the official visited her, expecting the ghost to avoid him. However, the two maidservants looked at each other and laughed. When asked why, they replied, "The ghost is in your sleeve and does not fear you at all." The official was greatly angered and heard that a certain scholar, known as a孝廉 (xiàolián), could capture ghosts. He went personally to inform him. The scholar said, "Tomorrow, set up tables and chairs outside the sick woman's window, and sit with me. This way, I can see inside the room and be able to act. However, the arrangement must appear natural and unforced, so as not to alert the ghost. If it finds out, it will flee." The official agreed. The next day, the scholar arrived, and the official invited him to sit outside the sick woman's window. There happened to be a chessboard nearby, and the scholar said, "Since we have nothing to do today, how about playing a game of chess?" The official replied, "Sure." As soon as they placed a few pieces on the board, the scholar suddenly seemed deep in thought, leaning on his chin and half-closed eyes, as if sleeping but not quite. The official smiled and said, "This gentleman seems a bit tired." He ordered the attendants to leave, not to disturb the scholar's thoughts, and so all the attendants dispersed.

After a short while, someone suddenly awoke and exclaimed, "The ghost is here!" Holding a bottle in hand, he said the ghost was inside the bottle, though he did not know where the bottle had come from. The female servants in the room all suddenly and loudly came out, saying, "The ghost has been caught by someone!" Before this, the servants had all seen someone enter the room, and the ghost immediately hid under the bed. The person followed him in, and the ghost jumped onto the top of the bed; the person followed him up again. This happened several times before the ghost was finally caught, so everyone knew the ghost had been taken away. The one inside the room was the spirit of the person, while the one outside the door was the physical form of the person, and thus the crowd could not distinguish between them. The gentleman asked why they were playing chess, and the person replied, "When I first sat here, I saw the ghost in the room and was in a panic, wanting to flee, so I temporarily played chess with you, as if having no interest in the ghost. The ghost did not mind me, and I was able to take advantage of an opportunity to enter directly and catch it." I heard this from Scholar Feng Mengxiang, who in turn heard it from his fellow townsman Mr. Tong E, who claimed it was a story about Public Official Yuan Wendada. I do not know if this is true. I dare not speak directly, but I have recorded the matter as such.

Governor Wan Chixuan, from Jiangxi, was temporarily residing in Hangzhou. In the fourth year of the Guangxu reign (1878), he died of illness. Before he fell ill, his daughter-in-law returned to visit him because her father was sick. Her father told her, "I am certainly not going to recover from this illness, and I fear that your father-in-law will not be long for this world either. Recently, there have been many deaths in the world, and the officials of the underworld have summoned us two to verify the records. You came to visit me, but you should also return to visit your father-in-law." Thus, his daughter-in-law hurried back home, and indeed, Governor Wan soon became ill and passed away. At the time of his death, there was a servant of the Han family named Tian, who was said to have the ability to see ghosts. That day, he was summoned by his master to visit the sick person and, upon returning, told others, "As soon as I arrived at his door, there was a divine Judge Cui there, who stopped me and said, 'It is not yet time to enter.' I lingered outside, and I saw the magistrate leave in casual attire, followed by another person, whom the common people call the messenger of death. Inside the door, the sounds of mourning began." So, does death indeed have a messenger of death? Who is this divine Judge Cui? The people of Hangzhou speak of it this way, so I record it here. In Cixi, there are two Confucian academies, the old one being located to the west of the lake, which is the temple dedicated to Yang Wanli. There is a statue of Yang Wanli, made of clay and wood, and dressed in formal attire, looking no different from a living person. Many years ago, there was a scholar who taught students here. The scholar went out, and one of his students stole the clothes of a deity and put them on himself to frighten his fellow students. One of his fellow students, caught off guard, died of shock.

Afterward, the statue of the Cihu Public truly gained the ability to move, often appearing outside and frightening passersby. Copper nails were driven into its four limbs, and a cloth was placed over its head, after which it no longer appeared. The academy was then locked up, and no one dared to live there. The new one was located in the east of Cihu, built by the local person Feng, who donated money, and also had many strange phenomena. There was a scholar named Zhao Chang who studied there. One night, he went out to relieve himself and suddenly saw a tall man sitting on the top of a tree. Zhao took pride in his courage and picked up a stick to strike the tall man. The tall man jumped down and fought with Zhao. Zhao could not withstand the fight, walked into the room, and the tall man followed him, knocking Zhao to the ground and beating him severely before leaving. Zhao then fell seriously ill, spitting out green-black fluid, and died within a few days. Another person saw the tall man emerge from the Cihu Academy on a moonlit night. This person also took pride in his courage and followed the tall man. The Cihu Academy was located outside the north gate, and the tall man disappeared under a large tree at Dabao Mountain, west of the city. This person followed him for several miles, determined to follow him to the end, and his courage was truly remarkable. Zhao died, but this person remained unharmed. If one does not provoke the tall man, it will not harm people. In Cihu, people often saw the tall man. Commonly, white-clad ones were called white affairs people, and black-clad ones were called black affairs people, or they were suspected to be mountain demons. Feng Mengxiang, the scholar-official, said, "Zhao saw the tall man sitting on the top of a tree, and this person saw the tall man disappear under the tree, perhaps it is a kindred spirit of the old tree spirit in the south of the city?" I believe Feng Mengxiang's words are correct.

In the spring of 1865, during the reign of Emperor Tongzhi, the city of Yanzhou was plagued by nightly ghostly cries. The sounds, described as "wu-wu ya-ya," resembled the barking of small dogs. Those living in the northeast believed the sounds came from the southwest, while those in the southwest thought they originated from the northeast. The cries fluctuated in distance and location, never ceasing throughout the night. Observers commented, "These must be the souls of those who perished in the Taiping Rebellion, wandering without a resting place." At the time, Mr. Yuan, known as Huixi, was temporarily in charge of education in Jiande County. He took the initiative to donate his own funds and led a campaign to collect money from the four city gates. At each gate, they performed the "fire mouth" ritual and burned an incalculable number of paper money. Indeed, there were no ghostly cries for three nights, but on the fourth night, the sounds returned. Following Mr. Yuan's example, the assistant military governor, Mr. Zhu, also conducted the same ritual, which similarly silenced the cries for three nights. Mr. Wu, the provincial examiner, was inspecting the examinations in Yanzhou and stayed in the examination hall. He too heard the cries and inquired about the matter with the scholars. Mr. Yuan informed him, and Mr. Wu ordered the same ritual to be performed at each city gate, following the example of Mr. Yuan and Mr. Zhu. Additionally, Mr. Wu wrote an article to offer a sacrifice. From then on, the ghostly cries ceased.

In Hangzhou, there was a house belonging to the Feng family near the Baohan Bridge. The house had some vacant land outside it, and they planned to build a wall around it. The tools were already prepared, but that night, they heard a very sad ghostly cry outside the window. Someone from the Feng family said to the ghost, "Why are you crying? If you are a ghost, it is indeed difficult, but being a person is not necessarily easy either." The ghost sighed and left. Those who heard it were very frightened. The next day, when they dug the soil to build the wall, they found four bodies in the ground. These were people who had died during the fall of the city to the rebels from the south (Yue). They realized that the ghost had known in advance that they would be dug up the next day and was afraid that they would be destroyed, damaged, or exposed. So, they bought coffins and reburied them, and no other unusual events occurred. In Cixi, there was also a house belonging to the Feng family. It was said that this house had a donkey spirit. One of the Feng family's young children fell ill and often became unconscious. It was said that a long-eared, hairy person came to the bedside and forced the child to eat mud, which they suspected was the donkey spirit causing the illness. After the child recovered, there were no further strange occurrences. During the turmoil of the Taiping Rebellion, the house was destroyed by fire, but the donkey spirit still existed. Its lair was in the throat of the river, which is the old channel where the water from Cihu Lake enters the city and merges with the other waters in the city. This channel passed under the Feng family's house and had been blocked for a long time. Some say it was blocked by Zhao Wénhuá when he built the city walls, while others say it was done by a former Feng family member who was a yushi (imperial censor) and disliked the noise of the water. Several years ago, the people of the county, following the advice of geomancers, pooled money to excavate this channel, but the project was abandoned halfway due to lack of funds. Therefore, the throat of the channel remained dry, and the donkey spirit could still live there and occasionally appear in its form.

Mr. Zhou of Anji said: His household had an elderly woman who worked as a servant. She was an old widow who had served the Zhou family for several decades. Everyone in the household treated her with respect, unlike the other servants. She was a kind and good person, always trying to explain things in a roundabout way to avoid conflict. Whenever there was a dispute, she would try to mediate and resolve it. She ate very simply, usually just salted tofu and fermented bean paste. She said that every night, when she closed her eyes, a woman dressed in a blue cotton robe would appear with her. This had been happening for several years, and she had become accustomed to it, no longer finding it strange. One night, the woman said to her, "Now there is a place of ultimate bliss. I will take you there for a tour." The old woman nodded. Suddenly, she heard a loud noise like thunder. Before her eyes, two doors opened, and the woman and the old woman entered together. The hall was spacious and grandly decorated, like a wealthy family's home. After they came out, the woman said to her, "Do you know where this is? This place is inside your body. The door we entered earlier is your top of the head." The old woman touched her head and found that her top had opened, like a baby's soft spot. From then on, every night she would go to that place, and the woman would always go with her, but she no longer heard the loud noise.

An elderly woman associated with women, she could see what others could not, sometimes perceiving divine beings passing through the air. Therefore, whenever she needed to relieve herself, she would close the windows and doors, and hang curtains, ensuring no light leaked out, fearing to offend the gods. One day, she bowed deeply toward the sky without ceasing. When people asked her why, she said, "The God of Guan is passing by. My son, after Guan, is followed by a black cloud, and it seems it will rain." At the time, the sun was bright and there was no sign of rain, but soon a heavy thunderstorm broke out, and people believed her. When asked what the God of Guan looked like, she said, "He is no different from the statue in the temple." When asked how the gods walk in the air, she replied, "Whenever a god appears, there is always a white mist surrounding them; one can only see the upper body, but not the feet." Several years later, the woman suddenly began buying and eating black dates, consuming them daily without limit. People found this strange and asked her about it. She said, "The woman I associate with every night is the Bodhisattva Guanyin. I am about to leave with her, but because my life has been too frugal, although my lifespan has ended, my quota of sustenance has not yet been fulfilled. I cannot immediately leave the mortal world, so I eat dates to fulfill my remaining days." Within a few days, the woman died peacefully without illness.

A San was a man from Xiangshan, whose name was lost. He came to Huzhou to cultivate wasteland and settled in Wunikan, Xiaofeng. One day, he stumbled and fell on the road. Suddenly, he saw five or six people wearing silk shirts and speaking in the local dialect of Huzhou, who challenged him to a gambling game. A San did not recognize them and angrily said, "When did I gamble with you? Why are you blaming me?" They then started to fight, and A San, alone, was overwhelmed by the group. Soon, an elderly man with a walking stick arrived and asked, "What are you doing?" A San told him what had happened and asked for help. The elder said, "You are all disrespectful. I will help you fight them." He swung his stick and struck, and the five or six people all fled into the rice fields. A San then picked up stones from the roadside and threw them at them, but they all disappeared upon impact. The elder pointed the way for him to return home and said, "They have disappeared, so there is no need to worry. You can go back now." A San followed the path back, and suddenly it was as if he woke up from a dream, finding himself still lying stiffly by the roadside. He returned home, very tired, and the places where he had been hit still hurt, with his skin and flesh turning blue-black, taking about ten days to heal. Later, he went to the land temple and saw a statue of the local land god, which looked very much like the elderly man he had seen earlier. He realized that the elder was a deity and offered sacrifices and liquor to thank him.

During his humble beginnings, Ge Jun Feixiong, the commander of Dinghai, was jobless. In Dinghai, it was customary for those who were ill to perform prayers and sacrifices, during which they would prepare a lot of food and drink. The offerings placed on a table were called "upper offerings" and were meant for the gods, while those placed on the ground were called "lower offerings" and were meant for the spirits or ghosts. The host would eat the sacrificial meat from the upper offerings, while the lower offerings were distributed among beggars, allowing them to drink and eat their fill before leaving. All households performing such prayers and sacrifices had to follow the advice of diviners, so the beggars would daily spy on the diviner's door and gather on the appointed day, and Ge was among them. One day, after the meal had ended, it was late at night with a faint moonlight. Ge, drunk, walked alone and suddenly saw a woman walking slowly ahead. He suspected she might be running away with a lover and followed her. When he reached the door of a house, the door did not open, but the woman entered, and he realized she was a ghost. He then eavesdropped from outside and heard the mother-in-law and daughter-in-law quarreling, and then everything became silent. Worried that something might happen, Ge hurriedly knocked on the door and entered. He found the daughter-in-law had climbed up to the second floor, while the mother-in-law was still angrily sitting downstairs. Ge told them what he had seen, and they were greatly alarmed, inviting him to join them upstairs. As soon as Ge stepped up, the woman he had seen rushed out and suddenly met him, falling down the stairs. Looking back, there was nothing. When he entered to check on the woman, he found her hanging from the beam. Fortunately, it had not been long, and he managed to save her, after which she thanked him generously.

The *Shi Ben* records that Wu Peng created medicine, so it follows that ancient doctors were also ancient shamans. The *Zhouguan* states that the duties of male shamans were to summon and pacify spirits, in order to cure diseases, which still retains the ancient meaning. However, by later generations, doctors gradually lost their knowledge, and shamans even more so. In Jiangxi Province's Nanchang Prefecture, there is a practice known as "soul-snatching." Each village and neighborhood has a temple dedicated to a deity called Prince, who has a white face and a smiling expression. He is clad in armor, wears a helmet on his head, holds a flag in one hand and a sword in the other, but no one knows who he is. For those who are seriously ill, a Taoist priest performs the soul-snatching ritual, using a noisy sedan chair to welcome the spirit of Prince to the patient's home. A noisy sedan chair is one that makes a clattering sound when carried. Once arrived, they burn paper money, and the Taoist priest kneels and prays for the patient.

That night, four strong men carried the deity on a pole, followed by Taoist priests and another dozen or so people holding torches. They reached an open field, where they circled three times, and all shouted. After completing the three circuits, they rushed back. Just as they reached the door, they called out the name of the sick person, and someone inside responded with "Yes." Then, they carried the deity back to the home. After three or four days, the Taoist priests sent the deity back to the temple, but the Taoist priests did not inquire whether the sick person had recovered. In Guangdong, there is a practice known as "calling the soul," also called "jumping over Mount Mao." The method involves several Taoist priests setting up an altar, hanging a deity's image, and reciting scriptures as usual. Afterward, they lay out a ladder, with each step fitted with sharp blades pointing upward. The Taoist priest, barefoot, steps on the blades and ascends the ladder. This is repeated several times and is called "ascending the knife mountain." Then, they place an iron ball and an iron rod in intense fire until they turn red.

The Taoist priest held a red iron ball in his mouth and ran a red iron wire through his hand. After a while, he threw the ball into cold water, which still boiled, and used this water to wash the patient's face. The priest then pierced his own hand to draw blood and applied it to the patient's temples and palms to expel internal evil. He lit two torches, entered the patient's room, sprinkled a little of the medicine powder, and a loud sound was heard, filling the entire room with firelight, which was called the fire powder, to expel external evil. Finally, he used one rooster and one green bamboo pole, took the patient's own clothes, climbed onto the roof, and called out the patient's name, which was called summoning the soul. When summoning the soul, if something came, such as a cow, sheep, chicken, or dog, it was all auspicious; if nothing came, the illness could not be cured. If a person came, it was also auspicious, but the person encountered would surely die. Therefore, when the Taoist priest performed the ritual, within ten households in front, behind, left, and right, the priest must ensure there was no blame; beyond ten households, he could not guarantee protection.

Unluckily, if one encounters this and dies, there must be a fire mark on their body. I consider all these to be the fraudulent practices of shamans and sorcerers, not the traditional methods of ancient shamans and doctors, which should be clearly prohibited by those in power. As for the so-called "烂泥膏药" in Ningbo's countryside, it is particularly laughable. The person selling it wears flowers in his hair, has his face powdered, and walks through the villages. If someone comes to see him for medical advice, he does not diagnose the pulse but uses a brush to draw three dots of ink in water. The ink trails down in a winding manner, and he uses a bamboo tube to observe it, immediately describing the symptoms. Some are right, some are wrong, and he usually communicates the diagnosis in five or seven-character poems. The words are extremely vulgar and coarse, but it is also somewhat melodious and pleasant to listen to. If you follow him, he will give you a ball of mud, instructing the patient to apply it to a certain acupoint, with no other medicine. Hence, it is called "烂泥膏药." He claims that his ancestors had a relationship with a fairy, who gave them a ball of mud, and that his descendants have used it without end. These nonsensical and absurd words cannot even be questioned.

Yu Jun from Cixi, as a living person serving in the underworld, was known by the name of "Living and Not Constant." Yu was also a man of letters who felt somewhat ashamed of this, so he hid his name. When he went on his duties, he would lie stiffly like a dead person, but his chest area remained slightly warm. Sometimes he would wake up after an entire day, and sometimes after several days. Over time, he grew accustomed to this and no longer found it strange. When asked about matters in the underworld, he would remain silent. Yu had a close friend who had always been his companion, and this friend asked to go with him to see the scenes in the underworld. Yu refused, but his friend persisted earnestly. Yu said, "If you must go with me, choose a quiet room to lie down in. Even if you do not wake up for several days, warn your family members not to move." His friend followed his advice. Yu led his soul to roam through the underworld, which also had cities, no different from the human world. Soon they arrived at an office, and Yu had something to do and entered. He told his friend, "Wait for me here, do not go elsewhere."

In this realm, it is fundamentally different from the world of the living. If you stray into a wrong path, I cannot save you." His friend replied, "Yes." After waiting for a long time, he felt bored and aimless. Suddenly, several women came out from the office, all of them extremely beautiful and charming. His friend was moved and followed them, entering a narrow alley. There was a small door half-open, and all the women entered. He unconsciously followed them in. Suddenly, he heard a loud sound like thunder, startled and looked around, and found himself in a pigsty, transformed into a small pig. He came out from the office, did not see his friend, and could not find him. He returned home with a heavy heart. When he went to visit his friend, he was still lying stiffly and not awake. His family blamed him, and he was also in a panic and had no solution. However, he felt that his chest was still warm, and then he comforted his family by saying, "This person's life is not yet extinguished; I should go again to seek him." So he went again to the afterlife. After more than a day, he woke up and said, "I have found him."

The soul is located in a distant county, having already been reborn, and only I can bring it back. I traveled to that place and discovered a family with a sow that had recently given birth to a piglet. Upon reaching the pigpen, I saw a piglet with a white tail. When it saw Yu, it made a strange sound, as if alarmed. I asked the owner how much the pig was worth. The owner, sensing something unusual, replied, "It cannot be less than ten silver taels." I paid the amount and took the piglet with me. I brought it to my friend's bedroom and struck it down with an iron mallet. My friend then revived. However, from that day on, his intelligence and knowledge were slightly diminished compared to before, perhaps because he had once been reborn as a pig. Yu himself has said that whenever he enters the underworld, the city god grants him two pairs of straw shoes and one fan. When he wears the straw shoes, he can walk like flying, moving wherever his heart desires, without any barriers. Even city walls can be crossed as easily as thresholds.

The fan has one side red and the other side black. If you fan someone with the red side, they will definitely feel hot all over; if you fan someone with the black side, they will shiver. It is said that in the afterlife there is a blood-stained pool, and it is believed that women who give birth must enter this pool, but this is false. The blood-stained pool is specifically for men. Men who have only one wife do not enter this pool; those who remarry must enter the pool once; those who have three wives must enter the pool twice. If they have concubines, the number of times they enter the pool corresponds to the number of concubines. What Yu Jun says serves as a warning to those who are excessively lustful; however, this is not entirely correct. The *Book of Rites* states, "Even if a clan leader is seventy, he must have a principal wife." Therefore, remarriage is not forbidden. In ancient times, officials had one wife and two concubines, while scholars had one wife and one concubine. How can something allowed by the rites of the sages be considered a sin by the underworld? I believe that if there is no blood-stained pool in the underworld, that is fine. If there is one, it must be for men and women who do not marry according to the rites. Those who have extramarital affairs will all enter the pool, and their guilt will be appropriately matched.

The customs of Cixi include the belief in a "stomach god." It is said that if a ghost owes someone money in life, it will enter the person's stomach. The person, relying on the ghost's power, can summon the souls of the deceased, and people must pay the ghost with money to settle the debt, after which the ghost will leave. Some people have only one ghost in their stomach, while others have multiple ghosts cohabiting in their stomach. When the ghost first enters, the person must suffer greatly, and every meal must result in severe vomiting. Only after the ghost's residence is settled, allowing it to enter and exit through the mouth and move freely, does the person recover. The people of Cixi believe this very much. There was a Mr. Feng who used this method to summon the spirits of the dead. He enjoyed smoking opium before his death, and when the ritual began, he immediately asked for it. They then laid down mats and cushions as he did in life, set up a tray on the bed, and filled it with opium in a tube. Soon, wisps of smoke emerged, as if someone was breathing, and before long, the opium was gone. This is truly strange.

A Wang Surname Man and His Lost Son During a time of banditry, a man of the Wang surname lost his son. He asked the Daoist master Dou to investigate. Master Dou returned and said, "This person was killed by cannon fire. Now his ghost is all black like charcoal, with an ugly and evil shape. Moreover, he has long been with various fierce ghosts, rough and resolute, and has no human reason. He has long forgotten his pre-death affairs. If he is invited into the house, he will surely bring disaster. It is better to let it be." But the Wang surname insisted on bringing him back, only after being forced. Soon after, Master Dou said, "Your son has arrived, he said nothing, and suddenly went into the inner room, and cannot be questioned." That night, the Wang surname indeed felt very uneasy, and one daughter and one old woman both died suddenly. The Wang surname was very distressed and again asked to drive him away. Master Dou said, "This is not something one person's strength can withstand. Fortunately, there are three ghosts in my belly, working together to drive him away, perhaps something can still be done." Soon, a fierce sound of struggle was heard in the air, from inside to outside, it took a long time to subside. Master Dou said, "He has been driven away, but I am very exhausted."

Twenty li east of Cixi County lies the Piaoqi Mountain, formerly known as Ling Mountain and commonly called Xiao Lingfeng. On the mountain stands the Huilong Temple, which houses an image of Ge Xian翁, a revered deity. All who enter the temple to worship must request a document before the deity, called a "guandie," which is said to guarantee 500 taels of silver in the afterlife if held upon death. Thus, local villagers climb the mountain and request the document, returning to store it in a chest and burning it at the time of death. The temple is situated at the summit of the mountain, with a climb of about five li from the bottom. Even the elderly and weak must walk up; it is said that if one is carried up in a sedan chair, the received document becomes worthless. Every year from the first to the tenth day of the first lunar month, and on the eighth day of the fourth lunar month, is the incense period, during which all the people of the county, regardless of distance, climb the mountain, and people from neighboring counties also come. Because of this, the temple's incense and offerings flourish.

The text is strange, vulgar, and lowbrow; it was printed using woodblock printing by a paper-selling shop and stamped with the seal of Immortal Master Ge. Scholars often laughed at it, believing that monks from temples had created this heretical doctrine for profit. However, when women were dying, they often cried out to burn the documents, and only after the document was burned did their eyes close. Sometimes, during the burning of the documents, the flames were seen to take the shape of intertwining dragons. Mr. Feng Mengxiang, a scholar-official, said, "The way of heaven and earth is nothing but sincerity. Countless ordinary couples, with their sincere intentions, are firmly united. Who knows if the underworld has added such a rule? Everything, if not sincere, is false even if it is true; if sincere, it is true even if it is false." I think this argument is correct. The *Doctrine of the Mean* says, "Without sincerity, there is nothing." It can be known that sincerity is the very thing. Ancient ritual objects and modern paper money can all be understood in this way.

**A Scholar-Official Haunted by a Ghost** Feng Zhenyuan, a scholar-official from Cixi, styled Yinfu, was proud of his talent and charisma. The house where he lived was haunted by ghosts that caused mischief, often appearing in daylight. Sometimes a human head would emerge from the ground, soon rising to the height of the house; at other times, a disheveled figure would be seen, its tongue stretched down to the ground. The various strange and grotesque occurrences were too numerous to describe, yet the scholar-official was not afraid. He always carried a precious sword and would chase away ghosts whenever he saw them. One day, while chasing a ghost, it fled into his wife’s chamber. His wife had just given birth. Common people called the room where a woman gave birth the "red room," and it was considered unclean to enter it within a month. The scholar-official observed this prohibition very strictly, and the ghost, knowing this, fled into the red room. His wife saw the ghost sitting on the edge of the bed, gasping for breath, and was greatly frightened. She called out for her husband to come in and drive away the ghost. The scholar-official finally refused to enter, but instead held the sword and stood outside the door, calling out, "If the ghost has the courage, why not come out and fight me?" Later, the scholar-official wrote a petition to the city god, having just completed the draft but not yet copied it. That night, he heard the paper and pen on his desk making noise. The next day, he found the paper torn and the pen completely worn out. Smiling, he said, "Is the ghost afraid of my writing?" He then changed the paper and pen and rewrote the petition, burning it at the city god’s temple. That night, he heard the sound of chains, and it was only at dawn that the noise ceased. From then on, the ghost was never seen again.

A man from Tangxi Town named Zhang Qing Sun, during the turmoil of the Taiping Rebellion, sought refuge in Shengze Town and fell ill, subsequently dying. After his death, his soul arrived at the land temple in Shengze. The land deity of Shengze, recognizing him as a native of Tangxi, sent him to the Water South Temple in Tangxi. The Water South Temple is dedicated to the local land deity of Tangxi, who was a woman named Lu, who died and became a deity. Tangxi Town is divided into the southern and northern banks of a river, with the temple located on the southern bank, hence the common name "Water South Goddess." Zhang Qing Sun was then sent back to the land temple in Shengze and revived. Within ten days, his wife, Lao Shi, died. Before Zhang Qing Sun's death, his wife had prayed extensively at various temples, asking to die in his place. One of Zhang Qing Sun's uncles, who had died over a decade ago, possessed the body of a servant woman and spoke to Lao Shi, saying, "Your intention is very good, but you are also a strong woman in our family, and we cannot do without you. Why not let your daughter take her place?" Lao Shi replied, "This daughter is still young, and I still hope for her to grow up." She firmly requested to die in her husband's place. The seventh uncle said, "Since your intention is already determined, I will not go against your wish. However, this matter is also quite complicated, and I will need to incur significant expenses." When Zhang Qing Sun was revived and Lao Shi died, it was known that the request was honored in the afterlife. Another person from Linping named Xu Shouzhi had a son named Meisheng.

Mei Sheng was gravely ill and on the verge of death. His elder sister, who was barely ten years old, suddenly called out [B17K] from a dream and awoke. Her cheeks were red and swollen, and her entire body bore purple bruises. When asked about it, she said that her grandparents had beaten her. At that time, however, her grandparents had long since passed away. He asked, "What crime did you commit that they beat you?" She replied, "My grandparents wanted me to die in place of my younger brother, but I refused, so they severely whipped me. I was in unbearable pain, and in the end, I agreed." Not long after, she indeed died, and Mei Sheng’s illness miraculously healed. From these two events, one can see that death is indeed substitutable. Only the sage understands this, which is why Duke Zhou spoke of substitution in death. Common scholars, upon reading the *Jinteng*, doubted this. How could they understand the doctrine of life and death, or the nature of ghosts and spirits? Furthermore, my second daughter-in-law, Yao, was greatly loved by her grandmother when she was young. At the age of six, she fell seriously ill and died. Her grandmother had ordered a coffin to be made, but it was not yet completed, so the body had not yet been wrapped. Whenever someone attempted to carry her out, her grandmother would weep bitterly and hold her tightly. There was a young sister, barely one year old, and the nursemaid placed a sword beside her. Her mother sighed and said, "Your sister is about to die, and your grandmother is grieving so deeply. Do you not know this? Why don’t you take her place?" That night, the whole family could not sleep, gathering around the sickbed. Suddenly, the sick person jumped from the bed to the ground, as if someone had thrown her. The people were shocked, quickly picked her up, and she was alive again. Soon after, her young sister died.

Fan, a man from Nanchang, Jiangxi, lived outside the Shunhua Gate. In his youth, he was mischievous and troublesome, causing concern among his clan members, who repeatedly sent him to the authorities for punishment. However, each time he was released, his behavior became even worse. There was a man named Jia in the clan who openly declared to the crowd, "Just bind him and bring him here, and I will gouge out his eyes so that he can no longer cause trouble!" The crowd followed his words, binding Fan and handing him over to Jia. Fan begged for mercy with great distress, but Jia did not listen, and he ultimately gouged out both of Fan's eyes, leaving him blind and incapacitated. More than ten years later, Jia died, leaving behind four sons. Fan befriended all of them, as if forgetting the past grudge. In the twelfth month of the fifth year of the Guangxu era, Jia's two sons had business in the city, and Fan accompanied them, staying overnight at the Fan family temple in the city. The three of them slept together on the same bed. In the middle of the night, Fan used a sharp blade to stab the bellies of the two sons and killed them, then went to the county office to report the incident. Despite being blind and incapacitated, Fan was able to kill the sons of his enemy. Although the matter is not worth mentioning, one can also call him a wise and brave person.

There was a person with the surname Shen from Linping who fell ill for several days and was quite exhausted. Suddenly, it seemed as if someone was taking him, and he was led to an official place where a supervisor checked the records and said, "This person is not yet due to die and was mistakenly taken. They should still be sent back." The supervisor then ordered someone to guide him out. Upon seeing countless criminal prisoners being severely beaten below the hall, Shen recognized a person named Shen Kai among them, whom he had known all along. Shen Kai was naked and receiving punishment, with his entire body rotten. Someone beside him said, "Tomorrow, a chain will be put through your nose." Shen saw this and felt very compassionate. Soon, he seemed to awaken from a dream and came to his senses. When he was asked to see Shen Kai, he found Shen Kai lying in bed, writhing and calling out, having endured this for several days and nights, unable to die. The next day, Shen Kai's wife brought medicine, but suddenly, two streams of blood shot out of Shen Kai's nostrils, and he died immediately. Shen Kai was a cruel and unrestrained person who had long been a trouble for the village. After his death, everyone was glad. I believe that officials of the underworld mistakenly taking people is a common occurrence, as recorded in texts like *Qian Ding Lu* (Record of Predestined Events), which mentions the pursuit of Wei Fan, the county magistrate of Jinxiang, and mistakenly pursuing Wei Fan, the county magistrate of Jintan; and *Chao Ye Qian Zai* (Record of the Court and the People), which mentions the calling of Xu Ziru, the vice minister of the Celestial Bureau, and mistakenly calling Zhou Zigong, the vice minister of the Earthly Bureau. If this case of Shen being mistakenly taken seems intentional, it is likely to show him Shen Kai suffering in the underworld, to turn and tell the world as a warning. Otherwise, Shen Kai's death, despite the unusual bleeding from his nose, how can we know it was a punishment from the underworld?

A man from Anqing, surnamed You, was living temporarily in Jiangxi. His daughter-in-law was pregnant, but beyond the expected time without giving birth, her belly swelled like a large gourd. Someone recommended a magician to treat her. When the magician arrived, he untied his hair, went barefoot, performed the ritual dance of Yu in the courtyard, wielded a sword and directed, recited incantations. He took a rooster, pricked its blood, and wrote two talismans. One talisman he burned in water and had the woman drink it, the other he had her wear on her body, saying, "You will give birth within two days, but I fear the child may not be human." After two days, the woman's belly shook violently, and she suffered great pain, nearly dying. Suddenly, a foul wind arose from under the bed, the light became dim and gloomy, and everyone in the room felt cold and fearful. The woman had already given birth. When they looked at the newborn, it had hair and was very dark in color, its eyes and nose could not be distinguished, its mouth was very wide, almost reaching its ears, and its hands and feet were also somewhat present. Its body was soft and lacked bones, stretching when lifted and shrinking when released. Everyone was shocked and frightened. The mother ordered them to pick it up and throw it into the wilderness.

My second daughter-in-law recounted that during her time at home, she saw a child born in a relative's household. By the age of ten, the child still could not walk or stand. They placed the child in a large wooden basin where both urination and defecation occurred. When held up, the child appeared as tall as an adult, but when released, it collapsed into a skin-like husk.
