That night, the official sat on the rice, holding a volume of the *I Ching* (Book of Changes), with a large candle burning on the table and two daggers placed nearby. Halfway through the night, a sound like tearing silk came from the left column. Upon closer inspection, a figure about an inch long was seen swaying and moving beneath the column, gradually growing longer until it reached a height of about a meter. Dressed in a red robe and black hat, the figure approached the table, causing the candlelight to suddenly dim. The official pointed a dagger at it, and it slightly retreated, only to return. Pointing the dagger again, it retreated as before. This back-and-forth occurred three times before the figure retreated to the base of the column, shrinking back to an inch in length and disappearing. The official drove the dagger into the ground to mark the spot, then returned to sit on the rice and read the *I Ching*, seeing nothing more that night. The next day, the official ordered someone to dig at the marked spot and found a red coffin with inscriptions that could not be read. The merchant wanted to burn it, but the official refused, ordering a new burial site and composing an article to honor the deceased, thus bringing peace to the house. 司马秀谷钟，是晴江的伯父。他在二十岁出头时就已经因绘画而闻名，擅长山水、人物、花鸟、鱼虫等题材，下笔便有生动之趣。在道光年间，他颇受公卿的重视。他曾画过一幅麻姑的画像，他的母亲韩宜人非常喜欢，便把它挂在房间里。房间狭小，画像便挂在卧榻旁，下面还放着一个便壶，也没有特别注意。不久，韩宜人得了鼻血，血流如注，连续三天三夜不止，情况非常危急。

A close relative named Xu Yunzhang reported that there was a man named Sun Mingxiu in the northern mountains who was skilled in the art of Qi Men Dun Jia, a form of ancient Chinese divination. Xu was then sent to consult Sun about the illness and its prognosis. After setting up the divination array, Sun said, "Although the illness is serious, it will not cause harm. There is a divine image hanging in front of the sick person's bed; it should be sent to a Buddhist temple for veneration, and the illness will heal on its own." Xu returned and reported this to everyone, but they all believed that there were no other divine images in the inner chambers except for this painting of Mazu, which had been hanging for a long time without any unusual occurrences, and that it was not appropriate to move it to the temple. Xu suggested, "Why not burn it?" They followed his advice and burned the image. From the flames, a column of blue vapor rose directly into the sky, reaching great heights before dispersing after some time. The next day, Han's illness was completely cured. In Jinling, there was an old monk at the Yongfu Temple named Changling, whose family lived in the old vegetable market north of the Drum Tower. From a young age, he enjoyed fighting and quarreling, associating with ruffians in his neighborhood, and his parents could not stop him. He was once arranged to marry a woman from a certain family, but he firmly refused to marry. His parents forced him, and he eventually got married. On the night of the wedding, he climbed over the wall and ran away, never returning. Not long after, he drank excessively and killed someone, fleeing to live in hiding. He then cut his hair and became a monk. After the legal case was resolved, he secretly returned to his hometown and took up residence at the Yongfu Temple, and his family did not know about it. His wife, who had been left alone when her husband ran away, was always depressed and eventually fell ill. After a long time, she was about to die and told her in-laws, "My illness has reached a critical stage, and there is no hope for recovery. I wish to invite monks to perform Buddhist rituals to gain merit in the afterlife. Even if I die, I will not regret it." Her in-laws pitied her and agreed. They immediately invited monks from the Yongfu Temple.

When the monk arrived, it was then known that he was indeed his son. He was commanded to return to lay life, but he refused. He was made to see his wife, and she took a ring from her finger and threw it to the monk, saying, "This is the dowry of your family, return it to you. I have no marital feelings for you, but I have died while guarding this empty chamber, thinking I have no debt to you. After my death, I will have the *Great Compassion Repentance* recited for forty-nine days." She also told her in-laws, "Please let my coffin be placed in the Yongfu Temple. If you do not follow my request, I will surely cause trouble after my death." The next day, she died. The in-laws, fearing that she would cause trouble, followed her request and placed her coffin in the west wing of the temple. After several months, the monk fell ill, growing increasingly emaciated. An old servant in the temple, responsible for incense and fire, was commonly known as "Daoist." One night, in the middle of the night, the wind and rain were chilling. The Daoist heard the west wing door suddenly open and secretly peeked. He saw a young woman walking along the corridor, entering the monk's room. The Daoist suspected that the monk had hidden her and rushed to catch her, but the woman suddenly disappeared. He then realized that she was not a human, and in shock, he fell to the ground. After some time, he regained consciousness. He then told the monk about it and questioned him about the matter. The monk did not hide anything and said, "My wife comes out every night and meets with me, and my illness is due to this." The Daoist said, "This is not difficult." He then used a stone worker's black string to draw lines on the coffin. Every time he drew a line, there was a sound inside the coffin as if it were splitting, and she no longer came out. The monk's illness soon healed. When the Guangxi rebels caused chaos and Jinling was lost, the monk left to wander in Huaiyang. When the chaos was settled and he returned, the temple had already been destroyed. He then stayed at the Shansiji Temple on the Drum Tower. In the third year of Tongzhi, some people still saw him, and he was eighty years old.

The ink lines used by carpenters and stonemasons, anciently called "string and compass," as recorded in the *Book of Rites*, "when the string and compass are properly set, they cannot be deceived into showing crookedness or straightness." However, scales, balances, and compasses are insufficient to ward off evil; only the ink lines of carpenters and stonemasons do ghosts and spirits fear. Why is this so? Because evil cannot overcome righteousness. The *Guanzi* *Zhouhe* chapter says, "The string corrects and makes things straight." The *Eastern Jin* *Ancient Text of the Shangshu* says, "Wood follows the string to become straight." The *Huainanzi* *Shizhi* chapter says, "The string is used to measure and correct all things." Gao You comments, "The string means to make straight." Ghosts and spirits fear the ink line because they fear its righteousness. Outside the western gate of Cixi, there was once a zombie that came out at night to harm people. One night, several carpenters climbed onto the city wall, hiding behind the parapet to observe it. Indeed, they saw a zombie fly out of the coffin, moving like the wind. The carpenters waited until it was far away, then went to the place and marked the coffin with an ink line around it. They then climbed back onto the city wall to watch it return. Soon the zombie returned, saw the ink line marks, and dared not enter. It wandered around, looking as if searching for something. Suddenly, it saw people on the city wall, leaping and trying to climb up. The carpenters quickly marked the parapet with an ink line, and the zombie could not climb up. They held it until dawn, when it fell to the ground, and then they burned it together.

In the rear of Xiaocang Mountain in Jinling, there is a Great Compassion Temple. After the chaos, the buildings were in disarray, but only the front hall and the rear pavilion remained. There was a man named Wu, who was poor and alone, and he lodged there. During the day, he taught his students on the hall, and at night, he slept in the pavilion. His disciples were all village children, who rotated to cook. The temple had been without monks for a long time, and apart from the master and his disciples, there were no others. The mountains surrounded the pavilion on both sides, and there were many deserted graves in the mountains. Every time the sun set in the west, the evening became dim and hazy, and he often saw a white-clad person moving among the pines. Wu foolishly thought that the white-clad person was a gold statue, suspecting that there was hidden gold, and he watched for it. He knew that it came out from a coffin, so he pretended to have an excuse and dismissed his disciples early, with an axe in his sleeve, he went. But before he could reach the coffin, the white-clad person had already emerged from it, and it was a zombie. He was greatly startled and retreated, but the zombie followed him. Wu thought that the zombie could not cross the ditch, so he ran to a place with ditches and pits to avoid it, but the zombie crossed the ditches and valleys as if walking on level ground. Wu was very窘 (in a difficult situation), and he hurriedly ran back, but the temple door was not yet closed, and the zombie had already caught up. Wu hurriedly ran, climbed the pavilion, and fell.

The next day, the disciples all gathered, but the teacher did not come out. Someone went inside to look and, as they approached the stairs, saw a white-clad figure standing stiffly on the ladder. They were frightened and ran away, informing the family. Upon hearing this, everyone came, using a long pole tied with a broom to hit the figure, which then fell. Its face looked alive, but its hair was scattered and about an inch long. The crowd pressed a balance weight on its chest, fearing it might rise again. They climbed the stairs to see Wu, who had fallen in front of the bed, spitting white foam, with his chest still warm. They poured ginger soup on him, and he began to revive. After questioning, they found out what had happened, and someone said, "You really had great luck!" It seemed that the zombie had great difficulty climbing, struggling all night, and only reaching halfway up the ladder when the sun came up, making it unable to move. Wu then led the crowd to the coffin from which the figure had emerged. Everyone recognized it, saying, "This is the son of someone named Jia." They went to inform Jia, who then returned his son's body to the coffin, gathered firewood, and burned it. After several months, Wu also passed away. Someone asked Jia, "Why was your son wearing white?" Jia replied, "My husband and I are over sixty years old, and we only have this one son. This son died, and there is no one else in the clan to inherit. Thinking that no one would wear mourning clothes for him after his death, I dressed him in white clothes in advance."

Zhen Hexuan, a native of Jiangning, studied the doctrines of Shen and Han. He had a concubine named a certain Lady, who was quite beautiful, and he greatly cherished her. When he moved to the magistrate's residence in Suining County, he took his concubine with him. The western study of the residence was known to have many foxes, and the concubine once mentioned this. That night, she dreamed of a young woman who was both beautiful and charming, with a slight hint of anger. The woman came to her bed and scolded her for careless speech, which had offended taboos, saying, "Without a light punishment, how can we prevent this from happening again!" She then gently brushed the concubine's face with her hand, smiled slightly, and left. The concubine woke up in shock but felt no discomfort. The next day, when she looked in the mirror, she saw five blue-black claw marks on her left forehead. She tried every possible method to treat them, but they could not be removed, and she felt very unhappy. When the host who provided the accommodation resigned, Zhen also moved elsewhere. After five years, he again accepted the invitation of the magistrate of Suining County and still took his concubine with him to live there. After a month, she dreamed again of the young woman she had seen before. The woman smiled at her. The concubine asked her to remove the scars, and the woman smiled and said, "Yes." By the next morning, the claw marks were still there. The concubine thought to himself that she had already promised and would not deceive him. So, he placed a bowl of clean water in the western study, burned incense, and prayed to her. Three days later, when he took water to wash his face, the marks indeed disappeared with a touch.

Zhou, a cavalry soldier from the Jiangnan military district, once accompanied a military officer to the area outside Gubeikou Pass and stayed at an inn. The region was characterized by deep mountains, dense forests, remote and sparsely populated, and often inhabited by tigers and wolves. After sunset, no one dared to go out, but Zhou was unaware of this custom. After finishing dinner, he went out to relieve himself and did not return for a long time. The innkeeper kept several fierce dogs, which were locked with iron chains during the day and released at night. The owner would always inform all guests not to leave their rooms, as the dogs could bite. Upon hearing this, the military officer called for Zhou, only then realizing he had gone out. The owner was shocked and exclaimed, "What time is this, still going out! Could he not have been eaten by a tiger?" They then gathered all their companions, carrying torches and weapons, and went to search for him. As soon as they stepped out, they saw Zhou lying face down on the ground, with a tiger sitting on his thighs. Everyone was shocked. They wanted to attack the tiger but feared hurting Zhou. Suddenly, the tiger stood up and licked his neck. The group then attacked it, and the tiger leaped away. They carried Zhou back to the inn, where he was bloody and barely alive. They applied medicine to him, and fortunately, he did not die. However, from this point onward, his bones were exposed, his teeth like saw teeth, with no skin or flesh, and no hair grew.

In the northern gate of Suining County, Xuzhou, there stands a temple dedicated to the Black Warrior, which is said to have been built during the Ming Dynasty. Over time, the temple fell into disrepair, leaving only a single hall, and there were no monks to be found. During the summer of the Dingwei year of the Daoguang era, two elderly men from the countryside, A and B, carried firewood into the market. After selling their goods, they returned together. Upon reaching the northern gate, they took a brief rest under the city wall and fell into a deep sleep on the spot. After some time, A woke up and found that B was missing, although all the bundles of firewood were still present. Suspecting that B had gone to the toilet, A waited for a long time but B did not return, so he went to look for him. Upon arriving at the temple, he saw bloodstains on the ground in front of the hall. Looking up, he saw B hanging in the air, his belly stuck to the rafters, with no apparent means of support, yet he did not fall. A was greatly alarmed and ran to report the incident to the authorities. The official arrived and ordered the servants to lower B's body. The crowd suggested using a ladder to ascend and retrieve the body, but suddenly, the body fell. Upon examination, it was found that B's chest had been pierced. They concluded that there must be a hidden monster above, so they searched the rafters but found nothing. The official had no recourse and temporarily ordered A to return home and inform his family. It was just noon, and soon thick clouds gathered, followed by a loud roar of thunder. In a moment, heavy rain came down. Suddenly, a loud thunderclap occurred, causing the beams and rafters of the hall to collapse. A black object flew away in the lightning, and the rain stopped immediately. Shortly after, someone came from outside the city and reported that a bat, as large as a cow, had been killed by an earthquake seven miles from the city. Thus, they knew that the killer of B was this creature.

During the Xianfeng era of the year Gui-Chou (1853), the Taiping rebels captured Nanjing. There was a certain Deng, a low-ranking official in the Jiangning General's office, who fled from the city, but his son was captured by the rebels and accepted a position under them, which Deng did not know about. Later, the main Qing army surrounded the city, and the rebels were in a desperate situation. Someone who had escaped from the rebels informed Deng, and Deng then wrote a letter to his son, urging him to escape. Upon receiving the letter, the son collected all the silver and gold he had accumulated over the years, divided it into four jars, buried them in the house where he had previously lived, covered them with large bricks, marked the spot secretly, and then fled to his father. Within a month, the city was recaptured, and Deng feared that his son's buried treasure might be found by others. He rushed into the city with his son. Upon returning home, they saw that the house was still intact, and the markings for the buried treasure were still there. They were greatly pleased, and that night, they dug up the jars, which were still there, and they were even more pleased. When they opened the bricks, however, they found that the jars were empty, and they were very frightened. Soon after, they heard a sound coming from one of the jars. They looked inside and found only one eel, about two feet long, with a diameter of about an inch, swimming inside. Deng suspected that the eel had turned into gold, so he cut it open, and it died, with blood flowing out just like any other fish. He hung it under the eaves, hoping it would turn back into gold, but the next day, it had rotted.

In the southern suburbs of Jinling, there is a village named Dong Village, inhabited by over a hundred families, all surnamed Dong. The area is surrounded by mountains and water, with dense forests and trees, appearing lush and vibrant, as if filled with auspicious energy. It is said that during the reign of Emperor Kangxi, there was an elderly man named Dong Lao, who was highly skilled in feng shui, and he was known for choosing auspicious places for others, none of which were ever inauspicious. His two sons frequently asked him, "Father, you have chosen many auspicious plots for others. Why not choose one for yourself for your eternal rest after a hundred years?" Dong Lao replied, "I have already found it." When they asked for the location, he smiled but did not speak. As Dong Lao approached death, his sons asked again, and he wanted to speak but hesitated. After repeated urging, he finally said, "The place is right in the house where I live, but I fear you may not be able to follow my instructions." The elder son replied, "Father's command, how dare we disobey!" Dong Lao then showed them the location of the grave and instructed them, "After I die, do not use any clothing, coffin, or coffin case. Dig a pit eight feet deep, place my body naked inside, upside down, with my head down and feet up, facing east, and cover it with soil. You must keep the door locked for forty-nine days, and only after that can you open it. If you cannot follow my instructions, do not use this place."

When the old man died, his two sons discussed together and said, "Although our father's will was like this, to be naked and planted upside down, would it be at peace with our hearts as filial sons?" They then decided to slightly modify it, wrapping the body with silk and standing it upright in the grave, following their father's will in everything else. Approaching the forty-ninth day, the old man's brother-in-law came from Xuzhou to visit the old man. When he arrived at their door, the door was not opened. He knocked on the door and shouted loudly. The two sons from inside informed him of their father's last wishes. His brother-in-law became very angry and said, "You did not tell me your father died; that is your first sin. You buried him without propriety; that is your second sin. Moreover, how can there be anyone in the world who buries his father like this? Do you not have some other plan? If you do not open the door quickly, I will report you to the authorities." The two sons had no choice but to open the door and invite their uncle in. That night, a five-colored aura appeared above their house. The next day, a great thunderstorm occurred, and lightning struck and broke the grave. The two sons rushed to see, and they found that the old man's body had transformed into a dragon, with all its head and horns fully formed, only the two eyes still closed, the entire body as if wrapped in a spider web, it was because the silk had bound him. Soon, a swift thunderbolt struck down, and the old man's body was reduced to ashes. The two sons soon died as well, leaving no descendants. This old man foolishly hoped for something beyond his station, leading to his death by lightning after his passing. Although his skills were refined, they ultimately led to his own destruction.

A man from the state of Chu was appointed as an official in Sichuan. The place where he was stationed was very barren and harsh. Although there was an official residence, it was said that a monster inhabited it, and all previous officials had rented houses from civilians. The official was so poor that he could not afford to rent a house, so he had no choice but to stay in the official residence with a servant. On that night, he was too afraid to sleep. Being a habitual drinker, he took some wine and drank it heartily, hanging a sharp knife around his waist for self-defense. At midnight, suddenly a giant pushed open the door and rushed in, with great force. When he looked at the giant, it was as white as frost or snow. The official immediately drew his sharp knife and struck the giant with all his might, and there was a sound of something falling to the ground. The giant fled, and the official shouted and chased after him. His servant heard the noise and came out from a nearby house. The official's courage grew, and they chased the giant to a place where it disappeared. They marked the location. They returned to the room and saw what had fallen to the ground. It was a bloody arm. They sat down and waited for dawn, and there were no other strange occurrences. In the morning, they saw that the arm was made of silver, and they were greatly surprised. They went to the place they had marked at night and dug, finding a silver figure, but it was missing an arm. They attached the severed arm to the figure, and it fit perfectly. They carried the figure back home and weighed it, and it weighed several thousand taels.

Luo Dalin was a man from Luo Dian Town in the eastern suburbs of Shanghai. He was tall and dark-skinned, with extraordinary strength, and a rough, fearless nature. Since childhood, he had been engaged in the business of carrying and selling goods. He wanted to marry a wife but lacked the means, so he consulted with his peers. At the time, there was a large house that was often haunted, and anyone who entered it always died. A curious person made an agreement with him saying, "If you can stay here for one night, we will pool ten thousand coins to cover your wedding expenses." Luo agreed to it, but was worried about him breaking the agreement, so the crowd wrote a contract and gave it to him. The owner of the house, surnamed Wang, heard this and said happily, "If you can indeed drive away the monsters, I have three rooms in the market area, and I will allow you to marry a wife without charging a fee." He also wrote a contract and gave it to Luo. That night, Luo pounded garlic into a paste, mixed it with liquor, drank until slightly intoxicated, carried several large candles, unlocked the door, and entered. The crowd feared he might secretly leave and hide elsewhere, so they locked the door from the outside. After Luo entered, decaying grass covered the path, bamboo shoots blocked the entrance, dust had accumulated to a thickness of about an inch in the rooms, but the last room was clean and free of the slightest dust. In the western part of the room, there was a bed with a canopy and quilts and bedding, which were left behind by the previous occupant.

Luo immediately sat in the tent, to observe any strange occurrences. After the second watch, he suddenly heard a loud noise coming from outside the tent. A black figure rushed in abruptly, wandering around inside the room, and its face was not very distinguishable. Soon, it approached the bed, and Luo leaped out of the tent and grabbed it. The black figure could not escape, both hands were held, and it could not raise them. It then blew air at Luo, which was as cold as ice. Luo turned his head to avoid it, and after a while, his neck hurt, as if it had been cut by a knife. He then forced his head to turn and blew air at the ghost as well. The ghost also turned its head to avoid it. The ghost blew air again, and Luo avoided it once more. They held on for a very long time, and Luo heard the rooster crow. The ghost suddenly shrank, the more it shrank, the smaller it became, and it could no longer blow air. Its body became increasingly hard, and Luo finally held onto it and did not let go. At that time, his companions were all nearby waiting, and at dawn, they all gathered to look. What Luo held was a piece of a coffin, which he let go and fell to the ground, making a splashing sound. They gathered firewood and burned it, and the smell was unbearable. The crowd was encouraged by this, and they gave Luo money and a house, as per the contract he signed. The strange occurrences in this house ceased, but Luo's neck was permanently bent and could not straighten. The villagers called him "Ghost-Catcher Luo Da."

Mr. Yu, a native of Haisi, resided in Shen Dang, a village in the western district. His wife mistreated a maid, who was quite intelligent and sixteen years old, often beating her severely. One morning, she saw dog droppings in the courtyard and was angry that the maid did not clean up. She beat the maid and forced her to eat the droppings. The maid had no choice but to bend down and eat a little bit, crying and going to the riverbank to rinse her mouth. Suddenly, she saw a boat docked at the shore, and a beautiful woman came out, adorned with elaborate hairpins, earrings, and splendid clothing. Two maids supported her as she walked up, and she looked at the maid and asked, "Are you a maid in the Yu household? Please tell the mistress that guests have arrived, quickly clean the back room, and make it my bedroom." The maid believed her and ran back to report to the mistress, just as she entered the door, she fainted and lost consciousness. The mistress had her carried inside and gave her a ginger soup to drink. After a long time, she regained consciousness and explained what she had seen. Behind the house, there was a three-bay building used for storing things, not for living. Upon hearing the maid's words, he went inside to investigate. He had not gone far when sand and stones hit his face, preventing him from entering. He then realized it was a fox and sent this maid to go. The maid entered without any issue and went upstairs, seeing that the stored items were neatly arranged in the side room.

In the middle was a sitting room, to the left a bedroom, adorned with brocade screens and embroidered curtains, all newly arranged. A beautiful woman directed two maids, laying out floor coverings and hanging paintings. The maid was afraid and wanted to run away, but the woman made her sit down and spoke to her, saying, "I heard that your master treated you cruelly, and I am very angry about it. If he does it again, I will punish him slightly." From then on, whenever this woman scolded the maid slightly, someone would slap her cheek. The woman feared this, and thus began to treat the maids well. After a month, the woman told the maid, "Tell your master to lend me fifty silver dollars; I have something to use them for. But I will still not leave your house, so there's no worry that I won't return." The maid relayed this to the owner. The owner was eager to see her, so he sent the maid to say, "Money is not something I吝啬, but it must be paid in person." The woman laughed and said, "Agreed." The owner took fifty silver dollars and went to the back room, but saw nothing. He wanted to climb the stairs, and just as he was about to reach them, suddenly he saw an arm in front of him, wearing a light red shirt, the red sleeves rolled up high, revealing the white wrist, adorned with gold and jade bracelets that tinkled. The palm was rosy, the five fingers slender like green onions, the nails long by an inch or so.

Yu's heart was stirred, and he suddenly heard a soft, gentle voice say, "Received, now go quickly!" His arm then disappeared, and the object in his hand vanished as well. At that very moment, Yu's daughter saw a young woman dressed in fine attire, wearing a light red shirt, about twenty years old, who smiled at her and then suddenly disappeared. The woman appeared to both Yu and his daughter in different places but at the same time, which was strange. Yu had a sister-in-law who had been a widow for three years, and he treated her poorly. One night, he found foreign coins beside his pillow, the same number as the money he had lost, and he realized that the woman had taken from one place and given to another. Yu also came to understand and began to treat her well. The woman lived in the house for half a year, and then she told the servant, "I am now going to Suzhou." She then disappeared. This happened in August of the fourth year of the Guangxu era (1878). Wu Hongji, a scholar from Haiyan, taught at the residence of Xu in the same county. Xu's residence was quite large, and during the chaos caused by the Cantonese rebels, it had once been the seat of a rebel prince. After the rebels were defeated, piles of bones filled the empty rooms, and even though they were moved to other places, strange phenomena still occurred. Wu taught there for one or two years and gradually became thin and weak. His relatives and friends found it strange and asked him why, but he always said there was nothing unusual. After a while, he gradually changed his usual behavior and started to mutter softly to himself.

After informing his family, they arrived, but he no longer recognized or responded. They carried him back home, planning to summon a doctor to treat him. Suddenly, Wu told his father, "There is no illness, there is no need for a doctor." Upon hearing his voice, it was discovered that he was a northerner and also a woman. The father then asked, "What kind of ghost or demon are you, coming here to cause trouble?" Wu continued to speak as a woman and said, "I was born into an official family, and as a child, I was well-versed in literature. I died in sorrow after suffering a misfortune. The night realm is lonely, and I have a connection with your son, so I came to join him, planning to seek a lifelong happy marriage, not to cause trouble." Thus, they realized that there was something possessing him, and despite trying every means to drive it away, it did not leave. The family became angry and cursed, and it cursed back; if they beat, it would beat back; but if spoken kindly, it would speak clearly and fluently, being ingenious and wise beyond compare. It also enjoyed singing songs, and its delicate and beautiful sounds stirred the souls of those who heard it. The family then agreed to be her ghost wife and set up a place for her. She described herself as surnamed Wang, with a pet name Mei Nong, and provided her birth date and month, but her illness did not improve. One day, she told the family, "Tomorrow is a good day and auspicious hour, I will return with my husband." On that day, Wu died. It was heard that Wu was quite diligent and willing, liked to read books, but was killed by a lustful ghost. Is it not due to some predestined sin?

Chapter Seven** Fan Xihidi, courtesy name Eclou, was from Xianning, Hubei Province, and was the third brother of my eldest son’s wife. When he was serving as an official in Guiding County, Guizhou Province, he fell gravely ill and died, only to revive. He said that when he died, he initially felt no pain, but instead felt himself moving from his inner office to the main hall, where he lingered for a long time before descending the steps. As he was about to exit the ceremonial gate, an old man rushed forward from outside and pulled him back in, saying, “If you leave, who will protect the millions of people under your charge?” The old man then escorted him back to the inner office, and Fan Xihidi revived, as if it had all been a dream. Fan Eclou possessed both literary and martial talents and served in Guizhou for over a decade, accumulating merit to become a supervisor. When he first arrived in Guiding County, the area was besieged by rebels. Fan Eclou took refuge on the battlements and shared the hardships with the soldiers, which earned him the full support of both soldiers and civilians. He fought numerous battles, both large and small, against the rebels and received dozens of wounds.

After the official was appointed, the people of Guiding loved and respected him deeply. Whenever he traveled to the provincial capital on official business, hundreds of people would follow him. Even when he stopped outside the city, dozens still accompanied him into the city. Upon arrival, they would gather at the provincial office and shout, "Do not take away our good official!" This is why he served in Guiding for the longest time. Although he had been promoted to the position of prefect, when he was still in the county, he conducted official business using the attire and formalities of a county magistrate, without openly acknowledging his promotion. Guiding is located ninety li northeast of the provincial capital, and at that time, the provincial authorities also relied heavily on him. The official, E Lou, was energetic and ambitious, skilled in military affairs. He once said that if the emperor ever had matters to attend to in the four border regions, he would not dare to decline, even if it meant risking his life. I greatly admired him. Unfortunately, he died before reaching the age of forty, which was a great loss! I had originally intended to write a biography of him to be included in my collection, but due to procrastination, I failed to do so. Therefore, I have summarized the main points and recorded them in my notebook. Throughout history, loyal ministers and righteous men have often been recorded through unofficial historical records or novels, and it is not necessary to rely on formal inscriptions or articles to ensure their deeds are known far and wide.

Yubingwen, courtesy name Langzai, was also from Xianning. He was the son of his wife's brother to his father-in-law, Fan Yunnong, who held the title of Guancha (an official rank). Therefore, he often managed the financial transactions for the Fan family, being honest and meticulous. He was also skilled in painting, and his eldest daughter-in-law had a basic understanding of realistic painting, which she learned from him. In his later years, he lived at home and was initially without illness. One night, he suddenly dreamed that Fan Yunnong called him away and entrusted him with family affairs. By then, Fan Yunnong had been dead for a long time. Upon waking, he told others, saying, "Am I about to die?" Shortly after, he died without illness. All his sons had died before him, leaving only one grandson, who was weak and frail, lacking no ugliness. Some doubted that Yubingwen, a virtuous man, should not have been like this. His eldest daughter-in-law said that the house where he lived, even when the weather was clear and pleasant, still felt dark and gloomy, suggesting that the family's decline might be the reason. However, his middle son, named Shouzhong, courtesy name Yunsheng, served as the magistrate of Longli County in Guizhou Province and had a good reputation for governance.

The bandits surrounded the city, and the defenders held out for over a year without being defeated. Just as the official was about to leave his post, the bandits suddenly arrived, and the city fell. The people carried the official out of the city, and they had already traveled dozens of miles, close to safety. However, the people carrying him became slightly tired, and he took the opportunity to run back to the city, meeting the bandits on the road. The bandits recognized him and said, “This is a good official.” They warned each other not to kill him. The official then seized a sword from a bandit, killed several of them, but the bandits still did not kill him. Ultimately, he drowned himself. Such a person should indeed be recorded in both the chapters of upright officials and loyal and righteous people. Langzai once sent his eldest daughter-in-law from Hubei to Suzhou, where she stayed in the house for ten days. Zeng stayed in Hangzhou and returned home, but did not stay overnight before Langzai left. The eldest daughter-in-law said that Langzai came to request that Zeng write a biography for his son, but it was not accomplished due to haste. Zeng felt sad about his intention and wrote about it here. Even though a book like *Qi Hua*, which records strange events, is not considered a true history, it is just to express Langzai's unspoken intention.

Sun, a student from the prefecture of Gu'an, was gravely ill and on the brink of death. In a daze, he saw a ghostly officer with a tablet arrive, and the tablet bore the inscription "Summon the student of the prefecture of Gu'an named Sun." Sun asked, "Can I not avoid it?" The officer replied, "The affairs of the underworld differ from those of the mortal world; you cannot be spared." Sun offered a large bribe, but it was refused. Urged to go, he was led to a grand official building, where he was brought inside and made to kneel before a table. The god asked, "Are you Sun?" Sun replied, "Yes." He then knelt and falsely claimed, "I have a mother who is over seventy years old, and I wish to extend her life by one cycle (12 years) to care for her." The god angrily said, "You have no good deeds in life, only knew to lend money at high interest, and it was fortunate that no interest was deducted. Do you dare to ask for an extension of her life?" The god took a book from the table, marked it with a red pen, and threw the pen down with a bang. Sun was frightened and woke up. He told his family about the experience, and that night he died. In fact, Sun's mother had died before him, but the god did not rebuke him for his foolish request, for the book was not checked, and even the god did not know. Sun owned dozens of acres of mulberry fields, and during the silkworm season, he lent leaves to people. When the new silk was produced, he collected the leaf money at several times the market price. The high interest the god mentioned, was this what he meant?

In the Inspectors' Street of Suzhou, where shops and residences are densely packed, there is a pen shop called Baisongquan, whose pens are of good quality. In the year 1888 during the Guangxu era, the shop frequently suffered from fires, the cause of which was unknown. One day, a column inside a double wall suddenly caught fire, spreading to the beam. A rice basket hanging in mid-air suddenly saw flames rise from all sides; the fire was quickly extinguished, and the basket was not burned. A skilled person in the morning rolled up their bedding and tied it up, but soon smoke emerged from the bedding; upon inspection, it was found that the bedding was partially burned. The shop became so afraid of fire that they dared not light any more, but the fires continued. Fires occurred once or twice a month, alarming the neighbors. Someone suggested that the fires might be caused by fox spirits, so an altar was set up in the room to worship them, but the problem continued as before. A scholar then advised, "Evil cannot overcome virtue. You should do good deeds to ward off the evil. Setting up an altar to worship the spirits actually helps fuel the fires; it is of no benefit." The owner followed the advice, vowed to the gods, did good deeds, removed the altar, and finally, the fires stopped.

Sheng, a native of Keqiao in Xiangshao, was a young servant of the Song family in Tangxi. The Song family had a daughter who married into the Yao family. Later, when the Song family fell on hard times and could no longer afford servants, Sheng followed the Song daughter to the Yao family. The Yao family lived together as a clan, with many people, and every night until the third or fourth watch, there were still people coming and going, so the main gate was always left open. To guard against petty thieves taking advantage of the situation, they made Sheng sit and guard it. Sheng lived with the Yao family for several decades, being diligent in his duties and having a little savings. Because the former master of the Song family was poor, he often helped them financially. Later, due to old age and illness, he returned home, while his son still stayed in Tangxi. When the old man was gravely ill, he told his family members, "I will die today, but my son has not arrived. I will wait for him." After two days, his son arrived, and the old man then died. Although Sheng was a lowly person, he had a certain degree of moral integrity, and it is appropriate that at the time of his death, he could come and go freely.

The Lán Hú, also known as the Dàn Tú, is a small fish found in coastal areas. It resembles a loach in shape and measures about two to three inches in length. During low tide, it jumps and hops in the mud, and there are countless thousands of them. The people of Liuheng in Dinghai (Oushan) always catch and eat them. However, when people walk in the mud, they often get stuck, and they must use a mud board, known as a "ní wān," to avoid getting stuck. The "ní wān" is a wooden board on which a person sits with one foot outside and pushes with the foot, allowing it to be pushed several meters at a time. The method of catching the Lán Hú involves first inserting bamboo tubes into the mud, then riding the "ní wān" and chasing the fish back and forth. The Lán Hú will jump into the holes and all enter the bamboo tubes. When the "Yue bandits" attacked Dinghai, they first arrived at Liuheng. The land of Liuheng is divided into eight sections, with the names of the upper four sections and the lower four sections. The lower four sections have already surrendered to the bandits, while the upper four sections have Wang Dashui, courtesy name Boquan, who is registered as a candidate for the imperial examinations and is highly respected in the entire village. He owns a good horse, and someone told the bandits about it, and the bandits wanted to obtain it.

One day, a group of lower-ranking individuals led four bandits to the port of Somewhere. The king refused to give them what they sought, and the bandits became enraged, drawing their swords and threatening the villagers. The villagers gathered, and despite being outnumbered, they managed to kill all four bandits. The next day, a large group of bandits arrived, and the king directed the villagers to defend themselves. The bandits abandoned their boats and climbed ashore, only to sink into the mud. As one foot lifted, the other sank. The king, seeing this, ordered the villagers to ride the mud eels, and their movement was as swift as flying. The bandits could do nothing but wait to be killed, and countless bandits were slain. Thus, the villagers became braver, and the bandits repeatedly attacked, but always failed due to the mud eels, and ultimately failed to capture the Six Crossings. Later, when the bandits attacked Dinghai, they also suffered repeated defeats, because they had already been defeated at the Six Crossings. The mud eel is just a fishing tool, but its shape is similar to what Su Shi called the "rice transplanting horse." At that time, the bandits were very powerful, and all the counties and prefectures in eastern and western Zhejiang were captured, but the small coastal people defeated them with the mud eels. The simple remedy for chapped hands, used in small ways to protect one's hands, and in large ways to defeat the Yue state, how can this not be true?

During the reign of Emperor Tongzhi in the year Ren Shen, Feng Mengxiang, a scholar of high moral character, had a younger brother named Zecheng who was studying in the city of Cixi. One day, there was a sudden rumor that a messenger from the Daoist temple of Zhang had arrived at the city god temple. They went to see for themselves and indeed saw a messenger carrying a yellow cloth bundle, which contained documents. These were taken out and burned before the deity, and the temple door was sealed with a strip from the Daoist temple for three days. As a result, the entire county spread the news, unsure of what had happened. Several days later, a local official explained that the incident involved a certain county in Liaodong. In that county, a couple was walking on the road, and the wife urgently needed to urinate, so she did so on the side of the road. That night, both were asleep when someone knocked on the door. The husband got up to see who it was and did not return for a long time. The wife also got up and went outside to check on her husband, only to find him with his waist broken. People suspected that the wife had actually killed him and reported it to the authorities. The wife vehemently denied the accusation. The official then asked in detail where she had been that day and what she had done, and she told him everything. The official then went to the place where she had urinated and dug several feet into the ground, finding a coffin. When they opened it, the face was still as if alive. The official was puzzled and sent a document to inquire at the Daoist temple. The Daoist temple replied: "This ghost was a person from the county of Zhejiang Cixi during the Yuan Dynasty who died while traveling for trade to Liaodong. This ghost was very strong and fierce, and the wife's act of urinating happened to be over the ghost's coffin, provoking the ghost's anger. It was this ghost who killed the husband that night."

A Ghost from Cixi The ghost, originally from Cixi, should be dealt with by sending a formal letter to the City God of Cixi. Therefore, someone was sent with two documents to Cixi. One document was burned in the City God Temple, setting a limit of three days to resolve this case, thus the temple doors were sealed for three days. The other document was submitted to the Cixi County, and the county officials learned the details. I believe this matter to be false. Recalling an event in the *Xin Qi Xie* by Mr. Yuan Suigarden that is similar to this, local officials handling local affairs would never send documents involving ghosts and deities to the True Person's office. This must be a story fabricated by Taoist practitioners to stir up the gullible people. I often saw people in Wu claiming to be judges from the True Person's office, giving out talismans and collecting money. This envoy must be of the same ilk. The two documents carried, one burned in the City God Temple, claiming to resolve the case in three days, who actually saw this? The other submitted to the Cixi County, and the county magistrate merely listened to it as a casual matter. The supposed county in Liaodong is far away, and who would cross the border to ask about it? Thus, the two documents submitted can be done arbitrarily, and no one can uncover the truth. Considering that the matter is not in Zhejiang East or West, nor in the neighboring provinces of Zhejiang, but is said to be in Liaodong, the intention is also clever. The people of Cixi, seeing that there is a formal document submitted to the county office, will surely believe it without doubt. They can then freely carry out their scheme. Although my book records strange things, I do not believe in such matters.

In the region of Dinghai, Daxie (or Aoshan), there were three brothers named Jia, Yi, and Bing. Both Jia and Yi made a living by catching sea jellyfish (as noted by one of my disciples, Feng Mengxiang: the term "sea jellyfish" first appeared in the *Minzhong Haichao Shu*; today's common usage follows this). According to the *Wenxuan* (Selections of Refined Literature), the *Nanyue Zhi* (Records of Nanyue) mentions that there are many jellyfish along the coast, which are called "宅" in the East Sea. The *Bomuzhi* (Record of Marvelous Things) describes a creature in the East River that looks like congealed blood and is called " fish." I believe that the ancient characters "宅" and "" were both pronounced like "yu" and "yu" in ancient Chinese, but the modern character "蜇" is pronounced differently, reflecting changes in pronunciation over time. Bing was still young and enjoyed playing around, which tired his two elder brothers. One day, they were about to go to sea but kept it secret from Bing. However, Bing had already found out and went to the boat first, hiding in a secluded spot.

When they had traveled halfway, B spoke to A, saying, "If I had not come today, it would have been much quieter." Suddenly, C appeared and said, "I am here!" They all laughed together. Soon, they arrived at a place to anchor the boat. A was about to get out of the boat, but as soon as he stepped out, he fell back into it. B then tried to get out and did the same. C did not understand the reason, so he tried to look out and see. There, he saw a huge snake, as thick as a wooden bucket, with a pure black color, coiled and lying on the sand. When it heard human voices, it seemed to have been startled awake, raising its head to the sky and opening its mouth to breathe out. Its mouth split into two forks, like a pair of scissors, and the inside of its mouth was bright red, like blood. Soon, he saw the snake's body slowly straighten out and wriggle away, with the sound of the wind following it. It moved very quickly and soon disappeared. They finally realized that their two brothers had fainted because of this, so C shouted, "The snake has gone!" A and B then woke up, quickly turned the rudder and returned, and no longer engaged in this business. Someone said: If C had not left, it would have been uncertain whether A and B would live or die. Was C's departure perhaps caused by someone?

There was a man named Wang, who studied the doctrines of Shen Buhai and Han Fei. He was a man of integrity. At night, he served as a ghost official, administering affairs in the underworld. Three officials shared the duty, all wearing the official attire of the present dynasty. The central official wore a hat adorned with coral, and Wang sat to his left. One night, a female ghost appeared, her hair disheveled and crying, holding a petition and presenting it to the central official. The central official ordered it to be brought to Wang, who read it and realized that the petition was against him. Previously, Wang had been a magistrate in a certain county, where a woman had been harassed and committed suicide. The harasser would normally receive the death penalty, but Wang altered one character in the legal document, reducing the criminal's punishment by one degree and sparing his life. Thus, the female ghost petitioned against Wang. Wang, realizing that the matter involved him, still ordered the petition to be presented to the central official. The central official conversed with the ghost for a long time, after which the ghost laughed and left. Wang did not know what they had been talking about. The next day, the county requested Wang to write the revised county chronicle. Wang then realized why the ghost had laughed and wrote a biography of the woman, detailing her story, and included it in the county chronicle.

The Zhang family of Dantu was a prominent clan. It is said that one of their ancestors, Mr. Zhang, went to Jinling to take the provincial examination. The rental price of the house he stayed in was very cheap, but dust had accumulated to a thickness of about an inch in the room. Friends staying in the same place suspected it was inauspicious and left, but Zhang, because of the low price, stayed there, on the ground floor. At midnight, he heard footsteps on the stairs. He watched for it and saw two people dressed in tattered clothes, with pale and gloomy faces. One was carrying a pot, and the other was holding a fan. They descended the stairs and stood in the middle courtyard. Zhang was very afraid and wanted to call his servants. Suddenly, another person appeared, wearing a white cloth hat and holding a green parasol. He told the two people, "The new top scholar is here, you must leave quickly." After speaking, the three disappeared. The next day, he asked the neighbors and learned that there was a hanged ghost in this building, which often caused mischief. Zhang lived there and was not harmed. In this exam, he indeed ranked first. The following year, he became a Jinshi (imperial scholar) and ended his career as a county magistrate.

Zhenjiang resident Liu, along with his younger brother and two sons, traveled to Jintan to take the annual examination. Because the rental prices of houses near the exam venue were very high, they chose to rent a modest dwelling in a remote location. The house was in a deep alley, and every night they heard ghostly sounds, described as "需鬼" (need ghost). Frustrated by these disturbances, they planned to chase away the ghosts. Liu said, "I can recite the *Yan Kou Jing* to summon the ghosts. Once all the ghosts gather, my two sons will stand at the alley entrance to block their exit, and my younger brother will hold a broom and strike the air vigorously inside. This way, we can punish them severely, so that henceforth ghosts will not dare to come." They agreed. That night, they arranged everything according to ritual, and Liu sat high up, reciting spells to summon the ghosts. His two sons stood at the front and back of the alley entrance, blocking their exit, while his younger brother held a broom and struck the air vigorously inside. Liu also rang a cymbal to assist. The ghostly sounds reached the alley entrance and then turned back, like this for a long time.

The eldest son called out to his uncle, saying, "Uncle, you are tired. Come and guard this alley entrance, and I will take your place to fight the ghost." The uncle went to stand there but had not yet settled when he suddenly fell to the ground, as if pushed down by someone. It seemed that the ghost had already fled. Later, the other two sons both became jinshi (advanced scholars), while the uncle ended his life as a scholar of the classics. Someone said, "Perhaps the ghost considered them too easy to mock?" I replied, "Not so. Ghosts fear humans because they fear their composure; once they move, their spirit becomes unstable. When the enemy comes, they attack the weak points; when illness comes, it takes advantage of the vulnerability. All this is the same principle." My contemporary, Ding Lianfu, also known as Shao Zhou, said that in his youth, he once playfully learned from Buddhist monks to perform the ritual of attacking the mouth of fire. He only learned the texts for summoning and did not master the techniques for dismissing. Ghosts gathered in his room, and the light was dim like a small soybean. Lianfu ignored them and did not inquire, wrapping himself in his quilt and going to sleep. He only heard the chirping sounds until dawn when it finally became quiet, and there was no other strange occurrence. This is because Lianfu had a deep foundation, thus the ghosts could not mock him. Otherwise, there would be no one who would not be trapped by them.

Lianfu said: In the year Xinchou of Daoguang (1831), I was on my way to the capital for the imperial examination and took a detour through Xuzhou. I checked into an inn, but it was full. The owner led me to a back room, where he swept the bed and spread the mat, preparing for me to lie down and rest. Soon, I heard a very foul smell. I looked around the four walls but saw nothing. I lit a candle and looked under the bed, and there was a corpse there, with both hands and feet nailed to the bed with four large nails. Lianfu remained silent in fear, sitting upright until dawn, then packed up and left. This must have been a lone traveler carrying heavy wealth who was killed. If he had seen it and cried out, he would surely have been harmed himself. In such danger, to remain so calm, his courage and wisdom are both commendable.

Zhenjiang Ganlu Temple, a grand Buddhist monastery, stood in the city. During the Jiaqing era, Governor Changbai Cegong, who was stationed in this prefecture, did not believe in Buddhism. One day, he visited the temple, and the monks engaged him in a lengthy discussion about the Buddhist scriptures and the benefits of cultivating virtue. The governor remarked, "Buddhism is profound and subtle, and it is difficult to understand it immediately. However, if teachings like the compassionate clouds and rain, which universally save the souls in the underworld, are truly effective, there must be tangible evidence of their efficacy. Tomorrow, I would like you to perform the ritual of releasing the hungry ghosts. I will personally observe it, and if I see something, my doubts will be dispelled; otherwise, it will indicate that your wisdom is insufficient to comprehend the visible and invisible realms, and I will have to choose a different high monk to uphold this temple." The monks agreed, and the head monk consulted with his disciples. One of the young novices, who was quite cunning, suggested to the head monk, "There are two beggars in the market whose appearances are like those of ghosts. When we summon the spirits of the dead, we can have them wander back and forth in the courtyard, moving from east to west. The governor, observing from afar, will surely think they are ghosts." The head monk followed this advice and made an agreement with the beggars, giving each of them two hundred coins to pretend to be ghosts and deceive the governor. However, the next day, before sunset, the governor had the three gates of the temple sealed, preventing the beggars from entering.

The monks, at a loss, decided to follow the usual ritual and perform the food-offering ceremony at night. Indeed, human-like shadows appeared moving around the temple courtyard. The monks were puzzled because the temple gates were closed, and they wondered how the beggar could have entered. The magistrate, greatly alarmed, regarded the event as strange, made generous offerings, and left in reverence. The next day, when the monks went to give the beggar money, they found him lying stiff and lifeless by the roadside. They finally realized that the one who had come the previous night was the ghost of the beggar, and they had him buried. In Zhenjiang, the Zou family was quite wealthy during the Qianlong and Jiaqing reigns. Their mansion was spacious, with many small rooms around it that were often empty. One day, a hired worker was found hanged in one of the empty rooms, but the family was unaware of this. When the family held a Buddhist ceremony, at night they performed the ritual of offering food to hungry ghosts. As the monks were reciting sutras on the altar, suddenly a strong cold wind arose, and the candles in the hall dimmed and went out. The monks were terrified and all fled into the side rooms, except for the head monk, named Xisheng, who remained seated calmly, muttering something under his breath, though it was unclear what he was reciting. After a while, the candles lit up again, and the monks gathered back. After the Buddhist ceremony ended, Xisheng told the host, "Today's events were quite strange. You should search everywhere to see if there is any other matter." The next day, when they searched the empty room, they discovered that the hired worker had been hanged there for several days.

In the district of Dantu, there was a man named Shen who was skilled in the imperial examinations but was very poor, having nothing to eat, and so he wrote legal documents for others. One night, he dreamed that his father severely scolded him, and from then on, he stopped writing legal documents. Later, he passed the provincial examination, became a jinshi (imperial scholar), and was appointed as a county magistrate, sent to Henan. After more than ten years, he completed his official career and returned home, with a bag quite full of wealth. On New Year's Eve, as the night was almost half over, his wife heard what seemed to be people gathering and talking in front of the main hall, and she looked through the door crack to see them. She saw several people in ancient attire sitting in a row on the hall, one of whom said, "Shen's unjust wealth should be burned." Another said, "This seems too harsh; cutting off his descendants would suffice." A third said, "Shen, as a county magistrate, still judged cases without being unfair; cutting off his descendants is too severe; it is better to disperse his wealth." His wife trembled with fear and entered the house. After that, his family indeed became increasingly poor.

Bao, a man from Yangzhou, firmly believed in the practice of fengshui divination. He made all his trading decisions based on the results of these divinations, and after a long time, he became very wealthy. One day, the fengshui spirit warned him, "On a certain day, Yangzhou will experience a flood. You should prepare early and avoid the disaster." Bao was doubtful and believing at the same time. However, he thought it was the beginning of autumn, the time when the Huai River was at its highest water level, and since the fengshui spirit was known for its supernatural powers, it would not deceive him. Therefore, he prepared boats in advance, pretending to have business in Zhenjiang, and took his family with him. On that day, the weather was overcast, and it rained heavily. As soon as they left the Gua Bu section of the river, the wind and waves became very strong, and the waves were surging, putting them on the verge of danger. Fortunately, they reached Jingkou, and the rain stopped. The wind also calmed down a bit. The next day, he inquired about the news in Weiyang and found out that there was no flood; the city remained peaceful as usual. A few days later, he returned home with his family and asked the fengshui spirit. The spirit wrote a message in the divination tray, asking, "Were the wind and waves in the river frightening on that day?" It seemed that the spirit was teasing him for asking too many questions. In Jingkou, there was a man surnamed Cai whose wife, Zhang, saw a ghost come to demand her life while she was ill. The ghost said they had a grudge from a past life, and now that they had met, it would not let go. Cai wrote a petition to the city god. Zhang saw a ghostly official holding a ticket to take the ghost away, and soon another official came to take her away too. Zhang then fainted as if she had died. Two days later, she revived and told her husband, "It is not possible to continue! I went to the underworld and faced the ghost, and he was right while I was wrong. The gods will not protect me." The next day, she indeed died.

In Yangzhou, there was an old man who became wealthy through pearls and jewels, becoming the leader among merchants, with a vast fortune and an age surpassing sixty. Once while riding in a sedan chair passing by the customs checkpoint at the ferry, he encountered a large number of ships coming quickly with strong winds, arriving in a moment, almost capsizing the ferry boat. The people on the boat were terrified and shouting, and the old man in the sedan chair was also very frightened. There was a sedan chair bearer nearby who saw that the person in the chair was not the old man, but rather a monstrous figure with a green face, red hair, large eyes, and teeth protruding from the mouth, resembling a ghost or demon from a temple. The bearer was greatly shocked and dared not speak. In a short while, everything returned to normal, and the boat did not capsize. It seems that the old man, due to his fear, had his spirit leave his body, and seeing this strange appearance, he might have been a reincarnated demon king. Shi Lanchi was a man from Xieshi Town in Hai Ning. He had no sons but one daughter named Dagu, who was twenty years old and not yet married. In the year Jia Xu of Tongzhi (1874), Shi suffered from severe dysentery, and all the doctors were at a loss. There was a local man named Dai who was skilled in the art of fumigation (a type of divination), and he was asked to seek a remedy from the gods. The gods prescribed four ounces of rhubarb, but everyone doubted that it could be used and requested a change. Another prescription involved a pig's spleen filled with glutinous rice, lotus seeds, and water caltrop, cooked until soft and eaten. Dagu was usually wise, and seeing the prescription like this, she sighed, "My father's illness must be fatal."

On that night, Dai Sheng was at home when suddenly a brush began to move on its own, as if it wanted to write. Startled, he tried to steady it, but it wrote quickly: "Who would have thought that such a great good thing could happen here? Shi Nu Dagu sacrificed herself for her father-in-law; her filial piety is commendable, but alas, her father's fate has already been determined and cannot be prolonged. The Heavenly Deity showed compassion, bestowing success upon his successor. She was also recorded as a servant of Mount Tai, with a ceremonial procession led by feathered banners passing by here not long ago." The crowd was amazed, and suddenly they smelled a strong fragrance, accompanied by faint music. Dai and Shi were about one li apart, so he went to inquire about Shi. Shi still did not know that Dagu had died, so he hurriedly went inside to see. There, Dagu lay on the bed, her body already frozen. Investigating the cause of death, it turned out that she had died from consuming raw opium. The next day, Shi's illness slightly improved, but after fourteen days, she finally died. After the period of mourning, her successor was admitted as a student of the Imperial Academy, confirming the prophecy of the successor's success. Thus, Dagu's role as a servant of Mount Tai must also be true. Although I am skeptical of spirit writing, such events, though seemingly unverifiable, serve to encourage filial piety.

Minor or unofficial historical records, what harm is there in speaking and listening recklessly! The practice of "fú jī" is also called "fú jī." I have examined the entry "Shen Chengwu and the Deity Zigu" in *Yijianzhi*, which states: "Zigu is the name of a deity, unknown in ancient times, but became known during the Tang Dynasty. The world merely uses a writing tool inserted into a tray, with two people supporting it, or writing characters in the sand." According to this, it should be called "fú jī." In a place named Changqian, about ten miles from Xiazhi, there was an old man named Wang, who was a kind and honest person. He had a son who was just coming of age and had taken the examination for young scholars. After he was married, his wife died; when he was about to marry another woman from a certain family, she too died. His son then fell ill with madness, and the old man also fell seriously ill. They then asked the deity for judgment, and the deity ruled: "This is a matter of past karma. You were named Chen in your previous life and had a large fortune. There was a poor scholar named Fan who owed you a hundred gold coins."

Your urgent demand led to Fan hanging himself; his wife followed him in death, and his son, orphaned by his mother's death, also died, thus ending the family line. Fan appealed to the underworld officials, who believed that debts must be repaid and that the demand was not unreasonable. However, the laws of the underworld first consider one's heart. Fan had originally intended to repay the debt, but Chen was too hasty, causing Fan's entire family of three to die simultaneously, a situation that is particularly pitiable. Chen was originally a wealthy person, and this amount of money was not significant for him, not a significant gain or loss. But he was penny-pinching and unkind because of his wealth, and his actions were especially detestable. Therefore, it was decreed that in this life, one son and two daughters-in-law would repay the three lives, and also cause them to have no descendants, as this is how it was repaid. But considering that Chen was still a respected elder in this life, if he can perform good deeds, perhaps it can still be redeemed. The underworld officials praised his good intentions and granted Fan a twenty-year reprieve, until his son has a son, then the repayment will be made. Later, the son's illness indeed recovered.

Liu Yunjiao, the scholar-official Tingjie, was from Tongzhou under the direct jurisdiction of the central government. He passed the imperial examination during the Wu Zi year of the Daoguang era but did not achieve much success in his middle years, living a life of unrestrained indulgence in poetry and wine until his death. Xu Xincheng, the high-ranking official from Renhe with the given name Zao, and Shi Shuping, the inspector from Wanping with the given name Zhi Kang, were among his closest friends from the same examination year. After his death, Liu Yunjiao's spirit attached itself to a divination tool and descended to Xu's household, composing hundreds of words of poetry and prose that closely resembled his works from life. Xu Xincheng was startled and said, "Are you Liu Yunjiao? How did you come to this?" Liu replied, "Yes, I died three days ago. The official report has not yet arrived, so you were unaware. My former life was as an official in the Purple Palace. In this life, I have committed no great sins. I have gone to the City God's office and am no longer bound by earthly constraints. Because I thought of you, I came here." On another day, he again attached himself to the divination tool and said, "My wife and children are left alone and suffering. It would be very kind of you gentlemen to look after them. I have heard that Shi Shuping has the intention of protecting the orphan like a bird protects its young, which is especially touching." At that time, Shi Shuping was visiting the capital for selection into an official position and received the news of Liu Yunjiao's death. He thought about how Liu's son was still young and feared that poverty might prevent him from continuing his education, so he wanted to take him along to his official post. He had just had this thought but had not yet told anyone when the ghosts and spirits, thousands of miles away, already knew about it. This is indeed a remarkable occurrence.

In the eastern suburbs of Hangzhou, there is a place called Hengtang, where the ancestral shrine of Hu Duanmin, the Ming Dynasty Minister of War, stands. Originating in the late Ming period, this shrine was included in the official rites for over two hundred years, with annual spring and autumn sacrifices. During the chaos of the Cantonese rebels, the Hu family's descendants dwindled, and the shrine was destroyed by fire. One of my disciples, Gao Haiyan, known as Pengnian, who had connections to the Hu family, proposed rebuilding it. He rallied gentry from Renhe and Qiantang counties and submitted a petition to the provincial administration. At that time, Yang Shiquan, the Viceroy of Zhejiang, found no records to verify the matter after the turmoil, so he consulted the Fujian-Zhejiang Governorate. After over a year of deliberation, the Hu Duanmin shrine was rebuilt in February of the thirteenth year of Tongzhi, and the Hangzhou government sent officials to offer sacrifices as per tradition. Three days after the ceremony, Gao Haiyan suddenly dreamed of an anciently dressed figure bowing to him. That very night, nine out of ten Hu family descendants shared the same dream, indicating that the spirit of the public official was still present. His name was Shining, with the courtesy name Yongqing. He passed the imperial examination as a Jinshi in the sixth year of Hongzhi. During the Zhengde era, he served as Deputy Governor of Jiangxi, where he offended Chen Hua and was exiled to Liaodong. After Chen Hua's execution, he was reinstated and eventually rose to the position of Minister of War and Grand Tutor to the Crown Prince. His biography can be found in the *History of Ming*.

Renhe Wei Yunguo Shilong was a fellow examinee of mine from the Jiachen provincial examination. Wei was the top scholar of that year, and he was already sixty years old. Throughout his life, he was deeply versed in the classics and histories, and he also had a profound understanding of Chan Buddhism. Every morning, he would rise and first recite the *Diamond Sutra* once, without interruption throughout the cold and hot seasons. In the Gengxu year of Daoguang, when he was sixty-six, he was leaning on a table reciting the sutra when he passed away while sitting upright, with his right hand still in the posture of holding open a book. After his death, there were those who claimed he had become a deity. His disciple, Cai Xiaoxi, who was a successful candidate in the local examination and served as an official in charge of education in Longyou, had a dream one night in which he entered the public office and saw Wei and Mr. Xiang Wulü sitting together on the upper seat, with small tables placed beside them. When Cai arrived, they rose to greet him, and Mr. Xiang said, "It's great that you've come. There is a great deal of official paperwork here, and you can share the labor." At that time, Cai was still in good health, but three days later, he passed away. Mr. Xiang's name was Mingda, also from Renhe, and he was a successful candidate in the Bingzi year of Jiaqing, the same year as Mr. Tongfeng.

Wei Tiao from Renhe, who was in charge of agricultural affairs, rented a residence outside the Xuanwu Gate and had lived there for many years. In the year 20 of the Daoguang era (1840), a fox spirit suddenly appeared. It did not have a visible form but communicated with people in the air, introducing itself as Hu Meiran, with "Meiran" meaning "beautiful beard." The fox spirit was quite knowledgeable in poetry and engaged in poetic exchanges with great joy with Wei Tiao. Its poems were written on small pieces of paper and flew down from the sky. The verses were fresh and elegant, and the handwriting was strong and vigorous, making the story a topic of beautiful conversation at the time. A magician passed by the door and said, "This room has an eerie atmosphere. I can remove the supernatural presence." Wei Tiao did not believe him, and the magician sighed and left. Shortly after, a major epidemic broke out in the capital, and Wei Tiao and his three sons all died. This might be what is meant by "supernatural evil." Sun Tongbo from Hangzhou was a student. He originally married into the Shi family but died before he could marry. In the year 5 of the Guangxu era (1879), he remarried a woman from the same hometown. The wedding was scheduled, but Sun Tongbo suddenly fell ill. The spirit of his first wife attached itself to his body and said, "I will leave with Sun Tongbo." Sun's parents pleaded with him, offering to bring her coffin for burial and set up a memorial tablet in the family temple, but neither request was accepted. Shortly after, Sun Tongbo died. How strong and persistent this spirit was, perhaps this was Sun's destiny.

In the fifteenth year of the Daoguang era, a great epidemic broke out in Hangzhou, resulting in many deaths, and the city's coffins were emptied. Outside Wulin Gate, there is a place called Cangji, where a man surnamed Jin, living there, heard ghostly sounds outside his home on New Year's Eve of the previous year. Soon after, he heard someone speaking, saying, "This household has a chaste widow." On the first day of the new year, when he opened the door, he saw a large red circle drawn on the wall, which he found strange, though he also thought it was done by children. By summer, the epidemic worsened, affecting all neighboring households, but the Jin family remained unharmed. He then realized that the red circle on New Year's Eve was made by gods and spirits to identify the family. The chaste widow was surnamed Qian, the aunt of Jin Zimei, a vice minister, and she had been a widow for over thirty years. My student Gao Hai, who is the great-grandson of the chaste widow, told me this story. Gao Hai also mentioned: Huang Chengfu, a scholar from Qiantang, was highly learned and skilled in poetry and prose, but he died at the age of thirty or so. His life's works were scattered and lost. In the twelfth year of the Tongzhi era, Zhong Yuchen, a palace scholar from Renhe, was compiling a collection of poetic anecdotes and wrote to Gao Hai, asking him to gather materials. Gao had an old acquaintance with Huang and searched thoroughly for his poems but could not find them, keeping worried about it. One night, he suddenly dreamed that Huang came to him, and the next day, he found one seven-character regulated verse in old books, feeling both surprised and delighted. He copied and sent it to Zhong Yuchen, also describing the dream. A scholar's ambition persists until death, which is also pitiful. In the past, when Gu Xiajun finished selecting Yuan dynasty poetry, he saw hundreds of anciently dressed people come to thank him, which is not a fabrication.

In Qiantang, there was an old man named Bei who, when young, possessed great physical strength and always boasted of his spirit and courage. One day, after drinking late at night outside the city, he rested under the White Wax Bridge. He caught a glimpse of a woman passing by and felt something was strange, so he followed her. They reached a village house, and the woman suddenly disappeared. He knocked on the door and entered, where he found only two women, a daughter-in-law and her mother-in-law, who were quarreling that night. He asked to see the woman, and discovered that she had already hanged herself by closing the door and tying a rope around her neck. He quickly untied her and saved her, and she did not die. Grateful for the old man's noble deed, they kept him to stay for the night because it was late. The old man thought there was no man in the house, so it was not right, and he took a lamp and left alone. Soon, a cold wind came from behind, and the leaves in the forest fell with a rustling sound. The old man knew it was a ghost, and he did not pay attention to it. The ghost suddenly made a sound as if cursing. The old man became angry, turned around and hit it, and the ghost then retreated. When the old man continued walking, the ghost cursed again as before. The old man became even angrier and chased it relentlessly, returning to under the bridge. And the roosters crowed all around, and the east began to turn white.

Renhe Zhonggǔ Chūnmíng Jīng Fèngshū, throughout his life, had many good deeds. In the Xin You year of the Xianfeng era, Hangzhou was recaptured, and he died. His two sons were captured by the rebels. His second son, Dengfu, who was a scholar-official named Shipai, was only twelve years old. He was coerced by the rebels and had already left the city for more than ten li. The rebels, thinking him too young and weak, abandoned him on the road. That night, he stayed at a post station. As if someone pushed his back and said, "Hurry up, hurry up!" Dengfu was startled and woke up, but it was dark and he couldn't tell north from south. In front of him, there was a faint light, so he followed the light.
