When it was light, he arrived at Beixin Pass. There was a person surnamed Wu in that place who knew him to be the son of a scholar-official. He took him in and raised him in his home. After the chaos was settled, in the first year of Guangxu, he passed the imperial examination as a supplementary candidate. The following year, he was listed as a talented scholar. The people of Hangzhou all praised that good people have good descendants.

In Hangzhou, there was a gentleman named Gao who was courageous in doing good. In his youth, he abstained from killing living creatures, and in his later years, he also stopped consuming meat and blood. Although he did not follow Buddhism, he was fond of discussing health preservation and could fast from grains for several days without feeling hungry. He lived to be over eighty years old and passed away peacefully without any illness. One day, a friend of his was visiting Chengdu when he unexpectedly met Gao on the road. The friend was surprised and asked, "Why have you come here, Master Gao?" At the time, his friend was about to return home, so he invited Gao to join him. Gao replied, "I still wish to visit Mount Emei. Please go back first. I have a fan and a fly whisk. Could you bring them to my home and convey to my children that I am well here and they should not worry." When his friend returned home and visited, he discovered that Gao had already died five months ago. The day they met in Chengdu was the day Gao had died. The fan and fly whisk were objects buried with him. This story is recorded in the Gao family genealogy. My student, Gao Hai, is a descendant of Gao and told me about it.

In the eighth year of the Tongzhi era (1869), a fierce wind swept through Suzhou. Clothes stored in bamboo or wooden boxes (笥) were carried into the sky, dancing and swirling everywhere, and some even fell as far as ten miles away. The people of Suzhou generally used rice straw as fuel, bundling it in quantities of ten or more pounds. A man named Lu had accumulated a large pile of firewood in his courtyard, which was also carried away by the wind. The firewood rose higher and higher, appearing to be only a few inches high from a distance, and eventually disappeared after some time. One of my students, Wang Mengwei Tingding, lived on Hulong Street at that time. Suddenly, a bamboo basket fell on his house. The basket was marked with the year and month and indicated that it belonged to the Huang family of Qincun, a place in Changshu, which is more than seventy miles from Suzhou. Wang Mengwei also recounted that his family used to live near Zhehu Lake in Pingwang Town. There were many old trees in front of their house, the oldest being a locust tree that was already 200 years old. One day, a heavy thunderstorm occurred, and the locust tree was struck by lightning and split into two parts. Where the lightning struck, smoke and flames rose, but it stopped the next day. Neighbors gathered to watch, and there were no other strange occurrences. However, they found about a dou of yellow fat at the base of the tree. This substance varied in size, all hard and solid like stone, and when struck with a hammer, it broke apart, revealing a very sticky and greasy texture. It is said that poria and amber grow under pine trees, and a type of fungus grows under maple trees. Perhaps this substance is of a similar kind, though its name and uses remain unknown.

Shouchang is a region with many mountains, hence it is home to many tigers and leopards. There lived an old man named Tong in Meilin, who kept a large female pig. In one year, the pig gave birth to more than ten piglets. Among them, one had a short snout and a long tail, distinguishing it from ordinary pigs. Within a few days, the piglet grew rapidly and ate twice as much as the other pigs. The old man disliked the piglet and refused to feed it, hoping it would die of starvation. His neighbor remarked, "This pig has thin fur covering its body, with a pattern that makes it look somewhat striking. Could it be of the tiger or leopard kind?" The old man replied, "If so, it should be killed immediately, lest it grow into a dangerous creature." He sharpened his knife and prepared to kill the piglet. However, the piglet suddenly dashed out, and the old man could not catch it. It escaped into the mountains. Several months later, a leopard came directly to the old man's house and entered the old pig pen. The leopard seemed very tame and peaceful. The other pigs saw the leopard and ran away in fear. The leopard lingered for a while before leaving. Afterward, the leopard visited occasionally, sometimes accompanied by other leopards and tigers. They entered and left the village without attacking people. However, the villagers found the animals annoying and drove them away several times until they no longer returned.

A merchant from Jiangxi, named Li, traveled back and forth between Suzhou and Hangzhou for trade. He was a seasoned traveler, having seen much of the world. Once, on his way back from Hangzhou, he encountered a young man on the shore of the river, asking to be taken along. The other passengers on the boat all refused him. Li saw that the young man was poorly dressed and standing alone on the riverbank, feeling sorry for him. He said to the others, "This traveler has no place to go, which is very pitiful. Why not let him have a seat here?" And so, he invited the young man to board the boat. When they reached Changshan and were about to disembark, the young man looked at the passengers and said, "We met by chance, and you have been kind enough to allow me to ride with you. Now we are about to part ways, and I would like to show my gratitude by providing you all with a meal. Please tell me what you would like to eat." The passengers replied, "We passed by a certain place yesterday and saw some excellent steamed buns in the market. We still remember them, and we are very hungry. If you can provide us with those, we will be very grateful. We don't dare to ask for anything more." At this point, they were already far from the place where the steamed buns were sold, and the passengers were jokingly making it difficult for the young man, knowing that he was poor and could not possibly fulfill their request. The young man said, "Very well." Then he returned to his sleeping place and lay down under the blanket. The passengers called out to him, saying, "Don't disturb our peaceful sleep." One of them said, "We are all hungry. Where are the steamed buns?" The young man replied, "Why are you so eager? I just got up to wrap the filling." He continued to lie there as before. After a long time, hot steam began to rise from under the blanket. The passengers were surprised. Someone said, "This must be the snoring of a thirsty sleeper."

A Young Man's Gift Soon, a young man stood up and said, "The steamed buns are out of the steamer!" He opened his quilt, and there were steamed buns piled up in abundance. The guests ate their fill, and all were satisfied. They found it strange and asked about his method, but he just smiled and did not speak. After eating, they went ashore, and the young man invited Li separately to meet three miles away. When Li arrived, the young man was already there, and he took out several pieces of paper as a gift. Looking at them, they were from Yushan to Li's hometown, and all the boats and vehicles were hired on his behalf. These were the tickets from the various brokers. Li was surprised and asked him, "I dare not deceive you," the young man said, "I am known in the world as the Iron Abacus. Without searching through bags or chests, I can use my skills to take people's money. The money brought by the guests on the boat has already been divided in half. Because you are a gentleman, I have taken nothing, and I have hired the boats and vehicles for you, to repay your kindness." Li was greatly surprised and very grateful, and he thanked him repeatedly. The young man said, "The cost of the boats and vehicles was taken from the guests, why thank me?" Li said, "It is hundreds of miles away, how can it be done so quickly?" The young man replied, "We can easily obtain things from thousands of miles away, let alone hundreds of miles, it is just a short distance!" He then told Li, "There are many like me in the world. You should keep a little grain or official stamps in your bag, so that you won't be tricked by magicians." He then bid farewell to Li and left. Li showed the tickets to the brokers, and they all matched. All the boats and vehicles along the way were provided as promised. Even thieves have their ways, isn't this what it means?

Disciple Wu Meixian from Hanyang mentioned that his family enjoyed the practice of spirit writing, naming their altar "Zhu Yun Tan." Five enlightened masters, known as "Ming Wu Xianshen," frequently descended to this altar, claiming to be a fox spirit from Jizhou during the Yuan dynasty. In his youth, this fox had bewitched a woman from a certain family and was pursued by thunder deities, nearly perishing. Afterward, he spent over two centuries in hidden cultivation, eventually being registered in the books of immortals. He also spoke of the path to immortality, stating that the greatest sin is committing sexual misconduct, and the most significant virtue is cherishing life. Therefore, for the past two centuries, he has devoted himself solely to cherishing life, refraining from harming even the smallest living things, such as grass and trees. When asked about auspicious or inauspicious signs, he often did not respond, but he enjoyed discussing poetry and ancient events, such as the rumor that Liu Ji (Chenghuo) had a fleshly scale on his chest and that Wang Yangming was tall and dark-skinned—details unknown to the world. Someone asked, "Why don't you hide your fox identity?" He replied, "Transforming from a fox to an immortal is like rising from humble origins to high social status, a source of honor, so why should I hide it!" In Linping, there was a widow from a certain family who lived alone in the small temple called "Bei Hua An," practicing long-term vegetarianism and devoting herself to Buddhism for many years. She kept a cat that also did not eat meat or blood. Every meal, she fed the cat a bowl of white rice with a piece of tofu placed on top, calling out to it and warning, "Cat, you must save and eat this!" The cat seemed to understand, first eating the rice and then the tofu, every day. My cousin's daughter, who married into the Zhou family, actually witnessed this. My eldest daughter, upon hearing about it, laughed and said, "It is unknown what this woman has practiced, but this cat will surely achieve enlightenment."

A certain family was plagued by a fox spirit, so they invited a Taoist priest to exorcise it. The priest wore a star crown and an elephant tablet, prostrated himself, and offered a petition, with two children holding swords in attendance. But before the priest could rise, the children suddenly disappeared. Soon, a sound was heard coming from a jar. When they looked, the two children were inside. The jar was not very large, and it is unclear how it could accommodate the two children, or how the children managed to enter the jar. They discussed breaking the jar to let them out. As soon as one struck it with a hammer, the children cried out in pain from inside the jar. The priest then found himself at a loss, and was forced to pray to the fox. A voice from the air said, "Since you know your fault, you may leave, and I will let the children out." So everyone moved to another place, and the children emerged. The jar was not broken, and it is still unclear how the children came out. I previously recorded the story of the Cixi Xiaoling Peak and its temple, and now I have heard of another temple, one that engages in excessive and improper worship. The god in the temple is unknown by name, but a statue of a woman was paired with it, which turned out to be a girl from the Zhang family. The girl first went to the Xiaoling Peak with her mother to burn incense, and upon returning, she said that the god was pleased with her appearance and intended to marry her.

From then on, she closed herself off and did not interact with anyone, yet there were sometimes fragrances emanating from her room, and the intimate conversations between husband and wife were often heard. Her family was troubled by this, but could do nothing about it. After several months, her belly swelled. Her mother told her father, who said, "A virgin who is pregnant is a disgrace to the family." He immediately forced his daughter to die. The spirit possessed the daughter and spoke, "If you kill this girl, you will surely bring disaster upon your family!" The father angrily said, "I do not fear it, I will definitely kill the girl!" The daughter knew she could not escape, and wept, saying, "I will die for this piece of flesh, and all women carrying a pregnancy will be protected by me." She then drowned herself in the water at night, and her body floated upstream to the temple. That night, the temple attendant dreamed that the deity said, "Rise, rise, welcome Madame." He did not understand what this meant. The next day, the family lost their daughter, suspecting she had fled, and searched for her everywhere, but found her body. Her brother was furious and went to the temple, slapping the deity's face countless times, and with his fingers and claws, he shattered the face of the deity. The people of Lingfeng heard about the incident and sculpted a statue of the daughter, calling her "Madame." Until today, women who are pregnant or giving birth always pray to her, and the incense and fire are very prosperous. On a certain day in February, it is Madame's birthday, and red skirts and green hair are seen everywhere, yet the marks of the deity's face and claws could not be removed, even after multiple re-sculptings, the scars remained as they were.

In Hangzhou, there lived an old man named Xu, who resided in Shuigou Lane near Qingbo Gate. Every year at the end of the year, he would offer a pig's head, a chicken, and a fish to the gods, which were traditionally known as the "three sacrificial animals." After the offerings were removed, they would always disappear. Initially, he suspected that the servants had stolen them, or that cats and dogs had eaten them. However, it didn't make sense for everything to be completely lost, and it shouldn't happen every year. One year, after the ritual was completed, Old Xu personally placed the offerings in a bamboo basket and hung it from the beam. He set up his bed beside it and lay down to watch. After the hour of man, there was a sudden sound of tiles and bricks coming from the kitchen. Soon, a person came slowly and deliberately, with a prominent head and horns, resembling the head of a cow carved in the temple. The person wore the basket on his head and left. Old Xu followed the trail slowly and found that the person had gone to the kitchen and disappeared. He marked the spot with an object. The next day, he gathered his family to dig at the marked spot. They did not dig very deep, but soon saw steps. Old Xu suspected that there might be hidden treasures inside and went in to see.

At the end of the steps, a faint light suddenly appeared, and the path twisted and turned forward. The buildings stood in orderly rows, and a lacquer lamp still glowed dimly, its light seemingly emanating from there. Proceeding further, they found a red coffin suspended by a thick rope, with burial objects arranged on both sides. Slowly, they took one of the bronze wine vessels and hid it in their chest, still lingering in the place, hoping to find something more. Suddenly, the door of the left room opened, and a figure with a cow's head emerged from inside. The person ran in extreme fear, and the cow-headed figure followed, stopping at the steps. The crowd helped the old man out, immediately covering the site with soil. Looking at the old man, his face was ashen, and he was still sweating profusely. They reached into their chest and pulled out the bronze wine vessel, which had an ancient and dark color. When planted with flowers, they all came to life, and the vessel could even bear fruit—indeed, it was not a recent object. This must be an ancient tomb from the Tang or Song dynasty, but the cow-headed figure was an unknown monster. The *Zhou Li* records the duties of the *fangxiangshi* (ritual official): "Upon reaching the tomb, entering the grave, striking the four corners with a halberd, to ward off the *fangliang* (evil spirits)." Could this cow-headed figure be the *fangliang* mentioned in the text?

Zhang, a native of Shaoxing, took his wife and children to Hangzhou to rent a house. His wife fell ill frequently, and Zhang suspected that she was not adapting to the local climate and environment. He had a doctor treat her. One night, she suddenly spoke in the Hangzhou dialect and told Zhang, "This is an old woman's house. How can you live here?" Zhang knew something was amiss and showed her the lease agreement, saying, "This is what I rented." She replied, "Even though the house is rented by you, there are hidden treasures (gold and silver) below. You might steal my wealth. What shall I do?" Zhang was tempted by her words and said, "You speak through my wife, so you must be a ghost. The treasures are things used by people in the world, and you have no use for them. Why keep them? It would be better if you show them to me. I will get your wealth and make many Buddhist rituals for you, so both of us will benefit."

Otherwise, if hidden underground, even after a hundred years, it would not be used, which is very pointless." He said, "Your words are also reasonable, but you must consider your son as my son, and only then will I entrust my hidden treasure to him." Zhang happily called his son over, and the son knelt before the bed. Zhang's wife patted him twice and then spoke in Hangzhou dialect, saying, "The hidden treasure is right under the bed." Following her words, they dug and found a jar, tightly sealed. Upon opening it, there were only three foreign coins and a few hairpins inside. When they told her, she said, "The old woman is forgetful and has already forgotten it, so it must not be here; it should be under the threshold." They dug again and found two jars, one filled with clear water and the other containing countless rusted iron nails. When they told her again, she continued to nag in Hangzhou dialect, accusing Zhang of defrauding her hidden gold. Zhang had no choice but to exchange all the previously obtained items for paper money and burn them. Despite this, the situation remained troublesome, so he rented another house and moved. Only then did his wife's illness finally subside.

Wu Mei Xian said: Her friend Jin, after his death, appeared through the family's female servant to cry and report to his mother, saying, "My life span was not supposed to end here; I only died due to a mediocre doctor. According to the laws of the underworld, anyone whose life span has not ended is placed in the City of Wrongful Death, where the cold winds and fog are too terrible to describe. Fortunately, if you burn a lot of paper money for me, perhaps you can buy another ghost to take my place." She asked, "What if the supervisor notices?" He replied, "Give six silver dollars to the supervisor every day, and there will be no trouble. The other ghosts are also hired by the supervisor." She asked, "Are there silver dollars in the underworld too?" He replied, "Everything in the world of the living also exists in the underworld. Just burn a lot of paper money, and you can obtain them one by one." Her mother agreed, and the female servant then woke up, but she had no memory of what happened.

In the western gate of Haiyan, there lived a man surnamed Wu who was rich in wealth. He had a maid named Autumn Flower, who was twenty years old and quite beautiful. In the fourth month of the year 1889 during the reign of Emperor Guangxu, a handsome man suddenly came and took advantage of her. Afterward, he told the maid, "Tell your master to clean and prepare the back room for me tomorrow, and arrange the household items beautifully. If anything is missing or not luxurious enough, disaster will come immediately." The maid informed her mistress, and soon the entire household knew about it. The old man, Wu, thought it was a concubine and did not believe it. The next afternoon, a sedan chair was brought into his door and stopped in the courtyard. The sedan chair bearer opened the curtain, but there was no one inside. He was shocked and said, "I was at the eastern gate, and a young man rented my sedan chair, ordering it to be carried to your house. Where is he now? Could he be a ghost?" He described the man as being in his twenties, wearing a silk robe, holding a white fan, with an elegant and handsome appearance. His speech was that of someone from the upper class, not speaking the local dialect. The old man knew something was strange and went inside to inquire, but the maid had already disappeared. In the old man's room, there was a bed with golden hooks and green curtains, extremely luxurious, but it also disappeared instantly. He then looked into the back room, where sand and stones were flying around, and people could not approach.

After a while, a maid came out from inside and conveyed the young man's message: "Order the master to call a female servant to do the new bride's hair; also, prepare a lavish feast for one table and two secondary tables; additionally, prepare four strings of money for rewards. 'If you do not follow my words, I will burn your house!'" The old man, fearing the threat, prepared everything as instructed. However, all the fine clothes and utensils in the house were taken away, even those carefully stored away. The next day, the maid relayed another message: "Follow the custom, feed the bride for three days, and besides the wine and food, there should be various entertainments to accompany the drinking. On the first day, it will be ten drum performances; on the second day, oral performances; and on the third day, a blind woman will perform narrative songs." The old man reluctantly complied. Someone who was curious asked to see the new bride, and the maid did not refuse. They saw only the maid, who was elaborately dressed and sitting at the head of the bed. The room was filled with luxurious decorations, dazzling with colors, but they saw nothing else. From then on, something was needed every day, and every few days, the maid would leave in a sedan chair, and those who carried her could only hear her voice but not see her form. The old man Wu was greatly troubled by this and intended to send someone to report to the Taoist priest's office in Jiangxi. One day, the young man suddenly told the maid, "I will be leaving now. Everything in the room is still here. If you remember our marital affection, do not move anything within one month; if I do not return within one month, it will be our final farewell." After saying this, he left. The old man Wu immediately moved the items to another place, called the maid to marry her off, and after that, the young man never returned.

To the south of Hai Ning Xiaoshi Town, there is a place called Da Hong Village. In this village, there was a man named Wu, who was originally a farmer's son. Later, due to drought and flood, his fields became barren, and he gave up farming to pursue a profit of one-tenth, which meant he engaged in trade and was often away for months. His wife, Chen, though a farmer's daughter, had quite a beautiful appearance. The young men in the neighborhood all admired her, but Chen was virtuous and pure, and there was no way to take advantage of her. She had a younger sister-in-law who was of average appearance, with a frowning face and crooked teeth, and she made various alluring gestures, deceiving many. She was cunning and skilled at slander, often speaking ill of her sister-in-law in front of their mother, who believed her, thus Chen always felt depressed and eventually developed an illness. Every time the illness struck, she would feel intense pain in her heart, her limbs would feel as cold as ice, and she would not feel warm all day. Only when she slept at night would the pain subside, and her body would feel warm. She found this strange. One night, after waking up, she felt someone lying beside her and was greatly frightened. She pushed the pillow aside and got up, but then she was gone. Terrified and at a loss, she untied her belt and was about to hang herself. Suddenly, she saw a young girl folding her sleeves and smiling at her, saying, "Are you a person? Or a ghost?" The girl replied, "I am neither a person nor a ghost. In fact, I am a fox. I have been massaging you for four nights. If you continue for three more nights, your illness will be completely cured."

Tchen saw her with long eyebrows and a graceful neck, her smile charming and radiant, and he loved her greatly. He sat with her and asked where she came from. She replied, "I am also of the Wu surname, my pet name is Xi Xi, and I used to live in the temple of the Tide God in Hai Ning. Now that the temple has collapsed, our family has moved elsewhere. I have a predestined connection with my sister, so I came to accompany you." From then on, the woman did not leave, and only Tchen could see her. The younger sister heard her sister-in-law talking casually with someone and suspected they had a secret affair, so she told their mother. The mother became angry and went with a stick in hand, first peeping through a crack in the window. Tchen was sitting alone under the lamp, sewing old clothes, and there was no one else present. The mother thought the woman had deceived her and was about to go back to ask her, but when she reached her room, she saw the woman was indeed engaging in intimate behavior with the neighbor's son, the situation being very indecent. The mother was extremely furious, rushed in, and raised her stick to beat them, but the neighbor's son still held the woman tightly. The woman cried out, and the mother feared the noise would be heard outside, so she suppressed her anger and walked out. The woman cried and cursed all night, unable to understand the reason. It turned out that the woman was indeed alone that night, and Xi Xi transformed herself to humiliate the neighbor's son, but from then on, no one could explain what happened. After living together for more than a month, Xi Xi suddenly bid farewell to Tchen and said, "The young master will arrive tomorrow." The next day, Wu indeed arrived, and Tchen recounted the events to him. Wu wanted to see her, and Tchen said, "She promised to return in about a month, so let's wait for her to come and discuss it."

After a full month, Chen Fang sat alone, and Xixi arrived, greeting each other with smiles and laughter as before. She told him that her husband wanted to see him, but he was unwilling. She repeated it several times, and he finally agreed to meet once on the side of the road. He warned him not to have other thoughts, and if he played a prank, then he would sever ties with his sister forever. Chen solemnly promised. On that day, Wu returned from outside, and suddenly saw a young lady sitting in the branches of a large tree by the roadside. She wore a light green blouse and a pale blue skirt, her silk stockings and embroidered shoes barely fitting her delicate feet. She smiled at Wu, radiating a hundred charms. Wu was overjoyed, hurried forward, and was about to climb the tree. Suddenly, dust flew into his eyes, causing such pain that he couldn't open them. He covered his face and returned home, explaining the reason to his wife. Chen Fang blamed Wu for being reckless. Suddenly, a voice from the air said, "We will sever ties forever!" Listening to the voice, it was Xixi. Chen pulled Wu down, and both knelt to apologize, crying and begging him to stay. She said, "Fate is fixed and cannot be forced, but we can meet once more in February next year." After speaking, the voice fell silent. It was the eighth month of the year Xianfeng Jiwu (1859). By the second month of the year Gengshen (1860), Hangzhou fell, and bandits roamed openly in the countryside. Wu went out and did not return, and Chen was already pregnant. In the neighboring village, there was a man named Mr. A, who had no good conduct and practiced dark arts, often taking the fetuses of pregnant women to use as medicine.

For a long time, I had heard of Chen Mei, and I knew she was pregnant. During the day, he brought three people into her room with the intention of seducing her and causing her to miscarry. Chen was terrified and almost died. Suddenly, they all ran back out, knelt down, and threw themselves to the ground. They then hit their own cheeks with their hands, causing their cheeks to swell and their mouths to bleed. The neighbors gathered to watch. Jia and others swore they would never offend Chen again and promised to protect Wu, ensuring she would not suffer any other harm. The crowd also interceded on her behalf, and she was finally helped up. She returned home and was gravely ill for several months, but later, they all became more restrained. That night, Chen dreamed that Xixi came to her and said, "Today's matter, I saved my sister. God has made me good and chaste and appointed me as a scribe in the Palace of Green Clouds. From now on, I will truly be separated from my sister." During the provincial examination in Zhejiang in the year Yimao of Emperor Xianfeng, in the first round, a scholar from Shaoxing wrote a quatrain on his scroll: "I have searched many times with my embroidered shoes. We met in the past, and our unfinished business. Do you remember the red railing on a moonlit night? We secretly enjoyed a branch of spring on the rooftop." People asked him why, but he shook his head and said nothing. On the tenth day at dawn, he handed in his scroll and left. My disciple Gao Haiyu sat in the same room with him. The day before, we discussed literature and were very compatible. His three essays were all finished, and they were very good. But at night, there was this strange occurrence—it must be someone who has stumbled in the land of pleasure. Many books talk about the retribution in the examination hall, and there are many similar cases. This is what Haiyu actually saw.

Gao Guishan from Renhe, along with his brother Xiaoluo, studied in the Ge Lin Garden by West Lake. Their neighbor was the temple of Liang Wen Zhuang Gong, which housed dozens of coffins belonging to other families. One night, they heard the sound of recitation outside. They opened the door to look, but saw no one. When they closed the door, the sound resumed. They laughed and said, "If there is a spirit reciting, why don't you come and meet us?" After a long while, the sound disappeared. Several nights later, they heard it again, coming from the southwest and gradually reaching the window. Upon careful listening, they realized it was the sound of sighing and lamenting, not recitation. Suddenly, a loud sound echoed, and their door opened by itself. The two of them were startled and looked around, thinking a ghost had come, but they actually saw nothing. When they lay down to sleep, they both dreamed of an old man who said, "I am Qian from Yue Dong, an old scholar. I came to Zhejiang to make a living and died here. Tomorrow, there will be someone wearing a blue cloth coat at the foot of the broken bridge; he is my son. My coffin is resting under the east veranda inside the Liang family temple. Please help me show him the way." Upon waking, they recounted their dreams, which were the same, and were both startled. The next day, they went to the White Causeway to wait for the person. Before reaching the broken bridge, indeed, a person came as described in the dream. They greeted him and asked, "Are you of the surname Qian?" The man was surprised and asked, "How do you know me?" They told him about the dream. The man was indeed searching for his father's coffin. They led him to the Liang Gong temple, pointed out the location, and gave him a generous gift to send him back.

Huang Gui Pu Pei Rong was from Kuaiji, Zhejiang Province, and served as the Tongzhi (equivalent to a prefect) of Wuchang Prefecture in Hubei Province at the end of the Daoguang era. In the second year of the Xianfeng era, the Cantonese rebels arrived in Hubei, and the city fell. Huang Gui Pu Pei Rong dressed in his official robes and sat in the main hall, cursing the rebels and seeking death. The rebels captured him, along with one concubine and two sons, and took them aboard a boat. Huang Gui Pu Pei Rong found an opportunity to drown himself in the river, while his concubine and sons all fled to Nanjing. His eldest son, Qi Xun, was about eleven or twelve years old, and was secretly carried away by a villager and escaped to Suzhou. Huang Gui Pu Pei Rong had a younger brother, Jinxi Cishi, who was stationed in Suzhou and raised Qi Xun. His concubine was imprisoned in a women's quarters by the rebels, and after a long time, she was released and lived in the countryside, supporting herself by sewing. His youngest son was still a child, and unable to support him, a neighbor village woman asked to adopt him, and she reluctantly agreed. She secretly stitched the characters 'Tian Ci' (Heaven's Gift) on the boy's left shoulder and tearfully gave him to the woman. Tian Ci was the boy's milk name. When the government forces recaptured Nanjing, Qi Xun had grown up, and he went to Nanjing to find his mother, found her, and brought her home. In the fourth year of the Guangxu era, Qi Xun was involved in a maritime transport mission and drowned in Tianjin. Jinxi Cishi, grieving over the lack of descendants for Huang Gui Pu Pei Rong, his concubine mentioned the story of Tian Ci, and he wrote a letter to Governor Jiangning Jiang Hezhuang to search for him. He found the mother and son, summoned them to the office, undressed the boy, and checked his left shoulder, where the two strokes of the character '天' were still visible, and the '贝' side of the character '赐' was also not completely erased.

Then he told the village woman, "This is the son of the Huang family." He ruled in favor of assigning it to the Huang family. Guipu sacrificed his life for the country, and it was indeed fitting that he should have descendants, and his concubine's wisdom and integrity were also worthy of praise. This story was told to me by Du Xiaofang, a high-ranking official. Xiaofang also said: When Qi Xun drowned, his body was lost, and after searching for several days, it could not be found. Xiaofang's younger brother, Xiaoshan, the governor of Jiangsu, who was then the head of the maritime bureau in Tianjin, ordered a spirit tablet made of paper, with the official position and name written on it, placed in a wooden basin, and utensils for food and drink set up beside it. They floated the basin in the Tianjin estuary and sent someone to row a small boat to follow it. When it first entered the water, the basin moved northward against the current. When the small boat was untied, the basin immediately flowed downstream. After traveling about two miles, it reached the front of Baolin Temple, where the wooden basin suddenly came ashore by itself, and the body had already emerged from the water. Although its appearance had changed, its clothing remained intact. Then they buried it with great respect and requested preferential treatment from the court, which they received. Xiaofang asked his brother where he learned this method, and he replied, "In the seventh month of the eighth year of the Xianfeng era, there was a local official from Zhili whose name I have forgotten. He received an order to escort the unloading of southern grain transport ships. On the boat, he carried a child who soon lost his footing and fell into the water. He wanted to search for the child's body. A local person passed on this method, saying." Indeed, matters concerning spirits and deities are truly beyond understanding, but this method has been repeatedly tested and proven effective, so it is also quite remarkable. However, Qi Xun died in the line of duty, and his spirit should not be buried in the belly of a fish; perhaps there were divine beings protecting him, but that is also uncertain.

Zhou Jingshuo, a native of Nanhui County, once took the imperial examination for young scholars, had a daughter, and then passed away. At first, he showed no signs of supernatural ability. Several years later, his daughter grew up and suddenly fell gravely ill. During her illness, she claimed that her father had protected her, which is why she survived. From then on, she was able to see ghosts, and the spirit of Zhou Jingshuo would attach itself to her to speak with others, and his supernatural abilities became widely known. He claimed that he signed official documents in the afterlife and could exercise power and grant favors. There was a man named Xie Xinyu who married into the Zhou family and was a brother to Zhou Jingshuo. In the autumn of the year Ji Mao, Xie Xinyu fell seriously ill. Zhou Jingshuo's daughter went to visit him and saw many ghosts coming and going. She asked the Kitchen God about it, but the god said there were none. The daughter replied, "I have seen them with my own eyes, how can you say there are none?" The Kitchen God then investigated thoroughly and told her, "This ghost entered through bribing the door god on a certain day." That night, the spirit of Zhou Jingshuo arrived, and his family knelt in a circle to beg him for help.

Mirror Tower questioned the ghost, who claimed to be surnamed Li and named Ru Kui, from Zhejiang. Eight generations ago, she was a daughter of the Wang family and a wife of the Pan family, while Xin She, eight generations ago, was a son of a certain family, and they had an affair. Afterward, she became pregnant, took an abortion pill, and died. Though the years have passed, the resentment remains. After generations of searching, she finally meets him today and must take his life before she will leave. Mirror Tower tried to persuade her repeatedly and agreed to have a Taoist priest recite scriptures. The ghost refused. Mirror Tower angrily said, "Don't you know who I am, Zhou Mirror Tower? Can you escape from my grasp?" The ghost finally obeyed and said, "If the Taoist priest recites scriptures, I wish to have the *Dafa Jing* recited. If the *Yuhuang Jing*, it is the lowest level of land in the underworld, and I do not wish to obtain it." The Xie family followed her words, and the illness was cured. Mirror Tower then mentioned that there is a person named Gu Chun Chuan, who passed the imperial examination in the year Wu Zi, and has already become a magistrate in the underworld, having been recommended by him. In this way, it is clear that in the underworld, things are just as they are in the human world.

In a cycle of sixty years, there is only one instance where the year, month, day, and hour all share the same Heavenly Stem and Earthly Branch. Specifically, in the year Jia Xu (甲戌), the month, day, and hour are also Jia Xu; in the year Yi You (乙酉), the month, day, and hour are also Yi You; in the year Bing Shen (丙申), the month, day, and hour are also Bing Shen; in the year Ding Wei (丁未), the month, day, and hour are also Ding Wei; in the year Wu Wu (戊午), the month, day, and hour are also Wu Wu; in the year Ji Si (己巳), the month, day, and hour are also Ji Si; in the year Geng Chen (庚辰), the month, day, and hour are also Geng Chen; in the year Xin Mao (辛卯), the month, day, and hour are also Xin Mao; in the year Ren Yin (壬寅), the month, day, and hour are also Ren Yin; and in the year Gui Hai (癸亥), the month, day, and hour are also Gui Hai. However, while the year Jia Xu must have a Jia Xu month and a Jia Xu day, the month Jia Xu does not necessarily have a Jia Xu day, and similarly for the others. Therefore, encountering this phenomenon is difficult. On the 13th day of the third month of the sixth year of the Guangxu era, the hour of Chen, it was the year, month, day, and hour of Geng Chen. In Hangzhou, there was an old man named Yang who was skilled in the study of "concentrated celestial phenomena." After his death, when his burial was being arranged, an astrologer selected this year, month, day, and hour for the burial, taking the four Geng Chen. Other astrologers believed it was not suitable and suggested using the 6th day of the month, Gui You. The family, knowing that the old man was particularly skilled in this art, consulted his spirit before his tomb and drew lots to decide. They ended up drawing the four Geng Chen and proceeded with the burial. A friend named Ding Songsheng participated in the funeral procession and later passed by Yu Lou, where he recounted this story to me, and I recorded it.

The Southern Dipper records births, and the Northern Dipper records deaths; this saying has existed since ancient times, indicating that there are indeed records in the unseen realm. In the world, whenever disasters of water, fire, weapons, and warfare occur, people often dream of writing ledgers in the underworld, which seems to be a natural phenomenon. At the beginning of the Xianfeng era, the Taiping Rebellion began to take root. There was a man from Haiyan named Zha, who dreamed of arriving at a place where documents were piled up like mountains, and dozens of people were writing, as if they could not keep up. On the pillars hung a couplet that read: "Even the delicate willow and jade flute are subject to fate," and "Even the falling flowers and bronze drums lack divine intervention." The meaning was elusive, but the language was very beautiful to recite.

A woman from the Yi family in Huanggang County transformed into a man, and I have already recorded this in the first volume. A servant named Song, who works for a family in LanYi County, Henan Province, recounted that a woman from a certain family in the countryside had already been betrothed. The old man of that family had no son and was naturally inclined to do good. One day, his daughter fell ill for several days and suddenly transformed into a boy. She told her parents, who verified the truth of the transformation. They then sent a messenger to inform her future husband's family. The father-in-law of the family said, "This is a reward for this old man's good deeds. I have a weak daughter, and I wish to marry her to him. The relatives will not lose their kinship, which is also a good thing!" The old man was very pleased and continued the marriage between the two families. Later, they had children, but the mother remained frail. The father took on the tasks of making clothes and sewing, as he had previously practiced needlework. Even though he was now a man, he had not forgotten his old skills. The game of *yapai* (牙牌), a traditional Chinese card game, is said to have originated during the reign of Emperor Huizong of the Song Dynasty and has a long history. In recent times, there is a book called *Yapai Shu* (《牙牌数》) that uses the game for divination, similar to ancient methods of divination using chess pieces and dice. My nephew, Fuqing, mentioned that during the Jiangnan provincial examination in 1899, the year of Gengyin under the Guangxu era, several scholars from Wuxi consulted the *Yapai Shu* before the results were announced. The divination said, "A large hunting ground is opened, and a deer is shot but turns out to be a roe deer. Looking around with self-satisfaction, the result will definitely be a double win." That year, two scholars from Wuxi passed the examination: one was surnamed Gu, and the other was surnamed Zhang. The character "Gu" was clearly visible in the divination, while the phrase "射鹿得獐" (a deer is shot but turns out to be a roe deer) subtly hinted at the surname "Zhang," making the prediction particularly coincidental.

A Military Governor from Jiangsu, his name and title were not mentioned by those who spoke of him. During his temporary assignment in central Zhejiang, his lodging frequently experienced strange occurrences. One day, he lost three foreign coins, and just as he was questioning what had happened, a voice suddenly came from the air, saying, "I borrowed them, and I will return them in three days." After three days, he indeed found two of the coins. He asked about the whereabouts of the third, and the voice replied, "This one will be returned in a day." The next day, he found it. Afterwards, there were frequent borrowings, some of which were repaid and others not, but the form of the borrower was never seen. One day, the Military Governor was sitting in front of his office when he suddenly saw a figure passing under his eaves, its form twisting and serpentine, almost ten feet long, and he suspected it was a snake spirit. His daughter, a noblewoman, was embroidering in the upstairs room. In the morning, she noticed that a face had been added to the blank space of her embroidery, which she found strange and showed to others. The voice from the air said, "This is what I have done, it is my image. You all wish to recognize my appearance, look here." The face was indistinguishable as male or female, with only two horns on top, the face being white, and the horns yellow. The rest, such as the mouth and nose, were still somewhat similar to humans.

To this day, this image still remains in the Sima family. When Sima Qian tried to avoid it, it followed him wherever he went, and it is unknown what its ultimate fate is. During the Xianfeng era, Fu Nanqiao, a scholar-official from Jiangdu, was the headmaster of the Zhe Lin Academy in Songjiang, and he played the game of spirit writing with his fellow scholars. A female immortal descended to the altar, identifying herself as a person from the Tang Dynasty, surnamed Han and named Bixia, whose father was the governor of Suzhou. At that time, Jiangsu was still under the control of rebels, and they asked when the rebels would be defeated. The spirit writing produced a poem, which included two lines: "Clearing away the clouds to see the blue sky, it is the year when pigs are offered in Liaodong." Later, the rebels were defeated in the year 1863, which matched the phrase about the year of pigs. Additionally, during the Xianfeng era, Mr. Jiuzhi, the father-in-law of Lu Fengshi, played the game of spirit writing and asked about Lu Fengshi's success in the imperial examinations. The spirit writing produced a seven-character quatrain, with the final line reading: "Bowing on the golden steps when the curtain is drawn." Later, Lu Fengshi won the highest honor in the imperial examinations in the year 1874. That year, Emperor Tongzhi personally took charge, and the Empress Dowager withdrew her regency. The spirit writing's phrase "drawing the curtain" indeed had foresight. It is said that mountain spirits know the affairs of a year, and this seems to go beyond just one year.

In Kunshan County, there is a place named Xiadun. In the summer of the fourth year of the Xianfeng era, a village boy surnamed Jin, aged fifteen, was paddling a small boat to release ducks into the lake. As he was passing through the reeds, suddenly dark clouds gathered, and a fierce wind roared, with white waves as high as mountains. Both the boy and the boat were carried into the air by the wind. Looking down, the clouds were so thick that the ground was not visible. He thought to himself that if he were to fall, he would surely be crushed. Suddenly, an old man appeared, wearing a yellow robe, with white hair and beard, holding a fly whisk in his hand and pointing, saying, "Go, go!" Both the boy and the boat fell from the air and landed safely on the ground, unharmed, three miles from his home. People gathered around and all marveled at the strange event. They pulled the boat back into the water and returned it to his home. In front of the Kaiyuan Temple in Suzhou, there are two large stone lions. After many years, they became quite famous for their supernatural powers. Inside the Pan Gate, the area is very remote and desolate, and the residents mostly make a living from vegetable gardens. One night after three o'clock, the guard saw something eating vegetables in the garden. It had a large shape, but it was unknown what it was. He quickly returned and called everyone to see, but it had already disappeared. Subsequently, this happened every night. They then gathered everyone to watch for it. At midnight, the creature indeed appeared, and they suddenly rushed out to chase it. The creature ran wildly away and stopped in front of the temple. Upon closer inspection, it was the stone lion. They then cast large iron nails and nailed it, and since then, it no longer came out at night.

Zhu Xianglin, a native of Suzhou, was naturally carefree and unrestrained, and loved to pursue beautiful women. One day, he drank at a friend's house and returned late at night when there were no people on the road. He reached Hulong Street and saw a young woman walking alone. He glanced at her beauty and found her attractive. He followed her from behind and gently teased her with words. The woman smiled and beckoned to him with her hand. Greatly pleased, he followed her. They arrived at a place where the woman pushed open the door and led Zhu inside. The house was not very large, but it had luxurious furnishings, and they entered the room together. The room was adorned with mirrors, makeup boxes, and cosmetics, all of which were exquisite. On the bed were luxurious curtains, embroidered quilts, and brocade bedding, even more splendid. The woman undressed and placed her clothes on a stand, leaving only a simple garment, revealing her pure and fair skin. She urged Zhu to sleep first. Zhu's heart was stirred, and he couldn't control himself, just as he was about to climb onto the bed. Suddenly, bright firelight appeared, and several people surrounded him, holding lanterns with the words of Suzhou City God. In a moment, all the people and objects disappeared, and the location was near a stone railing outside Fan Zhuang, close to the water. Terrified, he stood there in a daze. Just then, a military official passed by at night, knowing that Zhu was bewitched by ghosts, and took him back home. Such a scene must have been created by ghostly illusions. If not for the divine light illuminating, he would have stumbled in the land of pleasure. How can one not be afraid!

The Office of the Dengzhou, Laizhou, and Qingzhou Governors in Shandong Province had two wisteria plants, which had existed for several hundred years. The vines twisted and coiled, shading an area of several acres. When the flowers bloomed, they were purple and scattered in beauty, partially hiding the doors and windows, with a fragrant scent wafting over people. It was said that the tree had a spirit, and when the wind was clear and the moon was bright, it was often seen. Among the people who were sick, obtaining one flower or one leaf could treat their ailments, and because of this, people came to pray and worship, with the incense and candles never stopping. In the office, there were several empty rooms, and when the flowers fell, they were always swept and placed in the rooms to provide for the requests of the sick. All new officials entering the office must worship it. Zhongcheng Pan WeiRu, who once held this position, followed custom and offered sacrifices, and poured a large jar of wine over the roots. That night, both flower spirits appeared: one was an old man with a ruddy complexion and white hair, and a long beard down to the belly; the other was a woman. All the people in the office saw them. That year, the flowers bloomed especially profusely.

A Suzhou man named Jiang Shaofu and his friend Shi went to Tianzhu Temple in Hangzhou to burn incense and pay their respects. After completing the ritual, they began their return journey. As they reached the foot of Wansong Hill, Shi suddenly felt abdominal pain and relieved himself on the roadside. Suddenly, a hawk swooped down from the sky and snatched his hat away. They quickly tried to snatch it back, but the hawk flew further and further away, high into the clouds, disappearing from sight. The two men were greatly frightened and staggered back to their boat. By evening, Shi's illness began, with a headache and fever that were quite unbearable. At midnight, he suddenly dreamed of entering a city. He asked the people in the city what place this was, and someone told him, "It is Shengxian County in Shaoxing." Soon, he arrived at a temple, where there was a very high stele inscribed with the two large characters "Shen Zhuang." To his great delight, his lost hat was clearly visible on the stele. He reached out to take it, but a monk beside him stopped him, saying, "Cannot, cannot! Wait four years to come and take it." Then he woke up. The next day, his illness did not decrease much. Jiang had an old acquaintance with Wang Mengwei, my disciple, who was skilled in medicine. Because of this, he ordered Shi to rush to see Wang, and then described his dream. Wang diagnosed his pulse, saying it was just a common cold, nothing else, and gave him a prescription. He hurriedly took his leave, saying, "I will return to Suzhou today." It was not known what would happen in the end.

I was ill due to a cold, and when I had abdominal pain, the root of the illness was already present. The hawk seizing the hat happened to meet its suitability. Since there was this event, there was this dream, and dreams are created by the mind, which has nothing to do with the illness. A man named Mr. A from Lumu Village in Suzhou found an abandoned child on the road during a time of military turmoil and raised him as his own son. When he grew up, he married him a wife. Mr. A and his wife initially did not know who the child's parents were, and the child did not know that he had other parents. In the sixth year of the Guangxu era, Mr. A's wife fell gravely ill, died, and then revived. She called her son and told him, "I saw your mother begging to take back her child in the afterlife. I have already agreed to it. Your mother is a certain lady, your father is still alive, his surname is a certain one, his name is a certain one, and he lives in a certain place in Suzhou. You should take your wife back with you, so as not to make me break my promise to your mother." After saying this, she died. The son thought this was a disorderly command and did not believe it. The next day, a man from Suzhou arrived, and his name was the same as what Mr. A's wife had said, and he reclaimed his child. Mr. A asked, "The matter is more than twenty years old. Why are you suddenly reclaiming him?" The man said, "The deceased wife appeared in a dream at night and said that the child I lost in the past is in your home. I accepted the permission, so I came here, and I wish to take the child back with me." Mr. A asked where the man lived, and it also matched. Then he told his son, "This is truly your father. I am fortunate to already have a son, so you can take your wife back with you." The son then wept and thanked him before leaving.

At the former site of the Marbles Temple in West Lake, Hangzhou, there stands a large stone that overlooks the lake, known as the Eagle Stopping Stone. A monk from a certain temple intended to engrave the Diamond Sutra on this stone, so he built a wooden canopy beside the stone and gathered craftsmen within it, planning to begin work the next day. Suddenly, a man dressed in a black robe and wearing a blue half-sleeve arrived at the canopy and chatted with the craftsmen. He asked, "What brings you all here?" They explained their purpose. The man said, "When you start carving the stone, you should inform me first. If you do not inform me, you will surely regret it." The craftsmen then inquired about his residence, and he replied, "It is not far from the stone." On the next day, as they were about to begin the engraving, the craftsmen searched everywhere for the man but could not find him. Someone mentioned this in the tea pavilion of the Pine Wood Field, and a listener said, "There are no residents near the stone, could it be the turtle spirit?" They were startled and asked, "This stone has an old turtle spirit beneath it. People often see it, and it is as large as a five-stone gourd. It does not harm humans. Now, if you engrave the sutra on top of it, it will be suppressed and lose its freedom to move in and out. This is why it does not want it. There are many stones in the hills north and south of West Lake, and there must be many suitable ones for engraving. Why choose this one? Why not discuss it again with the monk?" The craftsmen were half-believing and half-doubting. That day, they did not proceed with the engraving. The next day, some of the craftsmen suffered from eye problems, while others had arm problems. They realized something was amiss and told the monk, who then decided to engrave the sutra elsewhere.

A certain man, a farmer, once caused someone's death by disputing over land. Later, he harvested rice on the very land that was the subject of the dispute. Suddenly, a thunderstorm came, and the farmer called out to his young son to return home with him. The boy followed his father. They saw a noisy crowd of people and horses in the air, about several dozen in number, some holding torches in their hands, others carrying an object like a fishing net. When they got closer, two people dressed in green took away the rice his father had harvested. Several more people then covered his father's head with a net, and the torch immediately struck down from below. A thunderclap, and his son fainted in shock. When he got up and looked, his father was dead. The people he had seen before all turned into clouds and soared into the sky and left.

A Scholar from Tongxiang Named Zhou Tiyan Zhou Tiyan, a young scholar from Tongxiang, served as a student in a Confucian academy but repeatedly failed the autumn imperial examinations. One day, returning from another place, he anchored his boat in a village area at night. He saw a house by the water with a building that looked like wings, and outside the window, there was green fire spinning unpredictably. Surprised, he called the boatman to ask about it. The boatman, seeing the phenomenon, was terrified and said, "This is often a sign of a ghost seeking a substitute, and I have seen it many times. This family must have someone who is about to hang themselves. Be careful not to make a sound, for if the ghost is discovered by people, it will transfer the misfortune to someone else." Zhou, moved by the situation, said, "To see someone die and not save them is not worthy of being a man!" He then went ashore, knocked on the door, and shouted loudly. The family came out to ask what was happening, and he explained the situation. They were greatly surprised, as it turned out that the mother-in-law and daughter-in-law were quarreling, and the daughter-in-law, crying, had gone to the top of the building. The mother-in-law was still arguing with her. Hearing Zhou's words, they quickly went up the stairs, pushed open the door, and entered. The woman was standing in front of the bed, holding a rope, and her mind was already in a daze. They called to her for a long time before she finally realized what was happening. The whole family comforted her together, and she calmed down. The next day, Zhou returned home and had a dream where an old man told him, "You are courageous in doing good, and you should receive your reward." Zhou replied, "How can this be considered good? If one must receive a reward, I do not dare to hope for anything else, but I dare to ask you about my career in the imperial examinations." The old man laughed and showed him his palm, which had the three characters "何可成" (How can it succeed). Upon waking, Zhou sighed and said, "There is no hope for my career in the imperial examinations!" The following year, he unexpectedly made the list of successful candidates. The chief examiner of that examination was Mr. He, and only then did Zhou realize the coincidence between his dream and reality.

In the year of Tongzhi, the second year of the ox, Zhejiang province held a supplementary provincial examination. That summer, a person from Sheng County dreamed of arriving at a temple, where there was a deity seated facing south. Around the deity were many clerks and officials, each carrying a tablet and presenting it before the deity. The deity wrote on the tablet, and then each carried it out. The dreamer followed and asked, "What is this tablet?" The person carrying the tablet replied, "The names on the tablet are all those who passed the provincial examination this year." The dreamer saw a tablet with the large characters "Qiu Zhicheng" written on it, and there were several lines of small print beside it. The dreamer asked what the small print meant, and the person replied, "These are all the good deeds done secretly by that person." Upon waking, the dreamer went to find out and discovered that indeed there was a person named Qiu Zhicheng among the scholars in the county. He went to tell him about the dream. Qiu smiled and said, "I have not taken the examination for over ten years; my energy and spirit have declined, and my learning has become neglected. I believe this life will never see me in the examination hall again, and I fear I cannot fulfill your dream." The dreamer then told all of Qiu's relatives and friends, and they all encouraged him. He then took the examination and was successful. Someone asked Qiu what good deeds he had done, and Qiu said he did not know. When pressed, he said, "Last year, a servant in my clan was about to be put to death, and I saved her and arranged for her marriage. This may be one good deed, but I do not know of any others." It is said that unintentional good deeds, even if the doer is unaware, are recorded by the gods.

The Prince of Kuaiji, Xiangzhou Jitai, was skilled in composing essays for the imperial examinations but struggled to pass them. He had a generous nature and enjoyed giving to others. One day, a villager brought a砚 to him, asking to exchange it for one thousand coins. Upon seeing the砚, he realized it was just a common stone and rejected it. The villager's face turned sad and dejected, and when asked why, he explained that his wife had just given birth, and the situation was very dangerous. He wanted to hire a midwife from Baiyang with the砚. The prince then kept the砚 and gave him the money. Later, he thought about whether the woman had given birth or not, so he sent someone to ask her family. It turned out that the woman had died, and her mother-in-law, who was old and sick, was wailing and wanting to commit suicide. The prince felt compassion and quickly took several sets of old clothes and ten pieces of foreign currency, personally delivering them to her. That year, he took the autumn examination and entered the examination hall. His seat number happened to be seventeen. When the questions were announced, ideas flowed like a spring, and he passed the examination. One night during the New Year of the Xianfeng era, he dreamed of a woman bowing to him, saying, "I am the woman who died in childbirth last year." She handed him a yellow paper strip with the words "臣十七" written in red. He woke up without understanding its meaning.

A man from Shan Yin named Ni, when young, once took the preliminary examination for scholars but did not pass and thus abandoned it. He was skilled in managing his livelihood, his family was originally wealthy, and he was extremely generous and kind in helping others, earning the reputation of a good person in his village. In the year 1873 during the Tongzhi era, he was more than fifty years old. Among his neighbors was a gentleman named Zhang, who was formerly a county magistrate in Guangdong and retired due to illness. Zhang claimed to judge matters in the afterlife, but Ni did not believe him. That spring, Zhang urged Ni to take the provincial examination. Ni laughed and declined, but Zhang kept talking about it. One day, Zhang privately said to him, "Have you forgotten the matter from fifteen years ago? Preserving one's reputation and integrity is what Heaven values. You will gain academic success for this reason. You must go, do not mislead yourself." Ni recalled that fifteen years ago, a young woman from a relative's family had her husband die and remained chaste. Her brother falsely accused her of something unfounded, planned to have her expelled and seize her property. The matter was almost completed when I worked hard to clear her name. Zhang did not know about this matter, but he began to be moved. However, he had long abandoned his studies and feared he would not meet the standards. So he first wrote more than ten essays and asked a renowned scholar to review them. When he entered the examination hall, three of the four Confucian classics questions were among them. He recorded them and left. The results were announced, and he passed. He then sighed, "Zhang indeed judges matters in the afterlife; he did not deceive me."

Wang Zongbo, a high-ranking official from Leping, used to read at home when he was young. He would secretly play cricket fighting with his peers, competing for victory. The cricket that Lord Zongbo kept had a large beak and a black body, and it always won in fights. Lord Zongbo treasured it greatly, closing it in a cage at night and placing it in the courtyard to drink the clear dew. When morning came and he checked on it, he found it dead, with its two legs broken. He was deeply regretful and angry, tracing its location, and found one leg in a crevice of a stone. A group of ants were dragging it, about to enter their nest. He then realized that the cricket had been killed by the ants. Lord Zongbo was furious and thought of a way to seek revenge. He went to the market to buy firecrackers, peeled off the gunpowder, and used stinky objects to lure the ants out of their nest. He spread the gunpowder on the ground and ignited it. Thousands of ants died simultaneously, and he clapped his hands in joy. Later, he regretted it, saying, "Ah! That was too extreme!" He no longer kept crickets after that. During the year Gengwu of the Jiaqing era, Lord Zongbo was appointed as Vice President of the Imperial Academy and concurrently as Governor of the Capital and Director of Education. He was friendly with General Sa of Shengjing. One day, a commoner visited the general's gate to request an audience, and General Sa received him.

The guest bowed deeply and sat down, his appearance dignified and old-fashioned. He introduced himself, saying, "I am appointed as a ghost official while still alive, often going to the underworld to judge cases. I have come today to handle a case involving Mr. Wang, the Xueshi. This case has been unresolved for over forty years, with enemies repeatedly filing lawsuits without resolution. It is time to settle it. Mr. Wang is indeed a righteous person, and his ancestors have accumulated significant merit. Therefore, I should favor him. I know that you are good friends with Mr. Wang. Tomorrow, please visit him. If he falls ill, instruct his family not to disturb him, nor to give him medicine. Just take care of him carefully, and he will recover on his own in three days." Sa Gong was surprised by his words. The next day, he personally went to see Mr. Wang. However, Mr. Wang had suddenly fallen ill and died, causing great alarm in the office. Following the guest's advice, Sa Gong had his family keep a close watch on Mr. Wang, waiting for him to recover. Indeed, Mr. Wang fell ill for three days and then recovered. He recounted that when he fell ill, he was in a daze and unaware of what was happening. He felt someone guiding him into a public office. In the hall, there was a long official sitting facing south, wearing ancient clothing and no beard, with many attendants on either side. The long official asked Mr. Wang, "Do you know that many people are suing you?" Mr. Wang replied that he did not know. The long official then ordered the clerk to check the records and showed them to Mr. Wang, which detailed his childhood act of killing ants. Mr. Wang apologized, saying, "It is true that I did that, but I regret it now and cannot make amends."

The magistrate ordered the summoning of all litigants, and there were countless victims with burnt heads and melted faces, who cried out in a circle for vengeance. The magistrate gently advised them, saying, "Ants and crickets are both insects. You should not have bitten and eaten the crickets they kept. The child was unaware and only sought to vent his anger, failing to recognize the gravity of the situation, and thus this case arose. Now that his rank and title have been reduced and his lifespan and fortune have been decreased, he will always have imperfections in his perfection, which is also sufficient to show the punishment." The crowd shouted, "We only injured one cricket, but he has killed tens of thousands of our kind. How can this light punishment suffice?" They spoke loudly, their momentum growing fierce, nearly ready to resort to violence. The magistrate became greatly angry and said, "Wang has already received divine punishment, which is sufficient to cover his guilt. You tiny and insignificant creatures, greedily craving for animal flesh, defile the kitchens of the living and intrude into the tombs of the dead. Your crimes are too numerous to be fully punished. If you continue to make a loud noise, you will be punished according to the law!" The crowd began to fear and retreated. The magistrate said to the official, "This case is now settled. You should do more good deeds to atone for your past sins." The official then awoke. The next day, he went to thank the general. As soon as he entered, the previous day's guest emerged and met him at the door, without exchanging a word.

Sa Gong greeted and comforted him, saying, "You must have been very tired from appearing in court yesterday." The official was surprised and asked, "How did you know?" Sa Gong told him what the guest had said, and the official also recounted the events in detail, adding, "The guest I met recently was the judge in the afterlife, and I still feel a sense of awe and fear when I think about it. If it weren't for the protection of my ancestors' virtue, I doubt I would have had any hope of returning to life." He then realized that the guest was no ordinary person. The official passed the provincial examination in the third year of Qianlong's reign (1772) and was initially ranked second in the examination, but was later demoted to eighteenth place. In the third year of Jiaqing's reign (1816), after the imperial examination, the examiners submitted the top ten essays, and the official stood out as the first. The emperor, known as the Benevolent Temple, reclassified him as the second place in the first class. Later, he rose to the position of Minister of Works, and with his qualifications, he was on the verge of becoming a high-ranking official, but ultimately did not succeed. In the sixteenth year of Daoguang's reign (1836), he died in office, and the emperor, known as the Temple of the Emperor, mourned and lamented his loss, posthumously granting him the title of Grand Tutor to the Crown Prince and bestowing a funeral sacrifice and burial. According to custom, when a minister dies in office, he is eligible for a posthumous title, and the minister of rites requested it. However, when the capital region suffered from drought, the emperor worked tirelessly day and night, and this request was left pending for a long time, ultimately not receiving a posthumous title. Perhaps this is what is meant by being demoted in rank, reduced in salary, and having some imperfections in perfection? As a renowned statesman of his generation, he killed a swarm of ants, and thus suffered punishment in the afterlife. The value of life is extremely important; how can one be careless?

During the Daoguang era, there was a certain observer, a local official, who was known for his clean and quiet nature. In his spare time from official duties, he would go to his study to read. A young servant attended to him, and he would go to bed around midnight, making this his daily routine. Coinciding with the Mid-Autumn Festival, a governor-general, known for his usual corruption, would slander him if the gifts or offerings he received were unsatisfactory. The official had no choice but to pervert the law to obtain five hundred taels of silver, which he placed in his book chest to prepare for letters and writing materials. It was a high autumn night, with the moon shining brightly, and he was just about to read aloud when suddenly a gust of wind entered through the door, causing him to shiver with fear. Suddenly, he saw a woman wearing a black silk headscarf and a pink silk robe standing near the desk. While he was still startled and looking around, another man appeared behind her, with red hair, deep-set eyes, and a beard like a hedgehog's quills. He approached the official and said, "Why not use the gold you have obtained as a gift for the elderly?" The official did not respond. The woman then took out a white ball from her sleeve, played with it, and it shone brightly like a silken ribbon. The official was afraid, opened the chest, took out the gold, and placed it on the desk. The woman put all of it in her sleeve. The man then said, "Since you have always been a clean official, we have not harmed you. But this unjust gain is not something you should have. I will take it away to maintain your integrity." After saying this, he and the woman both left, disappearing suddenly. Probably an ancient swordswoman or swordsman? The official kept the matter secret and soon pretended to be ill and returned home. After returning home, he then told those close to him about it.

Behind the wall of the Nanchang Prefecture in Jiangxi Province, there stood several old houses, formerly a gunpowder depot. In the third year of the Xianfeng era (1853), the Taiping rebels besieged the city, setting up camp near Sha Jing. There was a Wen Xiang Temple in the area, which was occupied by the rebels. The temple's walls were thick and strong, and despite the city's efforts to bombard it, they could not be breached. One night, a person passing by the wall behind the old houses saw dozens of black-faced individuals emerge from the gunpowder depot, declaring their willingness to assist the government troops in fighting the rebels. The next day, when the location was investigated, no one was found, leading to the belief that these were divine beings. The authorities were informed, and upon digging up the ground, many cannons of various sizes were discovered, including thirteen weighing three thousand catties each and one weighing four thousand catties. These cannons were carried to the Zhanjiang Gate artillery platform and used to bombard the Wen Xiang Temple, destroying its walls and causing heavy casualties among the rebels, thus lifting the siege of Nanchang. The people of Nanchang called these cannons "divine cannons." Huang Yisheng, the commander of the Yangtze River Navy, brought and supported his mother-in-law in Nanjing.

The lady-in-waiting accidentally fell ill and dreamed that a divine being told her, "I was trapped in the well on Lion Street due to the banditry. If you can rescue me, I will surely repay you; do not worry about your illness." The lady-in-waiting awoke and recounted her dream. The gentleman ordered someone to dredge the well, and indeed, they found a stone statue. He asked the local people, who replied, "There used to be a Stone General Temple in Nanjing city, which was destroyed by bandits, and the statue was lost. What we found now is exactly that." The gentleman then built a temple by the well, which became quite famous for its supernatural powers. Until today, the incense continues to burn without interruption. As for the identity of the Stone General, it is unknown, but it is said to be a person from the Eastern Jin dynasty named Sima Liu. According to the *History of the Jin Dynasty: Biography of Su Jun*, Su Jun sent his generals Han Guang and Zhang Jian to attack Gusu, advancing towards Cihu, where they killed the magistrate of Yuhu, Tao Fu, and the General of Pacification, Sima Liu. He did not show any other significant achievements. Even after thousands of years, he can still be worshipped by people, which is indeed strange.

In the fourth month of the eighth year of the Tongzhi era, a well in a civilian residence on Rice Noodle Alley in Nanjing produced several bottles of oil when water was drawn in the morning. The oil would run out before water appeared, and the next morning, drawing water again produced the same result. This continued for several days, and soon the entire city, both inside and out, heard about it. The crowd of spectators filled the doorways. The family grew tired of the situation and reported it to the county magistrate, who ordered the well to be sealed. Zhu Kongzhang from Changzhou, who was staying on Anpin Street, which was not far from the location, had someone draw water from the well to verify the claim. It was indeed like sesame oil and could be used to light lamps. Cui Zheng from Liyang was extremely poor, with nothing to make a home, and lived in a Buddhist temple. One night, while reading until the third watch, he suddenly heard the sound of carriages and horses on the mountain behind the temple. He opened the back door to look but saw nothing. Soon the sound became closer, and someone knocked on the door and said, "The Small Wu King has arrived." Cui Zheng entered and realized it was not a human. He did not dare to ask questions. The visitor said, "Do not be afraid. I am from Guangxi and also read books, registered as a scholar. I was captured by bandits and brought to Jiangnan, later joining the official army and fighting under General Zhang here, where I died. Hearing that you read, I secretly admire you. There are no good companions in the mountains. I hope to hear your refined voice to slightly comfort my loneliness." The visitor continued, "The Heavenly Emperor has appointed me to rule this mountain and bestowed upon me the title of Small Wu King. I no longer think about the human world. I have only one daughter who is fifteen years old, her small name is Aluan, and she is now stranded in Guangdong and Hong Kong."

You may go there to visit her, and if you do not find it humble or unworthy, then you may consider her as your wife. After saying this, he left. Cui was surprised by this and wanted to follow his advice to visit the girl, but because the journey was far and the preparation of funds, food, and travel gear was not easily arranged, he did not succeed. After some time, the Little Wu King returned, and spoke to Cui, saying, "Are you not interested?" Cui told him the situation. He said, "You have a problem with funds, why did you not tell me earlier? There is a buried treasure of gold to the south of this mountain, you can dig it up." He then pointed out the location. Cui dug it up and obtained five thousand taels of silver, and then went to Guangdong to visit Aruan in Hong Kong, where he indeed met her in a brothel. She was still a virgin, not yet sixteen, and was still a maiden. She was graceful and beautiful, barely literate, but had been wandering since childhood and did not know her own family background. Cui redeemed her for three thousand taels of silver and brought her back home, and they became husband and wife. This story I heard from Zhu Jun Kongzhang, who in turn heard it from Zeng Rongfang.
