Another incident is said: In the year of Wu Chen, when he was in Suzhou, he dreamed of people from his hometown playing flutes and drums to worship the gods. He asked what god it was, and they said, "It was Qin Mu Gong." In his dream, he composed an offering text. Upon waking, he remembered one couplet, saying: "A male fox determined the fate of Jin, foretelling the rise of a hegemon at Han Yuan; a female pheasant brought prosperity to Qin, receiving the divine sign at Chen Bao." Recalling the *Fengchan Shu* (Book of Sacrifices to Heaven and Earth), he noted that the temple of Chen Bao was dedicated to Qin Wen Gong, and he deeply regretted the mistake in his use of events. Later, he read *Suo Yin* (Hidden Meaning), which cited *Lieyi Zhuan* (Record of Strange Events), mentioning Qin Mu Gong instead. The dream was indeed accurate.

At the northwest corner of Shangshan Mountain outside the southern city wall lies the tomb of Consort Xing. Though the stele and inscriptions are unrecorded, stone beasts still remain among the grass. According to the old stories passed down by the elders, this concubine is none other than Yuan Yuan, for whom Wu Meichun composed the *Yuan Yuan Qu*. During the Jiaqing period, a man from Suzhou named Zheng, traveling in Yunnan, visited Shangshan Mountain in spring for a outing, seeking the tomb of Yuan Yuan but failing to find it. Lost among the broken brambles and wild vines, he suddenly became disoriented. Soon the sun set in the west, and evening smoke cloaked the trees. Looking ahead, he saw what seemed to be a house and thought to go and ask for lodging. Upon arriving, he found the vermilion gate wide open, with jade thresholds and golden inlays, clearly the residence of a prince or noble. He sent the gatekeeper to relay his message, and after a long while, he emerged and led Zheng into the eastern wing, where he was served a meal with wine and refined vessels. After the meal, an old woman came out and asked, “Sir, you speak the Wu dialect—what is your hometown?” Zheng told her. A short while later, the old woman came out with a candle and respectfully led Zheng into the hall. There was a woman of extraordinary beauty, clad in a feathered robe and rainbow-colored attire, dressed like a nun. She descended the steps to greet him, saying, “I am none other than the Xing family, buried beneath the earth for over a hundred years. Times have changed, things have transformed, and the hills and mounds have become level. I remember that you are from my hometown. I have ten small poems, which I ask you to spread.” She then ordered her attendant to fetch the poems and give them to Zheng.

The final chapter says: "The mandarin ducks transformed into fish-scale tiles, and the former Zhuolu Palace is hard to find." Zheng asked about the meaning of Zhuolu, and she replied, "Zhuolu is one of the eighteen color realms, known as the 'Southern Eight Heavens,' recorded in the *Daojing* (Daoist Canon), and it was the name of the palace where I once lived." She took a jade flute and gave it as a gift, and recited a poem: "Lamenting the easy changes of the world, the winds and rains of the western suburbs year after year. Moved by your visit to me at Shangshan, the desolate old burial grounds on the plain." Then she ordered Zheng to be escorted out. At the time, the east was just beginning to lighten, and the former mansions were all nowhere to be seen. Only the four sides faintly had walls, and upon closer inspection, it was just a deep forest. But the jade flute in the sleeve was still there, and looking at the poem, it was old and worn paper, almost rotten to the touch, and the ink was faded, clearly not of this world. Zheng thought the secret meeting was absurd, carved Yuan Yuan's farewell poem, and entrusted it to the writing brush. Donghai Liu Gushi compiled the legendary "Shangshan Phoenix Shadow," further lost its truth. The Suzhou man Jiang Jingchen told me this, so I recorded its general outline. As for the poem, there are many versions in the world, and it is not very well-crafted, so I will not record it. "Zhuolu is one of the eighteen color realms," as recorded in Daoist texts.

A woman from Changzhou, whose name and surname are unknown, was sold by her parents into a brothel when she was young. She was exceptionally beautiful, and the madam of the brothel valued her highly. As she grew older, she was pressured into sexual exploitation, but she refused, leading to daily beatings until she finally could not endure it. Guests who saw her were captivated by her beauty and tried to bribe her to satisfy her desires, so even though she did not engage in sexual acts, she received more gifts than other courtesans, which led the madam to be somewhat lenient with her, and the girl secretly saved money for her own purposes. In the year 1859, the madam took the girl to Huzhou, where a man surnamed Lu, who was gentle and skilled at reading people's intentions, and also had a refined appearance, appeared. The girl, being young and inexperienced in the ways of the world, saw him as a good man and thus arranged a marriage with him.

Lu left, and the girl told the old woman, "On a certain day, Lu will come with two hundred foreign silver dollars to redeem me. If you do not agree, I will die, which will not benefit you." Knowing she could not change the old woman's mind, she agreed. Worried that the old woman might change her mind, the girl went to all the neighboring boats to inform them of the reason and also to say goodbye. On the appointed day, Lu did not arrive, and she sent someone to call him. She asked why he had broken his promise. Lu said he had no money. The girl then took out her private savings of one hundred and fifty foreign silver dollars and gave them to him, promising he would come the next day. As soon as Lu got the money, he went to the gambling den and quickly spent it all. Some courtesans who knew what had happened told the girl, but she still did not believe them. The next day, Lu still did not come, and when she sent someone to call him, he came empty-handed. When asked about the money he had been given the previous day, he said, "It's all gone." The girl cried out in despair. Taking the opportunity, Lu fled, and the girl then committed suicide by drinking poison. The elderly and respected citizens of the Hu area heard about it and all felt pity for the girl and disapproved of Lu's behavior, forcing them to arrange her funeral. My late friend Magistrate Xu Cheng'an wrote a biography titled "The Story of the Girl from Lanling."

Moon桂 was a courtesan from Luan County, Zhili (modern-day Hebei Province). Born into a poor family, her parents forced her into this profession, which was not in line with her wishes. Courtesans in the regions of Yan and Zhao were often seen running around inns and flattering passing travelers, but Moon桂 did not follow this path. A scholar named Liu Doushan, who had passed the imperial examinations, once visited her. She welcomed him joyfully and they engaged in a long conversation that night, during which she spoke like a scholar and a hermit, which greatly surprised Doushan. One day, an elderly nun knocked on her door and offered her money and rice, but Moon桂 refused both. The nun wished to see her. Upon entering, the nun did not exchange any small talk and immediately laughed, while Moon桂 cried bitterly without stopping. The nun said, "Do not be too sad. If you do not forget your true nature, on a certain day I will wait for you at a certain place." Moon桂 nodded in agreement. On the appointed day, as the nun had said, she went to the place and indeed found a hermitage. Moon桂 entered the hermitage, paid respects to the Buddha and the nun, and then took out a pair of scissors and cut off her own hair. Those who followed her were too shocked to stop her, tried to persuade her to return, but she refused and they returned to inform her parents. The next day, she went back, but the hermitage was empty, and both Moon桂 and the nun had disappeared.

A woman from the Liu family in Shiquan County, Sichuan, was engaged to a boy from the Luo family in the same town. The boy was the same age as the girl and died at fifteen from an illness. The girl wanted to go to him, but her parents refused, and she often felt deeply sorrowful. Her family worried about her well-being and kept a close watch on her. The girl's parents secretly accepted a proposal from another family, keeping it hidden from her. She heard about it indirectly and pretended to be happy, and her parents thought there was nothing wrong. A year later, on March 16th, exactly one year after the boy's death, when her parents and elder brother and sister-in-law were all out, the girl took her younger sister to the house of a neighboring elderly woman. Previously, the woman's son had fallen into the river and drowned, and upon hearing this, his wife also threw herself into the river to die as a sacrifice. The girl knew that the woman's house faced the river. She told the woman, "I recently injured my hand and heard that your back garden has many medicinal herbs, so I wish to go and find them." The younger sister, who was twelve years old, was surprised and said, "Sister, if you hurt your hand, how could I not know?" She asked to see the injury, but the girl hid her hand in her sleeve, not letting her see. Then, she asked for fire to light incense, took it out, and the woman asked what she was doing. The girl replied, "To ward off foul odors!" The younger sister sensed something unusual and followed her out through the back door. The girl inserted the incense into the ground, bowed, and wept.

The younger sister asked her, but she did not answer; she ran to the riverside, and the younger sister held her sleeve. The girl quickly removed her outer garment and threw it to her sister, saying, "I will do good deeds for my parents, and I am leaving now." She jumped into the water. Her clan elder Liu翁 saw her from across the river, suspecting it was the girl, and quickly sent someone to pull her out. Her face was still alive, but her breath had stopped. Her clothes were sewn so tightly that they could not be undone, nor were they very wet, but there were about a square foot of her shoulders and back that were slightly wet. In Shu custom, those who die outside are not brought into the house, and they discussed burying her outside the door. Her sister-in-law said, "My younger sister-in-law calmly accepted her fate; although dead, she is still alive. Please bring her body inside, and I will take the blame." They then carried her body into the hall. Family and friends who came to mourn her all praised her, and the son of a certain family whom her parents had promised her also arrived, wishing to see her face once more. Some said, "No, you should not." Others said, "She is our old relative; what harm is there?" At that time, the girl had already been dressed in the small clothing, and when the son of that family came to her side, she suddenly bled from her mouth and nose, staining his face. The son of that family was shocked and fell, then was carried back home. After a month, he died. In the year Renchen of Daoguang, the officials reported her loyalty and bravery to the court, and honored her family's gate. On the night before the order was issued, her mother dreamed that the girl came to her, saying, "I am serving the command of the Heavenly Deity as the god of the River Jian, and I cannot stay by my parents' side anymore."

After finishing his speech, he bowed. Upon completing the bow, several dozen followers appeared, dressed in pure white, and were about to leave in their clothes. The mother tried to detain them, but he suddenly woke up, thinking it was due to accumulated thoughts. In the Jian River, there were people who had fallen into the water, and they often encountered a divine person who saved them. The appearance of this divine person was that of a young girl wearing white, and he then realized it was Liu's daughter. Afterward, her miraculous deeds were well-known, and the townspeople raised money to build a temple, naming it the "Jian River Water God Temple," which became very popular. In the year of Yinwei, his nephew Liu Doushan, who had passed the imperial examinations, heard that his hometown was on fire and did not have time for a carriage or horse, so he returned on foot. He had not reached home by evening. He came to a bridge and saw someone coming towards him, who was fat and short with a square body. After passing by, he thought that there was no one in the world like this, could it be a ghost? He looked back at him and saw two phosphorescent lights as big as a wooden block. He threw a stone at them, and the person came again, standing facing each other, with the phosphorescent lights flickering. He stared at them with his eyes, and they shrank to the size of a bean, gradually growing larger again, like this for a long time. The person let out a long sigh and left. Doushan unconsciously followed him. Suddenly he heard the sound of water and realized, "This must be a drowning ghost." He stood back and did not move forward, and in the darkness, he saw nothing. He felt that several people were pulling him. At this critical moment, he suddenly heard someone calling, "The Lady Goddess has arrived!" They all dispersed.

Someone patted his back and said, "What kind of choice did you make? If they do not bully you, why strike them? Fortunately, you have future good fortune; they still dare not act recklessly; otherwise, even if I come, it would be too late. You should return quickly, comfort your grandmother, and also tell the family not to move my grave." Dou Shan suddenly woke up from a dream, realizing it was his aunt. Upon returning home, his grandmother was crying; he asked her, and she said, "I dreamed that your aunt came, which is why I am sad." The girl's grave was near the water, with many graves of other families nearby. After the burial, the river eroded the area, forming a small island, surrounded by water on all sides, as if unwilling to mix with the rest. The family worried that it might sink into the water and discussed relocating the grave. Upon hearing what Dou Shan said, they stopped. Later, Dou Shan's two sons were still young. While returning from a neighboring village, a sudden spring flood occurred, and they were caught in the water. A woman carried them, walking about half a li before reaching the shore. She told them, "I am your grandmother's sister." Dou Shan's mother sighed and said, "My younger aunt has become a spirit, yet she still remembers her mother's family like this!" Dou Shan's mother was the one who previously decided to bury the girl's body in the hall. Dou Shan passed the imperial examination in the Ding You year and became a selected scholar. The spirit said that he would have future good fortune, could this be referring to this? I have a bond of the same age with Dou Shan and once met him in Wu. This matter was told to Xu Cheng'an by Dou Shan, and Xu Cheng'an wrote it down, so I learned about it.

To the southwest of Maozhou in Sichuan Province lies Ma Chang Mountain, whose height reaches tens of li. Even in the height of summer, its snow does not melt. The local people say that they often see *shanshi*—ghostly markets or cities—rising in the distance. It is said that there was a man surnamed Xiong, who made his living panning for gold. Hearing that this mountain was rich in gold deposits, he set out to search for them. When he reached deep within the mountain, he suddenly came upon a settlement, where several thousand households resided, their chickens and dogs heard one after another, and their homes standing closely together. Xiong wandered about, forgetting how far he had traveled, and after a long time, he grew very hungry. He saw a house facing south, quite grand and magnificent, and an old man with white hair stood outside, his hands behind his back. Looking at Xiong, he said, “Are you a traveler from afar?” Xiong replied, “Yes.” The old man then said, “Have you not been hungry? There is no place here to buy wine or dried meat. I will act as your host and offer you a simple meal of coarse rice, without asking for payment.” Xiong thanked him and followed him inside. The old man served him rice and millet as if they had been prepared in advance. After eating his fill, Xiong rose to take his leave. The old man asked, “What brought you here?” Xiong told him the reason, and the old man said, “The mountain certainly produces gold, but outsiders do not know its location—your search would be in vain. You would be better off working for my family. Although the wages are not generous, the work is much more peaceful.” Xiong agreed. The old man introduced himself as a man of the Li family, and treated him with great kindness and generosity. In his spare time from work, Xiong wandered through the marketplace, where all the people were warm and hospitable, and he found such pleasure that he forgot to return home. One day, Master Li ordered the servants to clean the courtyard and prepare everything anew. Xiong asked why, and was told, “It is the *Laxi* festival, when we honor our ancestors.” Xiong had been in the mountain for some time without knowing how long, and upon hearing the old man’s words, realized that the year was drawing to a close. He felt a deep sense of longing to return home. The old man comforted him and said, “You may return for now, but you should come back again.”

The old man said, "If the guest insists on returning, let it be tomorrow." That night, the old man took out an object, wrapped it up, and handed it to him, saying, "A modest provision of wages, not abundant, but sufficient for your family to enjoy several years of warmth and fullness. However, it must be kept secret, not even to be revealed to the person in the bed." The bear respectfully accepted the instructions, but found the words strange. Just as midnight approached, the old man urged the bear to rise and said, "It is suitable to travel under the moonlight." He placed several steamed buns in the bear's chest and said, "If you are hungry, eat these; you can reach home." Still come here after the Lantern Festival, do not break the appointment!" The bear just stepped out, and the door immediately closed. After walking several steps and looking back, he found that the hut and houses were all gone, only the slanted moon about to set and the deep black forest remained. Greatly frightened, he thought he had encountered a ghost. When he reached into his chest, the buns were still there. They were very sweet, so he took a rest by the roadside. At dawn, he took out the gift given by the old man, opened it, and saw that it contained gold worth more than twenty taels. He was both surprised and delighted. Worried about getting lost again, he broke dozens of bamboo branches and inserted them along the winding path as markers. Upon entering the city, he found that people in the city were just about to worship the stove, so he hurried home. He exchanged the gold for money, and the neighbors asked where the gold came from, but he did not tell them. The neighbors suspected this and spread rumors, saying that the bear had robbed on an outing. The bear could no longer hide and fully described what he had encountered. The neighbors enthusiastically insisted on accompanying him, and by the appointed time, they had gathered more than ten people. Together, they went into the mountain and searched for the bamboo branches he had planted earlier. They were all there, but there was no trace of human presence. They climbed high and looked around, and they saw dozens of dogs coming from the mountain. As they approached, they lay down and roared loudly, transforming into tigers. Several people were injured and stumbled back home.

Maozhou is surrounded by mountains, and barbarians and Han people live together. About ten miles from the city is the Ta Shan Dun, where a house was built by the mountain, called the Nainai Temple. They worship a goddess, whose name and surname are unknown, and all the barbarians treat her with great respect. The temple is always locked, and it is only opened during festivals. There was a scholar named He, who was a hired porter. He would always return home first after finishing his work. His fellow workers asked him, and he said, "I am waiting for you at Ta Shan Dun." Like this for half a year, his fellow workers began to suspect him, and they followed him to see where he went. They arrived at a small shrine beside the temple, where an old man came out and spoke with He. After speaking, they went in together, and the watchers followed them inside, but the old man had disappeared, and only He was left. He then went to the back of the shrine, where there was a door leading to the temple. When he reached the door, it opened, and He entered. The watchers still followed him, and there was a gatekeeper, but he did not stop them. A group of men and women welcomed He inside, and they changed his clothes and shoes, making him appear like a noble person. The temple hall was adorned with beautiful decorations, unlike anything in the world. He thought they were ghosts or spirits, so he shouted at them fiercely, and suddenly fainted and fell to the ground.

On the next morning, he awoke to find that everyone and everything had vanished. Only He lay on the ground, seemingly dead, but his skin was still warm, and his clothes were as dirty and worn as before. He called out to him many times without a response, and in panic, he wanted to leave, but the door and lock were very tight. He then called out through a crack in the wall, and the foreigner guarding the temple heard him, wondering how he had gotten inside, and opened the lock to let him out. He inquired about the cause, and it was known that there was a dead person inside, so he reported it to the provincial governor Zhou Gong, who ordered a carriage to go and investigate. When he arrived, He had already woken up. He asked him, and He said that last year, when he passed by the Tu Gu Temple, he met an old man who invited him to talk and wanted to give his niece to him as a wife, but He declined because he was already married. The old man said, "I already knew that, but there is a predestined connection, so you can take her as a concubine, and it will not be harmful." So He lived in her family. The woman was very beautiful, and her family was very wealthy. She said that his good fortune is thin, so she would only support him enough to meet his basic needs. From then on, he often stayed at her house. Last night, he drank a lot and got drunk, so he woke up late. He didn't know that he was lying stiffly in the temple. Governor Zhou thought it was an omen, and he wanted to burn the temple. The foreigners knelt and begged for mercy, and he finally relented. After that, all the foreigners regarded He as the son-in-law of the goddess and respected him greatly. He continued to carry loads back and forth, still staying at the temple overnight, and the watcher did not see anything else. He said that that night, both he and the woman did not know that there was a watcher.

Dong Sǒu, a man from Maōzhou, was skilled in the art of water talismans. He was old and widowed, without a son, and carried his daughter to rent a house in an abandoned government building. He had a cousin named someone who often went out, but only his wife and four-year-old son depended on the old man to live. One evening, the woman and the daughter talked together until late at night. The woman took her son into the room, and the daughter also went to bed. The next morning, the sun had passed noon, but the door of the woman did not open. Looking in, the old man saw her sitting opposite her son. Calling to them did not get a response, so he broke down the door. Then the woman and her son were both dead. The son had a burn mark one line long, from the tip of his toe to his penis, and the woman also had a burn mark entering her vagina. Looking at their faces, they were smiling and without sorrowful expressions. Touching their bellies, they were empty, as if their intestines had been burned away. The room had an old stove, but there was no reason for it to spread fire to people. The old man thought this house was unlucky and moved to another place. Soon after, he married a son of the Pan family as a son-in-law.

In the following January, the son-in-law and his daughter went out together to celebrate the New Year. As they passed by the gate of the abandoned official residence, the son-in-law suddenly wanted to enter, but the daughter stopped him. Immediately, the son-in-law suffered from an unbearable headache. The daughter quickly returned home and spoke to her father. The old man knew that his son-in-law had encountered a ghost and applied a talisman. The pain slightly subsided. That night, the old man went to bed when he suddenly heard a cry for help from his daughter's room. He held a torch and hurried towards her room. Upon entering, he saw that the daughter's back had been burned by ghost fire. The light was deep blue, and both her inner and outer clothes were scorched, almost reaching her skin. He poured water on it to extinguish the fire. The daughter was confused and could not speak. After a while, she said, "I saw my sister-in-law making her son hold my hands while raising a fire to burn my back. I was very distressed and unable to make a sound. Fortunately, my deceased mother came and shouted for help, and I was finally saved." The old man then applied another talisman, and the daughter recovered. The son-in-law also had no further issues. The maidservant of the Mao family recorded in the fifth volume and the woman of a certain family recorded in the eighth volume, are probably all of this kind.

Maozhou, an ancient district of Wenshan, was once occupied by the Tibetan Empire during the Tang Dynasty, and its population was sparse. Traveling east from the state capital for sixty li leads to Gangan; traveling west for twenty li leads to Dagushi. Along the way, there are inns set up to accommodate travelers; those who open the doors and serve at the counter are all women. Among the beautiful women, there are always those with strange illnesses, which the locals call "Poison Ghosts." Every year during the Spring and Autumn equinoxes, when menstruation begins, the illness occurs. When it occurs, the abdomen swells like a drum, the skin swells, and yellow fluid flows from the mouth, eyes, and ten fingernails. By night, the discomfort becomes even more severe. The women secretly carry small bamboo tubes with them, not even allowing their parents or husbands to know.

A tube contains the hair of various beasts, including dogs, pigs, cattle, horses, donkeys, and mules. In the dark, one picks a hair at random. The hair determines what the soul transforms into, and the transformed entity emerges into the wilderness to bewilder travelers, often causing trouble for them. Sometimes, someone with courage encounters the entity, grabs it, and beats it, causing it to wail and beg for mercy. As dawn approaches, the situation becomes even more dire, and the entity immediately reveals its name, surname, and place of residence. If someone kills it, the sick person dies. The sick person exudes a yellow fluid, and if a person slightly comes into contact with it, they will be poisoned, and the illness is also like this, but the sick person can recover slightly. Therefore, those with this illness warn each other not to eat in their homes, or if they have no choice but to eat, they place red copper in the food container, and the poison cannot harm them. Strange! This disease is even more severe than leprosy and蛊cultivation in Guangdong. The above are several accounts from Sichuan, all recorded by Xu Cheng'an, and his son Xizan compiled them to show me, as he had heard them from the Sichuanese Liu Doushan.

During the reign of Emperor Xianfeng, official decrees stipulated that anyone who could not return to their hometown to mourn a parent due to war or disorder, even after completing the full mourning period, would still be ordered to resign from their post and return home to observe a 100-day mourning period. Thus, my father-in-law, Master Fan Yunaong, who was then the Prefect of Henan Prefecture, was ordered to return to his native Xianning County in Hubei to observe mourning, and a Manchu official named Master De took over the duties of the prefecture. Within a month, one of his concubines committed suicide by drowning in a well. Previously, there had been a Mr. Wang who had served alongside Master De’s father in Jiangxi, and they had a relationship akin to that of sworn brothers. His wife, Wang’s wife, and the mother of Master De were also referred to as sisters. Later, Mr. Wang was sent on a military exile, and a servant accompanied him, carrying his belongings. Before departing, the servant knelt and bowed before the master’s wife, saying: “I am going with my master, and have nothing to be attached to. But only my daughter, named Lian, is the one I love dearly. Please, Madam, take good care of her.” Wang’s wife replied: “If you are going with your master, then your daughter will be my daughter as well.” Thus, Lian was moved into the inner quarters and raised as a daughter, and she was also taught to read and study. As Lian grew up and became beautiful, Master De saw her and became enamored with her. Therefore, he called Wang’s wife *Aunt*, and requested her permission, wishing to propose marriage.

The man was known for his moral virtue and graceful demeanor, and he was also skilled in composing poetry. He had a fondness for calligraphy, painting, and antiquities, and was a well-mannered and handsome young man. However, his character was frivolous and fickle, always abandoning the old for the new. He had initially married a woman with whom he was deeply in love, but later took a courtesan from Guangling as a concubine, leading to his separation from his wife. The elderly woman Wang was already aware of this situation and asked him, "You have already married, are you planning to make Ailian a concubine?" He replied, "Not so; my previous wife was not content in her marital home and had been living with her parents for a long time, as my aunt knew. If Ailian comes to me, she will be my wife." Wang responded, "The courtesan from Guangling is dangerous with her words, and Ailian is not her match." He said, "That woman has been dead for a long time." Wang sent someone to investigate, and it was confirmed that there was no courtesan from Guangling in the family, so she agreed to his request. He then married her according to proper rites and treated her as his legitimate wife. However, the courtesan from Guangling was actually still alive and had changed into Manchu attire, which the investigators from Wang's side did not notice. The man's nature was deceitful and treacherous; anyone he loved and favored would be undermined by deceitful means. When he became angry, he would either give them as gifts to his colleagues or marry them off to his servants. If there were children and it was known that they could not be removed, he would use schemes to kill both the mother and children, and this had happened to more than one person.

A Woman Named Lotus When Lotus first arrived, the woman from Guangling mistakenly showed her great respect, treating her with excessive deference. Over time, the woman from Guangling used subtle tactics to sow discord, but De deeply loved Lotus and could not be swayed. The woman from Guangling then further humbled herself, daily grooming Lotus's hair. Soon, her hair began to smell, and soon after, her entire body began to emit an odor. However, others could not smell it; only De could perceive it, though he did not know how she managed to do so. Soon, the entire room where she stayed also began to smell. De eventually stopped entering Lotus's room and grew tired of her, so he decided to give her to his younger brother. The brother was the eighth in the order of his siblings, and the family affectionately called him "Eighth Master." Lotus cried and refused to comply, and De spoke to her, saying, "My and your缘分is over. My younger brother, Eighth Master, is young and not yet married. Following Eighth Master is better than following me, isn't it?" He then chose a good day to formalize the arrangement. The so-called "Eighth Master" was also very happy and entered Lotus's room before the scheduled time, using sweet words to please her, saying, "What good thing do you desire? I will buy it for you." Lotus did not respond, and he persistently asked her. She then spoke loudly, saying, "I have no other needs; just buy me a good coffin." Eighth Master left in alarm.

When Wang Ao's son came to serve as the Prefect of Henan, Wang Ao planned to accompany him. Liang asked every day if Wang Ao had arrived. However, Wang Ao had not yet arrived, and the expected time had passed. Liang knew she could not wait any longer. One day, she took a young maid into the garden behind the house. The garden had a well, and Liang rushed to the well, ordering the maid to go to another place to pick flowers. The maid went a little way off, and then Liang plunged herself into the well. The maid returned too late to save her. She ran to report to De, who sent someone to pull Liang out of the well. Her underclothes were tightly sewn, indicating that her resolve to die had been long determined. After Liang's death, Wang Ao arrived and loudly scolded in the hall of the Henan Prefecture, calling De by his pet name, "Ah Qiao," and demanding, "Settle my daughter's life!" De kowtowed and confessed his guilt, and arranged for a grand funeral for Liang, dressing his younger brother, Eight Brother, in the attire of a wife as part of the mourning rites. At that time, my eldest son's wife, Fan Shang, was living with her mother in the residence of the Henan Prefecture, so she knew the details very well. Liang, a girl from a small family, deeply understood great principles, and calmly died for her principles, how does she compare to those pampered and indulged children? This is indeed praiseworthy. Unfortunately, we do not know her surname. My eldest son's wife said, "At that time, people in the office called her Wang Girl because she came from Wang Ao's family, that is why they called her so, but it was not her actual surname."

A man from Quzhou named Fu翁, childless, bought another man's son to be his own and had already arranged a marriage for him. After some time, the son learned that his biological parents were somewhere, so he ran away to visit them. His biological parents, having become wealthy through business after selling him, were sufficiently provided for in terms of food and clothing. Upon finding their son, they were very happy and told him, "When you were in the womb, you were promised in marriage to a certain family. Now her daughter has grown up, and we will marry her to you." The son informed them that he was already married to a woman from the Fu family. They replied, "This is a woman from the Fu family; what do we care?" and finally, they married her. The daughter of that family was beautiful, and the son was very attracted to her, so he wanted to sever ties with the Fu family. After losing his son, the wife of the Fu family cried day and night. Fu翁 then tracked him down, took him back, and spoke kindly to him, saying, "The natural bond of kinship cannot be severed; but I raised you from childhood, nurturing you until you became an adult, and you already have a wife; how can you sever ties? From now on, you should visit both families. Both families already have wives. In the future, each family will have its own descendants, and that will be fine." The son meekly agreed.

While the wife he married into the Fu family was not as beautiful as the one he married later, she felt desolate and neglected despite living in the Fu household. The first wife treated him as she had before, without holding it against him for marrying again, but the son grew increasingly to despise her. One day, while the old man and his wife were enjoying a leisurely conversation, they heard strange sounds coming from the son's room. Rushing to see, they found the son using a rope to strangle the wife's throat, with the wife's breath almost gone, yet the son still tightly held the rope without releasing it. Greatly alarmed, they hastily scolded the son out of the room and tried every means to save the wife, who eventually revived. When asked about the incident, the wife could only weep. A small maid在一旁，述知子引绳向妇，妇延颈受之，略不撑拒，婢欲出告人，妇犹摇手止之。舅姑问何意，妇叹曰：“新妇不得于良人，虽生何味？不如死良人手之为愈也。”舅姑益怜之。然自此其子竟绝迹于傅氏。舅姑不得已，乃归妇于母家，劝之他适。数年后，舅偶过之，妇犹出拜，舅劝喻之，执志如初，是亦可悲矣。 While the wife he married into the Fu family was not as beautiful as the one he married later, she felt desolate and neglected despite living in the Fu household. The first wife treated him as she had before, without holding it against him for marrying again, but the son grew increasingly to despise her. One day, while the old man and his wife were enjoying a leisurely conversation, they heard strange sounds coming from the son's room. Rushing to see, they found the son using a rope to strangle the wife's throat, with the wife's breath almost gone,

A man from Hangzhou married a woman, and on their wedding night, the wife refused to share the same bed with him, defending herself very strictly. This continued for a month, and the husband was unable to approach her. The husband was very angry, and when the wife returned to her parents' home, he took her quilt and showed it to his close friends. The quilt was tightly sewn with hemp thread, barely wide enough for one person, and every time she slept, she turned her feet toward the person, like a snake retreating into a pit. Everyone found this strange. The mother-in-law and father-in-law learned of the situation and tried to persuade their daughter, but she refused to listen. They told the husband, "It must be that the day you were married was an inauspicious day, hence this situation. We should prepare a separate room for you, choose a favorable date, and perform the wedding ceremony again." The husband nodded in agreement but did nothing. As the end of the year approached, affairs were busy and complicated, and he had no time to deal with it. The husband, thinking it was time for the New Year, came to bring his wife back home. The wife wept and did not want to leave, but her parents forced her to return to her husband's home. That night, the husband entered the room, and the wife hid behind the lamp, not speaking to him. The husband, having no choice, lay down first, while the wife sat waiting for dawn, even though the cold of the night penetrated to the bone, she did not care. From then on, the husband also came to hate his wife and they often quarreled. One day, they argued over a minor issue, and the husband cried all night. He wrote in detail about the state of affairs after his wife returned, stuck it to the main door, and left, not knowing where he went. Neighbors gathered to read what he had written, and it said, "Either enter a monastery or seek death," indicating that his fate was uncertain. This truly is what is called a resentful couple.

I once stayed in Yushan County, Jiangxi Province. In front of my study was the accounting office, which was located to the left of the second hall. The official sat in the hall to interrogate prisoners, and I could hear everything clearly; the sound of beating did not stop in my ear. Every night when it was quiet, I could still hear the sound of drums and music. One day, while sitting in the accounting office with my friends and chatting idly, I heard that a prisoner was being beaten in the hall, and the sound of her crying in pain (the character "々" emphasizes the sound of crying, pronounced "wei," as seen in the *Chuogenglu*). It was a woman. My friends hurried to see it, and pulled me along, but I sat upright and did not move, and they all laughed, saying, "Is there such a thing? You are so stubborn!" Soon after, the friends returned, and I asked what they saw, and they said, "It was just a slap on the cheek." I asked what crime she was punished for, and they said, "This woman had an affair with a monk and put poison in her husband's food, intending to kill him. The husband was poisoned, and his stomach pain was so severe that he could not bear it, and his cries could be heard by the neighbors. So the neighbors came, and they gave him a drink of feces to make him vomit, and he luckily did not die."

They collectively reported the matter to the official, who then arrested the woman and the monk. After interrogation, the truth was obtained. They saw that her husband was on the verge of death, so they called a doctor to examine him. The doctor said, "The poison has not yet fully left his body." They then gave him another dose of medicine. The husband lay on the ground and vomited violently; at first, the vomit was black, then purple, and finally red. The doctor said, "The poison should now be completely gone. However, the internal organs have been significantly injured. I will give you my medicine to nourish your internal organs and cleanse the remaining poison. If you survive for three days, you will be safe." The official, seeing how promiscuous and toxic this woman was, decided to slap her cheeks 200 times. If her husband died, she would be judged according to the law. During the discussion, a friend named Yuan remarked, "According to the national law, a woman who commits adultery should be stripped and flogged. This woman's promiscuity and toxicity are so great, yet without following the law to flog her, he only slapped her cheeks. Is it not a mistake to be too lenient?" I replied, "Not so. Since ancient times, there has never been a case of a woman being stripped and flogged on the buttocks. If we look at historical records, Emperor Wu Cheng of Northern Qi stripped Empress Li of Emperor Wenxuan and beat her. This was the act of a licentious ruler. How could this be a precedent? The law regarding women receiving beatings is most detailed in the *Yuan History: Criminal Law Chapter*."

The text says: For those who commit adultery, they shall be beaten with seventy-seven strokes, and women shall be stripped before receiving the punishment. It also states: For prostitutes who injure non-prostitutes, if the injured party dies beyond the prescribed limit, the prostitute shall be beaten with seventy-seven strokes and stripped to the waist before receiving the punishment. Additionally, it states: For wives who brutally beat their concubines to death, they shall be beaten with one hundred and seven strokes and stripped before receiving the punishment. Thus, stripping before receiving the punishment was already a practice in previous dynasties. However, the term "beating" refers to beating the back, not the buttocks. According to the *History of the Jin Dynasty* in the biography of Emperor Wanyan Liang, the Left Chancellor, Ao, stripped his sister-in-law and beat her back. According to the biography of Ao, he was angry with his clan brother's wife and stripped her, beating her back. Thus, it can be known that stripping before beating refers to beating the back, not the buttocks. By the time of the early Ming Dynasty, the laws were established such that strokes were applied to the buttocks or thighs, and there were no provisions for beating the back. Therefore, when women received the punishment, it was also applied to their buttocks or thighs. Furthermore, following the precedent of previous dynasties, women were stripped and beaten on their buttocks. This was a corrupt policy of the Ming Dynasty, comparable to the harsh treatment of women sent to brothels.

The legal codes and regulations of our dynasty follow those of the previous Ming Dynasty, where women who commit adultery are punished by having their clothing removed before being flogged. However, officials often understand and follow the benevolent intentions of the imperial court, striving for leniency and magnanimity, and rarely carry out such punishments literally. In the future, if there are wise and virtuous individuals who propose the abolition of laws that prescribe flogging for women, it would indeed be a great and virtuous deed.” At that time, there was a man named Wu, a legal expert and a master of his craft, who sighed and said, “Your words are correct. In the past, during the Qianlong era, there was a scoundrel in Jiangxi who pretended to be a woman, entered a woman’s private quarters under false pretenses, and committed acts of sexual impropriety. After the incident was discovered, a high-ranking official registered the women involved in the scandal and charged them with the crime of adultery, ordering the local officials to issue warrants for their arrest and to flog them according to the law. Rumors spread that when the women were brought to the authorities, they would all have their clothing removed before being flogged. Disreputable young men gathered daily outside the county office to wait for news. The women, upon hearing this, often committed suicide by hanging, and some died of shame after being flogged. If we were to follow your advice, it would bring immeasurable benefit.” This discussion has been on my mind for a long time. The *Da Zhai Congshuo* (Collection of Miscellaneous Notes) mentions the changes to the flogging laws during the Han and Tang dynasties, and the *Guang Yang Yuan Jinjian* (Recent Observations and Reflections) recounts a story about a county magistrate, both of which touch on this idea. Now, as I write these notes, I recall an old saying and thus describe it in detail as follows.

In the fifth month of the sixth year of the Guangxu era, people in Hankou, Hubei, saw a wooden board floating down the river. The board had several layers, bound together with large ropes. On top lay a woman, who was quite beautiful, with her limbs all pierced with iron rings and nailed to the board, unable to move. Beside her were three thousand coins, and there was also a ceramic jar filled with food, right next to her right hand. Under her hip was a human head, which turned out to be a monk, already rotten and unbearable. A wooden marker was inserted into the board, and written on it were the words: "This woman is from Jinkou, aged nineteen. The monk is forty-two years old. If the woman dies, kind-hearted people will take this money to buy a coffin for her; if she does not die, there is food that can sustain her for several more days. Those who see it need not save her, but those who save and keep her will be thieves or prostitutes." Thus, those who saw her did not save her, and they let her drift away. Jinkou is sixty miles from Hankou, and no one knew who her family was. Some say that the next day, it drifted to a place called Gedian, where someone saved her, but no one knew for sure.

In the rural areas of Zhenhai County, there were four brothers who went out to sea to catch fish and returned to Kunting. As they were about to enter the port, a ship loaded with goods was just coming out. Seeing the fishing boat approaching, the ship's crew feared a collision and shouted loudly. The four brothers refused to yield and intentionally collided with the ship, causing it to capsize. One person, while in the water, clung to the side of the boat with both hands, begging for help. The hired workers of the fishing boat asked their master, and the four brothers all said, "If we save him, he will surely say that we caused his ship to sink, leading to more trouble. It would be better if he died." They then took out a knife and cut off one of his hands. The man still clung to the side of the boat with one hand, but they cut off the other hand as well, and he finally drifted away with the current.

In that year, during the sixth month of summer, a great thunderstorm occurred, and the four brothers were all at home when they were struck by lightning in the courtyard and died. The hired worker was also struck by lightning; he could still speak and recounted previous events before dying. In the northern suburbs of Xiushui County, there were two brothers named Shen, both farmers. The eldest brother had two sons, while the second brother had one son. The second brother's wife, Yang, was fierce and greedy, constantly nagging her husband to live separately, which he had already agreed to. Yang then privately plotted that if the eldest brother's wife, husband, and children all died, his land would also go to the second brother. It was the busiest time for farming, and Yang invited her mother to help her. They then conspired to poison the eldest brother's family with the meal. This was on the twenty-third day of the fifth month. That night, a fierce thunderstorm raged, and lightning directly struck Yang's house. Yang's son, who was only four years old, cried out, "The lightning has come!" He had seen something, but Yang was unaware of the danger. The next day, she first fed her mother and husband the leftover rice from the previous day, then had her mother cook more rice, intending to poison it when it was ready. Another fierce thunderstorm occurred, and the mother, fearing for her life, stopped the plan.

Yang did not listen and went into the room himself to take the arsenic he had stored. Before he could exit, a thunderclap was heard, and Yang died in the room. The mother also fell to the ground; she was helped up and, after a long time, could speak, describing Yang's plot in this way. Zhong bought a coffin and prepared Yang's body, carrying him to a mulberry field. That night, the thunder continued to rumble. At dawn, they went to look and found the coffin broken and the body exposed. This incident and the case of the fisherman in Zhenhai occurred in the year Gengchen of the Guangxu reign. According to the *Lunheng: On Thunder*, it forcefully refutes the popular belief that thunder is a sign of divine anger and argues that thunder is the agitated energy of the sun. Modern Western scholars believe that thunder is the electricity in the sky.

The utility of electricity is vast; when harnessed and used, it can replace lamps, facilitate communication, and create or control devices. However, when people come into contact with it, the consequences can be severe. This energy particularly dislikes metallic objects, so ship masts and house pillars should avoid being wrapped in iron, fearing that it will draw down electrical strikes. This belief is spoken with great certainty. Yet, how can such events be explained without the presence of supernatural forces? I believe that lightning is essentially a form of energy. Since there is this energy, then ghosts and deities use it to execute their judgments. Just as water, fire, and wind are naturally present in the world, and Buddhism speaks of fire disasters, water disasters, and wind disasters, then ghosts and deities use these elements to bring about their calamities. King Wu of the Shang dynasty was struck dead by a violent thunderbolt for his disrespect towards the gods, clearly recorded in historical records. If we follow Wang Chong's account, during the Han dynasty, the Thunder God was depicted with a drum in the left hand and a hammer in the right, which is overly fanciful. Yet, to ultimately claim there is no deity in charge, and that people who die from lightning strikes do so by chance, this is the view that natural changes are not to be feared, which a gentleman would not accept.

In the sixth month of the sixth year of the Guangxu reign, the governor of Zhejiang Province, Tan Gong, reported: "Xu Zhibang, a resident of Tiantai County in Taizhou Prefecture, was born in the thirty-fifth year of the Qianlong reign and lived to be one hundred and three years old by the eleventh year of the Tongzhi reign. He was recognized by the previous governor, Yang Changjun, who followed the established procedure to request official recognition, and was awarded a piece of imperial silk and ten taels of silver. Now, according to the report from officials Chen Bugu of Tiantai County, Xu Zhibang is currently one hundred and eleven years old. His eldest great-grandson, Xu Zunzhou, had twins in August of the fifth year of the Guangxu reign, and his second great-grandson, Xu Zunxian, also had a son. Xu Zhibang resides in Fuxi, where he is said to have a unique longevity, living ten years beyond a century, and his family gathers five generations in one hall. We humbly request the emperor's grace and to be honored with special recognition." The imperial edict was issued, instructing the Ministry of Rites to be aware of the matter. According to the work "Yuzhitang Tanhui" by Xu Yingqiu of the Ming dynasty, there are many accounts of ancient people living for over a hundred years, and in the work "Gaishu Congkao" by Zhao Yunsong of the Qing dynasty, there is also a mention, though not as extensive as in "Yuzhitang Tanhui." In the past, when reading these books, I often marveled at their unusual content. Who would have thought that in our time there would be such a person, and I fear that there will be no second such person in the world.

Fellow scholars who passed the same examination, Wei Yunge Shilong, died and became a deity; I have already written about this in the seventh volume. Now I have heard from Hangzhou resident Wei Yuanming Ranyi that in the first year of Daoguang, the imperial examination for special candidates was held. Wei had a dream in January of that year in which he viewed the heavenly register, and the first name was Wei Shilong of Renhe. Upon waking, he searched for it, but there was no one named Wei Shilong in the Renhe school register. He then changed his own name to Shilong to respond to it. He did not pass this examination, but until the special examination in the year Jia Chen, he indeed passed as the first-place candidate, twenty-four years after the year he had the dream. According to popular belief, there are often those who dream of the heavenly register. When examining historical records, the *Qiandinglu* by a Tang dynasty author records Chen Yanbo dreaming of reaching the capital hall. He saw a very elaborate setup, with the courtyard decorated with brocade and embroidery. In the center was a table with a set of desks and chairs, and on top were scrolls that shone like golden letters. Chen Yanbo privately asked about it, and it was said, "The names of next year's Jinshi will be sent to the heavenly officials for review." This is close to the heavenly register, but it does not mention the list itself.

Again, the *Gan Ding Lu* (Record of Predestined Events) records: In the early years of the Yuanhe era, the scholar-official Li Guyan was about to take the imperial examinations. Suddenly, he dreamed of seeing the examination list, where he saw the name Li Guyan listed as the second-place winner. This dream mentioned the list but did not specify a heavenly list. The concept of a heavenly list is commonly transmitted by the masses, but it is not necessarily accurate. The idea that in the unseen realm there may be predestined entries, and that this should not be surprising, is a common belief. Wei, who passed the imperial examinations in the 24th year of the Daoguang era, first saw a dream in the first year of the Daoguang era, which seems remarkably early. I, along with Xu Renshan (the title "Ge Xue" means "Scholar of the Pavilion"), and Ying Minzai (the title "Lian Fan" means "Inspector"), all passed the examination in this year. In this year, we were all twenty-four years old, having been born in the first year of the Daoguang era. Xu was born in July, Ying in October, and I in December. When Wei saw the heavenly list in his dream, none of us three had been born yet. I wonder if our names were on that heavenly list. If they were not, then that list would be incomplete; if they were, then since we did not exist in the world at that time, how could we have been listed on the list? Moreover, I was born until the age of six, when my father finally gave me the name Sen, and later changed it to my current name. Since the name was not yet determined, how could the spirits have known it in advance? This is truly inconceivable.

The *Shuowen Jiezi* states: "孑" means "without a right arm," and "子" means "without a left arm." I believe that people born without both arms are also very rare; why would the ancients create these two characters specifically? According to the *Zuo Zhuan*, "授师孑焉以伐随" indicates that "孑" was a type of weapon used in battle, and I suspect "子" might also be a weapon. This explanation is detailed in Volume Six, Chapter Four of the *First Floor Series*. A woman named Fan, the wife of my eldest son, recounted that during her travels with her father in Central China, she saw a sister of a certain official who was very beautiful but had no arms. When asked, she said she was born that way, not due to an injury. This woman requires assistance for all daily activities, such as eating and using the restroom. In winter, with thick clothing, it is not very noticeable, but in summer, with thin silk garments, it is clearly visible. Her sleeves are empty and hollow, making her appearance very unpleasant. Therefore, she avoids going out in summer, and even close relatives rarely see her. Her brother and sister-in-law are very kind-hearted; because this girl cannot perform tasks for her mother-in-law and it is not right for her to spend her life alone in an empty house, they plan to invite a poor scholar to their home and make him her husband. Whether this plan was carried out is unknown. This woman truly possesses both the forms of "孑" and "子."

A Man from Meng County, Henan Province, firmly believed in the alchemy and spiritual cultivation practices of immortals, and worshipped Lü the Immortal with great devotion, often lamenting that he had never had the chance to see him. One day, he wandered alone to the Temple of King Tang on West Ridge, pacing and gazing around in hope of some encounter. Suddenly, a man came rushing toward him, carrying a bundle on his back. Looking at him, he recognized him as someone he had known before, named Yi, a performer from the Pear Garden. Yi asked him, “What are you thinking about? Are you once again longing to see Lü the Immortal? Lü the Immortal is in heaven—how could you possibly see him? If you insist, I can dress up as Lü the Immortal for you.” (*According to the *Shuowen Jiezi*, the character “扮” is pronounced like “fen.” However, the *Guangyun* rhyme dictionary, in its thirty-first section, has the character “扮,” which means “to dress up,” pronounced “pu huan,” suggesting that this usage is also ancient.*) He then unfastened his bundle and put on the hat and robe within, wearing a starry crown and rainbow robe, carrying a long sword on his back and holding a dust whisk in his hand. He asked, “Do you think I look like Lü the Immortal?” “Very much so,” replied the other. Yi said, “Then Lü the Immortal is no more than this. Why then seek to see him? About ten miles from here is a certain village where a play is being performed. Wait until I take the stage, and then I will be gone.” He then removed the clothes and ornaments he had worn, put them back into the bundle, and left with it. The man stood in a daze for a long time, and then slowly walked to the village intending to see the actors, but found nothing there. He asked the villagers, and they all said that nothing of the sort had happened, which greatly puzzled him. On another day, he met Yi again and asked him, “Why did you deceive me?” Yi replied, “On that day, I had not yet met you. How could I have deceived you?” Then he realized that the one he had met was indeed the real Lü the Immortal, and that he had missed the opportunity by a hair’s breadth.

In the west gate of Mengcheng County, there is an ancient tomb whose dynasty and person remain unknown. Above the tomb stand three locust trees, commonly known as the Three Locust Tree Tomb. In the past, it had the ability to lend objects to people. For any auspicious or inauspicious events in human life that required objects, from large items like chairs to small ones like plates and trays (as seen in the *Ji Du Temple Beihai Altar Sacrificial Vessels Stele* of the 13th year of the Zhenyuan era of the Tang dynasty, where "tray" is now mistakenly written as "plate," it should be "tray"). The day before, incense, candles, and paper money were burned as prayers at the tomb. The next day, the objects would be displayed in front of the tomb for people to take as they pleased. Later, those who borrowed often kept the items for a long time without returning them, and therefore, prayers no longer had any effect. I refer to the *Xu Er Tan* (Continued Oddities), which records that in Jiyuan County, the Beihai Temple allowed people to borrow and lend. Those who wanted to borrow gold prayed to the god, and the decision was made by a method involving a certain action (the character 交 is unclear and may need further research). If the god approved, a contract was thrown into the pool in front of the shrine, and silver would float out in the amount requested. The borrower, on the agreed date, prepared the principal and interest, offered a sacrifice of thanks, and threw it in. The gold would disappear, and the original contract would float back up. That gods and people could engage in borrowing and lending is already strange; as for what is inside the tomb, it is no more than funerary objects. How could there be objects for living people to use? Even more inexplicable.

At night, a man from Meng County named Li was tormented by a group of ghosts. He was extremely afraid. Seeing a light ahead, he hurried towards it and found a small house. Inside, there were three rooms, and in one of them, a woman said to him, "If you are afraid of ghosts, you can stay overnight outside this door, and you will be safe. Men and women have different roles, so I dare not ask you to come inside." Li followed her advice and lay down outside the door. The woman then said to him, "At dawn, you should leave early. Tomorrow morning, there will be something important, so please do not disturb me." When morning came, he found himself next to a small tomb, with no house in sight. Soon, several people came and opened the tomb, carrying the coffin away. He asked them, and they replied, "The coffin contains a young woman from a certain family who died unmarried. Her parents are now arranging a marriage in the afterlife with a son from a certain family, so they are moving her coffin to be buried together." Li then understood the meaning of the woman's warning the previous night and felt grateful for the kindness shown to him. He bought some paper money and burned it for her.

In the southwest of Meng County, there are three ditches, and the place is thus called San Dao Gou. Once, a man who was slightly drunk walked at night into one of the ditches and saw a cave door half open. He entered the cave and found a dimly lit lamp, which was about to go out. He picked up the lamp with his hand, and the light of the lamp became bright again. Looking back, he saw a silver ingot behind the lamp. He carried it back home. When he got home and examined it, there were words on the silver ingot that read: "Fifty taels of silver for picking up the lamp." The above are three stories about Meng County, all told by my disciple Song Boyan Huanfang. Song is from Meng County, and I admitted him as a student in the seventh year of Xianfeng. Once, someone on a winter day drank wine and got very drunk, returning home. He walked halfway and suddenly fell down. The village head passed by and saw him, thinking he was dead, so he had two of his followers guard him and himself went into town to report to the official.

That night, heavy snow fell, and the guard, feeling extremely cold, covered the body with a reed mat and took refuge in a cave. Midnight, the drunkard suddenly woke up and immediately returned to his home. When the guard returned, the body was missing, and he feared punishment by the authorities. He then thought of a man from a certain family who had died just a few days ago, and the burial place was not far away. He dug up the body, carried it back, and again covered it with a reed mat, guarding it. The next day, the official came to examine the body. The drunkard heard about it and knew it was for himself. He went to the official and confessed, saying, "I am actually not dead, I just happened to be drunk and sleeping last night." The official asked, "If that's the case, where did the body come from?" He questioned the guard, who could not hide the truth and told what had happened. The official examined the body and found actual wounds on the scalp. He then arrested the woman and asked about the circumstances of her husband's death. It turned out that she had been having an affair with someone and had killed her husband. He then judged the woman according to the law.

An uncle owed his nephew 200,000 units of currency. He said he lacked the means to repay it, but after his death, he would reincarnate as a cow to settle the debt. Soon after, the uncle died, and on the same day, the nephew's family gave birth to a calf. The nephew knew this calf was his uncle reincarnated and did not treat it as an ordinary cow. Whenever he went out, he always took the cow with him. One day, they encountered an old man carrying a basket of pots and jars for sale at the market. The cow accidentally knocked over the old man's goods, breaking them. The old man became angry and hit the cow, but the nephew quickly intervened, saying, "This is my uncle, please don't hit him. I will compensate you for the pots." The old man was surprised by what the nephew said and asked why. The nephew explained the situation, and the old man responded, "Who was your uncle?" The nephew told him the name, and the old man said, "While he was alive, I owed him some money, but I never had the chance to repay it. Now, the value of the broken pots just happens to match the amount I owed. My debt is now settled." He then left happily. On another day, they came across a heavily loaded cart struggling to climb a hill. The driver shouted for help, and the nephew, having no choice, used the cow to assist. After the cart was lifted up, the driver thanked the nephew, saying, "Your kindness is very generous, worth 200,000 units of currency." Upon hearing this, the cow let out a long cry and died.

A villager returned from the city, and on the way, he heard a voice calling out, as if saying "shāo shāo" (carrying). He listened again, and it sounded the same, but upon looking around, he saw no one. After walking just a few steps, he noticed several pieces of white gold on the ground. Overjoyed, he picked them up and placed them in his money bag, which he then carried with him. He arrived at an inn, very hungry, and ate there. When he reached into the bag to take out some money, he felt something moving inside, wriggling. Upon looking inside, he found that all the white gold he had picked up had transformed into tadpoles. Greatly surprised, he threw the bag away. Upon returning home, he told his wife and children about the strange event. One of them opened the bag to inspect it and found that only one tadpole remained. They poured it out, and when it hit the table with a clinking sound, they realized it was actually a piece of white gold, weighing three taels. It seems that the ghosts or gods had used a person to carry this gold to the inn, and these three taels were to reward the effort of carrying the gold. "Shāo shāo" is a term from the northern dialect, meaning to entrust something to someone to carry. These three stories were all narrated by Song Boyan, but it is unknown if they also occurred in Meng County.

In Henan, there was an inn with dozens of peonies planted in its courtyard, their flowers blooming in five colors and as large as a certain size. A man traveling with his family happened to break off a small flower and inserted it into his wife's hair. Suddenly, blood began to flow from the flower's stem, running down her cheek and reaching her shoulder. He was greatly alarmed, realizing that something unusual was happening. He then reattached the flower to its branch and wrapped and sealed it with paper. By night, he suddenly saw dozens of women arriving in a linked procession, scolding him in unison and saying, "You've hurt my sister!" They showed him a little girl with a wound on her neck, and the paper seal was still intact. The man knew they were flower spirits and carefully apologized, saying, "The matter was due to ignorance, not intentional violation. Fortunately, I have received forgiveness. I will write about this on the wall so that future visitors may know, and then there will be no more trouble with picking and breaking." The women nodded in agreement and left together. The man had no further trouble afterward, but his wife developed sores where the blood had touched her, and it took time for them to heal.

A man from Baoying, whose name is unknown, was a butcher. He was very stingy. His mother, who was seventy years old, often thought of meat, but he always refused to give it to her. One day, as he was preparing to slaughter an animal, his mother asked him for some meat again. The butcher became angry and said, "Even if I cut off my finger, I won't give you meat." He spoke these words without realizing it, and suddenly, without knowing, he raised his knife and hissed, cutting off one of his fingers. Painfully, he fainted to the ground. Slowly getting up and wrapping his wound, he sighed and said, "This is the warning from ghosts and gods about my filial impiety." From then on, he repented and served his mother with utmost filial piety, providing her with delicious food without fail. He often showed his hand to others and said, "How can a son not show utmost filial piety to his parents? See my cut finger, it can serve as a warning." The Book of Changes says, "A small punishment can lead to a great warning, which is a blessing for a minor person." Is this what this butcher means?

Wang Ziyu, a native of Hangzhou, was the son of a man who served as an official in Sichuan and died in poverty. Wang Ziyu, born with many illnesses, thus lost his education from a young age, managing only to read and punctuate sentences. He could paint flowers and plants but was not skilled. In his prime years, he did not marry, leading to a life without a family. His maternal family, the Yao clan from Tangxi, took pity on him and invited him to live with them, where he was tasked with teaching the younger son to read. Wang Ziyu thus relied on the Yao family for his residence. Although he did not explicitly claim to abstain from meat, during his ten years with the Yao family, he never touched any meat. When there was nothing to do, he would enter a small room and sit in meditation. On the day of the lunar cycle, he would meditate for an entire day without leaving. The Yao family often had a servant accompany him during his meditation. One time, the servant saw a large shadow outside the window as night approached, and fearing it, he left through the back door. After that, the servant no longer accompanied him, though Wang Ziyu remained unharmed. In the tenth year of the Xianfeng era, when Hangzhou was first captured, Wang Ziyu was indeed trapped in the city but managed to escape calmly and joined the Yao family in seeking refuge in the rural areas around Shimen. One day, he suddenly bathed himself, changed into new clothes, bid farewell to the Yao family, and then sat in meditation until he passed away at the age of forty or so. The Yao family bought a coffin and temporarily buried him in the wild. After the chaos subsided, they planned to relocate his coffin but lost it and could not find it. I believe this man greatly resembled one who had attained the Dao, as seen in his transition from life to death. Did he perhaps achieve what is called "shijie"? According to historical records, those who achieved shijie had coffins that were very light, and upon opening them, only their clothes remained, or they transformed into a bamboo staff. Unfortunately, we could not verify this with his coffin.

My maternal uncle, Mr. Yao Pingquan from Renhe, Guangjin, was a juren in the Daguang Yiyou examination. In his later years, he was appointed as the Confucian schoolmaster of Shangyu County, at the age of seventy-seven. Before he became the schoolmaster, he once dreamed of a place where mountains stood like walls, with a waterfall winding down the mountain. When he arrived in Shangyu and visited Xianggu Cave, it was just as he had dreamed, and he wondered, "Could my previous life have been an old monk in the mountains?" Later, he died in office at the age of eighty-one. The night before he died, a red light entered his house through the main door, seen by all the family. I once wrote a biography of Mr. Yao, detailing these events. The art of divination by splitting characters, known in ancient times as "xiangzi," has a long history. In the Song Dynasty, there was Xie Shi, mentioned in He Xian's *Spring Pond Chronicles*; in the Ming Dynasty, there was Zhang Chengcha, mentioned in [1234] Ji's *Frosty Snow Records*. Many people know about Xie Shi, but fewer know about Zhang Chengcha. The method involves picking any character and making a judgment based on the context. Today, those who claim to practice this skill in the rivers and lakes have pre-determined characters and memorized sayings, which they use to answer questions; how could this possibly be accurate? During the Qianlong era, there was a man named Fan Shixing from Suzhou, who was quite skilled in this art and once came to my county, staying at Ziyang Temple. His words were simple but meaningful, and he earned six hundred coins per day. When he had enough money, he would dismiss his guests and sit quietly, much like Zeng Xin selling his fortune-telling services.

One soldier drew the character "qí" (棋) for divination and asked about his future fortune. Fan said, "In Go, the number of stones increases as the game progresses, while in Xiangqi, the number of pieces decreases as they are captured. Since the character 'qí' refers to wood, not stone, it implies Xiangqi rather than Go. I fear that your family's population will gradually decline!" The soldier said, "That is true. However, what I am asking about is my future prospects." Fan replied, "By your appearance, you seem to be a soldier, which in Xiangqi is a pawn (卒). A pawn can only move one step forward on its own side of the board, but once it crosses the river, it can move in any direction. Therefore, you should leave home to achieve your goals. However, even if you leave home, you will not achieve great success." Another person drew the character "义" (义) for divination and asked about his fate. Fan asked, "How old are you?" The person told him, and Fan said, "Then you were born in the year of the Sheep. The character '义' consists of the radicals for 'sheep' (羊) and 'self' (我), indicating that you will remain unmarried and childless throughout your life."

Even one's wife and children cannot be had, so there is no need to ask about anything else!" Someone else asked Fan about predicting the gender of a pregnancy using the character "风." Fan replied, "Move the central '虫' character to the right side, and it seems to become the character '虺.' The poem says, 'Only the huǐ and the snake are auspicious for women,' so the pregnancy must result in a girl." There are many such examples. When I was young, I heard these old legends from the elders, but I cannot remember them all now. I will write down these three stories, hoping that the name of Fan Shixing might be remembered alongside historical figures like Xie Shi and Zhang Chengcha. Our town has a temple dedicated to Lord Dai, the Savior, with an impressive and grand appearance. The back part of the temple houses the actual body of Lord Dai. During the reign of Emperor Qianlong, a local family named Xu invited a medium (箕仙). The spirit of Lord Dai spoke through the medium, saying, "Our town is facing a great disaster, and I have asked God to allow me to take its place. God has agreed to my request." The people did not understand what was meant, but they only transmitted the divine words to others, urging them to improve their behavior and self-cultivation. In May of that year, a fire broke out in the temple, spreading to the back part and completely destroying everything, including the actual body of Lord Dai.

The Taoist's house was located to the east of the hall, less than one zhang away. Despite the eaves being scorched, they remained unharmed, and everyone finally understood the divine words, marveling even more at the wonder. The entire town gathered to plan the rebuilding of the temple. A wood merchant from Fujian, Shen, brought yellow jujube wood to Zhejiang to sell. Among the wood, there were four trees large enough to be embraced by two people. He either negotiated a price or had already reached an agreement. Shen dreamed of a man dressed in splendid attire with a golden glow on his face, who told him, "These are my pillars, do not sell them to others!" In the morning, he just told someone about his dream when the townspeople from his area happened to arrive and asked, "Do you have large pieces of yellow jujube wood?" Shen replied, "Yes. What will they be used for?" He informed them that it would be for the temple pillars. Shen realized that the person he saw in the dream was none other than Lord Dai, and he donated the wood to help. When the temple was completed, there was still surplus funds, so a garden was opened behind the temple as a place for the townspeople to visit. When I was young, I entered the temple and saw the hall and house were majestic, with four large pillars. The elders still recounted the story to inform others. By the end of the Xianfeng era, the town suffered from war and fire, and the temple of Lord Dai was destroyed again. After the chaos, it was rebuilt, but the beauty of the garden has not returned to its former appearance.

A townsman named Tong Puzhai lived in Xinshi Town, his family wealthy in property. His house was deep and secluded, with a two-story interior, each floor containing five rooms. Since Tong had few dependents, he used the front floor as his bedroom, leaving the back floor empty to store miscellaneous items. One evening, he heard loud, heavy sounds coming from the back building, but he did not find it strange. The next night, he heard voices and chatter from the back building, continuing without pause until dawn. At daybreak, Tong went to investigate. As soon as he stepped onto the stairs, he heard someone inside say, “The host has arrived!” The central door suddenly opened, and an old man emerged, his face ruddy and his hair white, dressed in dignified attire. He invited Tong inside. Tong was naturally courageous, knowing the figure was supernatural, yet he did not fear him. Upon entering, he saw that the tables and desks, though his own old belongings, were now arranged in a completely new fashion. The tables were lined with ritual bronzes, the walls hung with paintings and calligraphy, the shelves were filled with books, and the floor was thickly covered with felt rugs. Chairs and stools were all draped in brocade cushions. In the center, four pearl lanterns were hung, their tassels glittering brilliantly. After sitting down, the old man introduced himself, saying, “My surname is Zhong, my name is Zi Xia, and I am from Huayin, Shaanxi.”
