Every day, three or four pigs were slaughtered before dawn, and the butcher would already be at work with his knife. One day, after the slaughter was finished, the meat was hung on hooks, and suddenly, the pig's hind leg looked very much like a woman's foot, with a flat bottom and fingers, thin and not fitting in the palm. Everyone who saw it found it strange. They then asked where the claw shell was. The claw shell is the thick skin on the pig's foot. The butcher said, "It should still be in the boiling pot." When they went to check, they found a pair of embroidered shoes floating, exquisitely crafted, not something found in the countryside. They fished them out, and everyone passed them around to look at, all marveling at the sight. After this, the store only sold general goods and no longer slaughtered pigs, and it is still there today. My relative's family, the Fan family, lived in the provincial capital of Henan. At one time, there was a hired woman whose home was seventy li away from the city. One day, she brought her young daughter from her home, riding a donkey. In the middle of the journey, she fell off the donkey, picked up her daughter, and continued riding into the city, arriving at the Fan family's residence. The girl was dazed and unresponsive, not speaking or moving. When given food, she could still eat, but when not given, she did not ask for it. Like this for over a month, the members of the Fan family said to her, "Your daughter's illness has lasted so long without healing, why not go back temporarily?" The woman agreed, and so she picked up her daughter and rode the donkey out of the city. When they reached the place where she had fallen off the donkey, the girl suddenly opened her mouth and called out to her mother. When asked, she could answer. From then on, she could talk and laugh during the journey, and by the time they reached home, she was moving normally. It seems that when she fell off the donkey, her soul left her body, and now it has returned.

Lu Liankui, courtesy name Zhongshan, was from Leqing County, Wenzhou, Zhejiang Province. His family enjoyed a comfortable living standard. He was waiting for an official position as a magistrate in Jiangsu Province and came to see me with a letter from his fellow scholar Sun Qinxi. He was a very honest and sincere person, but during his time in Suzhou (Wu), he felt very isolated. He also requested leave to return home, and after a short time, he returned to his post. This became a yearly routine. Later, he returned to my residence and said he would go home to arrange the marriage of his daughter. The next day, I went to see him off, but I did not meet him. After that, we did not hear from each other again. In the twelfth month of the same year, his nephew came to Suzhou and visited me. When asked about the situation, it was revealed that Zhongshan had been killed on the road. He had traveled alone without any servants or attendants. He reached a place in Huangyan County, which was only a hundred or so miles from his home, but the specific location name was forgotten. At that place, Zhongshan rented a small boat and went himself to the market to buy food such as salmon and vegetables.

When he returned, there were already six or seven guests on the boat. He asked about them, and they said they were just passengers. There was nothing to be done, so he temporarily allowed it. However, these six or seven people were all thieves, and the boatman had conspired with them. At night, they moved the boat to a desolate place, killed Zhong Shan, and sank his body in the river, taking his belongings. No one knew about it. After one month, a fisherman pulled his body out of the water, and the wounds were still visible. He then reported it to the authorities. At that time, the magistrate of Huangyan was Mr. Sun Huanbo, who was a capable magistrate. He personally went to examine the body and found a roll of paper in his sock. Although it was faded and illegible, it could still be vaguely discerned. One piece of paper had the three characters "Lu Liankui," and no one knew who he was. He temporarily ordered him to be buried. After another month, Mr. Sun went to Taizhou to pay respects to the governor. He happened to meet with his colleagues for a drink, and they mentioned this matter. One person said, "This must be a person from Yueqing. You think this person is surnamed Lu? No. Lu Lian is a compound surname. There used to be the Lu Lian clan in Yueqing. I once served as the magistrate of Yueqing, so I know about it. And I have acquaintances among the Lu Lian clan." So he wrote a letter to inquire about it. The person he inquired about was Zhong Shan's brother.

The family had long known that Zhongshan would depart from Suzhou on a certain day, but he did not arrive, and they still thought that perhaps he would not return. It was only then that they learned of a change in the middle of the journey. They sent his nephew to investigate, and upon opening the coffin to see the body, it was no longer recognizable. They still hoped it was not true, so they returned to Suzhou to verify the situation. Having confirmed that it was not a mistake, they hurried back to inform Master Sun. Master Sun ordered his servants to capture the murderer, but they could not find him. After a long time, someone said that a certain person had sold court jewelry in the market. How could there be such an item in the countryside? This was suspicious. Master Sun sent someone to capture him. The pursuers went at night, and there were ghostly sounds leading the way. When they arrived at his home, six or seven people were there, knowing the incident had occurred, they were preparing to leave, and if they delayed, they would miss their chance. They tied them up and took them to the authorities, who all confessed, and they were executed to appease Zhongshan. Zhongshan was a true gentleman, to suffer such a cruel fate is incomprehensible, and the difficulty of traveling can also be seen. I went to welcome my mother-in-law after my elder brother Fu Ning's death, and also passed through this area.

Arriving at Taizhou Prefecture, I found that Chen Jun Lusheng was just then governing the prefecture. He provided me with a meal at the magistrate's office and also arranged for a boat. The boatman said, "We must wait until the tide comes at the second watch of the night before we can depart." So I boarded the boat after dinner. Chen Jun personally saw me off, but I tried to decline his accompaniment and he would not allow it. When I reached the boat first, I found it already full. I inquired and was told that these were also people taking the boat for free. At that time, I had only two attendants with me—one was following me at the prefectural office, and the other was left to guard the boat, able only to protect my belongings, but unable to compete with them. I was at a loss, without a plan, when Chen Jun arrived. He angrily said, "Who are these people?" At that time, the region of Jiantong had just emerged from a period of great disorder, and bandits were numerous. Therefore, whenever the magistrate traveled, he always had personal soldiers accompanying him. Chen Jun then ordered his soldiers to drive all of them ashore, and with four of his soldiers, he escorted me to Huangyan. For Taizhou and Wenzhou are remote areas, and boats are scarce. Ordinary travelers who hired a boat always had people who would ride it for free. These were not all bandits, certainly, but their behavior was quite difficult to endure. Without the magistrate personally seeing me off, how could they have been driven away? I am writing this account here so that those who serve may know what to be cautious of.

Jiaxing Yao Endian, courtesy name Shuyi, served as an official in the Office of the Secretariat. Yao was a capable individual with a sincere and honest character. Even as a young man, he kept his promises and was careful in his dealings. While in the capital, he was highly regarded by his fellow townspeople, who entrusted him with many tasks without a break. In the year 1888, he suddenly realized that he was about to die. He arranged for all the matters entrusted to him, everything in an orderly manner. At the time, his father, Fu Xian, was serving as the Inspector of Salt Administration in the region of Changlu, so his mother and wife were in Tianjin. Yao wrote a letter informing them of his impending death and requested that they come to the capital to bid him farewell. Upon receiving the letter, his mother and wife were greatly shocked and rushed to the capital. Although Yao had a minor illness, it was not something to be overly concerned about, yet he was eagerly awaiting his death. By the end of April, he instructed his gatekeeper: "On the sixth day of the fifth lunar month at the hour of the snake, Vice-Chancellor Xia will come to deliver an imperial decree. Inform me immediately, do not delay!" The family found his words strange. The Vice-Chancellor referred to was Xia Zirong, also known as Xia Jun and Tongshan. Although Xia and Yao were from the same hometown, they had only exchanged name cards during the New Year's greetings in the first month of the year; otherwise, they did not associate with each other. On the appointed day, Yao's condition worsened slightly. As the hour of the snake approached, there was an announcement that Vice-Chancellor Xia had arrived. The gatekeeper had just invited him in when Yao had already stopped breathing.

When Zisong arrived, he did not come for the sake of the Yao family; rather, it was because his father-in-law, Zhong Junsheng, the scholar-official, had become extremely close with Zisong. On that day, Xia went to visit Zhong, but Zhong had just gone to see Shuyi who was ill, and the gatekeeper refused him entry. Xia asked where Zhong had gone, and was told. Because Xia had business and wanted to see Zhong, he then went to the Yao family. Unbeknownst to him, as he had not yet sat down, the sound of weeping inside had already begun. Everyone present was surprised and wondered if Zisong's arrival happened to match what Shuyi had said, and what message was being conveyed? Why must this message be transmitted by Xia the Attendant? Shuyi's death made Zisong also seem vulnerable. However, Zisong was indeed unharmed, and that winter, he was immediately appointed as the provincial educational commissioner of Jiangsu. Until the autumn of Gengchen, Zisong ultimately passed away in the office of the governor in Jiangyin, less than three years after Shuyi's death. Ancient records often mention Liu Zhen, Xu Gan, and Wang Can, all of whom became heavenly Secretaries, and Wang Maohong became the heavenly Minister of the Imperial Secretariat. There are many such accounts in historical records. Zisong began his career as a literary official, served in various high-ranking positions, and participated in the completion of important tasks. After his death, he became a revered spirit, which is not surprising. Shuyi's words and deeds did not fail to make him a gentleman, and perhaps his death had a purpose? At that time, Zisong was still in the world, yet the divine message was conveyed through him, which is inexplicable. Could his spiritual awareness have already returned to heaven?

South of the city gates of Qingpu County, about twenty li away, there is a temple called Juehai, which originally had no monks. During the reign of Emperor Tongzhi, a monk suddenly appeared, naked and without clothing, covering himself only with a torn blanket. It was the height of winter, and he lay on the ground for several days without moving. Those who saw him pitied him, and some offered him money and rice, but he refused. One day, he suddenly put on his torn blanket and walked out. He saw an elderly woman who was blind in both eyes. The monk took a bowl of water from the stream and gave it to her, saying, "Try washing your eyes with this." She washed her eyes as instructed, and her blindness was cured; she could see again. Another young man had a twisted left foot. The monk touched it and said, "Straighten it, straighten it." Immediately, the young man's foot straightened, just like anyone else's. News of this spread far and wide, and people called him a divine monk. Every day, dozens of people came to him seeking medical help. Sometimes he healed them, and sometimes he did not, but whenever he did, his treatments were always effective. He stayed at the temple for more than a month before disappearing without a trace. Changshan Mountain in Qingpu County is one of the Nine Peaks. On the northeast corner of the mountain, there is a flat stone with a mark in the middle, about a foot long and several inches wide. Its origin is unknown, and no one knows when it was carved. Recently, a geomancer entered the mountain to find a burial site for someone. By chance, he placed his compass on this stone, and it aligned perfectly with the mark. The needle in the compass began to spin, pointing not south but north. The geomancer recognized that something unusual was happening. As he slowly lifted the compass, the needle continued to waver inside the compass until it was several feet away from the stone, at which point it returned to its normal position. Some say that there must be a magnet beneath the stone.

In Zhang Liantang Town of Qingpu, there was a man of a certain surname who bought a watermelon weighing over twenty pounds. He intended to cut and eat it, but the melon was rotating unpredictably on the table, and he was afraid to cut it. Someone nearby took the knife and cut it with a crisp sound, dividing the melon into two pieces. Inside, there was a centipede about a foot long and an inch wide, already cut into two segments. There was a faint light inside the melon, and when it was broken open, dozens of pearls were found. In the seventh month of the year Gengchen (1880) during the Guangxu era, a farmer's house near Shuipu Bridge in Lou county had a cow give birth to a calf with six legs and two tails. The four legs were like those of a normal cow, while the two additional legs were under the belly, very short. Each tail had its own anus, and when the calf defecated, both tails would release waste simultaneously. The owner found it strange and did not keep it. A person from the north bought it with six silver coins, but no one knew what it was used for.

A friend from a store in Ningbo, at the end of the year, went to collect debts from various villages. He shouldered a money pouch and held a ledger in hand, all written with the name of his store. Upon returning, the pouch contained three hundred and more silver dollars, and five hundred and more copper coins. It was already late in the evening, and he worried about encountering a robber, so he planned to find a place to stay but suffered from the lack of an inn to stay at. After walking about a mile, he saw a thatched cottage and knocked on the door to ask for lodging. The cottage was occupied by a mother and daughter. The mother firmly refused, but the daughter said, "This guest is at the end of his journey at dusk. Where else can he go? My mother and I can share a bed. What harm is there in letting him stay on one bed?" The mother followed her daughter's suggestion. The daughter also prepared food for him. The guest ate, then lay down with the money pouch as a pillow. At midnight, the daughter suddenly came to ask to share the bed. The guest firmly rejected her, but the daughter would not leave. The guest, having no choice, agreed to talk all night.

They talked until cockcrow, when the woman urged the guest to rise, saying, "My father has returned; you must leave quickly." Startled, the guest got up and hurried out. After walking more than a hundred steps, he remembered that he had not taken his money bag, and rushed back to retrieve it. Upon arriving, there was no thatched hut in sight, only two coffins piled up. Greatly surprised, he examined them carefully and saw that one corner of his money bag was still visible outside. Just as he was hesitating, a man suddenly appeared and asked the guest, "What are you doing here? Are you a grave robber? These two coffins, one is my wife, the other is my daughter." The guest then recounted the details of his stay that night, but remained silent about the daughter’s private approach to him. He also told the man, "In my bag, there are several foreign coins and several silver coins. The bag and the ledger all have the store’s name on them. Please check them. If there is any discrepancy, I will take the responsibility." The man then opened the coffins, and indeed, everything was as the guest had said. He allowed the guest to take everything and return.

In the town of Huangqiao in Taixing County, there lived a man surnamed He, who was also a scion of an old family. He married a daughter of the Sheng family from Jingjiang, a woman who was both beautiful and virtuous. However, He had a concubine, and thus came to harbor hatred toward his wife, daily indulging in cruelty and oppression. The wife endured it all without complaint. He was addicted to opium, and even at the third watch of the night, when others were asleep, he remained awake, compelling his wife to serve him tea and soup. If she did not please him, he would randomly stab her with the rod of the opium pipe, leaving her entire body without an inch of intact skin. Unable to bear the pain any longer, she hanged herself. Her father was indeed a man of virtue, and he did not report the matter to the authorities, so He escaped without consequence. One night, he suddenly saw his wife appear before him, covered in blood. She spoke to him, saying: "I served you without any moral failure—why have you been so cruel? Now I have obtained justice from the gods!" After speaking, she vanished. Months later, He suddenly fell victim to a strange illness. His ten fingers first began to itch; when he scratched them, the itching worsened. He then bit them with his teeth, and his fingers were broken piece by piece. In turn, the affliction spread to his shoulders and arms, then to his thighs and feet. Every place his mouth touched, he bit, and blood stained his bed and bedding. He cried out day and night, and after two months of suffering, he died.

In the eastern village of Luxiaozhuang, Taixing, there was a man named Lu who had borrowed money from a wealthy family. He had already repaid the principal but still owed one thousand in interest. Being poor and unable to repay, he requested an exemption. However, the wealthy son insisted on not allowing it and finally took the full amount. Lu was greatly resentful and then thought about the pile of grass outside his field and garden, which was several zhang tall. If sold, it would be worth ten thousand. So, at night, he climbed to the top of the mound, opened its cover, and placed fire inside. For several days, the grass did not burn. He consulted his wife, who said, "Last night at the fifth watch, there was a light rain, so it did not burn. It should be placed again." So, he climbed to the top again, opened it, and looked inside. Then, it was burned by the fire, and it was already empty. Because its cover was wet with rain, the fire could not penetrate.

When the fire was lifted, it immediately rose up, and Mr. Lu fell into it. In an instant, the intense flames flew up, burning both Mr. Lu and the grass to ashes. His wife ran to save him but it was too late. Mr. Chen Ziyu, the scholar, told me this story and said, "Being stingy with small things leads to losing the bigger picture; one should take the example of the rich man. Harming others harms oneself; one should take the example of Mr. Lu." An old scholar from Taixing, Mr. He Xuan Tai, once had business in Suqian and rented a cart to travel. The driver of the cart had his face covered with knife marks, like a grid pattern. When asked about it, he replied, "I dare not hide it; ten years ago, I was a big thief. There was a rich family who married a new bride, and the wedding party was very grand. I went with seven other thieves to rob her. At midnight, we entered the room, and the new bride was young and beautiful, only sixteen or seventeen years old. She shouted, 'Do not frighten my husband; I will give you silver and money.' The others waited outside the window. They saw her go behind the bed as if she was taking something, and suddenly, a knife flew out, sticking into the door. The man also flew out with the knife. The others knew they could not fight her and climbed onto the roof to flee."

I followed shortly after, just reaching the roof ridge, when he caught up with me and kicked me with his foot. I was unable to move a single part of my body and cried out in despair, begging for mercy. The new wife used a knife to cut my face, making horizontal and vertical cuts, each about ten. She then said, "Today is my wedding night, so I will temporarily spare your life." She picked me up and threw me outside the wall. Fortunately, I did not die, and thus changed my behavior. Mr. Zhang, a man from Shandong, was cooling off outside the village during a certain year in June. Late at night, as he was about to go to bed, someone suddenly appeared from the grass. He looked at it and saw that it was the deceased servant. He scolded him and said, "I treated you not poorly, why are you haunting me now?" The servant replied, "No, it is not so. I serve in the underworld. Now I have a document to summon the master, in memory of old kindness, so I came to warn first." The document lists thirty people in total, with the master's name first on the list. I moved his name to the end. They will summon one person per day, extending for a month. This is how I repay the kindness." After speaking, he disappeared.

The old man sighed and said, "I am going to die!" Reflecting on his life, he thought, "My food and clothing are roughly sufficient, and my marriages are all completed. What regret would there be in dying?" However, he remembered that he had once acted as a matchmaker for a son of a certain family. This son was alone and poor, lacking the funds for marriage. The family of the girl always had the intention to back out of the marriage. Because he was alive, he dared not speak. What will happen after I die? The next day, he summoned all his sons and told them, "The son of a certain family, his father was alive, once borrowed eighty thousand taels of silver from me. Because of trust, there was no written contract. Now I am old, and the borrowed money has not been returned for a long time, how can I face my old friend in the afterlife?" They all said, "Yes." He sent the money back by carriage and arranged a wedding date for the son of the old friend, informing the family of the girl. The family of the girl had no reason to refuse, so the marriage was completed. The old man said happily, "My affairs are complete." After a month, he was still alive. And his servant came to see him, congratulating him, "The master is not going to die, there is a subsequent edict in the afterlife, removing the master's name."

Suzhou has six gates, but only Pan Gate has the fewest residents. The area outside the gate is also devoid of shops and markets, making it extremely desolate. Hence, the local saying calls it "Cold Water Pan Gate." In the third year of the Xianfeng era (1853), Nanjing fell, and Suzhou also went on alert. All city gates were closed at sunset, but Pan Gate closed even earlier. A certain scholar left Pan Gate a few days before the Qingming Festival to visit his ancestral graves in the countryside. When he returned, the gate was already closed. While hesitating, two people arrived shortly after. Seeing their attire, they seemed to be officials. They smiled and asked, "Sir, you can't get in now. Do you have a place to stay outside the city?" The scholar replied, "No." They said, "Our home is not far. Why not come and stay for the night?" The scholar was pleased and followed them. They walked for half a li and arrived at their house. Together, they entered the study and said, "You haven't had your evening meal yet. Are you hungry?" They then brought out a bowl of tea and two trays of snacks, earnestly encouraging him to eat. After eating, they sat and chatted, but they never mentioned sleeping, and there were no beds or cots in the room. Soon, they heard people from neighboring houses talking loudly. The scholar asked what was happening, and they replied, "A neighbor has a sick person, and we fear he will not survive."

At the appointed hour, a man drew a document from his sleeve and said to the scholar, "Please blow a breath into this!" The scholar complied, and the man then put the document back into his sleeve. He stood up and said, "We have official business to attend to and should leave immediately. You, sir, should stay here and enter the city at dawn." He added, "When we depart, we will differ from ordinary people, but you need not be afraid." They then walked out, reached the courtyard, stretched and stomped their feet, and their bodies suddenly grew taller. In the blink of an eye, they were taller than the house and leaped away from the roof. The other man followed, and his appearance was the same. The scholar was extremely frightened, not knowing what was happening. Soon after, he heard crying and realized that the patient had died. The study was the home of the deceased. Someone entered the room, saw them, and suspected they were thieves. They also noticed a teapot and a stack of books on the table and said, "The items in the inner room were lost earlier. Did you steal them?" They were about to arrest them, but the scholar strongly defended himself and managed to escape. At dawn, he staggered back home. The encounter the scholar had was likely the common folk's so-called "Mara," wasn't it? The document they held required a living person to blow upon it. What is the reason for this? And how could they possibly get all living people to blow on it? Matters of the underworld are indeed beyond comprehension.

A gentleman from Gao You, who had once served as a county magistrate in Sichuan, retired due to illness during the early years of the Xianfeng Emperor's reign. One night, he was sitting alone in his study when suddenly someone entered the room and asked to accompany him on a journey. When he inquired about the destination, the person replied, "To Sichuan." The gentleman followed him out, and upon exiting the door, they were carried by a sedan chair, traveling at an incredible speed. In a moment, they arrived at the riverside, where a boat was already waiting. They boarded the boat and untied the ropes, and in an instant, they had reached Jiujiang. They then left the boat and traveled by land, passing through Jiangxi and Hubei, and finally arrived in Sichuan. The distance covered was several thousand miles, yet the entire journey took no more than the time it takes to eat a meal. Upon entering the city, they arrived at a government office, where they were led to sit. In the west wing, there were former officials, a certain governor and a certain prefect, all looking sad and silent when asked. Soon, it was announced that the king had emerged from the throne room and immediately summoned the gentleman in, asking, "Do you know about this case?" The gentleman replied, "Yes, I do."

In this case, the judicial official and the magistrate had accepted bribes, leading to the wrongful execution of several people. A certain official strenuously opposed this but failed, and thus resigned from office under the pretense of illness. He explained the full story, and the ruler said, “Your integrity is commendable.” He then ordered him to be sent back. Soon after, it was heard that the judicial official and the magistrate had been summoned, and they were reprimanded severely. Looking back, these two men had been reduced in size, appearing like infants. Shortly thereafter, a barred carriage carried the two men out. The official also emerged. He met a friend, someone he had once served alongside in Sichuan. The friend said, “I am about to take up a new post and will not have the chance to reunite with an old friend—how regrettable!” He asked, “What office do you now hold?” The official replied, “I am the city god of Yangzhou Prefecture.” Pointing to the men in the barred carriage, he said, “The case of某某 was handled by me. I cannot remain here for long, but I and you will not be separated for long. On a certain day in July next year, you will be the city god of a certain place, and we two will once again serve as colleagues.” After speaking, he took his leave. The official followed his old path home, but by then, he had already been dead for more than a day. At that moment, he revived. He told others of the events, saying that he would not live much longer, though people still did not believe him. When the time came the following year, he indeed passed away peacefully, without illness.

Hangzhou has no brothels. Occasionally there are courtesans, but without an introducer, one cannot enter their door. Therefore, Hangzhou's proverb calls it the "iron threshold." A certain man from Ningbo, who enjoyed pleasure-seeking, came to the provincial capital for the examination. The exam period was still early, and he was sitting idly, feeling bored. He suddenly felt a desire to seek pleasure and asked a friend to guide him to a house. The room had a young woman sitting and embroidering, with an old woman sitting beside her. When asked, they were revealed to be mother and daughter. The man looked at the young woman, whose skin was like jade, with a slender waist and delicate feet, charming and lovely. He then gave gold to the old woman to prepare food and wine for intimate drinking. After a while, the candle was about to burn down, and his friend bid farewell. The old woman and the young woman guided the guest into the room, and the old woman also left. The man invited the young woman to sit beside him, and in the lamp light, he fondled and teased her, about to loosen her clothes. The young woman was very embarrassed, even to the point of crying. The man asked, "Why do you behave like this?" The young woman replied, "My father and brothers all ranked among the scholars, and my uncle and husband were also scholars. Because they all died young, leaving me, a weak and lonely girl, in this situation. To think of the deceased, how could I not be heartbroken?" The man heard this and was also moved, saying, "Then why do you willingly do this? How many times have you done this?" The young woman said, "My mother has no food, and she often urges me to do this. To avoid going against my mother's wishes, I勉强相从. Today is only my first time." The man called out, "Since you wish to remain chaste, how dare I defile you?" He then gave her several pieces of gold, saying, "What I carry is not much, just to supplement the sweet and savory offerings for the elders. I will leave now."

Immediately, he went out through the back door, and the night was dimly lit, making it impossible to recognize the path. It was during a time of great turmoil, and the city also had many neglected and remote areas. After trudging for a long time, he suddenly saw a small house, barely revealing a light, with a sound of recitation inside. He then knocked on the door to ask for directions. The door opened, and he was invited in; it turned out to be an old man. A young man was sitting at a desk writing. The old man introduced himself by saying his surname, and the young man was his son. "The chief examiner of this year's examination, Mr. [name], I have known him for a long time, so I set this question to make my son guess what he likes and write accordingly, but it's still not enough," the old man said. He thought his son's writing was excellent and said, "My son's writing is beyond my ability. The old man still thinks it's not good enough?" The old man replied, "Yes. If you don't believe me, let me try to revise it."

The scholar immediately took up his pen and revised about a hundred words at his desk, saying, "Like this, it might be acceptable!" I read it again and was deeply impressed, exclaiming, "This old man is truly a master of revision!" I continued to recite and praise it. The old man smiled and said, "Since you love it, may I respectfully offer it to you as a gift?" I was very pleased and immediately read it by the lamp. Exhausted, I dozed off and soon fell into a deep sleep. When I woke up, the morning sun had already risen. I found myself at the foot of a hillock, with no house or shelter in sight, realizing that I had encountered a spirit. I then thought about the family of the woman I had visited yesterday, who shared the same surname as the old man—could he be her uncle or husband? I returned to the woman's family and inquired, and the description matched, filling me with dread. I gave her mother several dozen gold pieces and told her, "Your daughter is chaste; do not force her into indecent behavior." When I entered the examination hall, I unexpectedly encountered this question and promptly wrote the text in my scroll. When the list of winners was announced, I was the top scorer. I then gave a thousand gold pieces to the family.

In Suzhou, on Shantang Street, there were two men named A and B. A was inherently honest and straightforward, while B was promiscuous and enjoyed licentious activities. One evening, they had a drinking party at a friend's place, and B returned home first. As he walked to Wende Bridge, he saw a small house with faint light visible from the outside. Upon closer inspection, he noticed that all the doors from the main gate to the inner room were open, but no one was inside. Surprised, he went in to take a look. Inside, he saw a woman sitting on the bed, with a beautiful appearance and abundant charms, and she was exceptionally beautiful. The woman invited B to sit next to her and said, "Night travel is indeed hard. The bedding here is ready-made. Why not rest a little?" She helped B take off his outer clothes and hung them on a clothes rack. Just as B was about to lie down, suddenly someone arrived with a lamp. The woman was startled and avoided the person, who turned out to be A. A saw B sitting dazedly on the bridge railing and asked, "What are you doing here?" B replied, "You spoiled my good time." A laughed and said, "What good time is there on this bridge?" B then realized that he was sitting on Wende Bridge, with his clothes right beside him. The woman and the house he had seen earlier had disappeared. He understood that he had encountered a ghost and staggered back home. If not for A's arrival, B would have been in grave danger.

A Man from Wujiang A man from Wujiang, named Gu, came to Suzhou to take the imperial examination and stayed at the side of Jili Bridge. Nearby was a teahouse called Jin Feng Lou. After dinner, with nothing to do, he occasionally went to drink tea. Upon arriving, he found the seats were full. Only one table in the corner of the room, occupied by an old woman and a young woman, had no other guests. Gu sat down to join them. The old woman talked idly with him, and they got along quite well. The old woman said, "This place is boring. If you are interested, why not come to my house? I will prepare good tea for you." Gu happily agreed. After arriving, she led him upstairs, where the decorations were also very elegant. At that time, it was the late years of the Daoguang reign, and opium smoking had become widespread. Opium paraphernalia was present on the bed. She invited him to try it. He declined, saying he was not accustomed to it. The old woman said, "Just for fun. What harm will it do?" She ordered the young woman to prepare the opium for the guest. As soon as he sat down on the bed, the old woman removed his shoes and said, "You can sleep or sit as you please, there are no restrictions. I have something to do and will leave." Not long after, he heard a loud knocking at the door downstairs. The young woman quickly got up and went downstairs. Gu became suspicious and followed behind her downstairs, hiding behind the door. The young woman opened the door, and then more than thirty men rushed in. They asked where the person was. The young woman said, "He is upstairs." They all went upstairs. Gu took the opportunity to escape. In Suzhou, it was common for women to be used in a certain way. Young men often mistakenly entered these places. They would take all his belongings and force him to write a promissory note, sometimes tens of thousands, sometimes hundreds of thousands, only then would they let him go. This is known as the "Immortal Jump."

The Sea Hong Temple in Suzhou is also an ancient temple. There was a man named Mr. A who lived in Xiu Xian Lane, very close to the temple, and he frequently visited the temple monk. One winter day, Mr. A got up early with nothing to do and went to visit the monk for casual conversation. He entered the temple gate, but no one was at the door, so he went in. He reached the last courtyard and heard the monk calling for a pair of scissors from the next courtyard. Suddenly, a woman's voice responded. From behind a partition, he peeped and saw the monk wearing a large red silk fox-white cloak, standing under the plum blossoms, with more than ten people with white faces and black eyebrows arranged in a circle around him. Greatly startled, he hurried out. Just as he arrived home, the monk sent someone with a box as a gift. When he opened it, he saw one hundred taels of white gold and a sharp knife. Mr. A understood his intention, accepted the gold, returned the knife, and wrapped it in paper and put it back in the box. He then never dared to go back. This incident occurred during the late years of the Xianfeng era. During the Tongzhi era, the temple monk was disgraced for some reason, and the officials expelled him, changing the temple into a grain commissioner's office. Mr. A then told people about the incident.

The Mr. Zheng Mengbai of Wucheng, during the reign of Emperor Daoguang, was a high-ranking official (藩司) who was in mourning (居忧). In the year Jia Chen (甲辰), after completing his mourning period (服阕), he set out to present himself before the emperor (入觐) and was about to reach Beijing (京师) when he stopped at Changxin Village (长新店). Upon entering his room (甫入室), a guest sought to see him (有客求见). Seeing the guest's visiting card (视其名刺), he recognized the guest as an old friend (则故人也). He warmly invited the guest in (欣然延入) and they sat and talked for a long time before the guest left (坐谈良久而去). Shortly after (未几), the guest sought to see him again (外报此客又求见). He suspected that the guest might still have something to say (疑其尚有未尽之辞) and went out to see him again (复出见之). However, the guest had nothing to say (则殊无所言) and sat for a long time before leaving (又坐良久而去). Mr. Zheng was leaning on a small pillow (隐囊) and planning to take a brief rest (方谋小憩) when the guest sought to see him again (外报此客又至). Feeling tired and declining (厌而辞焉), he eventually saw the guest (客固以请，不得已，又见之). The guest asked, "Why do you keep coming? What do you wish to teach me?" (君数来，将何以教我邪). The guest replied, "Who do you think I am?" (君视我谁也?). Upon seeing him, Mr. Zheng realized that the guest was not the previous one, but a man with a gaunt face, long beard reaching his abdomen, who seemed like a being from the realm of immortals (殆神仙中人也). He was startled and felt strange, not knowing what to say (悚然异之，不知所云). The guest continued, "The first time you saw me, it was someone else; the second time you saw me, it was not that person but me (仆也). I wanted to tell you something (仆欲有言于君), so I transformed my form to seek you out (故幻其形以求见). I thought to myself that it was inappropriate to speak deeply with someone I barely knew (又自惟交浅言深), so I wanted to speak but still held back (故欲言而仍止). Afterward, I reflected that this matter was of great importance (此事所系甚大), and I dared not remain silent, so I came to see you again (不敢不言，故又来求见也)." Mr. Zheng asked, "What do you wish to say?" (问将何言). The guest replied, "I am a celestial fox (天狐), and I have heard the discussions of the heavenly bureaucracy (天曹) that a great calamity (劫运) is about to befall the world (世间劫运将至)."

Upon this occasion of your entering the capital, you will soon be appointed to a high military or administrative post. The fate of millions of lives will rest in your hands, so please be extremely cautious. After the words were spoken, the master was lost in thought for a long time. In that year, he was appointed as the governor of Shaanxi, then transferred to Yunnan, and soon after moved to Guangxi. Meanwhile, the rebellion led by Hong Xiuquan began to rise. At its early stages, it could still have been suppressed. The master, reflecting on the advice given, hesitated to act rashly and thus procrastinated, leading to disaster that spread throughout the land. If this fox was willing to seek an audience repeatedly, why not speak directly and clearly, rather than using ambiguous language that could lead to misunderstanding? Perhaps it was also due to the inevitable misfortune of the "nine calamities and six disasters," which could not be avoided? Someone said that there was a house outside the Xuanwu Gate in the capital, which had been plagued by a fox spirit. When the master lived there, the disturbances ceased. But after he left, the spirit returned. Someone who was angry questioned the fox, and the fox replied, "Millions of lives are all in his hands, how could we not fear him?" This statement is likely a distorted version of the story, not the actual events. What I heard from my fellow villagers is different. My respected elder once served in the master's household, and my elder brother, who was a magistrate, also served in his entourage. Among his descendants, there were those who passed the same imperial examination with me during the Daoguang era, and I met him once. The master was indeed a man of virtue and had always possessed administrative talent. In his later years, when he was appointed to govern a large territory, he was severely punished and criticized by the world, illustrating the difficulties of being a governor of a region.

In the eastern gate of Baoying City there stood a house, once belonging to the Liu family. My fellow examinee, Grand Secretary Wang Wenzhen, used to study in this house when he was young. Once, while eating pepper, he saw the green onions and found them lovely, so he took one sprig and planted it in the ground, saying, "If I ever achieve success in my studies, then this branch will live." Later, the pepper plant indeed took root. After more than thirty years, it grew into a large tree, taller than the eaves of the house. Wang Wenzhen passed the provincial examination in the year Bingwu of the Daoguang era, became a jinshi in the year Gengxu, and during the reign of Emperor Tongzhi was promoted from the position of compiler to the judicial official of Zhejiang, then soon transferred to serve as governor of Guangdong, and later was appointed governor of Fujian. Meanwhile, this house also came to belong to the Wang family, and the tree still stands, now flourishing more than it ever did during the Liu family’s time. All said, "This is an omen of good fortune for the Wang family." After some time, however, some people remarked that pepper plants do not retain their seeds, and once the pod opens, the seeds fall immediately. This was feared to be harmful to the descendants, so they proposed cutting down the tree. My eldest daughter, who was the wife of Wang Wenzhen’s second son, strongly opposed the idea, but could not prevent it. Before two years had passed, Wang Wenzhen returned from Taiwan, but died in Fuzhou; his wife, Lady Liu, died two months before him. The outcome was clearly evident.

The legal code states: "If a man has a wife and designates a concubine as his wife, he shall be sentenced to ninety strokes of the rod." If the legal wife is deceased, the law does not explicitly prohibit designating a concubine as the wife. However, in my family or social circle, there was a case where someone wanted to designate a concubine as his wife after the legal wife's death. The family sought the opinion of my maternal uncle, Mr. Yao Pingquan, to decide the matter, offering him a thousand gold pieces as a reward, but he refused. When I was in Henan, I heard that a certain Mr. Tao, an official, had four concubines after his wife's death. One of the concubines was older and managed all household affairs, and there were no complaints from inside or outside the household. The official intended to designate this concubine as his wife. Once the decision was made, he ordered a seamstress to make the official robes for the concubine. When the robes were completed, the official sent his younger brother to deliver them personally to the concubine, indicating his respect for her. The concubine, seated, did not rise to greet her brother-in-law, instead glancing at him and saying, "Put it on the table." The younger brother left in anger, telling his older brother, "This person has just gained power and is being so disrespectful. Can we still ask him anything in the future? If you consider her your wife, I do not consider her my sister-in-law." He strongly opposed the decision, ultimately leading to the cancellation of the plan. However, this concubine was usually humble and courteous, so it is unclear why she acted this way, possibly because she could not bear such a great honor, and thus the spirits took her soul. In my household, there was a maid who was initially married to a nobleman as a concubine but soon became his legal wife, wearing the robes of the first rank.

This maid is from Liuhe. During the Xianfeng era, Liuhe fell, and she came to Suzhou with her parents, entering the refugee relief station. My wife, Mrs. Yao, bought her when she was only seven or eight years old. Her parents' fate remains unknown, but she often inquires about their safety. She is one of the few with a human heart among maids and concubines, and it is fitting that she has such good fortune. In the western countryside of Huxi Guan, a large snake roamed along the riverbank, and many people saw it but dared not capture it. During the Gengchen year of the Guangxu era, many people in the area developed sores. Someone said, "This is due to snake poison." They encouraged those who could catch it. In the city, they found three beggars: A is the master, and B and C are his disciples. They asked for a lot of money, which the villagers pooled together to give them. After arriving, they discovered the snake's den. A ordered B and C to stand on either side, and opened his basket himself, which contained countless centipedes. A ate them all. Soon, from head to toe, he was swollen. A closed his eyes and regulated his breath, and after a while, the swelling subsided, but only his right index and middle fingers became as thick as thighs. He then used these two fingers to probe into the den and, with force, pulled it out. B and C also pulled his elbows to help. As his fingers came out, the snake followed and was already dead, but it still bit A's fingers and wouldn't let go. B and C removed the snake and washed it with medicinal water, and his two fingers soon returned to normal. The snake was about a zhang long and twice as thick as a human arm, its color dark as charcoal. They gathered firewood and burned it, and the smell spread for several li. These three beggars' skills were also divine.

A woman from a certain family in Shanghai, having learned embroidery from a neighboring old woman since childhood, was affectionately called "乾阿{弥女}" by her. When she grew older, the old woman still treated her like a child, showing excessive care and concern for her, even more so than her own mother. Once, the girl fell ill, and her menstrual cycle ceased. The old woman was deeply worried and told the girl’s mother, “There are no good doctors in Shanghai. The best doctors in the world are the Westerners. The Westerners have established a clinic in a certain place outside the city, where they provide medical treatment. Why don’t we go there to seek a cure?” The mother agreed. They immediately arranged for the old woman to accompany the girl, hiring two sedan chairs for the journey. They left in the morning and returned in the afternoon. That evening, the girl hanged herself. Her mother wept bitterly, and the old woman wept bitterly as well, but the mother was deeply suspicious. She secretly called the sedan chair bearers to question them. They replied, “On that day, just as we were leaving the city, the old woman and the girl switched to an East Asian small carriage, leaving us to wait at the city gate, and we did not know where they went.” The mother’s suspicions grew, so she instructed the bearers to investigate the driver of the East Asian carriage. The driver said, “The old woman and the girl rode my carriage to a house with a small gate. When we knocked, an old woman came out, holding a bodhi seed and reciting Buddhist mantras, leading them inside. She then paid me and dismissed us, and I do not know what happened afterward.” Despite their efforts to investigate, they found no clues. It is said that in Shanghai, there are often secret rooms where men and women meet for illicit encounters. Dishonest youths and promiscuous girls openly engage in sexual activities during the day, and they widely use matchmakers to arrange such meetings. It is suspected that this girl fell into one of these secret rooms, lost her chastity, and, overcome with shame, took her own life.

At the beginning of Suzhou's restoration, there was a poor scholar who had fled from bandits and returned home, alone and without a place to stay, constantly lodging with those he knew. Suddenly, someone invited him to tutor his son, and the scholar, pleased, accepted the position. At first, they got along very well. Before a month had passed, the host said to the scholar, "Tomorrow, there will be matters at my home, and our house is small. Even the study room must be cleaned to receive guests. I beg you, sir, to return for one day." The scholar replied, "Yes," and again took lodging with the family he knew, returning the next day. After another month had passed, the host spoke to him as before, and the scholar, having no choice, complied. After yet another month, the host again spoke to him in the same way, and the scholar finally told the truth, saying, "I have no place to stay in the city, and have only been lodging with those I know. However, that family also has no extra rooms, and my repeated visits are truly inconvenient. Since this study room is meant for guests, then regardless of where it is, as long as it can hold a single mat, I would not refuse even a place under the eaves." The host hesitated for a long time, then said, "You know my house is small. If there is no other choice, there is a room behind, which has long been used for storing firewood and kindling. I will now clean it slightly and lay out a mat for you there—would that be acceptable?" The scholar said, "That would be very good."

That evening, just after dusk, the host led him into his room and said, "Please lock the door and sleep deeply, Mr. Scholar. If you hear anything, do not peek or look, as it would be inappropriate for both of us." The scholar nodded respectfully and then found himself unable to fall asleep due to restlessness. By midnight, he began to hear loud conversations outside, unsure of what was happening. Curiously, he peered out and saw the main hall newly arranged, with a couch placed centrally, a small table beside it, and pillows on either side. The host, his family, and many guests from outside were all standing upright like cranes in the hall, as if waiting for something. After a long while, they heard someone say, "Here he is, here he is." Everyone rushed out to the courtyard to await him. After another long wait, a guest entered from outside. He was stout, dark-skinned, and had a thick beard, dressed in an unusual manner. He walked directly into the hall and sat on the left side of the couch. Soon, a woman in splendid attire emerged from the western room. Upon seeing her, the scholar recognized her as the host's second daughter. She bowed deeply to the guest, then ascended to the right side of the couch, sitting beside him. The host, his family, and all the guests then entered one by one to bow. The guest and the woman sat high and received their bows, barely reciprocating with courtesy. After the bows, everyone stood in order on both sides. Then, several people carried a table into the hall, which was laden with various dishes, numbering in the dozens. The host knelt and presented a dish to the guest, while the hostess did the same for the woman. Everyone standing in the hall knelt as well. The guest and the woman tasted a little and then stopped. Another dish was presented as before, and this continued for over ten dishes, until the guest stopped them. The table was then carried out again.

A guest rose, and the woman rose as well. The guest took the woman's hand and led her into the east room. After a while, lewd sounds reached the outside, and the host and guests all held their breath in anticipation. After about two hours, the guest and the woman emerged together, the guest dressed as he had been, but the woman appeared disheveled and in plain clothes. They sat back on the couch, and the host and hostess knelt to offer a cup, but it was unclear whether it was wine or tea. The guest and the woman drank, and then they all rose. The guest left, and the woman knelt to see him off at the hall, the guest helping her up. The host and guests knelt to see her off at the courtyard, but they ignored her. When the guest departed, the others began to enter in a loud, boisterous manner. The scholar feared being seen and secretly entered the back room to lie down, unsure of what would happen afterward. The next day, when he looked out, everything was as it had been, and the items from the previous day were nowhere to be seen. The scholar privately thought this was not a good place and, after about ten days, left for a reason. Several months later, when he visited again, the family had also moved, and their whereabouts were unknown. This is likely what is referred to as a heretical sect, but its name and secretive nature made it impossible for local officials to know about it, hence they could not suppress it. Sun Junzhang, courtesy name Shoubo, was the great-grandson of Sun Yujing, known posthumously as Wenjing Gong. He was acquainted with the scholar's family from a young age. Before his death, Sun Junzhang served as an official in the underworld. Whenever he went to handle matters in the underworld, he would appear as if asleep, sometimes sleeping for one night or two before waking. When asked about his duties, he would not speak.

On the eleventh day of the twelfth month in the twenty-ninth year of the Daoguang era, Empress Xiaoheshui passed away. At that time, Jiangsu and Zhejiang provinces had not yet received official notification, but blue-stamped documents from the Ministry arrived first, and everyone knew there was a major state funeral. However, at that time, Emperor Daoguang was also advanced in age, and public opinion was speculating, unsure of the exact situation. Shoubo, using an example from the *Guo Ce* (Strategies of the Warring States) where Lord Chudongjun flatters Empress Dowager Zhao, whispered to someone, calling her "granny." That night, he arrived in the afterlife and was severely beaten, being punished for leaking information. Upon waking, his legs were purple and blue, and he lay bedridden for over a month before he could stand. Some wondered why a government official in the afterlife would receive a beating. I replied that a line from a poem by Han Yu says, "Low-ranking officials are not worth talking about, but they cannot escape beatings in the dust," suggesting that the afterlife might still follow Tang dynasty customs. At that time, the imperial edict had been issued and was known in areas close to the capital, but Jiangsu and Zhejiang provinces, being slightly farther away from the capital, had not yet heard. This was not a matter of secret importance, so even if there was a leak, it did not warrant severe punishment, yet he was punished in the afterlife. Why was this so? My contemporary, Xie Mengyu, who was also a censor in the afterlife, was straightforward and often spoke about afterlife matters without hesitation. He was never heard to be punished for leaking information. In the early Guangxu era, General Chong of Shengjing passed away. His name was Chong Shi, with the courtesy name Pu Shan, and he was also a contemporary of mine. One month before his death, Xie told people, "Pu Shan won't be here for long, I saw his soul in the afterlife yesterday." People did not believe him, but after a month, when his memorial was submitted, they believed his words had a basis in fact.

Chapter Twelve: Gao Yuxin and Jia Zhuzhi, both from Fushan County, Shandong Province, jointly operated a pork shop in the Xidan area of the capital city. Gao was the second son, and Jia was the third son, each addressing the other by their respective seniority. Initially, they were very close, but later they had disagreements that gradually turned into deep-seated grudges. On a certain night when the night watch was almost over, Jia was still sound asleep, and Gao took a sharp knife and repeatedly struck him, injuring his head and forehead, causing him to die instantly. Gao confessed to the authorities without hesitation. The magistrate, being kind-hearted, wanted to change the charge from premeditated murder to manslaughter to spare Gao's life. He gently guided Gao with the words, "Why did you two, who were once so close, suddenly kill each other over a minor argument?" Gao replied, "I had a dream last night." The magistrate found his words strange and asked what the dream was about. Gao said, "Last night, I dreamed that Jia Lao San came and said, 'Gao Er, I died very painfully, you must avenge me.'" The magistrate realized that the voice speaking through Gao was actually Jia's ghost, as it was not Gao's voice. Gao continued to speak of the dream repeatedly. The magistrate said, "Then cutting off your head would allow you to avenge Jia Lao San." Gao bowed his head and said, "Thank you, Your Honor, for your wise judgment." The magistrate was startled and realized that the ghost of Jia was speaking through Gao. He then judged Gao according to the law.

In Fushun County, Sichuan, there was a peasant woman named Li who had an affair with a laborer surnamed Peng. Li's son was studying business away from home, and her daughter, Shunying, was unmarried and being raised by her husband's family. Li treated Shunying cruelly, and Shunying fled away. A carpenter surnamed Huang kept her as his wife, and she was a hundred li away from her home. Shunying's brother returned from outside and did not see his sister, so he sued the case to the authorities. The officials detained Li and Peng for questioning and found evidence of their adultery. They concluded, "They must have killed Shunying to silence her." Coincidentally, there was a pond several li away, deep over ten feet, where a floating female corpse was found. Her hands were tied behind her back, weighted down with a large stone, and her face was eaten by fish, making her unrecognizable. The officials said, "This must be Shunying." Li and Peng suffered severe beating and both confessed under duress. The case was finalized, but the provincial governor, Su Tingyu, doubted the verdict. He noted that the body was found one hundred and thirty days after Shunying was reported missing.

The corpse was not decomposed, what could be the reason? This must not be Shunying." They insisted it was impossible, and everyone thought Su was being impractical. Soon, Shunying returned on her own, already holding a child, and everyone then admired her. But the woman's corpse seen earlier was unknown. After some time, it was discovered that a couple had been traveling together, quarreled halfway, and the wife drowned herself in a pond, while the husband left her behind. The pond owner, fearing trouble, tied her up with rope and intended to carry her elsewhere, but fearing others would see, he tied a stone to her and sank her. This incident is recorded in Su Gong's *From Politics Miscellaneous Records*. Su, courtesy name Ao Shi, was from Tong'an, Fujian. I believe that without Su Gong's strong intervention, there would have been an innocent person wrongly executed in the Li case. How could a verdict be made before the corpse was even identified? But the woman's corpse in the pond might have had some other reason. Why would a couple quarrel and one of them take their own life so quickly? After the wife had died, the husband would surely have bought a coffin and buried her, so why would he just leave her behind? This matter is very suspicious, but at the time, they did not pursue it further.

During the sixth year of the Guangxu reign, He Pei, a clerk in charge of the treasury in Qiantang, was reported by the villagers for arbitrarily altering the tax rules during the collection of money and grain. Governor Tan was furious and seized and executed He Pei. Afterward, it was said that when He Pei was fifty years old, he went to Tianri Mountain to worship Buddha. An old monk in the mountain spoke to him and said, "It is very good that a layperson has come here. Since you have come, you do not need to go down the mountain! You can write a letter to your family to handle family affairs, and then stay here and practice with the old monk. You do not need to shave your head, and you can become a lay Buddhist follower at the Ling Shan Assembly. This is enough to avoid disaster and preserve life." He Pei did not understand the meaning of the monk's words and replied, "It is what I wish. But there are many family matters that need to be handled personally. I will come again once all matters are roughly settled."

The monk shook his head and said, "How could I possibly come back from here?" Péi returned, and within three years, he met disaster; this old monk must have known it beforehand. Wen Guyu from Xianyang, styled Yanshan, having passed the age of fifty, became even more devoted to learning. He traveled to study in Zhejiang, reaching Huangyan, and visited Jiufeng Mountain, where he saw a cave called Ziyun Peak below, located on a sheer cliff, so he climbed up. Upon reaching it, he found a very small door and had to stoop to enter. Inside, there were many bats, all pure white, some as large as a cartwheel. After walking more than a hundred steps, he came to another door that was half open and half closed. He squeezed through, and found the air and sun to be clear and gentle, with vast fields stretching out. It was late autumn, but the climate inside was like that of spring and summer. Mulberry trees and bamboo were lush and shady. Houses were also dense, but there were few people on the road. Looking far ahead, there was a high mountain reaching up to the sky. Following the path, at the foot of the mountain, there was a stone archway, carved with seal script that read: "The Land of No More and No Less." He sat down briefly under the archway, then pulled up his robe and climbed further.

About ten miles away, there is a temple that worships Xu You, accompanied by a deity associated with water or longevity. Asking the people in the temple, I learned that the villagers nearby are all descendants of Tai Yue. Next to the temple is a tavern, and I entered to buy and drink. I saw many people drinking, all dressed in elegant attire, speaking in lofty and ancient language that was not very understandable. At the far end, a young man was drinking alone. I bowed and asked for his surname and name; he introduced himself as Wang, twenty-four years old. When I inquired about his studies, the young man said, "I knew nothing in my youth, but I have focused my efforts on the *I Ching* and *Laozi*, privately annotating them, though I am not very confident in my work." I asked what the name of this mountain was, and the young man replied, "It is called Zhongtian Mountain, taller than ten thousand feet. At its peak, there is the elixir of immortality, and all directions are filled with rare flowers and precious trees, with divine beings guarding it. Climbing high to look, the eastern and southern seas and the northwest river sources are clearly visible."

Indeed, I have not climbed to its peak either." The ancient jade greatly admired this. By the time it was late afternoon, he took off his jade pendant and left it as collateral with the tavern, parted with the young man, and returned. Exiting the cave entrance, it was already evening. Fortunately, there was moonlight, and he climbed vines and pulled on ivy, barely managing to descend. He took shelter for the night at a temple and told the monks about it. The monks were all eager to go. The next day, he again followed the old path, but there was a thousand feet of stone wall, and there was no longer a cave entrance. He then placed the wine money on the rock and returned. Arriving at the temple, a piece of jade was floating down the stream. He picked it up and saw that it was the jade he had left as collateral at the tavern the previous day. This matter was written down by Mr. Wang Zizhuang, a scholar-official. The young man with the surname Wang must be none other than Wang Fusi. In Volume 39 of *T'ang Hui-yao* (Comprehensive Records of the Tang), *Kuang Yi Ji* (Record of Strange Events) records the affairs of a village in Mayang, where the old man he met was the River Master, and the gate-keeping boy was Wang Fusi. Therefore, such matters are indeed recorded in ancient books and should not be dismissed as purely fictitious.

Yellow Rock County borders the sea, and its inhabitants build embankments to defend against tides, with names such as First Embankment, Second Embankment, Third Embankment, Fourth Embankment, and Fifth Embankment. Locally, these are referred to as "tangs." From the Fourth Tang onwards, the area is desolate and haunted by ghosts. At the end of the Tongzhi era, a household in the county married a woman. On the night after the wedding, they were entertaining guests when the new bride suddenly got up and left through the back door. The family followed her. She ran like the wind, crossing the bamboo garden and going away, leaving her hairpins and other ornaments stuck in the bamboo leaves. Outside the bamboo, there was a pond, and searching yielded nothing. Lighting torches to illuminate all around, they saw women's footprints in the fields, each step several feet apart, slanting and leading southeast. Following the trail to its end, they found her in a stream half a li away, already stiff and standing dead. This was said by Shi Rong, a student of Mr. Wang Zizhuang. The husband of the new bride was a student of Shi Rong.

During the Tongzhi era, the people of Linhai County suffered from a serious epidemic in recent years, and many did not recover even after the hottest day of the year, Daxu. They decided to hold a boat-sending ceremony the following year as part of their traditional customs. The boat was built like a merchant ship, with masts, sails, and a rudder, and was equipped with tables, chairs, beds, pillows, bedding, food such as chicken, pork, fish, shrimp, rice, grain, beans, and wheat, as well as weapons like swords, spears, guns, and cannons. The only exception was the small bags used to hold rice, each holding only one liter, but there were thousands of them, all donated by villagers. Several days before the hottest day of the year, they built a large temple or shrine for religious purposes. On the hottest day of the year, they sent the boat out to sea, letting it go wherever it would, and it became known as the Daxu boat. Once, some pirates encountered the boat at night, mistaking it for a merchant ship, and fired at it. The boat also fired back to defend itself. When morning came, the pirates realized it was a Daxu boat and were greatly surprised, then left. Later, the pirate ship was defeated by the official military forces, and all the bandits were captured and none escaped.

In a certain place in Fujian, there was a middleman who sold rice. One night, someone knocked on the door to sell rice. Dozens of porters loaded the rice onto a boat, and by the next day, the rice was not fully loaded. The rice bags were as large as five-stone gourds, and the porters' shoulders were strained. When viewed in the morning, the rice bags appeared small like bowls. An elderly man recognized them and said, "These are the rice from the ship in Lianhai during the great heat." That day, an epidemic broke out, and rituals were performed to avert it. When I was a guest in Wang Village, Xiuning, every April, there was a custom of burning paper boats to drive away epidemics. The paper boats were made of paper, and everything was included, but everything was made of paper. They were burned in the fields, and it was said that they were sent to wander in West Lake. This custom has been passed down through generations and can be amusing. However, there are also those who have personally seen these boats in the Huizhou River. Once, a merchant who traded cloth accompanied the paper boats to Hangzhou. To this day, there is a figure standing at the back of the paper boat, carrying cloth and holding an umbrella, resembling a merchant. This is to recognize its supernatural nature. The ship from Lianhai is actually a real ship, and it is more likely to be considered supernatural.

Prince Wang Zhi, a scholar of high moral standing, resided in the soft bridge of Huangyan. In this place, there was a temple named Dongruan, which worshipped Ruan Sizong of the Jin Dynasty, or so it was said, it was the deity of Bai Long Mountain. During the year 1834 of the Daoguang era, the flower gathering was widely popular in the county of Huangyan. The flower gathering involved writing the names of thirty-four ancient people on slips, selecting one at random, placing it in a tube, and hanging the tube between the beams, known as "making a tube." The person in charge of this was called the tube official. Participants would choose one name from the thirty-four and contribute a certain amount of money to a chest, which was termed "participating in the flower gathering." If the chosen name matched the one in the tube, the tube official would pay back thirty times the amount contributed; if it did not match, the amount would go to the tube official. People of all ages rushed to join, and the entire county was in a frenzy. Consequently, the ritual of child-descending also gained prominence. Child-descending involved burning talismans to summon a deity and seek omens. When a deity possessed a person, that person was called the child body. When the deity descended, the child body would fall to the ground, foam at the mouth, and then leap up, taking the deity's seat, which was called ascending the altar. Everyone would then prostrate themselves and ask the deity for guidance, and the deity would respond with cryptic messages. After the exchange, the child body would fall back to the ground as before, which was called descending from the altar. People followed the deity's words, and their predictions often came true, so all who participated in the flower gathering also underwent the child-descending ritual. The deities who descended were not one, but the "little master" was particularly renowned for its supernatural abilities.

The Minor Deity, commonly known as the younger brother of Guanyin Bodhisattva, would always turn inward when questioned, seeking guidance from his sister before answering. In the village of Ruoqiao, a child was possessed at the Dongruan Temple. As soon as the Minor Deity descended, the villager Wang Guangrong hurried from the fields, and the spirit of Dongruan Temple took control of him. The Minor Deity hastily retreated from the altar. Wang Guangrong relayed the deity’s words, blaming the elders for summoning a demon and desecrating the sacred site. The elders, in haste, knocked their heads in supplication. The deity claimed to have the power to heal, and soon, people flocked to the temple seeking treatment. Despite Wang Guangrong’s daily activities of farming, chopping wood, or trading in the market, whenever someone asked for healing at the temple, after burning incense and silently praying, the deity would always appear immediately. Initially illiterate, Wang Guangrong suddenly became able to prescribe remedies, all of which proved effective. There were cases where even skilled physicians were at a loss, but patients were cured by the divine prescriptions. As a result, the temple’s incense offerings grew daily, and the temple buildings, gatehouses, and halls were renovated to焕然一新, all funded by the incense money. Wang Guangrong, however, did not keep a single coin for himself. At that time, similar phenomena occurred in the temples of other villages, but they primarily relied on folk rituals, and the prescriptions they occasionally gave were not very effective. Only the Dongruan Temple did not accept such offerings. After one or two years, the deities in other temples ceased to descend, while the Dongruan Temple’s deity continued to manifest for nearly thirty years, until the year of Tongzhi Jiazi, when Wang Guangrong died, and the phenomenon finally ceased.

On June 11th of the Dingchou year during the Guangxu era (1887), Wang Zizhuang, a scholar-official, dreamed of passing by his friend Zhang Ziyuan's place. Zhang Ziyuan showed him a collection of miscellaneous writings, which contained two essays. One of the essays was written by Wang Renkan. The text reads: "Between the islands of Penglai and Fangzhang, there is a divine being who built a flying pavilion on the stream of Yingzhou, just a few feet above the water. The immortal Miao Gu came to visit and sat to wash her feet, feeling very pleased. She invited Cishengzi to join her, and Cishengzi said, 'It is too close to the water; it should be raised higher.' So the divine being raised the pavilion three feet. Wang Qiao, flying with his magic shoes, arrived and said, 'It is beautiful, but it is still too low.' So the pavilion was raised another three feet. Hongyazi said, 'It is now high, but when you climb the pavilion and look out, Fangzhang blocks your view in front, and the sight is not expansive.' So the pavilion was raised another three feet."

The creature was more than ten feet away from the water, and even a small mountain could not hide it. Soon, a sea vessel passed beneath it, its masts and roof reaching the same height, unable to move forward. Then, divine beings increased the height of its base tenfold, making it stand thirteen and a half feet above the water. After waking up, the text could be clearly remembered, but its meaning remained unclear. Wang, who became the top-ranked candidate in the imperial examination that year, was said to embody the saying, "The head of the giant turtle is crowned like Mount Penglai." This was a sign that he would achieve great success. The outer part of the text resembles the character "井" (well), and the inner part resembles the character "亚" (second), but it is an unknown character. In the dream, it was read as "倪" (Ni), but it is not found in any standard Chinese dictionary. Zizhuang wrote a work called *Zhiyan* (Branch Words), which contains various strange stories and anecdotes. He shared this with me, and I have selected a few passages to include here. This passage, along with the story of the ancient jade inscription, is unclear whether it is a metaphorical tale or a historical fact.

Wang Guangping, courtesy name Hai Cun, was a guest in Jia浦 during the spring of the year Wuxu. One day, he took a casual walk in the fields and saw many people heading west. He asked them why, and they replied, "Beyond the western sea dike, the sea platform is appearing." The term "sea platform" is a colloquial name for the "sea mirage" or "Fata Morgana," which is also called "sea market" in folklore. Following their lead, he went to see for himself. In the mist beyond the sea dike, he saw faint outlines of towers and pavilions. Among them were palaces and gates, temples and observatories, residential areas and marketplaces, inns and post stations, all numbering in the hundreds, stretching for more than ten miles. At that time, the fog was still thick, so he could not see everything clearly. Soon, the mist cleared, and behind the houses, an overpass appeared like a long rainbow, with carriages and horses moving on it. The curtains and screens of the houses were being drawn and rolled, the windows opening and closing, and men and women were gathered for feasting and drinking, some washing and brushing their teeth, others combing their hair and dressing, and some sitting and looking out. All these scenes could be counted clearly.

To the east, there was a vast plain, resembling a military training ground, where some practiced archery, others held banners, some rode horses galloping swiftly, and others engaged in martial arts on foot. There was also a high-ranking official arriving with a clear path, accompanied by heralds and a retinue. Beyond this, there were scenes of market transactions, with people carrying, wearing, sitting, and hurrying, all clearly visible. In the distance, there were city walls and large ships sailing on the wind, their limits indiscernible. Suddenly, a mountain peak emerged, surrounded by clouds and accompanied by the sound of thunder. From within, a seven-story pagoda appeared, its spire reaching into the heavens, with bells and lights moving, and the clouds changing shape in a manner that defies description. After a while, the fog returned, and the sea breeze blew, causing all the scenes to vanish instantly. Mr. Wang has a record of the sea market, *The Record of the Sea Market*. A contemporary of mine, Mr. Fang, whose family name is Bao and courtesy name is Meicun, recorded and showed it to me. I have summarized its main points as follows. Mr. Wang is from Pingwang Town, Zhenze, and this account is said to be from the spring of the year of the earth rooster during the Qianlong and Jiaqing periods, though the exact year is unknown.

Under the South Peak of West Lake lies the Five Elders Peak, which is said to have a tiger's den, commonly known as Tiger's Den, though it is rarely encountered. In a certain year during the reign of Emperor Daoguang, a family named Gao prepared a grave at this site, with the sound of many hammers echoing. Soon, a fierce tiger roar was heard, and everyone ran in fear, causing the work to cease. The Gao family was troubled by this and sought someone who could catch the tiger. Nearby, there is a mountain called Wangjia Mountain, where the inhabitants are known for their strength. They responded to the call to catch the tiger. First, they used a wooden basin, placed a large copper mirror inside, and under the intense sunlight, the reflection of the mirror rose like flames, as if a fire had started. Thus, countless bees from the mountain flew and gathered, all leaving their urine in the basin. The locals said, "The bees come to help when they see a fire start, and they leave their urine to extinguish the fire." For several days, the urine accumulated to an inch. Then, they caught a frog and placed a small amount of urine on its body.
