A toad that was poisoned jumped one step and died. A tiger that was poisoned also jumped one step and died; a toad that jumped several steps died, and a tiger that jumped several steps also died. This was tested, and it was always without fail. Then, a large crossbow was set up where the tiger entered and exited, with the tip coated with bee venom. One person climbed to the top of a tree to observe the movements, and dozens of others held fire guns and lay in wait at the foot of the mountain, agreeing that when they heard the sound of firecrackers on the mountain, they would rush to the scene. On that day, the tiger did not come out. The next day, they heard the tiger's roar, and a shanggui (tiger's servant) came out first, reaching the place where the crossbow was set and saying, "This is not good for the big brother." It then moved the crossbow to another place. Continuing on, the person on the tree waited until it went far away, climbed down the tree and set the crossbow back in place, then climbed back up the tree. When the tiger arrived, the trigger of the crossbow was activated, and the tiger was poisoned and died. The shanggui, upon hearing the sound, returned and saw that the tiger was already dead. Looking up, it saw someone at the top of the tree, and it jumped up eagerly to climb the tree. The person struck a stone to light a fire and set off firecrackers, and the sound reverberated through the valley. All the people lying in wait at the foot of the mountain rushed up, and they all fired their guns. The shanggui then fled away.

A group of people carried the tiger back, and the Gao family rewarded him generously. Wang Qi, with the courtesy name Zi Feng, was from Huzhou and taught in the Xuanhua Prefecture. He suffered a minor illness for over a month, and one night he dreamed that someone told him, "Please hurry to your post." In the dream, he immediately followed the instruction, and a sedan chair was already waiting at the door. Four people carried it, and there were also flags and umbrellas in front. Soon, they arrived at a place that resembled a government office, with the layout of the gatehouse, study, hall, and corridors roughly present, though it was quite narrow and small. On the hall, a public desk was set up, with an official seal placed on it, and he paid homage according to the ritual. Then, he ascended to the official seat, and officials and servants paid homage. Names were called according to the register, and marked with red ink. After the matter was settled, he withdrew inside, where there were three small rooms, and two servants performed the duties. Soon, the meal was prepared, consisting of four bowls, sliced pork into threads, steamed chicken eggs into cakes, and the rest were vegetables. The taste was all like that of the human world. After the meal, he had nothing to do, with no official documents to read and no reports from officials.

Wang examined the seal's inscription, which read "Seal of the Badaling Division." Though the seal was in the box, it had never been used. After two meals, he undressed and went to bed. As soon as he closed his eyes, he suddenly woke up, finding himself still in the study of Xuanhua Prefecture. On that day, he was still sick and lying in bed as usual. At night when he went to sleep, he found himself there again, and the scene was just like when he first arrived. For more than a month, Wang grew quite tired of it. He asked his two servants, "Can I stop coming here?" They replied, "We should ask the officials." Later, they came back and said, "Since the official doesn't want to, we won't force him." He gave Wang two pills and said, "If you take them, you won't come here anymore." Wang swallowed them, and suddenly it was as if he woke up from a dream. He no longer came there, and his illness also disappeared from that point on. Later, Wang, as a patrol inspector, needed to serve in Zhili Province. He once went on a mission to Xuanhua and passed through Badaling. A whirlwind arose in front of his carriage, and he suspected that his former officials were still coming to greet him. After several years, he passed through again, but there was no more such anomaly. It was because over time, the connection had weakened.

In Shaoxing, there was a woman from the Sun family, a widow, who was nearly fifty years old. She had an affair with her neighbor, Mr. Xu, and gave birth to a son, whom she could not bear to abandon. The woman also had a daughter who was married and had become a widow without children. She sent her daughter to take care of the boy. The daughter was very pleased and claimed that she had obtained him from a nursery, treating him as if he were her own child. At five years old, the boy could not speak, but his nature was very fierce. As he grew older, he often wielded a knife and fought with his mother, which troubled the daughter. When the boy's mother arrived, the daughter told her about the situation. At that time, some of the daughter's husband's relatives were present, and one of them said, "This boy is not her own child, and he is also fierce and unreasonable. Raising a tiger and a wolf is very meaningless. It would be better to send him away." The mother, who had always loved this boy, did not believe what the daughter said and argued with her. The boy suddenly shouted at the daughter and said, "I am your younger brother, how can you treat me as your own child?" The mother and daughter both turned pale. The relatives, thinking he had never spoken before, were also very surprised. When they questioned him in detail, he fell silent again. Knowing that he was different, they did not investigate further. So they kept him for several more years, and as he grew older, his nature became even more violent. At that time, the boy's mother had already combined with Mr. Xu and lived together as if they were husband and wife. The daughter's husband's relatives eventually gave this boy to the two elders, and the two elders accepted him without hesitation. When the boy returned to Mr. Xu, he suddenly began to speak, just like any ordinary child. Now he is still a carpenter.

In the Qingtai Gate area of Hangzhou, there was a scholar named Xu Pengxuan who returned home drunk after a night of drinking. As he passed by a local temple, he saw that it was illuminated with bright lights and filled with loud voices, which struck him as strange. Curious, he peered inside and saw a deity seated on the dais, surrounded by officials and servants. Some were calling names from a register, and those standing respectfully on the steps responded, each receiving a scroll and entering one after another. Xu recognized many of the people and, wanting to understand the mystery, slipped inside. He saw the people seated in rows on both sides. Suddenly, a person carrying a sign descended from the dais, and the sign read "Write Your Own Biography." Upon seeing this, everyone frowned at each other, saying, "Difficult, difficult!" Some stamped their feet, shook their heads, and opened their mouths but could not close them. Xu thought this was the easiest thing and wondered why it was so difficult. He noticed a person at the western side of the hall, who turned out to be his brother. His brother was holding a pen, trembling, and hesitating to write, repeating, "Could my brother have forgotten so much?" Xu wanted to speak to him, but a guard was approaching, and he feared being seen, so he walked along the wall. He came to a place marked "Place to Receive Scrolls." Sitting at a desk was a person, and it was his deceased friend Cao.

曹见徐，惊曰：“子何来此？为逻者所见奈何？速匿此！”徐入，因问其故。曹曰：“所谓自写履历者，使其人将生平行事，为善为恶，一一自书。若无所讳饰，是尚自知其过，犹可姑容，冀其悛改。小有不实不尽，则转送冥司科罪，不能生还矣。”已而缴卷者纷集，略一层卷，率多恶而少善。徐思其兄素号端人，必无遗行。及视其卷，则曾奸一处女，又尝干没人千金之赀，皆平素所未知者。未几门启，诸人皆出，曹急麾徐出，曰：“及此不出，即不能出矣。”徐趋出，履阈而仆，则身卧庙庑，东方白矣。归省其兄，则夜间寒热大作，昏不知人，黎明汗出始愈。因询以冥中事，兄茫然不知也。微以所见隐事叩之，兄大骇曰：“此十许年来未尝告人者，弟何处得之？”乃以社庙所见告，因同至庙中，立誓忏悔。今其兄年五十余尚在，或神许其改过欤！ When Cao saw Xu, he was startled and said, "Why have you come here? What if you are seen by the guards? Hide quickly!" Xu entered, and then asked the reason. Cao said, "What is meant by self-writing a resume is that a person records all their lifetime actions, whether good or evil, one by one. If there is no concealment or embellishment, it shows that they still know their own faults, and they can be temporarily tolerated, hoping they will repent. If there is even a little falsehood or omission, they will be sent to the underworld for punishment and cannot return to life." Soon after, those who handed in their resumes gathered, and when a layer of them was reviewed, most were filled with evil deeds and few with good ones. Xu thought his brother was always known as an upright man and surely had no hidden misdeeds. But when he saw his brother's resume, it revealed that he had once seduced a young girl and had once embezzled a thousand gold coins, all of which were unknown to Xu. Soon the door opened, and everyone came out. Cao urgently gestured for Xu to leave, saying, "If you don't leave now, you won't be able to leave." Xu hurried out, stumbled upon stepping on the threshold, and then found himself lying in the temple porch, with the east already brightening. When he returned home to visit his brother, his brother was suffering from chills and fever that night, unconscious, and only recovered when he broke out in a sweat at dawn. Xu then asked about matters in the underworld, but his brother was clueless. When Xu gently probed with the hidden matters he had seen, his brother was greatly startled and said, "These are things I haven't told anyone for about ten years, how did you find out?" Xu then told him what he had seen in the village temple, and they went together to the temple, where they took an oath and repented. Now, his brother is still alive at over fifty years old, perhaps the gods have forgiven him for his repentance.

Wang Jun, known as Ban Yi, was the ancestor of my disciple Wang Mengwei Tingding. Once, while traveling by boat through the Siao Ji Gua near Taihu Lake, a storm erupted with wind and thunder, and black clouds gathered all around. Five dragons were fighting in the lake, appearing now far, now near. Suddenly, the boat was less than half a li away from them, and the lake water boiled. The boat was lifted several zhang by the wind, and when it fell, the water reached the ship's hull. The boatman shouted in alarm, at a loss, not knowing what to do. Despite his fear, Wang Jun believed that life and death were matters of fate and held onto the ship's rail to observe the changes. Suddenly, another dragon arrived, its body covered in scales that shone like gold. It circled among the five dragons, swaying its head, spreading its scales, and gracefully leaping. At that moment, the wind and thunder grew more intense, and the lake water stood upright. The five dragons could not resist. One dragon fled toward the southeast, faster than an arrow, and Wang Jun's boat was caught by it, and went away with it. After about twenty li, they arrived in front of an ancient tomb at Qian Jia Gua. The dragon touched a tree and fell. The ship also fell to the ground, and the front and rear parts were all destroyed. The five people on the boat were fortunately not injured or lost. The dragon lay on its back on the tomb, its nose making a rumbling sound, as if very exhausted.

Heavy rain suddenly came down, and the dragon writhed for a long time before leaping with all its might and slowly rising into the air and departing. It was already dusk, and the five people and the boatman struggled to move forward. They encountered several dozen men and women whose faces were indistinguishable. They asked them about the way, and they replied, "There is a village called Lu Village ahead. We are all residents of the village. You can follow us to the village." So they followed them. After walking about five miles, they indeed saw a village. When they entered the village, the men and women disappeared instantly. By the road, there was a temple. They knocked on the door and entered, telling the monks of their suffering. The monks allowed them to stay overnight and said, "The place where you fell from the boat is the ancestral tomb of the Lu family in this village." Wang privately said to his companions, "What we met must be the spirits of the Lu tomb. Otherwise, why would these several dozen men and women be walking in the rain and wind at night?" When morning came, they left the temple and saw more than ten people carrying baskets and shovels passing by the temple gate. They asked, and they were all descendants of the Lu family. Last night after midnight, they heard someone knocking on the door very urgently, but when they opened the door, they saw no one. When they went to sleep, they dreamed that their ancestors spoke to them saying, "The tomb area was destroyed by the dragon. It should be repaired urgently." Therefore, they are going to repair the tomb today. Wang told the people, "My words are true!"

Chen Xiangshu from Hangzhou, at the age of forty, had no children. Suddenly, he dreamed of arriving at a place where, in the green shade, there was a large mansion. Surrounding the mansion were buildings with green windows and red railings, and embroidered curtains hanging low. Young and beautiful women were sitting in rows, their faces like sandalwood and eyebrows like black jade, adorned with colorful silk decorations. He suspected it was a brothel and wandered around unable to leave. Suddenly, more than ten people gathered at the foot of the building, some holding torches and others holding woven grass. One person was holding a torch and directing the others to burn it. Suddenly, the fire was intense, blazing brightly. The women cried out, and some fell from the building. Chen looked at the people and recognized them as his classmate and friend Zhou Zijin. He immediately shouted at them, saying, "How can you burn someone's house?" He struggled to take the torch and then waved the group away. Then the fire went out, and the crying in the building stopped. Chen suddenly woke up, not understanding what it meant. The next day, he visited Zhou, intending to tell him about the dream. Upon arriving, Zhou had just been stung by a bee nest on the loquat tree in front of his斋 (a room for meditation or study). He ordered his servant to attack it with fire. Chen realized that his dream was about this. Then he told Zhou about the dream, stopping him from using fire. That night, Chen dreamed again of dozens of women bowing before him. They said, "You have saved our clan, no virtue is greater. You are a good person, you will surely have descendants!" At the age of fifty, he indeed had three sons.

In the summer of the Geng Mao year of Guangxu, Zhu Zhenfu from Qiantang lived in Fifteen Kui Lane. One day, he was sitting alone on the upper floor when suddenly a violent storm came. Zhu Zhenfu got up to close the window, but he was struck by a sudden thunderbolt and swept away. No one in the family knew what had happened. After the rain stopped, they went up to the upper floor and found Zhu Zhenfu missing. They saw a fire mark on the window frame and a shoe left under the window. The whole family was in a state of panic and searched everywhere. Later, they found Zhu Zhenfu, whose chest was still warm, but his face was burned black. The white cotton shirt he was wearing had turned dark and formed patterns, looking like characters but not quite, and like paintings but not quite. They carried him to the bed, and he woke up in the middle of the night. When asked about his experience after being struck by lightning, he could not speak. Just then, there was someone in his family suffering from malaria, experiencing alternating chills and fever. He happened to cover him with the shirt, and the illness seemed to disappear. They began to treasure the shirt. The next year, a woman from Yuhang was possessed by a fox spirit. She borrowed the shirt, and as soon as she entered the door, her family saw something as big as a dog escape through a hole. The woman in the bed smelled the sulfur smell and suddenly became clear-minded; the fox spirit disappeared. The shirt gained even more value. In the Geng Chen year, there was a man surnamed Xu at San Sheng Bridge. His son suddenly developed a strange illness, acting normally during the day but with his skin as cold as ice at night. He started shivering and then began to speak incoherently.

His speech was mostly in a northern dialect, and he demanded food and drink, as well as clothes and fine things. If they were not given to him, he would curse his parents, and even go so far as to beat them; if commanded to bring them in, he would only destroy them. He often claimed that his family had long been greatly wealthy and noble, describing how magnificent their palaces were, how rare and precious their clothing and possessions were. He would also say, "Such-and-such a high official is my subordinate; such-and-such a wealthy man is my slave." In this way, he would chatter endlessly until dawn, only stopping when daybreak came. When asked about it, he would say, "Every evening, a man with a round face and short stature appears, holding a basin of cold water, pours it on my head, and I then fall unconscious. All that I have spoken of, I do not know." The family could not bear the disturbance and decided to borrow a red robe to drive him away. He then shouted, "I am sent by the Emperor of Heaven, even the Wind Lord and Rain Lord must obey me! How can the remnants of lightning scare me?" He pulled his mother’s hair and struck her. The others knelt and begged him, and only then did he release her. The next evening, a relative, without consulting the family, put on the red robe and came. The sick person suddenly rejoiced, saying, "Tonight I am free at last." When asked why, he said, "This ghost has shrunk to about a foot in size, hiding in the northeast corner, still shaking. Also, I smell a strong sulfur odor—could there be a red robe of the Thunder Family in the room?" His relative then produced the robe and waved it around the room. The sick person said, "The ghost has entered the water vessel and disappeared." From then on, he recovered.

A Man from Qiantang, named Yi, married a woman surnamed Yang. Before the provincial examination in the year Gengmao of Guangxu's reign, his wife suddenly fell ill, and despite numerous treatments, her condition did not improve. One day, she spoke with a dialect from Jiangxi and said, "I came from Jiangxi with a document from the True Man's Office, to come to Zhejiang to handle the examination duties. I have a karmic connection with you, so I am staying here temporarily. I am hungry; quickly prepare food for me." The family knew that it was a fox spirit, so they placed fruits and melons there. She immediately cut a large watermelon and ate it all, then also ate all the other fruits, and still demanded rice. When the rice was brought, it was all eaten. When asked, she said, "It disappears immediately after swallowing, and one does not realize how much was eaten." The family was troubled by this, but could not do anything about it. They also sometimes saw her form, which appeared as a young woman. Over time, they became accustomed to seeing her and did not find it strange.

During the time when the provincial examiners were selecting talented individuals, the subject was worried that he would not be chosen. He asked his wife, who said, "They will definitely select you, but not highly." And indeed, that turned out to be the case. The subject then asked, "Can the exam questions be predicted?" His wife replied, "No." After asking repeatedly, she said, "Do not worry, I will also enter the examination hall and will definitely come to help you." When he entered the examination hall, there were no unusual occurrences, but the writing was not difficult. After the exam, he blamed her for not helping, but she said, "I have already helped you." He then asked if he had passed, and she said, "I fear it might not be the case." There were two students named Yu and Li, who were friends of the subject, and they verified the exam essays. His wife said, "Yu, the hero of literature; Li, the sage of literature." When asked if they had passed, she said she did not know. When the results were announced, neither Yu nor Li passed. The subject told his wife, who said, "I knew it all along." After the results, their contact became less frequent, and on the third day, he left. From then on, the contact ceased completely, and the wife's illness also recovered.

Li Qin, courtesy name You'an, was a student from Xindu County, Sichuan. He and two fellow students rented a house in the village to study for the imperial examinations. One night, they heard a sound on their desk, which they initially thought was a mouse. Upon waking, they found that their books and writing utensils had been moved. This happened every night, leading them to suspect that a ghost or spirit was causing trouble. They summoned a shaman to perform an exorcism. The shaman placed a drum and gong on the ground, which suddenly began to sound by themselves. The shaman then carved a peach into a wooden rod and planted it on the ground, only to have it pulled up and strike the shaman’s head. Unable to proceed, the shaman fled, and the exorcism was abandoned. One night, they heard the sound of grinding ink. Li Qin loudly said, “If you can grind ink, you must also be able to write. What kind of spirit are you? Why not write something to tell me?” The next morning, they found the words “土阜中也” (in the earth mound) written on the table. That night, they asked, “If you are the land deity, where is your temple?” The writing replied, “At a certain place, my land deity.” Following the message, they indeed found a statue of the land deity. They then raised funds to build a small temple and placed the deity’s image inside.

Afterwards, there was still a sound coming from the table. The group said, "This god must have a special connection with us, can it recite poetry?" By dawn, they indeed found two poems on the table, which were quite beautiful and pleasing. They then composed a poem in response, wrote it down, and burned it. From then on, there were daily exchanges of poems. The group said, "Though we have seen its writing, we have not yet had a conversation with it, can it converse with us?" Suddenly, a voice came from the room saying, "Yes, but you must extinguish the candle." So, whenever they wanted to converse with the god at night, they would extinguish the candle. The god was very good at discussing, covering topics such as the differences and similarities between Confucian classics and historical texts, the merits and flaws of literary works, and practical knowledge such as medicine, divination, astrology, and the arts of the itinerant scholars. It also encouraged good deeds and corrected mistakes, with its words being close but its meaning far-reaching. Everyone was amazed and respected it, considering it a beneficial friend. People from far and near heard about it and gathered at night. Some asked about doubts, others requested calligraphy and paintings, and all left as they wished. Like this, for over a year, the group said, "We can say we have a spiritual friendship, but we have not seen its appearance, it is a regrettable matter, can we see it once?" The god replied, "Yes, but you must light the candle to see it." They then lit the candle, and the three of them saw different things: one saw a middle-aged person in the attire of a noble from a previous dynasty; one saw an elderly person in the attire of a first-rank official of the current dynasty; one saw a young person in the attire of a scholar from the Ming dynasty. After seeing it once, the image disappeared, and they extinguished the candle and continued talking.

The group said, "Having seen your appearance, we admire and respect you even more. Where do you reside? May we come visit you?" He replied, "Yes." They then agreed on a date. When the time arrived, everyone went to bed, and they dreamed of walking out of the village. After walking for about half a day, the scenery was no longer the mountains and waters of Sichuan, but rather as if in the outskirts of Yan and Zhao. They traveled by carriage, arriving at dusk. The red gate was wide open, and green shade covered the ground. Within the vast courtyard, cranes danced and monkeys cried. An old man invited the guests inside, warmly receiving them. The arrangements were luxurious. On the next day, he set up another feast to entertain them, accompanied by music. The fruits on the table were all from the north. The old man took several pieces to give as gifts, placing them in their sleeves or pockets. On the following day, they returned along the same path, upon returning home, they realized they had dreamed for three days. They asked the servant, and it was just that this morning they had woken up a bit late. The fruits in their pockets were still there, the scenery was illusory, but the objects were real, and all found it strange. However, from then on, the spirit never came again. In his old age, Li traveled among the nobles as a doctor, gaining some fame, and spoke about the matter in great detail. This is just a summary of it.

Someone said that the land god must not be like this, it is likely that fox spirits are pretending to be it, and the reasoning might be correct. The people of Japan greatly enjoy eating eels. However, even though they love them, they also greatly fear them, saying, "They have spiritual power and can cause harm." Therefore, they dare not kill them themselves. Those who open wine shops must keep many eels, to supply people's needs and prepare them on their behalf. Once, three or four drunk guests knocked at the door of a wine shop at night, around midnight, and everyone in the shop was asleep. The guests asked from outside the door, "Do you have any eels?" The eels kept in the shop answered in unison from the water, "No." The owner was greatly surprised. By dawn, he released all the eels he had kept and changed his profession on the same day. Another wine shop was skilled in cooking turtles and had enjoyed its benefits for many years. The children they had born had short and small hands and feet, not exceeding three inches, and their shape was like turtles. People in that place all said it was retribution for killing living beings. There was also a tofu seller whose wife hated a dog. She poured boiling water over it, and the dog's body became rotten, shrieking for three days before dying. At the time, the woman was pregnant, and at the time of delivery, she gave birth to a girl. From the right forehead to the right ear, black hair grew five or six inches long, and when it was cut off, it grew back. Now she is married, and the hair is still the same. Many people who have seen it say it is no different from dog hair.

Mr. Red Village, a Japanese man, had his name lost in conversation, and thus it is unknown. He had a slave who was fierce and violent, who once found an egg of a wild chicken in the bamboo grove, picked it up, cooked it, and ate it. At midnight, he heard the slave's cries, thinking he had been poisoned, and went to see him. There, he found a snake about six or seven feet long coiled around his chest and back. The slave tried to free himself but could not, so he cut the snake off with a knife. The next day, he cooked and ate the snake again, and afterward, there was no further incident. It is said that when snakes and wild chickens mate, they give birth to a jiao (a type of dragon). It is speculated that this egg might have been laid by a wild chicken mother and a snake father, hence the snake came to seek its offspring. In Japan, there was an old woman who kept a cat, which she loved very much. However, the cat was extremely greedy, and it stole and ate countless fish from the neighbor's pond. The neighbor, out of respect for the old woman's affection for the cat, tolerated it without saying anything. One day, the old woman took the cat to a Buddhist temple, where a warrior was staying. The warrior was just about to invite guests for a banquet, with wine and dishes already set out, but the guests had not yet arrived, so he had not yet sat down. The cat climbed onto the table and began to feast, causing the table to be in a mess. The warrior became very angry and drew his sword to kill the cat. When the old woman heard that the cat had died, she rushed over to embrace and cry over the cat, suddenly struck by wind disease, with paralyzed hands and feet. The temple monks sent her back home. From then on, the old woman cried day and night, and when heard up close, her voice still sounded like a human's, but when heard from a distance, it sounded like a cat meowing. After three days, she also died.

In Japan, there was a person who sold vegetables and fruits. During the summer, when the pears were ripe, he would rise early in the morning to go to the garden to pick them. One day, he found a snake coiled around a pear tree. He chased it away, but it wouldn't leave, so he took a large stone to hit it. He struck its head, and it died immediately. When he returned home, his eldest daughter cried out, "Several snakes are chasing me very urgently!" However, there was not a single snake by her side. He realized that the snake he had killed was causing trouble. He then invited friends from the Buddhist assembly, surrounded them, and chanted the Buddha's name. He made the girl sit in the middle. After a long time, the snake finally disappeared. Just as the crowd was about to disperse, the second daughter began to cry and call out like the eldest daughter. They again surrounded her and chanted. The second daughter said, "The snakes have gone." The third daughter had the same experience. Because they chanted the Buddha as before, the snakes finally disappeared completely, and there were no other unusual occurrences. According to the Buddhist assembly, this practice is common in China, where men and women gather in groups of dozens to chant the Buddha's name all day, and it is not surprising that this custom exists in Japan as well.

Many people in Japan die after eating pufferfish (fugu). Folklore suggests that those poisoned by fugu will surely live if buried in the ground for one day and night, but this has never been proven. Once, a fisherman died after eating fugu. His family was extremely poor, so the neighbors collected money to buy a thin coffin and place his body in it, then carried it to a temporary storage place for coffins outside the city. A厝屋 is a small house in the wilderness, rented out to people to store coffins. Such places are common outside Hangzhou in Zhejiang province, known as攒基. At midnight, the dead man suddenly came to life, and found his body already in the coffin. Fortunately, the coffin was very thin, and he used his strength to lift the lid and came out. He heard noisy voices outside the door and peeked through, seeing several people gambling on the ground. They wanted to borrow a light to go home, so he opened the door and came out. The gamblers saw him and thought he was a ghost, greatly frightened, and ran away in all directions.

The money and valuables left behind were scattered on the ground, with no time to gather them, and the fishermen all took it with them and returned home. In Japan, there was a widow who was very poor and had saved and lived frugally for nearly thirty years, accumulating more than forty taels of silver. Someone borrowed thirty-five taels of silver from her to go trading outside. Soon after the woman died, her daughter dreamed that her mother came to tell her, "I have already died. I have more than forty taels of silver. Except for what someone borrowed, there is still more than ten taels left under the bed. You should quickly go get it, do not let it fall into someone else's hands." The daughter woke up crying in shock and immediately went back to her mother's house, indeed finding the silver in the amount mentioned under the bed. The borrower, who was hundreds of miles away, also dreamed that the woman came to say, "I have already died. The borrowed silver must be returned to my daughter, do not give it to anyone else!" Upon waking, he learned that the woman had indeed died and did not dare to be unfaithful, returning the borrowed silver to her daughter.
A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

A Japanese woman from a certain household died of an illness. One night, she suddenly rose up, took a few steps toward the back room, and collapsed. The people around her were greatly surprised and wondered if she had come back to life. Some said, "This is a case of *shi bian* (corpse transformation)." They all followed her to see what would happen. When the woman reached the back room, she fell over holding a box. They examined her, and her body was as cold as ice; there was no breath entering or leaving her nose and mouth, and she had never truly lived. They opened the box, but found no precious or unusual items inside; there was only a single letter. When they read it, they learned that the woman had been having an affair with someone before her death, and this letter was from her lover. Although she was dead, her spirit remained conscious and still thought that this letter in the box would be seen by others. She wanted to tear it up herself, but lacked the strength to do so. Just as she was about to reveal it, she was discovered, which was both pitiful and laughable. In Japan, there is a small village named Chōze, where a canal runs through it. The water in the canal is extremely clear, but only a few inches deep. One night, a villager was walking when he heard someone talking and laughing in the canal. He lit a candle to look, and saw it was his neighbor, an old man, who was bathing without taking off his clothes. The old man said, "How pleasant, how pleasant, but the water is too cold; it should be mixed with hot water." The villager realized he had encountered a ghost and helped him back home, where the old man finally woke up from his dream. When asked about the incident, the old man said, "Someone invited me to drink at an inn, and after we finished drinking, they invited me to bathe in a bathhouse. It felt very relaxing, but the water was just a bit too cold."

In Japan, there is a place where pine trees form a dense forest, stretching for about two li in width and length. The wind howls and the rain roars through the trees, and the sun and sky are nowhere to be seen. It is vast and uninhabited, with no rice fields or vegetable gardens, and thus has become a gathering place for fox spirits. Once, a man passed through this area and saw a fox emerge from among the grass. It picked wildflowers and inserted them into its hair, gathered fallen leaves and transformed them into clothing, then wore them and walked—appearing as a beautiful young woman. The man followed her, and saw the woman enter the market and spend money to buy goods. He then called out from behind, saying, “This is not a human, but a fox. Do not be deceived by her.” Soon after, someone struck the woman’s back very hard, and she immediately vanished. The people in the market looked at the money she had spent, and found it to be nothing more than leaves. Another farmer entered the market, buying fish, meat, and other foods, placing them in a bamboo basket and carrying them home. As he passed through this pine forest, he saw two people quarreling loudly. He asked them what the matter was, and they replied, “We found a silver voucher, but we cannot agree on how to divide it, so we are arguing.” The farmer said, “Why not exchange it for money and then divide it?” The two replied, “We have never been to the market before, and we do not know where to exchange it for money. Please take this voucher to the market, exchange it for money, and return. We will divide it into three parts, and you and we will each receive one part.” The farmer was very pleased and placed his basket on the ground, saying, “I will go to the market to exchange the voucher. You two will guard this for me. This place is full of fox spirits—do not let them steal it.” The two said, “We agree.” The farmer went to the market and showed the voucher to people, but it turned out to be nothing more than a withered leaf. He was greatly shocked and immediately ran back to where he had left the basket, only to find it empty. The two people were nowhere to be seen. He looked around in panic, but there was no trace of anyone.

In the shrines and temples of Japan, there are spirit tablets. These tablets are made of paper, with the larger ones measuring six or seven inches and the smaller ones three or four inches. They bear the names and titles of certain gods and bodhisattvas. All the people wear these tablets, believing they can prepare for unforeseen events, and they call them "Guardian Talismans." During the sixth and seventh years of the Tongzhi era of my dynasty, one day in Japan, a strong wind blew, and objects flew through the air, falling like dancing snowflakes. Upon closer inspection, these objects turned out to be spirit tablets. There were also copper and stone Buddha statues, as well as gold, silver, and copper coins, mixed among the falling objects. The coins were found stuck to branches and leaves, and often in flower pots, adhering so firmly that they did not fall off when shaken. At that time, the whole country went wild. People built spirit altars and formed spirit gatherings, with men dressed in women's clothing and women in men's clothing. They beat drums and gongs, rushing through the streets, shouting, "How wonderful it is! How wonderful it is!" Old and young people rushed about, filling the streets and overflowing the city walls. It took a long time for this frenzy to subside.

There are also young men from households who sometimes do not return home for five or ten days. When they return and are asked about it, they say, "A monk invited me away, and I went from one mountain to another, until I reached a certain mountain." These mountains are generally high and steep. The mountain they arrive at is always one that has had a shrine. Calculating the distance, they must have traveled thousands of miles in a single day, flying through the air. It is commonly said that this is the work of the tian gou. The tian gou, it is said, is mostly found in deep mountains and remote valleys, ancient sites of gods and Buddhas. Its shape is like a human, wearing monk's robes, holding a feather fan, with a nose and bridge seven or eight inches high, and wings on its back. There is also a variant called the wood leaf tian gou, which has a beak like a bird, four claws on its hands and feet, and wings on its back, resembling the form of the thunder god as described in China. It rewards good and punishes evil, which is its duty. If this is true, then it is a deity, but it is not known why it is called a tian gou, and at that time it is a nuisance to people, yet the reason for this is not understood. If that country has a *Wuxing Zhi*, such matters would certainly be recorded.

The *Tian Guan Shu* from the *Shiji* states: Along the coast, the vapor from the sea resembles towers and pavilions. In the region of Echigo in Japan, there is a port called Yoshitsugu, where this phenomenon is often observed. It appears suddenly when the sky is hazy. The color is like pale ink, stretching across the sky. Initially, it appears as a single layer, but soon another layer appears, or even two or three layers. The urban and architectural features are clearly visible, and there are also scenes of carriages and horses galloping, with people and figures fully present. The scene changes rapidly, taking on countless forms and shapes. However, it only appears when there is no wind; if there is wind, it disappears. The area is surrounded by two mountains, and the sea's vapor coalesces to form this phenomenon. In the open sea, however, such an appearance is never seen. I previously recorded the matter of Weng Guangping observing the sea market. Now, having heard the Japanese describe the mirage as "shen lou" (蜃楼), I have recorded it again. However, it is not as detailed as what Weng Guangping recorded.

In the seas off Chikuzen Province in Japan, there are often mysterious fires, which the locals call *Shiranui*. I believe this phenomenon is not unusual, as it is described in the *Maritime Assignments* as "hidden fires burning underground." Additionally, in the port of Tsuruga in Japan, every New Year's Eve, there are three divine fires that float on the sea surface. The light moves swiftly and erratically, and its speed increases especially during the third watch of the night, which the locals call *Ryūtō*. This phenomenon occurs every year without fail, with no increase or decrease. This is indeed a remarkable occurrence. The above events concerning Japan are all recorded by a Japanese scholar named Kitō, whose family name is East Sea and given name is Fu. He had read my works while abroad. Upon arriving in China, he learned that I had written *Right Platform Immortal Pavilion Notes* and recorded these ten or so events, entrusting my student Wang Mengwei to convey them to me. I then lightly polished the text and preserved it. My poem, which says, "Old stories are all written down, strange events are also sought from abroad," is indeed true.

*Yijian Zhi* records: A man named Zhou from Pingjiang City suffered from unceasing malaria. He had once heard people say that malaria was caused by spirits, and that one could avoid it by going elsewhere. This shows that as early as the Song Dynasty, there was already the belief in avoiding malaria. In the early winter of the Gengchen year of the Guangxu era, I was staying at the Right Platform Mountain Retreat, and my attendant, Yu De, fell ill with malaria, experiencing an episode every other day. After three such episodes, it was again the day when the illness was due to strike. At dawn, Yu De arose immediately, picked seven petals of peach leaves, placed them at his hairline, faced east and made three bows, then hurriedly set out. He ran to the first bridge of Su Causeway, then arrived at Jingci Temple, and proceeded to visit the interior of Leifeng Pagoda. He then took the peach leaves from his hairline, plucked some grass, tied them together, and placed them on the ground, saying a prayer: “You all remain here; I am going away.” After the prayer, he went directly into the city, bought wine and food, ate and drank until full, and returned in the late afternoon. The malaria did not occur, and thus it was indeed avoided.

Only those with strong blood and qi may use this method; otherwise, none fail to worsen (from *Yupian*: "Duo," pronounced *ding guo*, means "body." It is unknown why it has the meaning of "to avoid" or "to hide"). When I was four years old, I moved from my old home in Nandai, Deqing, to Shijia Dai in Linping, renting three rooms in the house of the Dai family to live in. One of the crossbeams under the floor of the eastern wing had been cut, and someone had written the character "jiu" (to save) upside down on a small square piece of paper and stuck it on the wall; no one knew who did this, nor could anyone say where it had originated. I lived in this house for twelve years, and not a single strange or supernatural occurrence took place. Only once did a boy from the Sun family come; he had just surpassed ten years of age and was suffering from a chronic illness. While sitting in the room, he saw a woman slowly walking from the eastern wing toward the back house, wearing a blue cloth shirt and a black cloth skirt. Sun asked, "Who is this?" At that time, my mother-in-law was sitting with him, but she saw nothing. Sun insisted, so my mother-in-law went to look, but saw nothing at all. She laughed and said, "Your eyes are playing tricks on you." The boy died the following year—was this a sign of decline?

My maternal family, the Yao clan, resides in the Zao Mountain Port of Linping. The hall, with rooms on both the east and west sides, was where the great lady (my mother) once walked from the west room to the east room in the middle of the night. A young maid held a candle in front of her. She saw a woman leaning on the hall's railing, enjoying the moon. The great lady asked who she was, but the woman did not respond. When they approached, they could not see her. They searched for her, but the hall was empty with no one in it. All the inner room doors were closed, so she could not have gone elsewhere. They suspected it was a ghost. My wife, Lady Yao, has seen more ghosts than anyone else in her life. Her second sister, who married into the Dai family, lives in Huzhou. Lady Yao once visited her when she was just over ten years old and had not yet married. One evening, she heard the sound of clothes rustling behind a screen. When she looked, she saw her second sister's uncle, who had died several years ago, calmly go up the stairs and leave. Lady Yao said, "The ghosts I have seen are none as close as this one." Later, our family rented a house in the Chen family's property along the Dry River in Linping. Lady Yao often saw something there but did not tell me.

In the year Renxu of Tongzhi, my family boarded a steamship to travel north, arriving in Tianjin to avoid bandits. My wife saw countless ghosts in the high parts of the boat, some sitting, some lying down, and wondered if the ghosts were also boarding the ship to travel north to avoid bandits! In my collection of poems, *The Hundred Laments*, I wrote: "The sea vessel, drifting, set out on a northern campaign, without effort, the demons welcomed us. How could I clearly see them with my own eyes? Humans and ghosts are traveling together." This records the event. When the Spring Hall in Wu was completed, we moved in to live there, and she told me, "This house is peaceful, and I see nothing." I once discussed this with my disciple, the upright scholar Feng Mengxiang, and said, "My wife, being inherently weak in nature, this is a sign of her decline." He replied, "That is not the case. Ghosts are originally in the space between heaven and earth, coexisting with humans without contradiction. If a person has a clear and discerning spirit, they can see them. The fact that she saw them before was not a sign of decline, but a sign of strength; the fact that she no longer sees them is not a sign of strength, but a sign of decline." Feng Mengxiang's words were like this, and perhaps they contain some truth? My spiritual awareness has been declining early, and recently it has become even more dim and hazy. Even seeing people's eyebrows and beards is not very clear, so it is appropriate that I can no longer see ghosts.

My elder brother's son, Zuzui, styled Ful卿, is the younger son of my late elder brother, Foning Jun. In the Bingzi year of Guangxu, he was selected for the provincial examination, just completing a cycle of sixty years since my late father was listed in the register of scholars in the Bingzi year of Jiaqing. This is also a commendable story in the realm of scholarly success. On the day before the announcement of the results, my wife, Lady Yao, dreamed in her room in Wu that my late father came from outside, dressed in the attire of a seventh-rank official, as if he were alive. My wife greeted him and asked, "I am going to express gratitude, and I will borrow a string of court beads from your grandmother." At that time, my late grandmother was still present, and my wife woke up, saying happily, "Ful卿 has succeeded!" In my "Hundred Sorrows" poem, I wrote: "Ah Mai is clever, and I have always pitied him. In the autumn wind, he happily composed a poem on 'The Herd of Deer.' My late father entered the dream very clearly, to borrow a string of mani beads." This records the event. When my late father changed his mat, he wore the attire of a seventh-rank official but without court beads. Now, he is borrowing the beads from my late grandmother. Wearing court beads as a seventh-rank official, perhaps still using the rank of Yu as a Hanlin scholar? At that time, my late father had already received the rank of second grade, is it still considered more important to be a Hanlin scholar?

During the Daoguang era, in the year 1828, I was staying at a village called Wangcun in Xiuning. During the autumn and winter of that year, there were nightly sounds of ghostly wailing within a radius of dozens of miles, and everyone heard it, but I did not. Throughout my life, I have never heard or seen ghosts. However, during the Xianfeng era, in the year 1860, I fled the war to my uncle's place in Shangyu. Mr. Yao Pingquan, who was the head of the school in Shangyu, had recently donated a building, and his family still lived in the official residence. He allowed me to stay in the flower hall. At midnight, I was half-asleep, and I saw a woman passing by. How could there be such a thing at such a late hour? Could it be a ghost? In the Guangxu era, in the year 1879, my wife, Lady Yao, passed away. I was deeply moved and hoped to see something, but nothing happened. Although I dreamed every night, these were merely the "thinking dreams" described in the *Rites of Zhou*, not dreams of her soul entering my dreams. In the year 1880, I stayed alone in the Right Platform Immortal Hall. I could not sleep at night and tried to practice the three-character rule I mentioned on the pillow (as seen in *Yu Lou Za Zuan*). Just as I was about to fall asleep, I was disturbed by a mosquito, which greatly troubled me. Thinking that my diet had decreased and my spirit had weakened, I relied only on the peaceful nights on the pillow for some comfort, so I could still manage. If I did not sleep tonight, I would be greatly exhausted tomorrow. Soon, the mosquito landed on my face again. I had no way out, and then the mosquito made a faint sound, as if it had been struck by someone. From then on, it was silent. I sighed and said, "My wife's spirit must have driven this mosquito away."

During the years of Xianfeng Wuwu and Jiwei (1858-1859), my family rented a house in the southern part of the city owned by Mr. Shi Zhuotang in Wu. The house had a garden called Wu Liuyuan, which was very beautiful with springs and stones. In the southwestern corner of the garden, there was a small three-story building, and some people said a fox lived there, but we often went up there and saw nothing. At that time, we kept a small dog with long fur that reached the ground, a short tail, and short legs. Its shape was charming, and its nature was alert and intelligent. It would come immediately when called, and Madam Yao loved it very much. One night, the dog suddenly disappeared, and a thorough search failed to find it. We suspected it had escaped through the main gate and blamed the gatekeeper for not watching carefully, but the gatekeeper insisted he had not seen it leave. The next morning, as soon as the garden gate was opened, the dog jumped out. It was in the garden. However, we had indeed searched the garden thoroughly that night, so why hadn't we seen it? We suspected it was hidden by the fox, perhaps because the fox knew people loved this dog and just played a trick on us. During the chaos of Gengshen (1860), our whole family fled. Every time someone got into the carriage, the dog would run up to the front of the carriage. Madam Yao still ordered the caretaker to take good care of it. When the city was taken, the dog and the caretaker both died. Later, I spoke to my brother Fu Ning about this, saying, "They both died in the line of duty." Fu Ning smiled and said, "Then there should be a posthumous honor." I replied, "Yes. I have a poem called *Recalling Past Travels*, and the second half says: 'Looking back at Jinxiang, only overgrown weeds and ruins remain. Where are the green blood buried? Sighing at the gray-haired and yellow-eared ones, all turned into phosphorescent light.' This is the highest praise and recognition for them."

In Renhe Linping Town, there is a temple called Yongping. In a certain year during the Daoguang era, it was suddenly reported that there were hollow sounds underground in the temple. At once, everyone went to see, and the market was empty. Some said there were treasures inside, others said a dragon was about to emerge. I was still young at the time, and I felt anxious when I heard about it. After one or two months, fewer people went to see it, and the rumors gradually subsided. However, no one really understood the reason behind it. Was it that it was only discovered this year, or had it not been like this before and suddenly made noise this year? Some suggested digging to find out the mystery, but they were all afraid and dared not proceed. In the year of the provincial examination in the Gui Mao year, I was studying in the temple and asked the monk. The monk led me to this room. The room was made of mud, having neither bricks nor wooden boards. When I stepped on it, the sound was loud and resonant, like striking a large drum, and I suspected that it must be hollow underneath. After the war and destruction, this temple had become overgrown with grass. The sound had long since ceased amidst the rubble, and it could not produce such a sound again.

Lang Peichang, a resident of Linping Town in Renhe, was at home when he suddenly received a letter from an unknown person. The letter stated: "At the foot of Linping Mountain, behind Jingxing Temple, there is a stone shaped like a toad. Beneath it lies an infinite amount of silver, which cannot be obtained unless you personally open it. You can come to the place at a certain day and time, open it, and take the silver." Lang thought it was a false statement and did not believe it. The next day, someone knocked on his door and delivered another letter, with the same content as before. Looking at the person, Lang did not recognize him. He asked where he got the letter, and the person replied: "I met someone on the way who asked me to deliver it to you, but I don’t know where he came from." The person then hurriedly left. Thus, those who knew Lang all said to him: "Since there is such an unusual matter, why not try going once, what harm could it do?" Lang followed their advice, arrived at the place at the appointed time with a spade, dug it, and indeed found a large amount of silver. But Lang did not bring a basket or similar container, so he had no way to carry it. He then covered it with soil and went to a house at the foot of the mountain to borrow a basket, which he carried back up the mountain. He met several people who seemed to be gathering firewood, each carrying a basket or similar container, but he did not have time to examine them. When he reached the place where he had dug, it was already empty. He then realized that the people he had met earlier were carrying the silver. Lang dug it but could not get it, but if it were not for Lang’s hand, it would not have been opened. The several people must have known the reason. The two letters were sent by these several people, but no one can understand why they did so.

Behind the examination hall in Hangzhou, there was a temple called the Five Deities Temple of Ducks. Near the temple stood a broken stone mortar. The temple monk would discard the leftover ashes from burning firewood or straw in it, and occasionally keep a fire burning inside, to provide light or warmth for people in the evening or at night. The residents nearby found this very convenient. Even on rainy or snowy days, the fire was never extinguished. No one knew where the fire came from, and everyone believed that the temple monk was responsible for maintaining it. There was a man named Mao, whose home was not far from the temple. Every day, as he passed by, he would stop to smoke by the fire, making it a daily routine. One day, the stone mortar suddenly disappeared, and everyone was surprised. The temple monk then said, "This mortar was indeed unusual. I would occasionally keep a fire in it, sometimes every three or five days, sometimes every seven or eight days. How could it have kept burning all year long without interruption?" As a result, everyone began to suspect that the mortar was a treasure, but they could not trace its origins. Mao’s son, Yongxiao, with the courtesy name Bao Yuan, told me about this. Bao Yuan still saw the stone mortar when he was a child.

In the first year of the Tongzhi era, I was in Tianjin when suddenly news spread of a mother in the countryside who had killed her children. It was said that the mother, a widow, had been involved in an affair with someone and feared that her children would discover it. She planned to kill them to silence them. Her son, just ten years old, was studying at a village school and had heard a little of the plot. As the sun passed noon, the village school teacher allowed the students to go home for meals, and this boy, fearing to return, did not go. The teacher, not understanding his meaning, thought that because he was young and walking alone, he might be afraid of something, and personally escorted him to his home. Later, after the other children had finished eating and gathered together, this boy did not come, so the teacher went to his home to ask him, but he had already died along with his two sisters. The teacher was greatly surprised and asked the reason. The mother's words were disjointed, and the teacher forced her to examine the bodies, which revealed that they had all strangled themselves. The evidence was clear, so he reported it to the authorities. The officials investigated and found the truth, and were angry at her lewd and cruel behavior. They chained the adulterous man and woman and paraded them through the market.

I recently called a barber to comb my hair and asked, "Is this true?" He replied, "Yes." "Have you seen it?" I asked. "Yes," he said. "This woman is tall and fair-skinned, but she has large eyes and high cheekbones, so she is not a good wife." The next day, a friend from the same hometown arrived from the county office, and I inquired in detail about the whole affair. My friend said, "How could there be such a thing? Yesterday, the county magistrate went to the countryside for official business and has not returned yet. The county is quiet and devoid of any incidents. How could there be such a thing?" I couldn't help but laugh. Su Dongpo once said, "Can one make a judgment about whether something exists or not without seeing it with one's own eyes or hearing it with one's own ears?" Although I did not see this event with my own eyes, I had heard about it, and the person who told me claimed to have seen it, speaking with great certainty. It must not be a lie; yet, it turned out to be completely false. Rumors can become true through repeated telling, and most things in the world are similar to this.

The Vice Minister Peng Xueqin was born in the twelfth month of the twentieth year of the Jiaqing era. At that time, there was severe cold and snow. His mother, Lady Wang, had been in labor for four days without giving birth, and she was on the verge of danger. Suddenly, a strong wind blew from outside the window, the doors and windows opened by themselves, and all the lights and candles went out. Everyone was startled and fell, and Lady Wang also fainted on the bed. Soon after, the Vice Minister was born, and after some time, Lady Wang regained consciousness. She said, "Just now, I saw a great and powerful man with a black face, who entered bent over, as tall as a building. I was very frightened, almost losing my soul." The story is recorded in the Vice Minister's own compilation, "Biography of Lady Wang." I jokingly said, "As recorded in historical texts, there is a black-faced Vice Minister and a black-faced Wang Shenggong. Who were you in your past life?" The Vice Minister replied, "No, this black man must have been protecting me. I was a woman in my past life. If you don’t believe me, look at my ears, they still have holes from earrings." However, I looked at them and saw nothing, and I do not know what basis the Vice Minister has for this statement.

In a village in Anhui, there was a widow of the Chen family who was forced to remarry by her deceased husband's brother. Unable to bear the pressure, she hanged herself, but the family falsely claimed that she had died of illness, and no one reported the incident. At that time, Mr. Peng Hegao, a magistrate from Hengyang, was serving in the area as a local official, and he was also the ancestor of Shi Lang, a high-ranking official known as Xueqin. He happened to be in the county at the time. Shi Lang's mother, Wang Taifu, was living alone in her official residence when one night she suddenly heard the cries of a woman, as if saying, "I am suffering in injustice." At first, she did not pay much attention, but the cries continued for several nights in a row. A wet nurse named Yang Mei, who had heard of the Chen family's story, said, "Could it be the wife of the Chen family?" Wang Taifu then said, "If it is her, we should call out three times." As she called out three times, a chilling wind passed through the treetops, and then all was silent. Upon returning, Mr. Peng reported the matter to Wang Taifu, who secretly investigated and confirmed the truth. The case was then reported to the superior authorities, and the matter was judged according to the law. This event is also recorded in Shi Lang's memoir, *The Biography*. However, Mr. Peng initially served as the magistrate of Sanqiao Town in Huaining and was later transferred to serve as the magistrate of Liangyuan Town in Hefei. The text does not specify the location of this incident, so it is unknown where it occurred.

In Poyang County, Jiangxi, there was a man named Ye Zuo'en who married a widow named Chen from the same county. They had a son named Fuli. After Ye Zuo'en died, Chen gave birth to another son, named Fude. Chen could not remain faithful, so she married a man from the same county named Yan Moseng. Yan Moseng lived with Chen and her two sons for five years before bringing them back to his own home. The two acres of land left by Ye Zuo'en were given to Yan Moseng to farm, in order to support the two sons. The land was frequently destroyed by floods, leading to a lack of food and clothing, and Fuli had already turned nine years old. Yan Moseng then sent Fuli to Xu Mao Guaizi's house in the village of Kengxia to learn tailoring, giving him 3,400 coins each year. Soon after, he sent Fude to Liu Guangyu's house in the village of Kengxia to work as a herder. The location was forty li away from Yan's residence, which was called Chumenhu.

On the twenty-fifth day of the twelfth month of the third year of the Guangxu era (1877), Morsheng arrived at Keng Xia Village to fetch Fulai and Fude to return home for the New Year. On the morning of the twenty-sixth, they ate hastily and then set out. Fulai carried a blue cloth bag containing one silver dollar and a thousand copper coins; Fude carried a white cloth bag containing several measures of rice. They reached Yuan Yang Ridge and encountered rain, and Morsheng also had a cough with phlegm. They stopped briefly to rest at the pavilion. Coincidentally, Lei Xi Mao, a laborer for the Liu family, arrived carrying two baskets. Lei lived near Yan and was also from Keng Xia Village. Morsheng said, "I am too ill to get up. You two can go ahead with him. I will come soon when I am slightly better." He then placed the money and rice into Lei's baskets, and Lei and the two boys continued on their way. When they reached Yuan Yang Ridge, he told the two boys, "I will part ways with you here. You can sit here and wait for your father to return together. I will go now."

He returned his money and rice to the two sons and went home. Meanwhile, Mosheng was still lying in the pavilion. After a long time, his illness healed, the rain stopped, and it was also dusk. Then he took a shortcut and returned directly to his home, having already reached the second watch of the night. He asked his wife, who told him that the two sons had not yet arrived. The next day, he sent Yan Fulei to ask Lei Ximao, who said that they had lost each other at Yanyangling. They searched around Yanyangling but found no trace of them. In Shangwan Forest, there was a man named Ouyang Liuma who said that on the 27th day, he met two young boys asking for directions. He gave them a rough indication, but after they asked for directions, he did not know where they went. There was also a man named Wang Tongxing who had opened a cloth shop by the roadside. He said that on the 27th day, two young boys came to him because of hunger and asked for food. Wang fed them and they left. When asked where they went after eating, he did not know. When asked if anyone saw them, he said, "There was Ouyang Fale who happened to be in the shop. The two boys left, and he also left."

On the twenty-eighth day, Yan Mo Sheng finally recovered the bodies of his two sons at Chen Gong Shan. Fu Lai had injuries to his cheek, ear, and throat, while Fu De had injuries to both kidneys. The money and grain were all present, with nothing missing. Chen Gong Shan is located two miles closer to Che Men Hu, but no one knows who killed them. Some say it was Ouyang Fa Le, others say it was Ouyang Si Lin. Thus, Yan Mo Sheng accused Ouyang Fa Le and Ouyang Si Lin of killing his two sons and took the case to the authorities. However, the Ye family said, "It was Yan Mo Sheng who coveted the land left by his deceased father and killed them himself." They also took the case to the authorities, and the lawsuit remained unresolved for a long time. In the fourth year of Guangxu, my father-in-law, the Vice Minister Peng Xueqin, toured Jiangxi Province and arrived in Raozhou. Both Yan and Ye submitted petitions to the traveling office, and he ordered the Raozhou Prefecture to investigate the matter. In the summer of the sixth year, the Vice Minister arrived in the provincial capital of Jiangxi, and all officials below the level of Governor welcomed him at Tengwang Pavilion.

The wife of Yan Moseng, Chen, reported a previous matter. Someone ahead on horseback rebuked her, and she threw herself into the river. The deputy minister quickly ordered someone to rescue and lift her up, received her document, and reported to the governor. The high-ranking officials in Yuanchang had known the matter for a long time and all suspected that Yan Moseng actually killed his two sons. They reasoned that the two sons were young and there must be no one who would kill them out of enmity; if someone benefited from having something, why would the money and rice still be present? Therefore, there was no doubt that the stepfather killed them. Before the deputy minister arrived, they had already ordered the case to be moved to the provincial capital for trial. Wang Jun, the magistrate of Poyang, who was known for his honesty, sighed when he first took office, saying, "There is such a case in the jurisdiction, and we could not determine the name of the person who committed the murder. This causes concern for the provincial administration. What is the use of a county magistrate if he cannot solve such cases?" At the time, all the witnesses in the case were detained in the county. Magistrate Wang secretly sent spies to observe and monitor every action and word of the people involved.

By this year's fifth month, rumors circulated among the common people that Vice-Premier Peng was about to arrive for his inspection tour along the river and would personally come to the county town to review and judge wrongful cases. Upon hearing this, Ouyang Fale immediately became suspicious of himself and frequently inquired about the news from the laborers. On the sixteenth day of that month, the Vice-Premier arrived. Prior to this, there was a man named Shen Ke Fa from Fuliang who privately carved a wooden seal, forged credentials, and claimed that he had served in the Vice-Premier's camp; the Vice-Premier questioned him and found the truth, and immediately executed him according to military law. Ouyang Fale became even more afraid, and that night in his sleep, he mumbled and repeatedly called out "bad" twice. Mr. Wang observed his condition and knew that the murderer must be Ouyang Fale. Then, he set up an altar for the City God in a private room and prayed. In a dream at night, he came to a place where he smelled the stench of a corpse but could not see it. There was a person covering the body with his body; upon looking, it was Ouyang Fale. By dawn, Mr. Wang personally led the prisoners to the temple and questioned them, saying to Ouyang Fale, "You are the real killer; the god has already told me."

Fa Le, though he did not immediately confess, his expression greatly changed. The next day, he was interrogated again at the Temple of the City God. All the prisoners wailed and cried out, asking the divine beings to clear their names, but Fa Le said not a word. Around midnight, he suddenly shouted, "I dare not deceive the divine beings; I will reveal the truth." It is said that Yuanyang Ridge is still more than thirty miles away from Chemen Lake. On the night of the twenty-sixth, the two boys stayed overnight at the local shrine on Yuanyang Ridge. The next morning, they proceeded on their way, met Ou Yang Liubo, and asked for directions. They continued on, eating at Wang Tongxing's cloth shop. Fa Le saw that they were young and could be deceived, and he wanted to lure them to another place to sell them. He then caught up with them, walked with them, and asked to be their guide. He led them to his own home, where they stayed in a room made of earth outside the wall, and even his family members did not know about it. On the twenty-eighth, just before dawn, he called them again and walked with them to Chen Gong Slope, which was close to Chemen Lake.

Fu had recognized the place, climbed the mountain, and saw the village, but refused to go with Ouyang Fale. Ouyang Fale forcibly pulled him, leading to a loud cursing, followed by a severe beating on his head and face, and ultimately choking him to death. Fu ran and shouted, saying, "They killed my brother!" He kicked someone with his foot, injuring their kidney, and also killed them. They found two cloth bags, saw money and rice inside, but threw them on the ground and did not pick them up, fearing that this would lead to being tracked down by others. Wang investigated and found the truth, then immediately reported to the deputy minister. The deputy minister, at the time in a temple on Jiao Mountain, read the report and was extremely happy. He personally approved the document, with comments expressing, "Years of accumulated frustration are suddenly relieved. Bowing from afar to the sky, pray to the spirits of the two victims to thank the kind magistrate. There are many cases of injustice in the world, how can officials be so devoted? I cannot help but feel moved." That year, the deputy minister came to Wu, knowing that I have a notebook, showed the entire case to me, and thus recorded the main points as follows.

During the Dazhong period of the Song Dynasty, Shao Zongyi of Wu County cut open a clam and found a statue of a bodhisattva, with the right shoulder exposed and the head turned to the left, all the clothing patterns complete (as recorded in *Tianzhong Ji* citing *Yiyuan Cihuang*, though I have not seen the original text). In the Xuanhe period, Yu Ji of Lizhui found a statue of Guanyin in a clam, with two bamboo branches beside it. The bodhisattva's appearance is beautiful and dignified, adorned with a crown, clothing, and jewelry, and the bamboo leaves and branches are all made of fine pearls (as recorded in *Yiji Buzhi* Volume Thirteen). Such sightings of Buddha and bodhisattva statues in clams are recorded in historical texts. Peng Xueqin, a high-ranking official, found two clam shells with Buddha statues on Putuo Mountain in Ningbo. He donated one to Ziran An in Jiao Mountain and brought the other to Suzhou, where I was able to see it. There are twelve Buddha statues in total, arranged in three rows: three on the left, five on the right, and four in the center. The statues resemble the Maitreya Buddha statues in temples, with smiling eyes and eyebrows, fingers, and navels, all extremely lifelike, and it is indeed a miracle. Xu Huanong, a scholar, said, "The *Diamond Sutra* mentions one Buddha, two Buddhas, three, four, and five Buddhas; these are the offspring of the clam, and they can truly be called three, four, and five Buddhas."

Peng Xueqin, the Vice Minister, studied at the Shigu Academy in Hengyang when he was young. There was a man named Xiao Man, who, in his youth, was gifted and skilled in writing legal documents. However, his intention was to help the weak and suppress the strong, not to use cunning and deceitful tactics for profit. After middle age, he deeply regretted this and turned to studying Daoist teachings, becoming proficient in Daoist rituals and enjoying the practice of fengji. Whenever he performed fengji, he always did so with the Vice Minister. Xiao Man could only burn talismans to summon immortals, but when it came to writing on a sand tray, it was always the Vice Minister who did the writing. The messages produced during fengji were written by the Vice Minister himself, but they often accurately reflected the inquiries of the questioners, even though the Vice Minister did not understand why. Over time, their fame grew, and people came to ask about good or bad fortune and seek remedies for illnesses, almost every day. One day, an old official from the county, whose wife was ill, sought a remedy. The Vice Minister, using the fengji method, wrote a poem: "Suddenly, a terrible illness struck the heart, but old rice and tea will cure it. I give you only these two remedies, and your illness will surely be cured by the time you descend from the high tower." The next day, the official came to thank them, and indeed, his wife recovered after drinking the old rice and tea. Xiao Man became more confident, but the Vice Minister thought it was just a coincidence, and such incidents occurred countless times. At that time, the magistrate of Hengyang County was Jin Risheng, a person from Zhejiang.

His grandson was just three years old when he suddenly fell ill, and Mr. Jin sent a doctor to treat him. The doctor prescribed a remedy and left. According to the prescription, the decoction was prepared to be given, but at that moment, his grandson was sound asleep. Mr. Jin's wife told her daughter-in-law, "The child is sleeping peacefully; do not rush to give him the medicine." She then instructed a hired woman to place the decoction on the rice steamer to keep it warm. Soon after, the child woke up, and the mother-in-law and daughter-in-law both tried to give him the medicine. The child cried and did not want to drink it, but they forced him to take it. He could not finish it, and when they looked at the bottom of the bowl, they saw a thick residue like paste. Mr. Jin's wife scolded her daughter-in-law, "I specifically told you to pour out the clear liquid, why did you do this?" The daughter-in-law was surprised and said, "I already poured it into the bamboo strainer and removed all the sediment, how could there still be residue?" However, she did not suspect anything unusual. Soon after, the child began to cry loudly, his complexion changed abruptly, and his hands and feet clenched, making him look extremely distressed. They suspected that the medicine had gone wrong. They called the doctor back to question him, and the doctor said, "My medicine may not have been effective, but how could the illness suddenly worsen?" He took the remaining medicine to examine it and exclaimed in shock, "This is opium paste! It is dangerous!" It was during the middle period of the Daoguang era, a time when opium was not yet widespread, but it was already common in government offices.

The cook of Jin had always been fond of opium, and on that day, he happened to use a bowl of opium paste on the steamer for cooking rice. The bowl was of the same size and shape as the child’s medicine bowl, and the elderly woman, in her haste, mistakenly took it. Jin flew into a rage and hastily summoned the cook, intending to give him a severe beating. Jin’s wife said, “It would be pointless; we should consider the child’s welfare.” Instead, she called for various doctors, but when they arrived, they were all at a loss. Someone suggested that Mr. Xiao and Mr. Peng were skilled in using the spirit board and could seek a remedy. So, two messengers were sent with letters to them. One of the messengers arrived at the academy, where the Vice-Minister was already present. The messenger delivered his message. The Vice-Minister privately thought: “A three-year-old child drinking a whole bowl of opium paste will surely not survive. I should not go.” But the messenger insisted, and the Vice-Minister, unable to refuse, said, “Then I should go with Mr. Xiao. But I don’t know where he is.” He asked the messenger to help find him. The Vice-Minister believed that Mr. Xiao might not be able to help, and if they delayed for a little while, the child would surely die, and there would be no further need for action. As soon as they stepped out of the academy gate, they met Mr. Xiao. He had no choice but to accompany him to the county office. Upon arrival, the incense table and spirit board were already set up, and Mr. Jin, dressed in official robes, had been waiting respectfully for a long time. After briefly describing the child’s condition, they asked Mr. Xiao to perform the spirit board ritual. Mr. Xiao burned the talisman as usual, and Mr. Jin knelt and bowed with great reverence. The Vice-Minister, filled with anxiety and fear, had no idea what to do. He held the spirit board pen but could not form a single character, only making frequent rotating motions.

Jin repeatedly requested with great effort, and the spirit repeatedly wrote "I have arrived." After writing, the spirit continued to make a rotating motion. Xiao Man saw the writing tool (箕笔) behaving differently from usual and became doubtful and fearful, looking around anxiously, and his sweat flowed like water. The official became increasingly distressed, unable to recall the commonly used medicines, but suddenly, the words "castor seeds" appeared in his mind. Thinking that castor seeds are indeed a medicine, but they are not commonly used and should not be prescribed lightly, he pondered but found no other suitable option. He then wrote "castor seeds" on the plate. Jin asked again, "Having received the medicine, I dare to ask how much should be used?" The official wrote "one liang" on the plate, unable to control the situation. Jin then invited the two men to a side room to rest and offered them food. Before the meal was finished, Jin came out to thank them, saying, "The child vomited heavily after taking the medicine, and the poison was completely expelled, so now there is no harm. This is a gift from the divine, and it is due to your two efforts." The official and Xiao Man were both greatly relieved and shared the news with others. A knowledgeable doctor said, "Castor seeds indeed can draw out poison, but they are strong in nature, and when used together with croton seeds, the dosage should not exceed three qian."
