Therefore, the character should be read as "子兑" (zǐ duì), according to the *Collected Works of Zeng Guofan*, which includes a *Memorial to the Full-Moon Maiden* that states: "My father had nine children, and the youngest daughter, whom the family called 'Full-Moon Maiden,' was last in the line." Her name was also written as "满" (mǎn), taking the meaning of fullness or completeness. The character "税" (shuì) is a vulgar character and not reliable. The Vice Minister Xueqin further recounted: One day, while he was writing in the study hall, Xiao Man arrived and shouted loudly, "Hurry and help me, or else it will be ruined!" When asked what the matter was, he explained that at the time, there was a bookstore called Jixian in the city of Hengyang, and the owner, a woman, was possessed by an evil spirit. She had invited Xiao Man to perform the ritual of *Chile* (敕勒之术), but he was greatly troubled and a stone flew and almost crushed his skull. Therefore, he wanted to go with the Vice Minister to perform the divination ritual. The Vice Minister privately thought that divination was false and not effective against evil spirits, so he did not want to go. However, Xiao Man insisted, and they went together, though the Vice Minister walked very slowly because he was not truly interested. On the way, Xiao Man explained: "This woman lives upstairs and throws stones from the window to hit people. All the books in the shop were destroyed, and nothing else was left intact. For a month, no one dared to go near the shop, and those who did were trapped by the evil spirit." While he was still speaking, the Vice Minister became furious and said, "On a clear day, and yet demons are roaming freely like this! I will definitely go and eliminate them."

Rushing to the place, Xiao Man called out from behind, saying, "Stop, stop, I still have something to say." Asked what it was, he replied, "Going together gives a bit more courage." The official became even angrier, saying, "What do I have to fear?" He directly knocked on the door of the bookshop. The door opened, and he rushed in. The owner asked for his name, but he did not reveal it, saying, "I came to drive away the demon!" He immediately took off his hat and exposed his head, calling out from the top of the building, "If the demon can throw stones, why not hit my head? If it can't, then I'll climb up and expose your body, pull down your dryness." The top floor remained silent. The official continued calling out, and Xiao Man arrived, seeing him and saying happily, "We've won, let's quickly go up." They both climbed up. The woman was in the canopy, holding onto it tightly without letting go. The official called out to her husband, "Cut it open!" Once the canopy was opened, the woman quickly pulled the quilt over her head. Xiao Man took a bowl of clear water and drew a talisman in it, instructing her husband to lift the quilt and pour it over her. The woman drank the talisman water, showing no discomfort. Xiao Man said, "Drinking this will slightly clear your spirit. Let's use the divining rod." The official then used a bamboo pen to write a prescription, using a little cinnabar to calm the mind and茯苓 to soothe the spirit, giving it to the husband and saying, "Use this to treat your wife." They then left together. The next day, they inquired, and the woman had indeed recovered.

During the first month of the seventh year of Guangxu, around the Lantern Festival, the lantern market in Yangzhou was very lively. From the goose-necked bend on Jiaochang Street to Lihou Street, more than one li in length, tall booths and wide canopies lined the streets, with lights shining brightly like paintings, and men and women were playing and strolling, filling the streets and alleys. There was a man surnamed Wang from Zhanggang Town who brought his entire family to the market to watch the lanterns. Soon, they all got separated, leaving only the old man and his son Wang Da. Those who were lost were: one elderly woman, aged fifty-four, who was the wife of the old man; one young woman, aged twenty-four, who was the wife of Wang Da; another young woman, aged twenty-three; three young girls, the eldest seventeen, the second thirteen, and the youngest nine, all daughters of the old man; and one young boy, aged eight, who was the son of Wang Da. Thus, the old man and his son both cried bitterly on the road. Some say there are people in the rivers and lakes who are called 'Sprinklers of Heavenly Flowers' and are skilled in using drugging powder. They want to take people's children, first sprinkling the drug on those who come with them, so they cannot see each other. Then they sprinkle the drug on the one they want to take, and the person will follow them dazedly. The old man and his son said, "This is true; at that time, we both felt dizzy and almost fell, it must have been the drugging powder." Wang Da said, "Father, please go back first. I will try to find them. If I fail, I won't return." He threw a cup and cast lots to ask the deity for guidance, and the deity showed him to travel southeast. After traveling for several days, he arrived in Yizheng and saw a boat at the river mouth, where clothes were hung on a pole, including his wife's skirt.

Wang Da went to seek to join a boat, where an old man was sitting at the bow, speaking in the dialect of Chu, and firmly refusing him. Wang Da offered a large bribe, and the old man wanted to accept it. A young woman at the stern of the boat spoke to the old man, saying, "Do you not care about your life? I want to keep this body to take revenge, and it is absolutely impossible!" Listening to her words, she seemed to be a person from the Central Plains. The old man then waved Wang Da away. Wang Da wandered along the riverbank, and suddenly, a patrol boat came by on the river, arriving under sail with the wind. Among those on board was someone Wang Da knew, a man surnamed Chen. Wang Da immediately boarded the boat and told them the reason, and they lay down at the mast to wait. In the late afternoon, they saw someone enter the boat, and Chen then gathered his companions to capture him. The person leaped into the water, leaving only the old man and the young woman on the boat. The young woman introduced herself as surnamed Huang, from Yanshi, Henan, and said she was also someone who was taken by his art. "All four members of my family were killed by him, their bodies were thrown into the river, and only I am left."

The elderly man was surnamed Zhou and hailed from Chaling, Hunan. He was old and begged in the market, and the person who took him in treated him like a slave. Chen asked about the person's name, and the woman replied, "He is from Shucheng, Anhui. We only know his surname is Li, but we don't know his given name. He is thirty-eight years old. He is skilled in water and brave in swimming, and he will not die even if he enters the water." Wang asked where the family members were, and it turned out they were indeed in the boat, locked in the hold (as the Tang poet Ouyang Zhan wrote in his poem, "Through the curtain, a faint moon enters the middle warehouse." In ancient times, this character was written as 仓, but now it is written as 舱, a common variant). They were released and found to be mute and unable to speak. The young woman said, "They drank mute medicine; when I first came, it was the same. Drinking water would cure it." They were given water to drink, and they regained their ability to speak. They were taken back together. The military officials on the warship transferred the boat to Yizheng County, and the county official ordered the capture of the person surnamed Li. Whether they were eventually caught is unknown.

A man surnamed Cheng from Huizhou opened a shop in the Liufang Lane of the New City of Yangzhou. On the 24th day of the 12th lunar month in the year 1880 (the 39th year of the Guangxu era), after the kitchen god ritual, he gathered with his apprentices for a drinking session, all of whom drank heavily and then stopped. A man surnamed Li, after drinking, went to a shop owned by an acquaintance to chat. Just then, someone came to borrow money, and the promissory note was ready, but there was no guarantor, so the owner refused. Li, with great enthusiasm, offered to guarantee the loan, but the owner declined. Enraged, Li started cursing and then tore off his clothes, showing his arms, almost ready to use force. The crowd watching around him was like a wall. Nearby was the residence of the reserve magistrate Wu, and Wu's son heard the commotion outside and came out to see what was happening. Suddenly, Li raised his hand and injured Wu's son, breaking four of his teeth. Wu was angry and ordered the village head to watch over Li, planning to send him to the authorities at dawn. In the middle of the night, Li sobered up, became extremely frightened, and struck his head against the wall, causing blood to flow over his face and making him faint on the ground. The crowd was shocked and unable to save him. Just as they were trying to help, a sudden old man appeared and said, "Don't move, I can treat him." He took a basin of water, wrote a spell in it, inserted it between Li's teeth, and poured it in. Li finally revived, and the bleeding stopped immediately. The old man said, "If there is no wind for ten days, he will be fine." Wu, upon hearing this, asked the old man to examine his son's broken teeth. The old man said, "Although the teeth are broken, the roots are still there, so they can grow back. If it's a child, they will recover in a hundred days; if it's a man, he should abstain from sexual activity for a year, and they will also recover." Wu asked for treatment, but the old man did not accept thanks, only asking that Li be released from his crime. Wu followed his words and let Li go. This old man must have been highly skilled in the art of Zhubaoyu, a traditional Chinese healing practice.

In Tianjin, there are so-called "Gūniángzǐ," who are female shamans. When rural women fall ill, they often send for them to treat the illness. Upon arrival, the shaman lights incense in a brazier and mutters something indistinct, then claims that a deity has descended upon her, which is called "dǐngshén" (topping the spirit). The deities she tops include "Báilǎo Tàiyé" (White Old Lady), who is a hedgehog; "Huáng Shàonǎinai" (Yellow Miss), who is a rat-wolf; "Hú Gūniáng" (Fox Girl), who is a fox; and two others, a snake and a rat. The people of Tianjin collectively call these five deities the "Wǔjiā Zhīshén" (Five Family Gods). In Zizhu Forest, there was a Mrs. Li who contracted a cold illness. The shaman told her, "Your name is already written on a copper plate in the underworld, and after ten days, it will be written on an iron plate, which even the gods cannot reverse. Fortunately, you still have ten days, so you should quickly perform a ritual to ward off the illness." The patient only listened to the shaman's words and prepared a large offering of meat and wine to pray. Within one or two days, the illness seemed to improve slightly. The shaman became more confident and began to give her secret prescriptions of decoctions and pills. The medicines were mostly compounded by the shaman, costing a great deal, but they proved ineffective. The illness worsened day by day, and the shaman repeatedly changed her prescriptions in hopes of a miracle. Sometimes she would prescribe a morning remedy and an evening one, or a remedy that would cause a fever yesterday and a cold today. Before the ten days were up, Mrs. Li died.

Fan Xi Di, a man from Hubei, whose courtesy name was E Lou, was once dead and revived, as mentioned in the seventh volume. Now, it is said that E Lou had a concubine named Yao, who was always gentle and beautiful, and good at serving her husband's wife. She often said, "A woman, when married, should treat her in-laws as she would treat her own parents, which is in accordance with the rites. Now that I am a concubine, I dare not encroach on the duties of the primary wife; therefore, I can only treat the husband and his wife as I would treat my own parents." Because of this, E Lou and his wife loved and pitied her greatly. When E Lou served in Qianzhong, due to the constant turmoil caused by bandits and robbers, he entrusted his wealth to Shu, and Yao also followed him. Soon after, Yao died. E Lou, who was in Qianzhong, did not know about her death. One night, he saw Yao appear slowly at the door, wanting to enter but hesitating, and then reported to the ground and bowed. He saw that she was dressed in a bright and distinct garment, and was surprised, saying, "How can you wear such clothes?" And then he saw nothing. A few days later, a letter came from home, saying that Yao had died. Later, when the chaos in Qianzhong was somewhat settled, the family returned from Shu to Qianzhong. E Lou told his wife, "Yao died, but you dressed her in fine clothes and buried her, which is not in accordance with the rites." His wife replied, "Not so."

E' Lou laughed and said, "You are not lying to me!" He then enumerated her hairpins, earrings, and clothes. The wife could not hide it, so she said, "I am very sad, so I slightly fabricated it. How did you know this?" E' Lou told her what he had seen. It was only a weak woman, yet after her death, her spirit remained intact and was able to travel from Shu to Qian, which is quite extraordinary. My eldest daughter-in-law's uncle died early. Her aunt-in-law was very sad, fearing that her mother-in-law, who was old, would be hurt, so she dared not cry. She wept day and night, and when her tears ran out, she followed with blood. After a long time, the blood also ran out, and then something like grease or fat came out of her eyes, making her vision cloudy and dim, almost causing her to lose her sight. One day, there was a family of relatives who played the divination game of *fupi*, and her uncle descended, speaking as he did in life. Someone asked him, "Since you have a spirit, why don't you return home and meet with your family?" The spirit board wrote, "I lived without committing any sins, and after death, in the underworld, I have a slight rank, not constrained by the officials of the underworld. Even if I go home every day, it is not impossible. The reason I do not return is that returning would be of no benefit, only to make my family grieve." Her aunt-in-law heard this and had someone talk to him again when he descended, asking him to definitely return and swearing not to cry. They then agreed on a date, and she said, "If you cry, I will immediately disappear." When the time came, they set up the spirit board in a secret room, and he indeed came. The spirit board moved with sound. Her aunt-in-law, hearing from outside the window, unconsciously sobbed, and the spirit board stopped moving. Later, despite repeated requests, he did not come back. Her aunt-in-law deeply regretted it, but it was too late.

A Man from Shu, Shen Qiu Feng, had a connection with the family of his eldest son's mother, the Fan family. During the reign of Emperor Tongzhi, he once served as the Governor of Guizhou Province and fell ill. A doctor was called to treat him, and he had already recovered. The doctor said, "One must not wash feet for a hundred days, only then can one be saved. If one washes their feet, there is no hope of recovery." Shen Qiu Feng greatly enjoyed washing his feet, and at that time, his sleeping, waking, eating, and drinking were all back to normal. He did not believe the doctor's words, and the family members strongly opposed it. It was almost a hundred days, and Shen Qiu Feng wanted to wash his feet. The family sent someone to invite Fan Jue Lou, and they spoke with him from dusk until late at night. Fan Jue Lou then left, thinking it was late and time to go to bed. Shen Qiu Feng ordered hot water to be prepared, and the family members repeatedly tried to stop him, but he did not listen and ultimately washed his feet. The next day, he died. Strange indeed, Shen Qiu Feng's illness, whatever it was, led to his death after washing his feet, which is quite remarkable. The doctor knew this well, but ultimately could not follow his advice. Perhaps it was fate? The *Southern History* records that Yin Zichun washes his feet once every few years, and each time he does, he loses money and fails in his affairs. Like Shen Qiu Feng, he ultimately died because of washing his feet, and it was even worse than Yin Zichun.

A Woman from the Ma Family, a native of Hunan, found herself in a dire situation when her mother-in-law fell ill and was on the verge of death. The woman wept and said, "My mother-in-law and I relied on each other for life. If she must pass away, I shall be left alone, solitary and without support, and I too shall die!" Her mother-in-law replied, "Do not worry. After I die, I will transform into a bird and remain with you." Soon after, the mother-in-law passed away, and indeed, a bird appeared in the room and did not leave. It would often perch in the woman's lap, and she fed it rice daily. After a month or so, the woman wept and prayed, "My mother-in-law pitied my loneliness and hardship, transforming into a bird to protect me like an egg under its wings. This is very good! But how can my heart find peace? Please let my mother-in-law be at ease." After her prayer, the bird flew away and did not return. My granddaughter-in-law Peng told me this story, and the woman from the Ma family is a relative of mine. Another woman, from the Fu family, was also a native of Hunan. She was young and followed her father to serve in the central region. Her father loved and cherished her greatly. When she was sixteen and about to be married, he sent her off for dozens of miles. Before returning, he took off his half-sleeve robe and gave it to her, saying, "Use this to keep warm on your journey." After her marriage, she and her husband got along very well. She was gentle and good at serving her in-laws, and there were no quarrels in the family. However, her father later died, and her in-laws kept it a secret and did not tell her. Her husband informed her, and she wept bitterly. Her in-laws tried to comfort her, but she said, "I am grateful for your kindness, but how can I not be careful with myself?" She stopped crying, but a few months later, she died suddenly. After her death, a maid mentioned that the woman had hung her father's half-sleeve robe in a private room, bowed towards it, and wept softly. She remained in the room for a long time before coming out.

When she served her in-laws, she maintained a pleasant expression and gentle demeanor, as usual. Yet, in her private moments, she would never fail to weep profusely. Thus, it is known that this woman died of grief, and she may be called filial. A young boy named Liu, from Su'an in Zhejiang, was fourteen years old and gathered firewood to support his mother. One day, returning from the mountains, he sang as he walked. A neighbor ran to tell him, "The tiger has taken your mother away; are you still singing?" The boy was greatly alarmed, dropped his firewood, and returned home, carrying a steel fork to chase after the tiger. When he caught up to it, he stabbed the tiger's back with the fork. The tiger, enraged, released his mother and turned to bite the boy, opening its mouth wide. The boy stabbed it with the fork, hitting its palate. The tiger leaped, and so did the boy, driving the fork deeper into its jaw.

A Child's Spear Stuck in the Ground A child wielded a spear that was stuck in the ground, and the tiger's mouth could not close around it. The tiger's front paws were in the air, unable to exert force, and it was extremely exhausted. After a long time, it jumped again, attacking with the spear still in its mouth. The child also fell. He quickly carried his mother back home, called neighbors to come and see, and the tiger was dead. The tiger was submitted to the authorities, and the government bestowed ten thousand coins on the child. His mother's injuries were not very serious, and she recovered after treatment. This story was told by a peer named Zhang Zihou. It is said that a person surnamed Mao, from the same village as Liu, actually witnessed the event. The "History of the Yuan Dynasty, Biographies of Chaste Women, Biography of Lady Yao" states: "The tiger carried her mother, and Yao hastily went to chase it, immediately striking its side with her hand, and the tiger then dropped her and left." This is similar to the event. That one woman, and this one child, facing a fierce beast, their courage unmeasured, can inspire the timid. If the story of Tong Ban Na in the "History of the Song Dynasty," who gave her life to save her grandmother, is considered especially pitiable.

Feng, a person from Hai Ning, gave birth to a son in the year 1880 during the Guangxu era of the Qing dynasty. The child was born without a right arm, and people found it strange. Zhang Zihou, a historical figure, once said, "In the past, in Quzhou, I saw a woman give birth to a child without a left arm." I have previously mentioned a similar case in the tenth volume of my work, where a woman from the central region of China gave birth to a child with two heads and two arms. These two cases, therefore, represent one with a missing right arm and one with a missing left arm. Some say that pregnant women should avoid seeing a lunar eclipse, for if they do, the child they bear will surely have a physical defect. Mr. Xu, from She County, Anhui province, was a man of great wealth. I once saw him at the gathering of my old friend Wang Jingxuan, who was his father-in-law. Mr. Xu's family was extremely wealthy, running over forty pawnshops in Jiangsu and Zhejiang provinces, and he himself remained prosperous. He was a very honest and sincere person, but his speech was slow and hesitant, as if he were reluctant to speak. However, among his children and grandchildren, there were several generations who took pride in their extravagance, living luxuriously with fine wine and meat, their lifestyle exceeding that of kings and princes. They had over a hundred servants and dozens of horses, with colors ranging from blue-black to red-white, all perfectly matched with their saddles and bridles. Whenever they went out, they were preceded and followed by a grand procession, showing off in the streets. One day, a local official suddenly arrived with an official document, and the governor considered Mr. Xu arrogant and domineering, wanting to arrest and interrogate him. Fearing for his safety, Mr. Xu bribed people at all levels to avoid punishment, spending an incalculable amount, and finally, the matter was dropped.

The group of three or four individuals conspired and said, "Our hometown cannot be lived in anymore, why not go out and travel?" Each prepared boats and carriages, traveling to Jiangsu and Zhejiang, visiting all the places where their families had shops, no matter how far. Upon arrival, they would daily send notes to the shops, taking silver and money without limit. The shop owners sometimes hesitated or refused, and they would then become angry, saying, "This is my family's property, what business is it of yours!" They would send their favorite courtesans to the shops to take whatever they wanted, causing the shop owners to be greatly afraid. All wrote letters to inform Mr. Xu. Mr. Xu considered himself unable to discipline his children, and he said, "Now I will close all the shops, and if they have nothing to take, they will stop." He wrote letters to all the shops, instructing them to close on the same day.

After a while, the patrons in the shops all raised a great outcry, saying, "The owner's lack is not of wealth; why then close all the shops? If it benefits the owner, then it's fine, but what about us?" Mr. Xu heard them and said, "Indeed, as you all say." He then ordered that from the managers down, everyone receive a gift. The managers were given either a thousand or two thousand taels of silver, depending on the size of the shop, with the amount decreasing progressively for those of lesser rank, down to the servants and laborers, all of whom received something. Even the lowest-ranking person was given ten thousand taels. When Mr. Xu first decided on this plan, he had not counted the number of people. But when the plan was put into effect, the manager tallied the numbers, and found that in more than forty shops, there were nearly two thousand people, all of whom received their gifts and left, leaving Mr. Xu with no money. The accumulated wealth of several generations, amounting to several million in assets, was all gone in a single day. This was truly astonishing. When I saw Mr. Xu, he was still wearing a hat with a turquoise stone top adorned with a blue feather plume. Mirror轩 told me, "The old man has nothing left but this!"

The Late Magistrate Yun Fu of Hai Ning State, whose given name was Fu and style name Zi Kuan, once served as the magistrate of Jia Shan County. He was known for his benevolent governance and was enshrined in the Hall of Distinguished Officials. To this day, the people of Jia Shan still recount his remarkable anecdotes. One such story tells of a young man from the Qian family who came to the county town to take the examination. He stayed in a house beneath a certain building, where a woman living in the building took a liking to him and sent a letter to arrange a meeting. The young man, being a gentleman, was ashamed of the situation and left on some pretext. A butcher named Lu, who lived nearby, found the letter the young man had discarded and went to the building on the appointed day. After climbing the stairs, he immediately extinguished the lights. The woman, unaware that it was not the young man from the Qian family, did not realize the mistake. When he left, the woman’s parents discovered what had happened and chased him away. Lu then drew his blade and killed the woman’s father before fleeing. The woman’s family then filed a lawsuit accusing the young man from the Qian family of murder. The magistrate, however, realized that the young man was not the murderer and questioned the woman: “The lights were already out—how could you know it was the young man from the Qian family? Did his body have any scars or moles that could be used to identify him?” The woman replied, “There was a tumor on his buttocks.” When they examined him, they found no such mark. The magistrate then sent someone to search the bathhouse, where they found the butcher Lu. After a single interrogation, he confessed. Another incident is said to have occurred involving a commoner named Jiama, who lived with his youngest uncle, their voices often reaching one another.

Jia returned home after a long journey, wanting to test whether his wife was virtuous. He placed some money he had brought with him into the incense burner of the village temple, pretending to be poor, and then returned home. His wife treated him as she always did. After they had rested, Jia revealed the truth to her. In the morning, he went to retrieve the money, but it was gone. He took the case to court. The judge investigated the situation at Jia's home and learned that Jia's younger brother had once tried to sell his wife. The judge deduced that Jia's brother must have overheard the couple's private conversation and taken the money in the morning. To find the thief, the judge pretended to consult the gods. The next day, he had the village god brought to the court, and the entire village came to watch the unusual event, including Jia's brother. The judge asked the god, but the god did not respond. He then ordered a large stick to be used to hit the god's head, and a piece of paper came out of the god's ear. The paper read, "The thief is Jia's brother." Seeing the evidence, Jia's brother lost his composure and confessed his guilt. The case was resolved. The paper in the god's ear was actually written by the judge. The judge was known for his integrity and had shown great wisdom in uncovering hidden truths. In the spring of 1881, the sons of the Yu family asked the author to write a biography of the Hai Ning family, through which he learned about this incident. After writing the biography, the author recorded this story in his notes.

During the year of Tongzhi's reign, the second son of the author fell ill with a sudden madness in Wu. His wife, Yao, was greatly distressed and could not sleep at night. In the middle of the night, in a half-asleep, half-awake state, it seemed as if someone called out to him, saying, "Why be so sorrowful? Life has no flavor, why not die?" Upon looking, he saw that it was his cousin. At that moment, he felt a daze and asked, "How can one die?" His cousin replied, "There is nothing better than hanging oneself." Just as he was about to ask more, he suddenly heard his mother's loud call and immediately responded, "Yes." There was a woman named Zhongying from the Zhou family, the wife of the author's inner person Yao, who was the sister of his wife. She was sleeping with her daughter-in-law and, in her sleep, heard the response and woke up in surprise. She immediately asked, "Who called you?" At that moment, her daughter-in-law also suddenly woke up and told her the reason. Both of them were too frightened to sleep again and sat up until dawn.

Afterward, I heard people say that this room once had the ghost of a hanged person. Yet the mother of my daughter-in-law had already passed away before, and her nephew was indeed still alive at that time—could it have been the hanged ghost that conjured this illusion? Later, when my wife and I returned from Tianjin, my daughter-in-law happened to mention this matter, and I said, "Is it only your nephew's appearance and voice that the hanged ghost has conjured? Even if your mother's spirit is still present, it does not need to be with you every day—it could also be the work of a divine being conjuring this illusion. You must still have future blessings, and benevolent deities are guarding you on all sides. They saw that you were about to be bewitched by the hanged ghost, so they pretended to be your mother calling out, to wake you up." As the *Book of Poetry* says, "He is in your room." The common saying goes, "Three feet above your head, there are divine beings." These words are trustworthy. Good people are followed by benevolent deities, while evil people are followed by malevolent ones. If indeed one's heart is upright and one's dealings are harmonious, then wherever one goes, benevolent deities will protect them. Even if calamity comes, it will be transformed into auspiciousness. Otherwise, it is the opposite.

Cai Junfu Zhaoqi from Deqing, Zhejiang, is my great-nephew of my late mother's sister's son. At the age of twenty-nine, while ill, he dreamed of two officials holding documents and summoning him to accompany them. They arrived at a place that resembled a high-ranking official's office, and they led him inside to pay respects. Soon, they led him into a room with a desk, and they asked Cai to sit. Cai said, "I am just a scholar, how could I sit here? Have I died and become a deity? My son is still young, and there are family matters that remain unresolved, what should I do?" He was moved to tears. The two officials said, "If you do not wish to, you should express your wishes in writing, for empty words are of no use." Cai then took up a pen and wrote several hundred words, which he handed to the officials to take away. Afterward, they returned and said, "We have delayed your fate by twenty years!" They led him out, and he woke up, and his illness quickly recovered. In the year 1880 of the Guangxu era, Cai was serving as a magistrate in Jiangsu and was sent on a mission to Zhenjiang. At that time, Feng Junyiting, the magistrate of Dantu, also from the same county, injured his foot while descending from the hall and spoke to the prefect, asking Cai to act as magistrate in his place. By the end of the year, Cai suddenly fell ill and saw the two officials again in a dream, summoning him.

Upon waking, he spoke to someone, saying, "I am now forty-nine years old, and it has been exactly twenty years since my previous dream. Am I going to die?" Someone comforted him, saying, "Dreams are not reliable. Even if there is something to them, it could have been delayed earlier, so why not now?" Cai then wrote a request, asking for another twenty years, and added, "If my lifespan is exhausted and cannot be extended, I have four sons. Please grant each of them five years of life, and thus I can extend my life by twenty years." The text was written by Cai himself, and he had his attendants polish it, then burned it at the temple of the city god of Dantu. Afterwards, his illness indeed improved, and he could drink porridge, glad that he had not died. On the twelfth day of the first month, late afternoon, he suddenly opened his eyes and told his attendant, "Alas, I still cannot escape, the ones who are coming for me have arrived!" When asked what he saw, he replied, "There are many people coming, each holding a lantern, with the characters of the city god of Jimo, Shandong." The next day, at noon, he passed away. Cai had shown talent from a young age and was highly regarded in his hometown. He just obtained an official position but did not live long, and people all felt pity for him. However, after his death, he became a deity, unlike those whose lives end like a fire that is extinguished when the wood is gone.

At the place where the Qiantang River flows into the sea, there is a small village called Wengjia Bu. In this village, there was a man named Jia who, while strolling in the market, encountered a person offering a single pill of sha medicine and insisted that he buy it, demanding five hundred coins. Jia refused, but the person persisted, saying, "If you miss this and don't buy it, can you get it again?" Jia ignored him and left. By evening, Jia fell ill with sha fever, and despite numerous treatments, none were effective. Jia recounted his encounter during the day, and people searched everywhere for the seller, but they could not find him. The next day, Jia died. The person he met must have been a strange or extraordinary individual, and it is regrettable that he missed the opportunity. Sha fever is a common ailment in summer. My two daughters-in-law are from the Yao family of Tangxi, and their family has been renowned for two hundred years with their sha medicine from Zhihe Hall. However, the sha character is not found in any ancient or modern dictionaries, and it is unknown what the correct character is. A scholar named Feng Mengxiang, who is knowledgeable about medical principles and has read extensively in ancient texts, was asked about it but could not provide an answer. I checked the *Jiyun* and found the character '疒蘇', which is pronounced 'su' and means 'illness'. Perhaps the sha character should be written as '穴乙', as '疒蘇' and '穴乙' are phonetic variants. The *Jiyun* also has the character '疒烏', which also means 'illness'. There is a disease called 'wu sha' in the present day, and perhaps it was written as '疒烏' or '疒蘇' in ancient times. The *Shuowen Jiezi* has the character '[C071]', which means 'urgent pain in the abdomen'. The *Guangyun* pronounces it as 'guqiao' and it sounds the same as '绞'. It is the same as the modern term '绞肠痧' (intestinal sha).

The Yellow Soil Master was a Manchu person, and those who talked about him did not mention his name. In the eleventh year of Tongzhi, he was selected and appointed as the magistrate of Jingzhou, Hunan. His family was originally poor, and he traveled alone to Chu, not bringing any servants or attendants. After arriving, he submitted his official documents to the provincial administration and then visited all the high-ranking officials to pay respects. The gatekeeper demanded money, and he refused, so they did not allow him to see the officials. After a long time, he could not take up his post, and his funds ran out. His clothes were all gone, and he became a vagrant in the marketplaces. He carried yellow soil for others, earning several dozen coins a day to make a living. One day, because the wages he received were not enough, he argued fiercely. Governor Tu happened to be out and saw him, stopping his carriage to ask the reason. He told him, and introduced himself as a low-ranking official (the term "卑职" has been used since the Song dynasty, as noted in Yuan Guo's *Qingrongji*).

Governor Tu suspected that the man had a mental illness and ignored him, waving him away. After a long time, the man became increasingly desperate and ended up substituting for the night watchman, striking the watchman's staff. There was an official who was actually in charge of the night curfew; he heard the sound of the staff being struck but did not see the person. He had someone call out to the man, who then emerged from the shelter. The official angrily said, "Your duty is to keep watch at night, yet you are sleeping soundly?" He was about to whip the man, but the man urgently shouted, "No, I am an official!" The official was surprised by his words, his anger turning into laughter, and he asked, "What is your position?" The man replied, "I am the magistrate of Jingzhou." The official was greatly surprised, and upon hearing his voice, he noticed it was a northern accent. He then said, "Is that true?" The man replied, "Yes." "If it is true, then you can meet me at the government office tomorrow." The man said, "Yes." The next day, the man did not show up, and the official asked him why. The man said, "I am wearing a short, coarse robe that barely reaches my knees. It is suitable for night duty but not for meeting a superior official." The official said, "This is my oversight." He lent the man a robe, and the man came, and after examining his family background, official rank, and service record, it was confirmed that he was indeed the magistrate of Jingzhou. The official then took him to see the governor of Changsha. The governor reported to Governor Tu, who said, "Then the man who previously referred to himself as '卑职' is this person?" He summoned the man and said, "You have truly suffered." He ordered the officials to check the records, and it was found that the magistrate of Jingzhou had reached the end of his term, and the successor had not arrived, so the handover had not been completed. Governor Tu ordered the officials to expedite the handling of the official documents, allowing the magistrate of Jingzhou to take office. Afterwards, he told the governor, "This man has truly suffered; he should have a little drink to help him." They collectively contributed four hundred taels of silver to provide funds for his journey.

After several days, the magistrate of Jingzhou came to bid farewell. The official saw him again and said, "I previously ordered the prefect to give you 400 taels of silver as a gift to assist you on your journey. Since you have endured much hardship for a long time, you should further strive and encourage yourself, and strive to be a good official." The magistrate bowed deeply and said, "How dare I not follow your advice. However, I have not dared to accept the 400 taels of silver; I have already stored it in the county treasury of Changsha." The official asked why he had done this, and the magistrate replied, "Although my current position is modest, it is still better than carrying the weight of yellow earth. Plain clothes and simple food are enough to sustain oneself; why need so much money? I have carefully stored it in the county treasury, to prepare for the needs of the public one day." The official greatly praised him and said, "You will certainly be a good official in the future." Thus, the story spread widely, and in the region of San Xiang, he was called the Yellow Earth Lord. I checked with the official records published in the capital, and the name of the magistrate of Jingzhou was Shou Song, or perhaps he is the same person. Governor Tu Gong, who is now the Governor of Henan and Vice-Chancellor Lang Xuan, is also my contemporary.

It is said that during the Ming dynasty, Xia Zhongjing, a man of integrity, came to Songjiang to manage water control and slayed a dragon, as it was fighting with a clam over a pearl. Later, this clam migrated to the sea of Baoshan County. In the early years of the Tongzhi era, the sea dyke in Baoshan was being repaired, and Wang Gengyu, an official from Fuan, participated in the work. One evening, returning from the county, as the night was halfway through, he traveled on the dyke in a sedan chair and saw a red light illuminating the sky, burning like a rainbow. He suspected it was a fire. The sedan chair bearer said, "This is called the clam offering a pearl, it is the light of the pearl, not firelight." They led him to a high place to observe it, and indeed, the light came from the sea. From the Yongle period of the Ming dynasty to the present, over four hundred years, this clam is still there, and it is appropriate that the pearl's light reaches the heavens. Pearl light should be white but is red, as noted by the ancient commentator on the biography of Sima Xiangru, who mentioned that the southern region produces fire pearls, is this what it means?

Mr. Wang, the observer, was from Huaining, Anhui. The local custom was not to use toilets, and people would often relieve themselves in the open fields. Mr. Wang recounted: one day, while squatting in the grass, he saw a lizard chasing a centipede in front of him. Soon, they came closer, with only an inch between them. The centipede suddenly stopped and curled its body like a bow, and the lizard also stopped and did not move. After a while, the lizard turned around and went back. Mr. Wang then struck the lizard, killing it, and when he looked again at the centipede, it was already dead, still curled as before. He found this strange, so he dissected it and found that the skin and flesh were unharmed, but the body was dry and shriveled like wax, indicating that its essence had been absorbed by the lizard. The lizard, when eating a centipede, does not eat its skin and flesh but absorbs its essence, which is indeed unusual. On another day, he saw a snake eating a lizard, starting from its hind legs and gradually moving up its body. There is a folk belief that when a snake holds a lizard's head in its mouth, it is called "dragon and tiger unite," because lizards are also known as "wall lizards." If the lizard is cut and worn, it can ward off misfortune. Mr. Wang hurried back home, took a sharp knife, and waited for the snake to swallow the lizard's head, then struck it fiercely. He accidentally hit the snake's waist, cutting it into two pieces. The rear half of the snake was already dead, but the front half was still alive, and it quickly spat out the entire lizard and fled wildly. Upon examining the lizard, it seemed to have no external injuries, but its skin was already rotten. Although these two incidents were small, they could not be seen unless one was in the open fields.

Geng Yu says: There are actually worms in human teeth. In the past, I once had a toothache, and someone recommended a man from Anhui named Wang who is good at catching worms. I summoned him, and when I asked what he needed, he said, "I need nothing, just one silver needle." I gave it to him. He held the needle and probed into the gums and palate. After a while, he caught two large worms and six or seven small ones. The larger ones were three or four fen long, the smaller ones one or two fen long. They had black heads and white bodies, all seeming dead. He wrapped them in paper and told me to keep them in a warm place, saying to look at them the next day. By the next day, when I opened the paper to look, they were already alive. Their bodies were covered with fine hairs, they had whiskers, pincers, long hairs on their tails, and six legs on their bellies, and they moved very quickly. I mixed insect-killing medicine with them, and not only did they not fear it, but they even seemed to enjoy it. After three days without food, they died. When people see insects, they change color in fear. Who would have thought that such creatures exist in the mouth? Geng Yu also says: When I was in charge of Changshu County, my temporary residence was very damp. While I was sleeping, a bug entered my ear. I heard the sound of its legs moving, like a crab crawling on sand. Sometimes I heard it make a sound, like 'ch-ch,' and the sound was quite long. I tried everything to get rid of it, but finally, I had a barber pull it out, and it turned out to be an ant. I learned that ants can make sounds, and it was also strange.

In the rural area of Renhe, there was a commoner named Jia, who made a living by grave robbing. During the turmoil of the Taiping Rebellion, many hurried burials were taking place, and Jia would go out every night, wielding his tools, and always returned with something he had obtained. The villagers were concerned, saying, "If this matter comes to light, it will surely bring trouble upon us." They then told Jia's wife, "If you are a wife of a thief, you will be implicated, and there will be no place for you to escape death." The wife was greatly afraid and sought advice from the villagers. They said, "We will eliminate him for you, and we will inform you when there is an opportunity." One day, Jia returned home, drunk and sleeping, and the wife informed the villagers. The villagers gathered with weapons and surrounded the house. Jia woke up in a panic, rushed out the door, crossed two small streams, but was eventually caught by the villagers. They gathered firewood, set it ablaze, and burned him to death. The next day, they collected his ashes, bought a coffin, and buried him. The wife continued to mourn in deep sorrow, wearing the most severe mourning attire. They had a daughter who had not yet married and was living with her husband's family. Upon hearing of her father's death, she returned home, hitting her mother with her head, crying and cursing her. Although Jia's actions were indeed worthy of death, his wife, by planning with others to kill her husband, is not even more guilty? The chapter "Xiangshu" in the *Book of Songs* (《诗经》), according to the *Houhanshu: Baihu Tongyi* (《后汉书·白虎通义》), is said to be a wife admonishing her husband, and the poem says, "If a person lacks propriety, why not die quickly?" The relationship between a wife and her husband is governed by the Three Bonds, but emotionally, they are only half a pair. How could it be appropriate for her to speak such angry and harsh words within the confines of the home? Although ancient poetic sayings exist, I dare not believe them.

Chen Ziyu, the scholar, recounted: One night, a thief entered a grand mansion, climbed to its upper balcony, and saw a young girl, about sixteen or seventeen years old, reading by the light of a lamp. A small maid was sleeping with her head hanging down. The thief took the situation lightly, searched through bags and cabinets, taking whatever he wanted. As he was about to leave, he glanced at the girl as if he hadn’t seen her, finding it strange. He playfully patted her shoulder, and the girl, greatly angered, stood up and said, “How dare you be so rude? Do you think my blade is dull? You entered my room, took my valuables—so you must have extraordinary skills. Show me your skills, and you might escape death; otherwise, I’ll make your blade wet with blood.” The thief was greatly frightened and said, “Let me show you my skills.” He immediately climbed the wall, as if there were steps, and reached the eaves, where he began to climb down. The girl smiled and said, “That’s all you’ve got.” She grabbed his foot, threw him to the ground, and broke one of his feet. The girl then lifted her slender foot, climbed the wall, reached the eaves, and gracefully flipped backward to descend. She taunted the thief, saying, “What do you think of that?” The thief knelt and begged for his life. The girl said, “I won’t kill you.” She then grabbed him and threw him over the wall. I refer to the *Book of Liang*, *Biography of Yang Kan*, which says that Yang Kan once climbed the wall of the Yao Temple in Yan Prefecture, reaching five *xun* (a unit of measurement), and left seven footprints.

In the *Ear and Eye Records* by Zhang Lu of the Tang Dynasty, it is recorded that the younger brother of Princess Chai's husband once wore sturdy boots and climbed up to the brick city walls, reaching the parapet without any assistance. At the time, people called him "Wall Fly." Such extraordinary skills have existed since ancient times. However, for a woman to possess such skills is remarkable. I state this fact earnestly, and it may not be false. Unfortunately, I do not know her name, nor can I ascertain her time and place of origin. During the reign of Emperor Qianlong, a monk came to Tongzhou in Jiangnan with two small jade rings inserted into his ears. He was called "Jade Ring Monk" by scholars and officials. He was skilled in conversation and enjoyed drinking alcohol. Initially, there was nothing unusual about him, but later he suddenly disappeared without a trace. People recalled him, wondering how the jade rings could be inserted into his flesh, suggesting that he must be an unusual monk. However, I suspect that he might have first cut open his ears, inserted the rings, and then applied medicine to eliminate the scars. This is similar to what is recorded in *The Collection of Lost Stories*, where King Wu of Wu used the blood of a white otter mixed with jade and powdered amber to heal the scars on Lady Deng's cheeks. Monks might use such methods to deceive the public. Later, in Lushi Town of Tongzhou, a beggar arrived with a piece of bamboo and a small saw. He cut the bamboo into segments, each about three or four inches wide, and then joined the segments together to form a series of connected rings, selling them to children as toys. By evening, he had disappeared without a trace. People examined the bamboo and were amazed, wondering how it could be joined together without any cracks. It must be a truly extraordinary person.

A wealthy man's son married a woman from another wealthy family, and the dowry was very lavish. On the night of their wedding, as they were about to join the wedding banquet, the woman suddenly sighed deeply. The man asked her why, and she said, "Originally, I was promised to marry the son of an elderly scholar. After the scholar died, his family became even poorer, and my father also passed away. My mother then regretted her decision, broke her promise, and married me to you instead. Though it was by my mother's command, I cannot help but recall the past and let out a sound. Please do not blame me." The man was startled and asked, "Where is the son of the elderly scholar now?" She replied, "I heard he is wandering in the marketplace." The man immediately left and told his father, "Our family is fortunate to be wealthy; why should we worry about not having a wife? How can I take the wife of a poor man?" He then searched for the son of the elderly scholar, found him, and brought him home. He clothed him in his own clothes, cleared a separate room for him, arranged for their wedding, and gave him all the dowry that had been sent. After several years, the man's father sent him to the Imperial Academy to take the provincial examination. Although the man had been studying under a teacher since childhood, he spent his days playing around, and all the poems and essays he wrote were done by his teacher, unbeknownst to his father. When he entered the examination hall, he took up the brush and racked his brains, but couldn't produce a single word all day.

Exhausted, the young man fell into a light sleep. Suddenly, an old man pulled aside the curtain and entered. He gently woke the young man and said, "My writing is complete, but the scroll was stained with ink and is now useless. Knowing that your writing is not yet finished, I respectfully offer it to you." The young man was greatly pleased and recorded the old man's words before leaving. He then showed the draft to his teacher. The teacher remarked, "It is good, but it will not match your performance in the next few exams. What can be done?" When the young man entered the second exam, he still wrote not a single word all day. Late in the afternoon, he urgently needed to use the restroom and encountered the old man again. He pleaded, "Do you still have something to give me?" The old man smiled and said, "Yes." He took out five chapters of the classics from his sleeve. The young man showed them to his teacher, who remained silent. In the third exam, the young man met the old man again. His teacher said, "You will surely pass this time." When the results were announced, he indeed passed. The teacher then explained, "The one you encountered was a ghost. There are no such good people in the world. Moreover, in the first exam, you were dismissed due to the ink stain. What would be the point of entering again? When you met him in the second exam, I already knew he was not human. I did not say anything because I feared you might be frightened. You did not take away the wife of the poor man, so it is fitting that you received this reward. That old man must be his father." Everyone agreed, and they generously rewarded the poor man. Later, the poor man also achieved fame, and the two families visited each other as if they were close relatives.

In Hangzhou, there was a poor scholar who, in his youth, crossed the river to Xiaoshan and became a tutor for young students. At the end of the year, he returned home to rest, and in his chest, he had thirty foreign silver dollars, all gifts from his students. When he reached the riverbank, he saw a young woman sitting on the ground, weeping bitterly. He asked her why she was crying, and she replied, "My husband has died, and my family is pressuring me to drown myself in the clear stream. However, I am still pregnant, and I am hesitating, unable to bear it, which is why I am so sad." Before she finished speaking, her family members arrived, and the scholar went with her to her home, rebuking them with great righteousness. They said, "What you say is true, but her mother-in-law has already received a dowry payment from another family to arrange the funeral for her husband. Now, we cannot repay that money, and the situation cannot be stopped." When asked how much the dowry was, they said it was thirty foreign silver dollars. The scholar then gave the money to them, and upon returning home, he had no money left. Someone said, "With such high virtue, you will not remain poor for long." However, the scholar was still struggling with the imperial exams, and at the age of fifty, he continued to take them. The examination hall where he sat was located at the very end, close to a communal latrine, which was extremely foul-smelling. Despite this, the scholar remained calm. Soon, a young man came and sat next to him, covering his nose and saying, "How can one sit here?" He then planned to leave.

The scholar rebuked him, saying, "My seat is behind yours, yet you are plotting to move to another desk. What about the rules?" The youth apologized and did not move. Afterward, their conversation went well. When the topic was given, the youth wrote the article quickly, while the scholar struggled to write. The youth looked at the scholar's work and saw that it consisted of only a few lines, which were awkward and lacked coherence. The scholar, embarrassed, said, "I am not good at writing, and this year, due to old age, I have become even more negligent." The youth said, "Please let me help you finish it." He wrote swiftly, with no need for revision, and completed it, handing it to the scholar. The scholar was greatly moved and said, "In terms of age, I am older than you; in terms of writing, you are my teacher. Please treat me like Wang Xiaoyi, bowing respectfully to you." When the results were announced, both passed. The scholar went to the youth to thank him, following the etiquette of a disciple. The youth said, "I am fortunate to have the same age as you, how dare I receive such a great honor!" They were still exchanging polite words when the youth's mother, who had been watching from behind a screen, sent a maid to ask, "Did you, sir, save a woman by the riverbank last year?" The youth replied, "Yes." The mother immediately came out and bowed, saying, "Thank you, sir, thank you!" It turned out that the youth was the woman's posthumous child.

Tai Xing Li Donghuai was famous for his martial prowess. One day, a monk visited and asked to compete in martial arts. Li Donghuai, fearing he would lose, pretended to say, "Li Donghuai is my teacher, and he is currently out." The monk handed over a name card and said, "Convey my greetings." He then lifted one hand and separated a column in Li Donghuai's reception room from its foundation by several inches, placing the name card inside and saying, "Placing it here should prevent any loss." Then he left. The monk stayed at Qingyun Temple. Li Donghuai watched him leave and went to return the visit. He told the temple monks, "Please convey my greetings to the master, but I fear it might be lost." He spat on the back of the name card, leaped up straight, and stuck the name card to the central beam of the hall, which was about twenty zhang high. The monk saw this and did not compete and left. One was afraid of his bravery, and the other of his agility.

D Liu Yu Xiang, a man from Xiaoling Village in Renhe, was originally from a place where a merchant named Fan was trading in Tianjin. Fan sent Liu to manage the accounts. In his later years, Liu returned south and settled in Tangxi, where he had considerable wealth. When he fell ill and was near death, suddenly a woman dressed in red appeared, accompanied by a man, and they knocked on the door and asked, "Is this the house of Liu Yu Xiang?" Liu's people answered, "Yes." The two entered directly and met Liu's concubine in the courtyard. The woman asked, "Where is Liu Yu Xiang?" The concubine replied, "He is in the room." The two entered the room, and everyone in the room saw them. Liu was lying on the bed, suddenly got up, knelt down, and threw himself forward, saying, "The debts I owe will certainly be repaid." The woman responded, "Having reached this day, can they still be repaid?" Liu suddenly fell to the ground. His family members helped him up, but his breath had already stopped. The woman and the man disappeared, and no one among Liu's people recognized them. No one knew who they were. However, debts not repaid, repaid with one's life.

At the Stone Gate, there was a boatman named Mr. A, who tied up his boat by the water. It was almost evening when two people came to rent his boat, asking where they were going. He replied that it was only a few miles away. They agreed to pay seven hundred coins. Mr. A was very happy and immediately loaded the two people onto his boat and set off. After traveling about a mile, another boat came towards them from the opposite direction. The person operating the boat was a neighbor named Mr. B. Mr. B asked, "Where are you going?" Mr. A replied, "I am taking these two guests to a certain place." Mr. B saw that there were no people in Mr. A's boat. The other boat came with oars and went away with paddles, passing by in an instant. Mr. B did not have time to ask and was suspicious of it. When they arrived home, Mr. B told Mr. A's son. The son immediately caught up with them in a small boat and saw that his father's boat was indeed empty. He called out to his father and asked why. Mr. A saw that the two guests were nowhere to be found. He then realized he had encountered a ghost and was bewildered for a long time. He returned home with his son, and less than a month later, Mr. A died.

In the spring of the Xinsi year of the Guangxu era, a woman from the countryside of Deqing visited her husband's grave. After the ritual, she burned paper money, which caught fire on her skirt and spread to her clothes, unable to be extinguished. In a short while, she was burned to death, and everyone found this strange. Later, it was known that her husband had been a doctor, and after his death, there were several hundred foreign coins left, along with a young child who was just starting to grow up. The woman could not bear to stay, and she was about to remarry. She planned to take the money and leave, but she did not take the child with her. After bidding farewell at the grave, she suffered a tragic death by fire. This spirit can truly be said to have a presence. Tang Canru, a man from Liyang, traded in our lake region and settled there. He had one wife and one concubine, and at over sixty years old, he had no children, and his desire for one was very strong. When he was nearly seventy, his concubine finally gave birth to a son, but soon after, financial losses depleted his assets, and creditors began to demand payment. He fled to another place to avoid them. His wife and concubine had never gotten along well, and now, with their family falling apart, she grew even more resentful of the concubine and plotted to get rid of her. The concubine said, "I already have a son, where else can I go?" Despite this, the wife sold her to a village. As she was about to leave, the concubine wept and said, "This journey will surely end in my death." And indeed, she hanged herself in the village where she was sold. Her son, who was already three years old and could speak, suddenly stared blankly one night and said, "Grandmother called me." Soon after, he fell ill with fever and died the next morning. Tang heard about it and hurried back, but it was too late. The wife, being harsh and domineering, cut off her husband's lineage, even if she were ordered to sell soapberries and brooms, it would still be a lenient policy.

In Volume Fourteen, a villager from Dangkou Town in Wuxi County caught a wild goose and intended to kill and cook it. A scholar saw this and felt compassion, so he bought the goose and took it home to keep as a pet. Afraid that the goose would escape, he tied its two wings with a string, preventing it from flying. The goose lived among chickens and ducks and became quite tame, but whenever it heard the sound of wild geese flying high in the sky, it would raise its head and call out. One day, a flock of geese flew overhead, and this goose called out loudly. Suddenly, another goose flew down from the sky and landed on the eaves of the house. The two geese looked at each other, raised their voices, and flapped their wings as if they recognized each other. One goose wanted to call the other down, while the other wanted to lead it up. The scholar realized that these two geese must have been former mates, so he cut the string and let the goose fly. However, the captured goose had been hanging its wings for a long time and could not fly. It tried repeatedly but fell each time, and it could not escape. The goose on the roof guarded it all day, and then it flew down from the roof, calling out in sorrow together. The next day, when the scholar looked at them, they were both dead. The scholar was moved by their loyalty and buried them together, naming the site the Goose Tomb. Alas! Even the smallness of birds did not forget their mate in this way, making people even more value the importance of marriage.

Outside the city of Yangzhou, a farmer kept a cow that was quite fat. He used it for plowing, and it proved to be very effective. One day, the herder hit the cow against a tree, and while the cow was lying down, a horse suddenly appeared, unknown where it came from, and also came to the tree, lying down opposite the cow. Those who saw this initially did not think it was unusual. Suddenly, the horse sniffed the cow's nose, and the cow immediately jumped up, breaking its rope, and struck the horse with its horns. The horse also got up and kicked the cow. The herder shouted, and the villagers gathered. The fight became more intense, and it could not be resolved. As they fought, they were about to enter the city. The guards were afraid, closed the gates, and then retreated. After one or two miles, they reached Jiaoli Village, where many people followed to watch, but no one could do anything about it. The fight lasted for two hours. Both the horse and the cow fell down, exhausted and died. Upon closer inspection, the horse had a hole in its chest, its abdomen was torn open, and one eye fell to the ground. The cow's two ribs were broken, its abdomen was torn open, and its intestines were exposed, and it lay stiff in the blood. Perhaps this is what the Buddhists call a long-standing grudge? Alas! A kind of karmic root, even though both have fallen into the animal realm, they still cannot let go of each other, which is indeed frightening.

In front of the drum tower in the city of Jiangxi Province, there was a large house that had been occupied by foxes for a long time, and no one dared to live there. To the west of it was a house belonging to a certain family, which had nothing unusual about it. There was a person surnamed He who rented the house to live in. One day, he sent a maid to the back courtyard to collect the clothes that were drying, but she did not return for a long time. Her trace disappeared. There were no doors or windows connecting to other places, and the walls were high and steep, making it impossible to jump over them. He found it very strange. He searched for several days but could not find her. An old servant woman came to report, saying that she had recently heard laughter and talking in the neighboring house. This house had been empty for a long time, which was strange. He gathered his family to go and look. He opened the door, and the door was covered with weeds. Spiders and cobwebs were on the door. He searched through the hall and inner rooms but found nothing. He gave up and was about to return. Someone said that this action offended the immortals, and he should pray to them with his intention. He burned incense and silently prayed before returning. By night, the maid suddenly appeared from the back courtyard. He asked where she had been for several days, and she said, "In the neighboring house."

The questioner asked, “When everyone searched inside, where were you hiding?” The answer was, “When everyone came, I saw everything, but I could not speak or move. I only wondered why no one saw me.” The questioner then asked, “What did you see during the several days you were there?” The answer was, “There were many people, and there was a young girl whom everyone called ‘Guzi.’ She was exceptionally beautiful and the closest to me. She fed me delicacies and dressed me in fine silk. Her home was beautifully decorated with embroidered screens and curtains, and the house was well-constructed. Every night, the women gathered to sing and play music. Sometimes she would ask me to sing, but I could not. When I wanted to leave, she would angrily scold me, saying, ‘You little servant, are you not happy here? Why do you want to leave?’” The questioner then asked, “Then how did you manage to return today?” The answer was, “This was also Guzi’s wish. She said that her family requested it politely, so I could not refuse.” When asked about the path taken to come and go, the maid could not explain, only feeling that her body was lifted and carried as if by some unseen force. There was no fear of being harmed by fox spirits. The next day, they prepared a lavish offering of food and wine to thank her. Afterward, nothing else unusual happened.

A woman from the Xu family in Hubei Province married into the Shao family and became a wife. After passing thirty years of age, she became a widow and raised her orphaned son until he grew up. When her mother-in-law fell ill, Xu wished to visit the Four Great Mountains to extend her life. The Four Great Mountains are traditionally known as places like Mount Emei and Mount Wutai, all ancient Buddhist sites. After her mother-in-law's death, once the mourning period was over, Xu took her son and set out on horseback. Over the years, they traveled a great distance. In the late years of the Xianfeng era, they arrived in the south and stopped at Jizu Mountain in Binchuan County, Dali Prefecture. It is said that this mountain is where Kasyapa, a disciple of the Buddha Shakyamuni, attained nirvana. There was an old copper temple there with vermilion halls and green pavilions, quite magnificent, but it had been in ruins for a long time. Xu was moved to discuss its restoration and chose an auspicious day to gather materials and hire workers to begin the project. Someone dissuaded her, saying, "Your wish is grand, but nowadays bandits are rampant and the people are weak, so the matter will surely fail. It would be better to abandon the plan." Xu replied, "Not so, in all matters, those who have determination will succeed. I will try it, and I am not worried about lack of help." Soon, people from far and near heard of her story and indeed began to contribute.

Some gave money, others gave grain, and Xu used all of it to provide for the costs of construction, without taking any personal benefit. He wore coarse clothes and ate simple food, fighting even harder against himself, and everyone respected and trusted him. The horse he rode was allowed to roam freely in the mountains and valleys, following the convenience of water and grass, without being herded. But when Xu happened to go somewhere, the horse would always arrive on its own, and no one knew why this was so. At that time, a large army had gathered, with ample supplies of food and building materials. Various officers and soldiers sometimes came to the temple, and Xu would always prepare clean and simple vegetarian meals. The officers and soldiers were all pleased and each gave something. Within one or two years, they had collected a huge sum of money, and then they began a large-scale construction project, with hundreds of carpenters and stonemasons working daily. The woman personally prepared the cooking and took charge of all domestic duties, and before a year had passed, the project was completed. Both monks and laypeople who came to pay respects all praised it. At the time, General Ma Xingwu, who was stationed in the area, personally witnessed this. He was not yet fifty years old, with well-groomed eyebrows and slender feet, and he did not lose the demeanor of a noble person. His son was only in his twenties. After the work was completed, the woman asked the authorities for a travel permit to go to Tibet to worship the Buddha. They could not keep her, and she took her son and rode away on horseback. Later, someone who returned from Tibet said he had met this woman on the road.

In Xingguo Prefecture of Hubei, there lived an elderly farmer named Yang, who was known as a wealthy landowner in his village. One day, he returned home on horseback from the city, accompanied by a young boy. As they passed through the fields, Yang looked around and saw that the entire landscape was his own property, which made him feel quite pleased. He turned to the boy and asked, "Do you think my fields are many?" The boy replied, "They are indeed many, but have you not heard the saying? 'One life event can lead to three fires, and after three years, it won’t belong to me.'" Yang became furious and shouted, "Fool!" He immediately dismounted his horse and kicked the boy with his foot. The boy collapsed suddenly and died. The boy's parents filed a lawsuit against Yang, depleting their family wealth by six or seven tenths, and they barely managed to avoid further punishment. Later, Yang's own property was struck by three fires, leaving him completely destitute with nowhere to stand. After Yang's death, his son's wife went to work for the Fan family. My eldest wife, Fan, heard this story from someone else. Confucius said, "When full, it overflows," and indeed, it is true.

The eldest daughter-in-law, named Fan, said: In a village, a farmer had exposed his grain to dry in the field, and an old woman was guarding it, with her grandson accompanying her. A crow flew toward her and called out. The old woman said, "The crow's call is inauspicious. Shall we return home?" So they returned, but before they reached the door, they encountered a procession of a man who was taking his new bride home, with a sedan chair in front. A young man, richly dressed in embroidered clothing, was following behind. By local custom, when a girl is married, her older brother or younger brother must accompany her, and this is called the "maternal uncle." The wealthy use a carriage and horse, while the poor walk on foot. Hence, there is a saying: "Maternal uncle, maternal uncle, walking behind the sedan chair like a dog." Her grandson saw this young man and teasingly recited these two phrases. The young man became angry and raised his arm to strike. Her grandson fell to the ground, hit a stone, and split his skull, dying shortly after. The old woman's family took the young man to court, but it was indeed an accidental killing, and the court could not impose a death penalty. However, her grandson died an unnatural death. The *Classic of Birds* says that crows are inauspicious. Is this really true? Yet if they had heard the crow's call and not returned, they would not have encountered the man taking his new bride home, and thus would not have been killed by his playful words. Trying to avoid disaster but instead bringing about disaster, there are often things in the world that are like this.

Chen, a resident of Han Kou, opened a shop to sell dried meat. One day, a yellow dog entered his shop to find food. Chen, at hand, chopped it with a knife, hitting the vital point and causing it to die immediately. He then dragged the dog and threw it into a ditch. Several years later, Chen saw an old man sitting outside his shop. He suspected that the old man had come to buy meat and asked what he needed. The old man replied, "I did not come to buy meat; I came to find you." Chen asked why he was looking for him. The old man said, "You threw me into the ditch three years ago, and now I am fortunate to have been dug out. Could you have forgotten me?" After speaking, the old man disappeared. Chen asked the people in the shop if they had seen this old man, but they all said they had not seen him, only a dog barking at Chen. Chen returned inside, and soon he developed a fever and headache, fainting and becoming unconscious. Suddenly, he spoke loudly, saying, "I am from the yellow earth slope outside the city of Jiangning, and my surname is Zhou. Because I committed sins in life, after death I was punished to be a dog. You killed me and threw me into the ditch, trapping me and preventing me from escaping. Now, because someone dredged the ditch, I was able to get out."

A complaint was lodged with the King of Hell, who ruled that the accused had mistakenly killed him and that, by precedent, no compensation was required. I repeatedly explained my injustice and suffering, and the King of Hell was moved with pity. He ordered two servants to come with me, not seeking your life, but instructing you to widely spread this matter so that people would know that the lives of creatures cannot be wantonly harmed. Thus, with my one life, many lives are preserved, and I can erase past sins and be reborn as a human. You not only escape punishment but also gain merit. My wife and children were terrified and asked to wait until Chen's illness was cured before following the advice. Chen's illness quickly recovered. Someone came from Hankou and personally heard Chen say this. In Jiangdu, Huang Huisan kept a dog and loved it very much, preventing it from going out of the house. The dog was female, and it was the time when she sought a male, but unable to satisfy its desire, it became ill. Huang's son was eating, and the dog came, not knowing that it was already sick. When offered food, it did not eat and suddenly bit Huang's son's hand. Family members rushed to save him, and then it left. Looking at the place where it bit, the blood flowed like ink. He cried out for over a month, but could not be saved and died. This is also something that dog owners should not be unaware of.

In the Renjia Hutong of Tianjin, there lived a man surnamed Shen, who was over seventy years old and served as a low-ranking official at the Changlu Salt Transport Office. He was quite wealthy, and his house was also splendid. There was an ancient tree in the courtyard, over a hundred years old. Shen wanted to build a wall in the courtyard to separate the inside and outside, but the tree happened to be in the way, so he planned to cut it down. One night, he dreamed of an anciently dressed person who claimed to be the tree spirit and warned him not to cut it down. Shen considered it a strange dream and did not believe it, and finally allowed the axe to be fetched. There was red sap flowing out like blood, and all the family members were afraid. Some tried to dissuade him, but Shen's mind could not be changed. After cutting down the tree, within five days, a fire broke out, and no one knew where it started. That night, there was no slight breeze, but the fire was intense and could not be saved. Shen woke up in a panic from the dream, rushed out of the door, and then remembered there were documents in his bedroom. He went back in to retrieve them and was caught in the fire and died. In total, four people died, but the fire did not spread to other houses. The next day, people went to see it and saw that the main gate was unharmed, the walls and fences around it were not toppled, but the inside had become a field of rubble, which is also quite unusual.

Peng Xueqin, the Attendant, was in his humble early years when he served as a private tutor in the household of General Ma. General Ma was from Guangdong but was actually a Huihe (Uyghur) person who had been resettled in the interior. His mother, who was over eighty years old, suddenly contracted a mysterious illness. When the illness struck, she would disappear, and the family would search everywhere but could not find her, often discovering her on the roof. Sometimes the illness would occur once every few years, and sometimes several times a year. When Peng Xueqin was teaching in General Ma's household, he personally witnessed these occurrences. The mother would sit high on the roof, laughing and speaking to everyone, saying, "Come, come, this place is very pleasant!" The family tried every means to call her back, but she refused to come down. Her son would climb up the ladder and then bring her down, but he must support her, and the roof tiles would all break, and no one knew how she got up there. After she was brought down, she would be asked about it, but she could not explain it herself. The mother lived to be ninety-five or ninety-six before passing away, and there were no other unusual occurrences.

Chen Dong, a scholar-official from Taixing, was admitted to a local school during the inspection of schools in Jiangsu by the high-ranking official Tang Wen Duan from Xiao Mountain. In the eleventh year of the Daoguang Emperor's reign (1831), he passed the provincial examination in Shuntian and became a successful candidate. He spent ten years in Beijing as a guest scholar, specializing in philology, phonology, and mathematics. His contemporaries, Wei Mose and Bao Shenbo, highly regarded him. Chen Dong died in Beijing in the year of the Fire Horse (1835) at the age of thirty-four. The day before his death, Tang Wen Duan visited him.
