It was near dusk, and they saw two children, one dressed in red and the other in green, enter Chen Dong's room and then disappear. Tang Wen Duan told others, "This man will not survive." Next door to Chen Dong's family lived an elderly man named Qin Lao, who rose early every day, regardless of the weather. When Chen Dong was about to be born, Qin Lao saw a fireball the size of a bowl fall into Chen Dong's bamboo garden, and Chen Dong was born the next day. Now, Qin Lao saw another fireball emerge from the bamboo garden and move northward, accompanied by a faint sound. When the news of Chen Dong's death arrived, it turned out that the day Qin Lao saw the fireball was two days before Chen Dong's death. Chen Dong's son, Zi Yu, told me about these events.

The scholar Ziyu Guangwen reported: His eldest son, Rongzu, had not yet married at the age of twenty when he drowned accidentally in the fifth year of the Tongzhi era. The woman he had been betrothed to, a Zhu family girl from the same county, starved herself to death upon hearing the news. At the time, Guangwen was in mourning for his mother and had no time to bring back the body. The Zhu family temporarily buried the body beside the family tomb. Later, Guangwen prepared a boat to go and bring back the body. The boat stopped at the riverbank, about 200 to 300 steps away from the tomb. Guangwen first set up a sacrifice before the coffin and burned a written text, informing them that they would be brought back for a joint burial. Suddenly, a whirlwind arose, blowing the ashes directly into the boat, and all who saw it were amazed. After more than two years, his second son, Guangzu, gave birth to a son, who was designated as his heir. The day before the son was born, Guangwen's wife dreamed of a young girl who smiled at her without saying a word. Upon waking, she saw the girl's face and dress clearly in her mind, but did not recognize her. She mentioned the dream to the Zhu family, and the girl's appearance was exactly as in the dream, and her dress matched what she wore at the time of her death.

Wang Zi Xian, an old guest at the Jiangxi provincial governor's office, had many illnesses after middle age. He could not sleep at night and could not eat during the day. Every morning, he would drink a large bowl of medicine. All beneficial medicines were included in his regimen; drinking this allowed him to manage his affairs like a healthy person. If he did not take medicine in a day, he would become ill and unable to function. A Mr. Xiong told him, "Eating and sleeping are the most important things in life. Without sleep and without food, relying solely on the power of medicine to sustain oneself, and being able to live long and see clearly, I have not heard of such a case. Now I will teach you a method: whenever you have free time, no matter how early or late, close your eyes and sit quietly, gather your mind, and prevent any thoughts from arising. Do this either four or five times or one or two times, each session lasting the time it takes to burn one stick of incense. If you practice this for a long time, it will surely show results." Wang followed his advice. After one month, he happened to sit quietly at midnight as instructed, and suddenly, without realizing it, he achieved a deep sleep. Upon waking, he felt hungry, but it was already late at night, and there was nothing to eat. He reached into a small cabinet and found some baked goods, which he immediately ate.

After eating, he went back to sleep and did not wake up until the next day at the third watch. Upon waking, he felt extremely hungry and quickly requested soup and noodles, eating everything in the bowl. After eating his fill, he patted his belly and told the person with him, "The joy of eating and sleeping is to such an extent. For a long time, I have not known this kind of joy!" Xu Xingtai, who served as a censor in Jiangxi, personally met this person and later told me about him when he came to Jiangsu. Xu Xingtai also vigorously practiced this method, saying, "In the past, when I was a prefect or an observer, there was little official business, and I would meditate several times a day. Now that I am a censor, I cannot do that anymore, but I still ensure that I meditate for the duration of a whole incense stick, without a single day of interruption. Even if there are gatherings of guests and high-ranking officials, with music and entertainment, a slight distraction would make me as if I were not hearing or seeing anything." I sighed and said, "Xiangshan's poetry says, 'After learning to regulate my breath, I remain healthy in old age, and I remain unmoved in noisy places.' This is what you are like." Xu Xingtai encouraged me to follow this practice because of my liver and stomach ailments. I have had a set of three-character maxims, inscribed in the *Yu Lou Za Zuan*, and I am not ignorant of these principles. However, my mind and body are both engaged, and I have not been able to achieve complete detachment. It is just like Su Shi, who wrote the *Longhu Qian Gong Shuo* to his brother Zhe, knowing that his nature is prone to change and unable to fully adhere to these words. Because of this, I have written down the idle matters of Prince Zi and am greatly ashamed.

A man from Lin Dun Road in Suzhou, surnamed Jia, made a living by weaving cloth. There was another man, surnamed Yi, who apprenticed under him for three years. Jia admired Yi's diligence and wished to marry his daughter to him, consulting his wife, who also agreed. However, Jia's wife privately asked their daughter, who did not want to marry Yi, and they abandoned the previous plan, leaving Yi feeling resentful. On the day of the summer solstice in the year of Xinsi during the Guangxu era of the Qing Dynasty, it was customary to stop work, and Jia and his wife left, leaving their daughter alone in the house. Yi entered the room with a sharp blade and said to the daughter, "Your parents both wanted to marry you to me. Why are you unwilling? Today, I will cut your belly open." He then struck her repeatedly with the blade, injuring her breast and ribs. Realizing that the daughter was going to die, Yi took the blade and cut his own throat, dying instantly. The daughter died the next day. Was this perhaps due to a long-standing grudge?

Dai Keting, the Chancellor of Dàyǔ, resigned from his post and lived at home, enjoying discussing cultivation techniques with his guests. During the time when Shao Shan's Tang Wending had not yet passed the imperial examinations, he served as a tutor in Dai Keting's household and also participated in these discussions. One night, the Chancellor invited his guests to sit in a dark room, without lighting candles or lamps, and they all closed their eyes and sat quietly. They sat until midnight when the Chancellor's head suddenly emitted a great light, as large as a plate and as bright as a mirror, with something faintly visible inside, but it could not be clearly distinguished. Tang Wending experienced the same phenomenon. The other guests each had their own light, small like a bean, and flickered uncertainly. The next day, the Chancellor told his guests, "The depth and thickness of our roots, the shallowness and depth of our cultivation, are all revealed here." The Chancellor died at the age of ninety-six. Xu Xinchén, the senior scholar, inspected education in Jiangxi and still managed to see him. At that time, he was already over ninety years old, not very talkative, but smiled when he saw people, and never had a sad face all day. Xu Xinchén remarked, "This old man saw everything in the world, all things, all objects, as nothing but delightful." The cultivation he had truly cannot be surpassed.

Xiangshan Tang Wenden Gong served in the capital city during the Qing dynasty, residing in Dongdan Pailou. The house he lived in was said to be the former residence of He Shikun, a grand councilor during the reign of Emperor Qianlong, and was long known as a haunted house. However, when Tang Wenden Gong lived there, there were no strange occurrences. Only the seven rooms at the back of the house were spacious and unoccupied. The rooms contained stored books, and the courtyard had randomly planted flower varieties. The most western room was a place for worshipping Buddha. Every morning, Tang Wenden Gong would go to this room to pay his respects to Buddha, and his attendants would stop outside the door and not enter. When Tang Wenden Gong reached the outdoor area, he would often see an old man already prostrating himself in worship inside the room. After the old man disappeared, Tang Wenden Gong would enter and pay his respects, and this became a daily routine. Knowing in his heart that the old man was a fox, but because it did not harm people, he tolerated it. When Tang Wenden Gong passed away in that year, the old man suddenly appeared in the main hall. Guests who arrived saw the old man with a white beard, leaning on a pillow, and suspected he was Tang Wenden Gong. They were about to ask about his well-being, but the old man suddenly vanished. After Tang Wenden Gong's death, supernatural phenomena became rampant, and folding chairs and other furniture moved on their own. Sometimes, when several people were drinking tea together, the tea cups would suddenly fly to another place. The Tang family found this repulsive and no longer lived in this house. I heard this from Wen Duan's grandson, Bo Shu, who is a military officer.

Outside the east gate of Xiaoshan County, there is a temple dedicated to the local earth deity, the area being near the sea. Behind the temple lies a large pond, one li square. In the summer of the year Xinyou during the Tongzhi era, a heavy thunderstorm occurred, and a black dragon descended into the pond. Its body was larger than a tree that could be embraced, with its body in the pool and its head still halfway up in the sky. Clouds protected it, making it invisible, and its length remained unknown. At once, there was a widespread rumor that the divine dragon was fetching water, and many people went to watch in the rain, though they dared not look closely. They saw water on its back, splitting into two paths: one path went from down to up, the water being clear; the other path went from up to down, the water being black. Someone knowledgeable said, "There is a water-dividing pearl on the dragon's head. When the dragon fetches water from the human world, the water is separated by the pearl, with clear water going up and turning into rain, and turbid water going down and returning to its original place. Therefore, the water that goes down is all mud and silt." After a while, the rain started to subside, and the dragon then rose into the air and left.

A student from Xingguo Prefecture in Hubei Province was known for his kindness and integrity. He had a young maid named Er'er, whom he treated well. One day, he sent Er'er to sweep the courtyard and steps, but became angry when she did not clean thoroughly enough. In his frustration, he kicked her, accidentally hitting a vital area, and she died instantly. Deeply regretting his actions, he offered a generous funeral for her. That year, he took the provincial examination and entered the examination hall. While sitting in the examination room, every time he licked his brush and mixed ink, he would see two fingers appear before him, but no one was visible. This phenomenon occurred both day and night, tormenting him so much that he could not write a single word. He then realized, "These two fingers are a sign from Er'er, so it must be her." He returned his test paper, waited for the door to open, and performed a grand Buddhist ceremony for Er'er. The next year, when he took the exam again, he no longer saw Er'er's fingers, and the problem was resolved. There was a man surnamed Cai from Zhejiang who lived in the capital. His son was still a young child, and he had already arranged for a young girl to marry his son when he grew up. Soon after, this girl died, and he arranged another marriage for his son without informing the bride's family. On the wedding day, a table was set up outside the bedroom door, incense and candles were lit, and the new bride was made to bow toward it. Someone accompanying the bride from the Fang family said, "This is part of the custom, called 'bathing the room,' which applies to a second wife but not a first wife." The bride's side insisted that this practice not be carried out, and it was removed. Suddenly, a wind blew out the candles, and the bride fell to the ground, as if someone was choking her, making a "kakaka" sound. Everyone was shocked and puzzled. The family then explained the previous arrangement, wrote the girl's name on a tablet, and performed a ritual to honor her. They helped the bride up, and the ceremony continued as usual, and she was unharmed.

In Hubei's Xian Ning County, there was a young man from the Yu family who had been betrothed to a woman from the He family. The woman died before the wedding. The Yu family proposed to bring her coffin back home, but the woman's father refused, saying, "Moving the burial place of a deceased young bride is against propriety." He chose a burial site for her. When the Yu family's son went to fetch his bride, following the custom in the Chu region where the groom must personally go to fetch his bride, he passed by the tomb of the deceased woman and saw a whirlwind rise from the tomb entrance, but he initially paid no attention to it. After the wedding, the young man's personality suddenly changed, and a few days later, he fell seriously ill and became unresponsive. He was especially cruel to his wife, forcing her to kneel in front of the hall, beating and scolding her almost every day. His mother-in-law and father-in-law tried to reason with him gently, saying, "Our son has become ill, losing his normal character, and is making our new daughter-in-law suffer, which we cannot bear. However, if we go against his wishes, his illness will only worsen. Please show compassion for us two old people and do not hesitate to endure the labor and humiliation." The new wife was very compliant, but the young man's illness persisted for a long time. His in-laws investigated the cause of his illness and realized that the whirlwind at the tomb entrance was an unusual event. They then set up a wooden tablet for the deceased woman to worship her. The woman's father also personally visited her tomb and scolded her for being disrespectful, but none of these actions had any effect. After more than ten years, when the young man's second wife died, his illness finally subsided. The Yu family was connected to the Fan family, and a few years ago, the Yu family's eldest daughter-in-law from the Fan family visited her parents' home and once saw the young man, who was already as usual. Soon after, he remarried, but his illness flared up again, and it is unknown how he is doing now.

The daughter of the Fan family from the Xinning period was the aunt of my eldest wife's wife. After she married, she suddenly fell ill with a severe mental disorder, which lasted for a long time without improvement. The Fan family brought her back home and had a shaman treat her, and her illness was cured. They then made her new clothes, replacing all her old ones, and carried her back to her husband's home, covering her with a fish net and making the bearers move quickly. The old clothes were left behind. From then on, the woman fully recovered and returned to normal. Several years later, her mother happened to check the clothes stored by the woman and found several sets of new clothes. She sighed and said, "Such good clothes, not wearing them would be a pity. They have been here for a long time, so they must not be harmful." She personally took them to the woman. The woman reached for the clothes but did not put them on, and suddenly laughed loudly, saying, "I have been looking for you for many years and now I find you here?" As soon as she said this, she relapsed into her old illness, becoming increasingly confused and distressed. Despite many treatments, she could not be cured and ultimately died from her mental illness.

In Hubei, people refer to someone who dies by hanging as "eating oil noodles." Oil noodles are made by mixing water and wheat flour into long strips, which must be slightly oiled to form, hence the name. In Jiangsu and Zhejiang, they are called "sofa noodles," and in ancient times, they were referred to as "maibing." According to the *Yupian*, "maibing" is pronounced "bólǐng" and means "sofa noodles." The *Book of Southern Qi* mentions "water-drawn maibing," which is the same thing. In Xianning, there was a man surnamed Zhang who built a house and was treated harshly by the craftsmen, who harbored resentment against him. One day, they ate oil noodles and buried the remaining ones under the threshold of the house. Soon after, Zhang's wife hanged herself, and Zhang himself often asked others where he should hang himself. The family was troubled by this. A skilled feng shui master inspected the house and said, "There must be something unusual under the threshold." He dug up the oil noodles, discarded them, and the problem was resolved.

Building a house places special emphasis on the ridgepole. Wen Zisheng's inscription for the ridgepole of the Changle Gate reads: "Choose a good time, and ensure no mistake in divination." This shows that even the ancients placed great importance on this matter. The Liu family of Xinning recently completed a new house, and those who lived in it often suffered from coughing up blood. A geomancer said, "Is there something unusual about the ridgepole?" Climbing up to inspect it, they found a red worm, wriggling, about several inches long, but as thin as a hair. It was stuck to the wood and could not be removed. They then replaced the ridgepole, and those who coughed up blood all recovered. Later, it was discovered that the carpenter had accidentally cut his hand on the day of the ridgepole installation, and a drop of blood had seeped into the wood, causing this strange occurrence, which was not done intentionally as an act of malevolent magic. There was a Cheng family from Huizhou, who dominated their village with their wealth, a legacy that had lasted for generations. They had a son who was foolish from a young age, and as he grew older, he remained ignorant and confused. His family had two servants who managed his daily life, controlling his hunger, thirst, cold, and heat. If he refused to follow their instructions, they would severely beat him, and he would submit without resistance. Their method of managing him was like controlling a cow or horse. As a result, people in the surrounding areas knew of his condition, and no one would consider marrying him. The Cheng family had a pawnshop in Wuxi, and there was a family surnamed Wang who had been managing the accounting for generations. Wang had a daughter who was around the same age as Cheng's son. The elderly Wang, moved by a sense of gratitude, said, "My family has served the Chengs since the time of my ancestors. Now that Cheng has a son, but no daughter-in-law, why should I be reluctant to give my weak daughter, who is no more than a young girl, to repay the kindness of several generations?" He sent someone to convey his intention to Cheng. Cheng initially refused, but Wang persisted in his request. Cheng, unwilling to go against his will, finally agreed to marry the girl to his son.

Upon the completion of the marriage, the ceremony of sending geese, the lavish wedding feast, and the preparations were quite complete and splendid. The green pavilion had been set up, and the mutual bowing ceremony was about to begin, but the man from the Cheng family moved clumsily and awkwardly, failing to complete the bowing. Afterward, he entered the room and looked around at the embroidered screens and curtains, realizing that this was not the place where he had slept before. He was greatly shocked, shouting and running around in confusion, making it impossible for anyone to approach him. Unable to bear it, they still ordered two servants to push and pull him away, and the woman lived alone for the rest of her life. The husband's parents spoke to him, saying, "Your son is not human; he torments my daughter-in-law. Please take good care of yourself." The next day, they divided a huge sum of family wealth among him. After a year, they chose one of his brothers' sons as his heir. But the woman was very virtuous and wise, respecting her in-laws, raising the heir, and treating her sisters-in-law well, without any complaints. The husband's parents liked her even more and helped her son gain an official position through financial contributions. The woman then received the dress of a fourth-rank official and lived separately from her husband for thirty years, although she was a high-ranking lady, she remained unmarried. The man from the Cheng family died first, and the woman died several years later.

In his old age, when his years and seniority were both advanced, all matters of the household were submitted to her for decision. The woman was skilled in managing affairs and could also discern the character of others. No matter how big or small, all matters handled by her were perfectly appropriate. Although Mr. Cheng was lost in confusion all his life, his appearance was dignified, fat and white like a calabash, and after middle age, his beard was very beautiful. Looking at him, he seemed like a statue in a shrine. Moreover, since Mr. Cheng's birth, his family became increasingly prosperous, and by timing the market and trading goods, they never lost. After Mr. Cheng's death, the family gradually declined. Yet, did this husband and wife have a natural affinity for each other? When I was a young teacher at the Wang family in Xiuning, the Wang family had connections with the Cheng family, so I learned the details. In my poetry collection, there is a poem titled "The Ivy," which is about a woman from the Wang family. Mr. Shen Zuwěi from Xiaoshan said: His friend Mr. Li was a guest teacher at the Gao family, whose host, Mr. Gao, was a well-read scholar. One day, a neighbor dug up something on the mountain that looked like a duck but had no feathers, was entirely black, and had both eyes closed, and showed it to Mr. Li.

Li did not recognize it, but Gao said, "This is a dragon, fortunately its eyes are not yet open, so it does not pose a threat. Cook and eat it, it is also a strange flavor. However, on its body, not a drop of water can be applied. If it gets water, it can change, creating waves on flat ground, and houses will become ruins." They then carried it to the kitchen, burned the pot until it turned red, put this thing in and covered it. The pot boiled, black water overflowed, about four or five stones, when the water was gone, they opened the lid, and it was already cooked. It was white like congealed fat, they took it out and thinly sliced it, soaked it in fine wine, seasoned with vinegar and meat sauce, and ate it with Li. The taste was very fresh and rich, the beauty of the food, probably none surpasses it. I investigate that dragon meat sauce has existed since ancient times, so dragons are indeed edible. However, Guo Pu, in his commentary on the *Classic of Mountains and Seas*, says, "A dragon looks like a snake with four feet." Wang Yi, in his commentary on the *Chu Ci*, says, "A dragon without horns is called a dragon." The *Northern Dream Trivial Talk* describes the shape of a dragon as like a snake, and also like a leech, all of these are unlike a duck. This thing looks like a duck, probably not a dragon.

In Xiangshan County, there is a place called Linpu, where the mountains are filled with venomous snakes. It is said that a judge from the True Man's Office in Jiangxi Province could capture them. The people pooled their money and hired a judge to come. Huang Jun, the magistrate of Xiangshan County, happened to be in the area at the time and accompanied him to observe the proceedings. The judge walked around the mountain ridge, drew his sword, and pointed or gestured in the air, reciting incantations. He then dipped a willow branch in water and sprinkled it all over. He descended to the flat ground below, drew a sword to create three large circles on the ground, each about three to four zhang in diameter. He stood behind the third circle with his sword, instructing Huang to stand behind him and warned, "If you see anything, do not be afraid." Soon, small snakes began to wind their way towards the first circle, where they died as soon as they arrived. More snakes followed, growing larger, and some died after emerging from the first or second circle. Suddenly, a fierce wind arose, uprooting the large trees on the mountain. A snake, as long as a dozen zhang and as thick as a five-dou rice sack, covered in golden scales and exhaling blue smoke, crossed two circles in succession. It raised its head and directly attacked the third circle. Huang Jun was greatly frightened and jumped away quickly, but the judge did not move or react. The snake crossed the third circle but did not advance further, and it died. The judge turned to Huang and said, "I warned you not to be afraid, why were you so cowardly?" This story was told by Shen Sheng Zuwei.

I have examined *Yijian Jia Zhi* and read of Fangcheng man Wang San's capture of a venomous python. He drew three trenches on the ground and told others, "If it were an ordinary snake, it would die after crossing one trench, at most two. If it could cross three trenches, then it would turn around and bite me instead." Soon after, the snake moved forward fearlessly, intending to approach Wang. Wang quickly tore his pants and split the snake in two. The snake died, split into two, and the matter is similar to this. Perhaps the snake could actually cross the third circle, and the judge must certainly have a method to control it. Mr. Shen Zuwěi also said: Someone once saw a snake, its shape was square, about seven or eight inches in length and width, like a thin plate. When asked the locals, they said, "This is called the flower-patterned snake, a rare species among snakes, and its venom is particularly potent." I have examined *Bencao Gangmu Shiyi* by Zhao Rushuan of Qiantang, which states that in the mountains of Hangzhou there is a snake named Fangshengban, because it has patterns all over its body like brocade, with a central victory pattern and a flat shape like a board, hence the name. Is this the same thing? Mr. Shen also said: Someone caught an object by the seaside, its shape was like a turtle but without a skirt. It was as large as a mat, with a shell on its back and no shell on its underside. In water, it had great strength, but on land, it could not move. No one could identify it. Some say this is a turtle shell, but examining *Guihai Yuheng Zhi*, it states that the turtle shell looks like a turtle or tortoise, with a back shell of thirteen pieces, black and white spots interwoven. Its edges have a skirt-like fringe, with teeth like a saw, no legs but four whiskers, and it moves through the water with its four whiskers. This thing has no skirt, and it is unknown whether it has legs or not, nor does it mention spots on its back. It is not necessarily a turtle shell.

Henan Prefecture has many shrines, all managed by women, who are called "Shi Po." However, they are mostly filled with empty and deceptive words, used to deceive women and servants. Only one of these "Shi Po's" worshipped a spirit that was quite famous for its miraculous powers. Those who sought help would come to her door, and their prayers would often be answered. There was no day without offerings of meat and wine, and the "Shi Po" gained great benefits. My eldest daughter-in-law, Fan, was living in her father's residence as the governor of Henan Prefecture. She heard from the maids and old women about these things, which were too numerous to recount. Once, a lady from an official family prayed to the spirit for her son's illness, and the illness was indeed cured. She planned to go in person to thank the spirit. The "Shi Po" firmly declined and said, "The spirit's dwelling is small and narrow, not worthy of your lady's presence. Please allow me to send a servant to perform the ritual." The lady refused and went in her finest attire. As soon as she bowed, a turtle slowly emerged from under the table. From then on, the spirit's miraculous powers ceased. My second daughter-in-law, Yao, said, "This woman must be a virtuous person, so the evil spirit feared her." I agreed. My eldest daughter-in-law also mentioned that in Henan Prefecture, there was a place known for producing large butterflies, as big as a plate. Once, someone sent two of these butterflies to a distant relative or friend. The family received them and kept them in their garden. One butterfly died soon after, while the other escaped and flew into the wild. A "Shi Po" captured it and claimed it was a divine butterfly, saying it had miraculous powers, and the worship became increasingly fervent. After a month or so, a child from the family that kept the butterfly happened to see it. He said, "The butterfly that escaped from my family, is it here?" From then on, prayers no longer had any effect. This is the story of a gentleman named Bao Shizhen, and such things have been known since ancient times.

During the time when Yun Cishan, the Zhongcheng, served as the governor of Changde, he had a friend named Guo Jun who was a student from Xiangtan. Guo Jun was well-versed in literary style and was also kind and generous, getting along very well with his host. After staying for several months, Guo Jun suddenly told Yun Cishan's nephew, "I am going to leave. The governor has been disrespectful to me, and I have also been disrespectful to the governor. How can I stay here for long?" The nephew strongly denied this, saying, "You are indeed a gentleman; do not be misled by rumors." The next day, Guo Jun again told the nephew, "Now it is truly impossible to stay! The governor is handling my case and is not being lenient with me; he is about to cut off my head. What can I do?" The nephew was startled by his words and reported this to Yun Cishan, who suspected that Guo Jun had a mental illness. Yun Cishan sent a doctor to treat him, and the doctor said, "All six pulses are disordered; this is beyond treatment. Hurry to send him back." Yun Cishan then personally went to visit Guo Jun's illness, first peering through the window and seeing that Guo Jun was in the room, acting as if he were fighting with someone, and then as if he were bowing and yielding, his behavior seemed strange. Yun Cishan then entered and asked, "Is there any improvement in your illness, Master?" Guo Jun replied, "I am fine." "Have you decided to return home?" "No, no," he answered, his responses still within normal bounds. Yun Cishan felt puzzled and unable to understand, so he temporarily provided a boat for Guo Jun. There was a man named Zhou, whom Yun Cishan had hired as his son's tutor, and he lived near Guo Jun. One midnight, Guo Jun suddenly entered Zhou's room, kneeling at the foot of his bed.

Zhou awoke and asked the reason, Guo then wept and said, "It is over, the magistrate upholds the law, he will surely chop off my head, this is a matter of past life, death is not unjust. However, I have an old mother, you hold the position of a guest teacher, can you speak to the magistrate for me and seek a way to stay alive?" Zhou tried to persuade him but failed, he then deceived him and said, "At dawn, I will speak to the magistrate, you may go." He pushed him out and closed the door, Guo knelt outside the door all night. By the next day, the boat was ready, but it was raining heavily, they could not set out. Guo sat upright on the pillow, unmoving, people came to see him and said, "I will die today." They tried to feed him, but he did not eat; they forced him to eat, and he finished two large bowls, the evening meal was the same. After eating, he sat up on the pillow again and asked why he was sitting there, he said, "Waiting to die." That night, the vice-premier sent four strong servants to guard him. The four servants sat in the side rooms. At the third watch of the night, they suddenly heard a noise from Guo's room, they went to see and found Guo had disappeared. There was a door in the courtyard, after going out, they entered a large garden. The people in the garden saw someone vaguely ahead, all said it was him. They followed him and he entered the toilet, they suspected he was using the toilet. They entered the toilet and saw nothing, they then realized they had seen a ghost. They searched the garden thoroughly but found no trace. Someone on night patrol said, "In the northeast corner of the garden, there is an earthen wall, under which there is a well, perhaps he is there?" They searched as suggested and indeed found his two shoes by the well, and another pair of pants on the well's edge.

A body was retrieved from the well, and it was found to be covered in bruises and discoloration, as if it had been trampled on by feet and squeezed by hands. The state was very pitiful. Upon examining the area around the bed and seating, the prescription from the previous day's doctor was still there, and it included the herb *Nuzhenzi* (女贞子). Gu wrote next to it, "Women are inherently chaste, but she made her not chaste. Is her crime not worthy of punishment?" One of his close acquaintances saw this and sighed, "This must be the sin of her past life." He then mentioned that Gu had fallen ill and was on the verge of death before he was twenty years old. His self-narration during the illness was very strange, and he even composed a poem. It is likely that the ancestors of the Gu family still had some lingering virtue, which delayed his death by several years, waiting until he had a son. Gu's son was still a child and had not yet come of age. Moreover, the official responsible for selecting scholars praised his literary talent and intended to appoint him as a *bagong* (拔贡) in the year Xianfeng Xinyou (咸丰辛酉). However, due to karmic entanglements, he died an untimely death, which is also very tragic. Observing the few words he wrote on the prescription, it is clear that he must have stumbled in the land of pleasure in his past life. Shen Zhongfu, the censor, said: When his posthumous title, the *zenggong* (赠公), was traveling in the seven regions of Fujian, his stepmother, Lady Ma, passed away. At that time, the house where he lived was very narrow and cramped, consisting of only three rooms. The eastern room was a bedroom, with six silk window screens inside, divided into two sections. A place was set up for Lady Ma in front of the screens, facing west, and covered with a cloth curtain. The *zenggong* wrote a couplet by hand and hung it on the curtain. Every time the curtain was opened, the couplet would touch the window frame, making a *gu gu* sound. The ground was damp and low-lying, and a mat was placed on it. Walking on it, it felt soft and spongy. To the south of the window, there was a table and a stand. (In the *Wen Yun* (Classical Rhymes), the 48th section has the character '橙' (orange), pronounced 'dū dèng'.)

The stool, now commonly known as a "凳" (stool), is made of vine. When one sits or stands on it, it makes a creaking sound, which is quite normal. At the time, Zhongfu was around eleven or twelve years old, and he shared a bed with Zengong, lying in the room behind them. His mother, the Lady, loved Zhongfu more than her own biological children, so Zhongfu had a very strong longing for her. One night, as he slept until midnight, he heard the sound of a curtain or screen touching the window frame, as if it were being opened. He also heard the sound of someone walking on a mat and sitting on the stool. Zhongfu cried out, "Could this be the spirit of my mother? Why don't you come and touch and look at me!" Before he could finish speaking, he heard another sound from the stool, as if someone were sitting and then standing up. Soon after, the window opened, and then the curtain opened, revealing that Lady indeed stood before the bed. She spoke to Zhongfu, saying, "You miss me, and I miss you too. But life is limited by fate, and there is nothing we can do about it! You should love reading, and one day you will surely achieve fame. You can hope for the top ranks in the imperial examinations, and your official position will not be insignificant. Strive! Strive!" Zhongfu cried out loud, and then he saw nothing at all.

The person being addressed was startled awake and asked for the reason behind the apparition. He had never been particularly concerned with ghostly matters and said, "Your deep longing for her has manifested in this form; it is not truly the spirit of your mother." Later, Zhong Fu took the final imperial examination in the year 1856. The chief examiner initially presented the top ten scrolls, and because Zhong Fu's responses to every question cited imperial edicts as evidence, he was praised for his attention to current affairs. He was initially ranked second and then third. However, my teacher, the Viceroy Huang of Xian County, changed his ranking to seventh, making him the fourth in the second class. The prediction that he could aspire to the top rank proved accurate. Soon after, he began his career as a Hanlin scholar and served as the governor of Sichuan. He resigned due to illness and returned home. His future official position is hard to predict, but all the predictions made by the grandmother were accurate. Zhong Fu had a younger brother named Tao, with the courtesy name Shuhe, who served as the magistrate of Shunyi County and the governor of Bazi Prefecture. He observed mourning for his biological mother and resigned from office. After completing the mourning period, he awaited a vacancy and lived in the capital for a long time. He enjoyed writing poetry and also enjoyed drinking. His friends who enjoyed drinking and poetry would always fill his room. Several days before his death, he was still drinking and writing poetry with guests, and he was very happy. The next day, he suddenly told his guests, "I will be saying goodbye to all of you forever." The guests were surprised and asked why. He said, "I dreamed last night that the city god of Shunyi County told me, 'I will be taking over, and the one taking over is you.' I did not want to, but the god said, 'You should have been a god for a long time, but because you have an elderly mother, it was postponed a bit. When your mother passes away, how could you not go?' I said, 'I still do not have children.'"

The god said, “Whether one has a son or not is a minor matter, what’s there to discuss!” Since he said it like that, I knew it could not be avoided. The guest interpreted it as an omen dream, but from then on, he gradually became weaker, and on the day of his death, he took a bath, shaved his head, changed into new clothes, and lay down to sleep. He asked, “Have you all gathered yet?” Soon after, he said, “They have all gathered, I am going now.” After speaking, he closed his eyes and passed away. Several years later, there was a great famine in Zhili, and the court issued gold to relieve the situation. A county magistrate in the jurisdiction of Shuntian Prefecture embezzled the funds for himself, and when the incident came to light, he died by taking poison out of fear of punishment. At that time, there was a certain gentleman who was also waiting for a vacancy as a county magistrate. As a living person serving as an underworld official, he told people, “Yesterday, I met with the gods to interrogate a certain county magistrate, and all the city gods of the six prefectures and twenty-one counties under Shuntian Prefecture were present. I recognized one of them, the city god of Shunyi County, Shen Shuhe.” This gentleman had never known Shen Shuhe’s words at the time of his death, but his words matched them, which is truly remarkable.

In the fourth year of the Tongzhi reign (1865), a heretical sect incited rebellion in Shiqian Prefecture of Guizhou Province, breaking into the city. The governor, Yan Jun, died in street fighting. Yan Jun, a man from Tongxiang, Zhejiang, had a luminous halo about a foot in diameter above his head for three days before he met his disaster. After Yan Jun's death, the rebels entered the government office. Yan Jun had a sister in her twenties who told her mother, "When the rebels come, they will surely defile us. There is a large pond behind the government office, and I will jump into it with Mother to die." Her mother agreed, and they went together, with other women following. However, the mud wall had no gate, and they could not push through it. The sister then untied her belt, carried her mother, and leaped with all her might, managing to jump over the wall. She threw the belt inside the wall, allowing the other women to climb up using it, leading each one out. Finally, she lifted her skirt and entered the pond.

The pool water was very shallow; ΝΜ reached the center of the water, still unable to reach the top of the head, suffering greatly and unable to die, when the bandits arrived in large numbers. From afar, they called out, "Are you all part of the strict governor's household? The governor is a good official, and we also did not intend to kill the governor. The governor has unfortunately died, but you are fortunate to be alive. Why don't you follow me out?" Her sister shouted at him from the water. The bandits became angry and hit him with a spear, but it missed because of the distance, and they could do nothing, so they left on their own. The bandits' goal was to loot gold and silk, having satisfied their desires, they scattered back to their lairs. The remaining people gathered together, rescued them from the water, and protected them out of the city, thus they were saved from danger. Her sister later married Shen Zhongfu, the Censor-General, as his second wife; she could write poetry and was skilled in calligraphy. Shen Zhongfu asked, "You, as a weak woman, carried your mother over the wall and led the other women down the wall. What kind of divine courage is this?" She could not explain it herself. Was it divine assistance, or perhaps the spirit of the strict governor secretly helping her?

A Man from Luoyang County, Henan, and His Concubine A certain man from Luoyang County, Henan, had a concubine who was formerly a nun. After she joined his household, she was unfamiliar with household chores and lived a life of luxury, wearing fine clothes and eating delicious food, playing all day. The principal wife did not approve and often reprimanded her, leading to their inability to get along. The sound of quarrels could be heard outside every day, and living in the same room, they were like enemies. During the early years of the Xianfeng era, the Cantonese rebels attacked Henan Province, failing to capture it and plundering the countryside. The man fled in panic and could not look after his family. His family members could still walk, but as the rebels approached, they all fled. Only the wife, with her delicate feet, could not walk and thought she would surely die at the rebels' hands. The concubine bravely said, "I will carry you away." She carried her for three days and nights, her knees bruised, falling and rising many times but never letting go. The wife comforted her and wept, saying, "I did not know my sister loved me so much!" When the rebels retreated, they returned together, and they became as close as sisters. A neighbor woman asked the concubine, "You and the wife could not get along, why did you exert such effort to save her?" The concubine replied, "In the past, we quarreled; it was just personal grudges. In times of hardship, life and death are at stake. How could a concubine sit idly by and watch her wife die without helping?" Those who heard this thought her even more virtuous.

Li, a man from Huaining County in Anhui Province, lived in the capital city, serving as an official, and was very poor. After a long time, he was finally appointed as a magistrate in Gansu Province, serving alone in his position. Several years later, he had accumulated a considerable amount of wealth. In his area, there was a broker who dealt in the trade of horses and other goods, and he became acquainted with Li. The broker asked Li why he had been living alone for so long, and Li told him that he had not yet married. The broker then gave his daughter to Li in marriage. After they were married, the wife asked Li if there were any other family members in his household. Li replied, "None." However, Li actually had a mother and a wife, and he had been away from his hometown in the capital for over ten years, without any news from them. At this point, a letter from home arrived, but Li had gone out, so his wife opened and read it. When Li returned, his wife showed him the letter and rebuked him, saying, "You married me while you already had a wife, which is no harm. But you have a mother and a wife, and you abandoned them without a care. How can you still call yourself a man? Quickly write a letter home, take everything you have accumulated, and I will carry it for you."

During the early Guangxu era, there was a man named Li Yu. His wife urged him, and he then wrote a letter, putting two thousand taels of silver in a leather bag. The wife carried the gold and rode a horse directly to Huaining, visited his family and found him, then entered and paid respects to Li's mother and wife, delivering the letter and the gold. They stayed for several days, and Li's wife had a minor disagreement with her. The wife bid farewell to Li's mother and wanted to leave, but Li's mother strongly tried to keep her. At that time, the wife was pregnant and was nearing her due date, so she stayed for a short while, soon gave birth to a boy, and returned on horseback three days later. This woman is virtuous and wise, and her comings and goings are swift and mysterious like a dragon, truly a remarkable woman! Tong Misha told me this, and said that Li still holds the position of patrol inspector, and the wife is still alive. However, I checked the official register published in the capital, which lists Wang Wenlin, an Anhui Huaining native, as the patrol inspector of Jiayuguan in Gansu, not a Li. Is there a discrepancy in the reports?

In the countryside of Guian, Huzhou, there was a scholar named Yu Yunshen. During the year of Ren Shen in the Tongzhi era, he came to the prefectural city to take the imperial examinations and stayed at the Guan Di Temple near Gan Tang Bridge. Behind the main hall of the temple, there were three rooms that were deep and secluded, even during the day it felt like night. Yu slept in one of these rooms. One evening, as he lay down to sleep, he suddenly saw an old man and a young man, both holding a dish and presenting their necks with blood. They looked at Yu and said, "Do you see our suffering? Our blood still flows even now." The next day, Yu told others about this, and they said, "This must be the ghost of a soldier who died in the chaos of war." They advised Yu to move away, and he did so. In Qingpu, there was another scholar named Lu Lancha, who suffered from a mental illness. One day, while returning from the market, a ghost possessed him and spoke through him, saying, "I was in the capital, where your father killed me. Now I demand that you give your life to repay me. I have traveled far and am very hungry; prepare food quickly, and burn many ghost money." Lu's elder brother, Qing Shi, a scholar, angrily said, "How could my father have done such a thing?" He slapped his younger brother three times. The ghost replied, "Are you going to hit me because you are a scholar?" It wept softly. Realizing the ghost's fear, Qing Shi shouted, "What kind of wild ghost are you, causing such chaos here?" The ghost then admitted that it had once been a servant in the capital, having seen Lu's father, and was using these words to get food. Qing Shi slapped the ghost three more times, and it stopped speaking. Lu Lancha's mental illness also quickly improved.

My Hometown's New Market Town had a man surnamed Chen who opened a shop to sell silk and cloth, named Yuantai. One year, a false fire broke out (as described in the *Shanhaijing: Xishan Jing*, referring to an unusual or supernatural fire). Here it was extinguished, but there it flared up, destroying countless items. They watched over day and night, but the fire still did not stop. Soon, something attached itself to the maidservants and spoke, saying: "There is an empty space on the west side of the house, where three rooms can be built." Chen, afraid of being disturbed, immediately gathered workers to build a tower. After the tower was built, they carefully worshiped it, and the fire then stopped. However, from then on, both buying and selling were unsuccessful, and finally, the shop was closed. Family members also died one after another, and the house was sold to another family. My student, Mi Sheng, who is a scholar, said: His friend Chen Shaofang watched a performance at the Dongyue Temple in Huzhou and saw a woman with several hairs growing on her chin. Another historical figure, Tug Jinqing, the same official in Haimen, once heard a private lawsuit between a man and a woman about their daughter having a beard. I mentioned that Li Guangbi's mother had a beard, and both the old and new *Tang Shu* record this. Ming dynasty scholar Xu Yingqiu's *Yuzhitang Tanhui* records cases from the Xuanhe period of the Song dynasty, such as Zhu Jie's wife and a tavern keeper's wife Zhu, during the reign of Emperor Shun of Yuan, a beggar woman in Bianliang, and during the Hongzhi period of the Ming dynasty, a woman from Yingzhou and a woman from Yunyang, all of whom were women with beards. Such occurrences have existed in ancient times, and they are not unusual.

Mi said again: Xu Zhuangmin Gong's younger brother (the younger brother of Xu Zhuangmin, whom I once met in the capital, I do not know if he is this person) had a dream in which he reached a mountain, and there was a monastery on the mountain. Entering it, he saw the person seated in the middle as Mr. Yao Jingtang, with Xu Zhuangmin on his left and an empty seat on his right. Xu Zhuangmin told his younger brother, "You go down the mountain; I will stay here." Soon after, Xu Zhuangmin died in office as the governor of Jiangsu Province during the conflict with the rebels (粵賊, referring to the Taiping Rebellion). Mr. Yao Jingtang, named Xue Shuang, was from Shuanglin Town, Guian County. He served in the Ministry of War for several decades, living alone in the capital without bringing his family. He was known for his profound and elegant writings and high moral integrity. The people of his hometown all said that he was a reincarnation of a monk named Zigu (紫皋). Xu Zhuangmin sat next to him, suggesting a spiritual connection between them. Mr. He Zizhen, a respected scholar and calligrapher, had a dream when he was young in which he reached a place and saw many steamed buns on a table. He took one to eat and then took another, but suddenly someone forcefully took it away, preventing him from eating it. He did not recognize the person. In the year 1810 (Jiaqing's Gengchen year), Mr. Chen Lian Shi from Guilin became the top scholar in the imperial examination and thus became the top scholar in the country. Mr. He recognized him as the person from his dream and sighed, "Can I compete with this person?" In the year 1835 (Daoguang's Yiwu year), Mr. He became the top scholar in the provincial examination, but the following year, when he became an imperial scholar, he did not win the top position in the imperial examination. The dream was indeed a sign.

Zizhen the elder, during the year of Jihai in the Daoguang era, presided over the imperial examination in Fujian. On his return journey, while staying at an inn, he dreamed that his younger brother Ziyi came to say goodbye. He tried to keep him but could not. Looking at his attire, Ziyi was already wearing a monk's robe. Upon waking, Zizhen cried and said, "My younger brother must be in misfortune!" From then on, he cried morning and evening. When he returned to the capital and reported back, he rushed to the private residence of his father, Wen'an Gong. At that time, Ziyi had indeed already passed away. The family members, because he was far away returning, did not immediately inform him. But Zizhen had already cried out loud, and thus could no longer keep it secret. When asked how he knew, he said he had dreamed it. In Haining's Xieshi Town, there was a merchant named Xu, who rose to prominence through trade. He was adept at seizing opportunities and making profits, being meticulous even in small matters, and would not spare even relatives. He had one son who loved gambling and privately took money from his father to cover his gambling debts, amounting to tens of thousands of cash each day. Xu was troubled by this but could not stop him. There was an organization called Guangshantang in the area, which, every November, would collect and bury unclaimed bodies from the wild, as well as those whose children or grandchildren were too poor to bury them. However, the funds were insufficient to cover all cases. One day, Xu passed by and suddenly felt moved. He quickly donated three hundred acres of his best land to support this charitable cause. When asked why, he said, "My son is unworthy; in a few years, all my land will be gone. Is it better to provide him with money for his gambling or to use it for burying the dead?" Soon after, his son's gambling became even more extravagant. Xu then stored hundreds of thousands of cash in one room and told his son, "If you lose gambling, you should repay it. You can take as much money as you need from this room."

However, how can gamblers be allowed to enter my home? Let them stay outside, and if they want to give money to guests, that is acceptable. Then, gamblers gathered daily at the door, and the son carried money on a rope, walking out through the hall, doing this dozens of times a day, becoming very tired. When he looked at the money in the room, it was initially abundant, but soon nearly depleted, unable not to feel regret. He sighed, "It is not easy to take it out, but is it not more difficult to bring it back in?" Then he wept before his father, vowing never to gamble again, being frugal and hardworking beyond his father, and the family became even more prosperous. Everyone said that Mr. Xu was good at educating his son, but I say it is all due to fate. A father who is meticulous about every small amount will have a son who gambles recklessly. It is not that the father did not teach his son well, nor is it that the son did not follow the teaching. A father who practices virtue will have a son who becomes prosperous through diligence and frugality. Observing the father and son of Mr. Xu, the reasons for the rise and fall of families are no different from this.

The eldest son of the esteemed Wearing the Lotus, whose career was likened to the flight of a phoenix, served as the county magistrate of Guangdong. He did not live long in this position before passing away. His wife was pregnant, but they were in dire financial straits, unable to remain in their current residence for long. At that time, Wearing the Lotus was still in office in the central provinces, so they had to carry the coffin northward to rely on her father-in-law. They traveled as far as Hunan, where they rested at an inn, and during their stay, the wife gave birth to a son. Since they had no other children, they were very happy to have this new addition. However, the mother lacked milk, and the baby cried day and night, causing the wife to weep while holding the child. A woman from a neighboring room came to visit and asked, "Are you suffering from a lack of milk? Why don't you hire a wet nurse?" The wife replied, "We are strangers in a foreign land; how could we possibly find one? Moreover, we lack provisions and funds, and we are already fearful that we might not even reach our destination. How can we even consider such a thing?" She then wept, saying, "I am the bereaved, and this is my only piece of flesh. If the child dies, I shall die too." The woman heard this and felt deeply moved. After some time, she said, "Our family is fortunate to be well-fed and warm, but we are not going to be a wet nurse. However, hearing your words, my heart is troubled. I have just given birth to a son a few months ago, and I am willing to feed your child with my own milk, although I must first return home and inform my husband." Having said this, she quickly went back to tell her husband, who was angry and said, "Our family is fortunate to be well-fed and warm, how could we possibly be a wet nurse?" The woman replied, "Indeed."

A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms. Indeed, if this child dies, his mother will also surely die; the fate of both mother and child is at stake, how can one stand by and do nothing? I have already spoken, so you must not obstruct me." Thus, she entrusted her child to another to be nursed, and she herself accompanied the wife of Dài. Dài's wife asked, "How much money is needed per month? When we reach the central region, I will request it from the family, and we will surely honor the agreement." The woman angrily replied, "Am I willing to act as a wet nurse for someone else? It is out of compassion for you. Even if I return from Bian to Chu, I will cover the travel expenses myself, and I do not need your money. Go now, and do not say more!" So they set off from Hunan, passing through Hubei, and arrived in Bian. The couple, Liánxī, were both moved to tears and said, "Without this woman, how could we have had this grandson!" They offered her generous compensation, but she refused it. Liánxī then had his wife dress formally to thank her and prepared a grand feast to entertain her. After several days, as she was about to leave, he spoke to her, "The expenses for returning to Chu are already prepared. Your noble character surpasses the ordinary, but you did not consider the situation of our couple! We have provided a modest carriage and escort, hoping you will not refuse!" They then provided the means for her to return home. I consider this woman's actions to be similar to those of ancient wandering knights, who prioritize others' needs over their own, and she can be called a remarkable woman. My eldest son's wife, who was in Henan, heard about this from people of the Dài family, and the story seems true, but it is regrettable that the woman's name and hometown cannot be recorded.

In the south of Dali Prefecture, ten miles south of the city, there stands the Guanyin Pavilion atop a huge rock. At the base of the rock, an old woman is carved in the posture of carrying a stone, as recorded in the *Dali Prefecture Gazetteer* as the story of the woman carrying the stone. It is said that once an enemy army was about to invade Dali and, as they approached the border, they saw an old woman carrying this stone and walking. The enemy soldiers were startled. The woman said, "The people of this land are strong and powerful. I am a woman, old and sick, with weak strength, unable to carry heavy loads, so do not laugh at me." The enemy dared not advance and fled away. The woman then placed the stone on the ground, and no one knew where she went. Later, people recognized her as an incarnation of Guanyin, and thus built a pavilion on the rock in her honor. The exact time of this event is unknown, as the story seems too absurd to be taken seriously. However, the Guanyin enshrined in the pavilion is quite famous for its miraculous powers. In the third year of the Xianfeng reign period (1856), the bandit Du Wenshou occupied Dali for more than ten years, during which all monasteries and temples were burned down, but this pavilion alone remained.

In the winter of the Ren Shen year of Tongzhi, Governor Cen of Yunnan supervised the large army in besieging the city, with Observer Ma Xingwu and his assistant Si Liang participating. Ten days before the city was captured, they saw a whirlwind spiraling upward from within the city, followed by swirling dust like clouds and mist. It rose to the halfway point of the sky, then descended, splitting into two, circling the eastern and western parts of the city and moving southward, before recombining into one and dispersing at the Guanyin Pavilion, astonishing all who witnessed it. Cen's main camp was located in front of the pavilion. At the time, the rebels were desperate and plotted to feign surrender, waiting for an opportunity to escape. Cen had a dream where a child handed him a book, saying, "Conveying the command of the Bodhisattva, I bring this to you." Upon opening it, he found twenty-eight pages, each depicting a person with a strange appearance, and he was startled awake. The next day, the rebels came to the main camp to surrender, and when asked their number, there were twenty-eight. Cen realized, summoned them in, and had armed soldiers ambush and kill them, thus pacifying Dali. He contributed funds to repair the Guanyin Pavilion and recorded the event on stone. I heard this from Observer Ma.

In the southern Yaozhou, there is a mountain called Longhua Mountain, on which stands a temple known as the Living Buddha Temple. It was established since the early Tang period, and the temple monks number in the hundreds. Suddenly, a wandering monk arrived from Jizhushan in Dali Prefecture. He wore tattered clothes and shoes, his face was dry and dark, and his speech was erratic, as if he were suffering from a mental disorder. The master temporarily kept him to do cooking. After several years, the monk suddenly requested to return home on New Year's Eve and asked for a porter to carry his belongings. The master said, "People are busy celebrating the New Year; who would accompany you on such a long journey? Jizhushan is hundreds of miles away, and it cannot be reached in one night. Let us wait until tomorrow." However, the monk continued to plead persistently. The master playfully pointed to the statue of the Four Heavenly Kings in the Maitreya Hall and said, "The Four Heavenly Kings are all idle and have nothing to do. Why don't they let you go?" The monk replied, "Yes, yes."

On the morning of New Year's Day, one of the statues of the Four Heavenly Kings had mysteriously disappeared, and the monk who was in charge of them did not know where it had gone. Everyone was surprised and alarmed. The next day, they heard from people who were spreading rumors that there was a statue of a Heavenly King on a hill called Puguan Mountain, ten miles away from the temple. They went to see it, and indeed, it was the missing statue. They then built a temple on the spot, and it still exists to this day. This is also what Governor Ma Xingwu said. Governor Ma Xingwu was from Yunnan, so what he said should not be false. However, I have read in *Yijian Zhi* that in the Miaoguo Temple in Raoyang Prefecture, there was a monk named Feng. One day, he asked the elder to find a porter to accompany him on his travels. The elder said, "There are two guardians at the gate; take one with you." The monk agreed, placed the porter on the shoulder of one of the guardians, and the guardian followed the monk. The monks shouted and chased after them, but the monk Feng took the porter, carried him himself, and flew away on a cloud. The guardian remained motionless in the field, and people built a temple over it, calling it the Temple of the Guardian. This story is very similar to the one about the missing statue. Could it be that events from ancient and modern times are indeed the same? Or could it be that Buddhist monks are fabricating stories to glorify themselves?

Chapter Fifteen: Tan Lisheng, whose given name is Xin Zhen, was from Hengshan, Hunan. In the year Gengchen of the Guangxu era (1880), he passed the imperial examination as the third-place laureate. In the year Xinsi (1881), he traveled to Hangzhou and stayed at the Hunan guildhall. In the first ten days of April, after returning from a trip to Taoguang, he fell ill. After several days, he recovered slightly and was about to leave. Someone asked him to write large characters, but before he finished, he suddenly felt pain in his chest and vomited several liters of blood. Like this for five or six days, he vomited an object six or seven inches long, the nature of which was unknown. He then became increasingly weak. His contemporary, Xu Hua'ong, a scholar, visited him every day. On the twenty-third day, he took Xu Hua'ong's hand, wept bitterly, and requested a couplet in advance. He also asked Xu to write an inscription for a portrait and gave him a copy of his nine-year-old son's imitation of the *Xuanmisi Stele*, with a hint of entrusting his son to Xu Hua'ong's care. The next day, Xu visited again, and谭丽生was on the verge of death. Before his death, he requested wine, which his nephew served him in three cups, all of which he drank.

The flower farmer presented a cup and wept, saying, "I shall treat your son as if he were my own." He then rested and passed away. At the moment of death, his mind remained calm, suggesting that he might have had a deep spiritual foundation. Could it be that in a past life he was a monk at Lingyin Temple, and thus his visit to Lingyin was a return to his true spiritual home? When Li came to Hangzhou, I was just leaving the Yu Pavilion on the shores of the West Lake, heading back to Chunzai Hall in Wu. I had already boarded my boat when Li heard about it and immediately ordered a sedan chair to be brought out through the Wulin Gate, arriving at my boat to greet me formally. I had not yet had the chance to return the courtesy when we agreed to meet again in Wu, but I did not expect his decline to be so swift! It is said that he was a man of many friends, skilled in calligraphy and writing, and spent his entire life devoted to these pursuits. Like a tree that falls on its own, or a lamp that burns itself out, the proverb "It is the passerby who kills the horse" is truly a cause for sighing. The flower farmer wrote to me, detailing the situation, and I was deeply moved. I was just writing the 15th volume of my notebook, and so I recorded this event.

Hubei Xian Ning Yu family had a maid who was cherished by the head mistress. There was someone who wanted to make her a concubine, but the request was denied. In the countryside, there was an old man named Yu, who had modest land and property, barely providing for food and clothing, and was over forty-five years old. He considered buying a concubine, but found the price too high, so he thought it would be more economical to marry a wife. Hearing that the Yu family's maid was very virtuous and that her owner did not demand payment, he pretended to be someone marrying a wife and asked the Yu family for permission. The Yu family wanted to see the person, and the old man, thinking himself too old and fearing that the maid might not approve, sent his nephew instead, and they agreed. After the marriage was completed, the maid did not see the new groom, but instead saw an old man in the room, who sat there for a long time without leaving, which she found quite annoying. Sitting until the third watch of the night, the maid could no longer bear it and said to the old man, "It is very late now, why do you sit here so long?" The old man could not answer and still did not leave. After a long while, the maid urged him again, and the old man left, instructing his legitimate wife to tell the maid the truth. The wife entered and pretended to be the maid's older sister, saying to her, "Why don't you know, my younger sister? This old man is your husband." The maid said, "No, no, the husband came to the master's house before, and I have seen him."

The primary wife said, "This is the son of my husband's elder brother. My husband sent him to your family to serve as your husband. Is your master deceiving you with this?" The maid was confused and did not know why. Thinking that things have come to this, being far from the master's family, what can be done? So she followed. After several days, the maid saw the woman referred to as her sister-in-law, who was often in the room and very intimate with her husband. She wondered, saying, "Is it like this between a sister-in-law and her brother-in-law?" Later, she heard people calling her husband "Dragon Lord" and her sister-in-law "Dragon Lady." So she asked her husband, "Why do you call yourself Dragon Lord?" The husband replied, "Dragon is just my nickname." "Then why do you call your sister-in-law Dragon Lady?" The husband could not answer. The maid pressed him for an answer, and the husband, unable to avoid it, told the truth. The primary wife then spoke kindly to her, saying, "I was indeed deceiving you, but from now on, the two of you will be husband and wife, and I will be your sister-in-law." The maid said, "No. Should I cause you to lose your marital relationship for my sake? Dragon Lord is my master, and Dragon Lady is my master's wife." She treated her husband with great respect and her sister-in-law with great respect. Later, the master of the house, Yu, heard about it. He was angry that he had been deceived and wanted to send the maid back and end the marriage. The maid refused, so he stopped. The maid soon gave birth to a son. The master and his wife were very happy and held a ceremony to wash the baby on the third day. Guests came to congratulate them, and they named him "Happiness Found." However, the family's population increased, but the years had been poor harvests. The master was old and sick, needing medicine, so they became very poor.

During the farming season, unable to hire workers, the maid entrusted her child to the eldest son and took up the hoe herself, laboring in the fields under the scorching sun and freezing rain, with little rest. Soon after, her husband passed away, and she managed the funeral arrangements with great care and attention to detail. Shortly thereafter, her wife also died. The maid said, "The master and the mistress are one in the same; how could there be any difference?" She handled the second funeral in the same manner. Consecutive losses left her with only a small plot of land, half of which she had to sell, and food and clothing were almost insufficient. Relying on the growth of her son, she helped him work, and after several years, they gradually had some surplus. She then prepared to keep a young girl in the house to marry off her son. When the son turned seventeen or eighteen, they were about to hold the wedding ceremony, with a feast prepared for friends and relatives. Before the auspicious date arrived, the news of bandits came first. At that time, the Cantonese bandits had already captured Wuchang and were spreading out with their troops, and Xian Ning had also fallen, causing unrest in the countryside. The son was eventually taken by the bandits. The girl kept in the house returned to her mother's family. The mother's family requested to break off the engagement, but the maid said, "Let us set a three-year period. If my son does not return, then I will marry her off." Soon, everyone was talking, saying that the son was dead. The maid cried all day and became ill, unable to work in the fields, and had nothing to eat. Coincidentally, the old master's mother, Lady Lv, was traveling to Henan with her son-in-law, Mr. Fan. She took the maid to Bianliang, and the maid then lived with the Fan family.

He was extremely gentle and amiable, loved by all his peers, yet his longing for his son was so intense that after a long time, it seemed as if he had a mental illness. Sometimes he would lie alone, sometimes sit with others, often staring with wide-open eyes, saying, "贺得 has come!" Even if he was lying down, he would leap up immediately. If he could not see his son after getting up, he would cry out loud, then become dizzy and collapse. After a while, he would regain consciousness. Later, there was someone who came from Xianning, saying that the girl he had previously raised had been married by her mother's family, and he would weep bitterly, his illness becoming severe. At the moment of his death, he shouted, "贺得 truly came today!" and then died. Thus, everyone believed that 贺得 had actually died, so when his mother saw him at the end, she thought it must be his ghost. However, 贺得 was not actually dead; the servant had died several years ago, and 贺得 escaped from the bandits and returned home. 樊氏 then gave him money, sending him to bury his mother's remains. Later, 贺得 married a woman from Nanjing, who was also kidnapped by bandits and taken to Hubei. Both husband and wife worked hard, the family regained comfort and sustenance, and they had several children. I heard this from my eldest son's wife, 樊, who was the mistress of the servant, 吕宜人, and also my son-in-law's maternal grandmother. I wrote the biography of 吕太宜人, so I know it very well. This servant was virtuous and accomplished, and an old chaste widow, yet her life was so full of misfortunes. However, 贺得's children and grandchildren benefited from her legacy and did not fall into poverty, which is certainly true.

In a rural household in Xianning, Hubei, during a time of military conflict, a family lost their son. Someone advised the mother: "Take your son's shoes and place them under the bed, with the laces tied inward. Call out your son's name every night, and he will surely return." And indeed, this method proved effective. Another woman named Yao, who was a second wife, recounted that before the war, her husband's family lived in a village called Zhoujiawan. A neighbor's son was abducted by bandits, and someone taught the mother to beg for lamp oil and wick from forty-nine different families. At night, after everyone had gone to sleep, she would call out her son's name forty-nine times, doing so every night with a voice that was very sorrowful. After several months, the son did indeed escape from the bandits and returned home, saying that he heard his mother calling his name every night. However, by then, he was already in the jurisdiction of Dantu County in Jiangsu, a distance of several hundred miles away. It seems that the bond between mother and son is so strong that they can communicate across great distances, much like a mountain collapsing and a bell ringing, which is not a mere coincidence.

Mr. Fan of Xianning, a member of my father-in-law's family, the Governor Yu Nong, served as an official in Guangdong and died in office. His son, being unworthy, left his father's coffin in a temple and took all his wealth with him, disappearing without a trace. Several years later, a cousin of Mr. Fan came to Guangdong on business. Mr. Fan appeared in a dream and said, "My son was unworthy and abandoned me without regard. My coffin is at a certain place and cannot be brought back for burial. Now that you have arrived, I hope to return with you." The next day, his cousin visited and indeed found the coffin. However, the cost of the journey was also quite substantial, and he still hesitated. He dreamed again that Mr. Fan urged him, and his cousin said, "Traveling such a long distance is not easy. If you open my coffin and put my bones in another box, can we bring them back to the old grave?" Mr. Fan replied, "Yes." Before leaving, he saw another dream in which Mr. Fan said, "When passing through checkpoints, bridges, and high mountains and great rivers, you must call my name, so that I will not be delayed." His cousin followed all his instructions. As they approached home, Mr. Fan appeared in a dream to his family the night before and said, "I am returning with my cousin!" From this, we can understand that just as a fox dies facing its den and a leaf falls to its root, the burial at Yanling and Yingbo, even the wise and far-sighted cannot hold such a heart, but filial sons and kind grandsons cannot bear such a heart.

A Woman from the Yu Family in the Reign of Emperor Wu During the reign of Emperor Wu of Jin, a woman from the Yu family died shortly after her marriage. Her mother, a widow, was old and sick. When the southern rebels were approaching, the entire family panicked and planned to move elsewhere. One night, the mother sat alone in a room and suddenly saw her daughter appear. The daughter said to her mother, "The land is filled with smoke and fire; where will we go? Mother is also very sick and cannot withstand traveling. It would be better to die happily rather than wander on the road." The mother replied, "Indeed. I ask myself what life has to offer. If I can follow you, it is truly my wish." People from neighboring houses all heard the mother speaking alone, but did not hear the daughter's words. They only heard ghostly sounds. The next day, they asked the mother about last night, and she told them, but they still did not believe it. A few days later, the mother eventually died.

My great-grandmother, Lady Dai, had a nephew named Junbo, a man of talent, who died young. His wife, Zhang, gave birth to a daughter named Weisheng, who married into the Cai family and died. The Dai family originally came from Deqing but had been living in Hu Prefecture for many years. In the year 1860, the Cantonese rebels invaded the provincial capital, and the Dai family planned to move elsewhere. Zhang hesitated. At night, she suddenly heard Weisheng urgently shouting, "Hurry up, hurry up!" This happened several nights in a row. Zhang then decided to act. This is different from the intention of the Yu family girl, probably because their mothers were in different states of health. However, after that, Zhao Zhongjie Gong firmly defended Hu Prefecture, and the residents gradually returned. The Zhang family returned to their old house in the city. When Hu Prefecture fell, she ultimately died in the disaster. It is regrettable that she did not ultimately follow her daughter's advice. A daughter of the Fan family from Xianning, betrothed to a son of the Li family, had a wedding date set, but her fiancé was very ill. The fiancé's family requested to postpone the wedding. At that time, the girl's father was far away as a guest, and her mother had died early. Although she had several brothers, none were from the same mother, and they paid no attention to her. The girl then took it upon herself to say, "Since I have already been promised in marriage, there is no other matter."

The husband, though unfortunate, still intended to proceed, and being ill was not a significant issue; he still requested to go as planned. Thus, the wedding ceremony was hastily performed. After the ceremony, the husband became very weak, fortunately, the wife treated him well. After several more years, they had two sons, and then he died. The wife lived alone, adhering to her vows, and cared for her two sons. But her father-in-law was not a good person; because the wife was young and beautiful, he planned to marry her off for a large dowry, intending to take away her resolve. The wife knew of his plan and fled back to her mother's home. All the affairs of her mother's family were entrusted to her maternal uncle. Her maternal uncle said, "If your father entrusted me with family matters, the income is insufficient to cover the expenses, and now there are more people to support, what should we do?" The wife replied, "That is true, but I am not a person who sits idle. Please let me work as a laborer."
