Both sisters-in-law went to her to buy flowers, and later, the sister-in-law whose child was in danger exchanged her flowers for the other's, and then woke up. The next day, when she looked at her child, it had improved somewhat, while the other sister-in-law's child's pustules had all sunk, and soon after, the child died, whereas the first sister-in-law's child remained unharmed. She realized that the exchange of flowers in her dream was a sign of the changing conditions of their children. In Yangzhou's Ganyuan County, there was a man named Chen Sheng Duanfu, who lived south of the Fairy Temple Town. His mother and wife were both surnamed Xiong, making them aunt and niece. When his wife fell ill, his mother moved her bed into the room. One night, after waking from sleep, she saw a faint, flickering light moving from the bottom up. She was startled and looked closely, and saw a large rat carrying a piece of burning material running along the wall. She quickly called her son to wake up, but by then the fire had already reached the ceiling. In a short while, the fire grew intense. Chen carried his mother out, and then had someone carry out his sick wife. Fortunately, they were all unharmed, but the house was completely destroyed. The incident of a rat carrying burning material is a common occurrence, but for Chen, it led to a devastating fire. As Ouyang Xiu said, "Disasters often originate from small, seemingly insignificant events," and indeed they do.

In Suzhou there lived a woman named Lady Wang, who had become a widow at a young age and was poor, with no children. She earned her living solely by sewing and mending, and had done so for nearly thirty years. Her neighbor, an old man who was quite wealthy, had a son who was just ten years old and had been sickly since childhood. Whenever the illness struck, a spirit would possess his body, and they would summon a witch to perform exorcism rituals, but these could not drive the spirit away. However, whenever Lady Wang happened to visit his home, the spirit would flee. This continued for several years, and the boy's mind and body grew increasingly troubled, and he grew weaker day by day. His father was deeply concerned. One night, he dreamed of a deity who spoke to him, saying: "If this child were to be given to Lady Wang as her heir, his illness would be cured." The old man then approached Lady Wang and proposed that he give the child to her to raise together, so that both families' ancestral rites might continue. Lady Wang was greatly pleased. The boy, now under the care of his mother Lady Wang, indeed recovered from his illness, later married, had children, and cared for Lady Wang until her death. Was it not likely that the ghosts and gods pitied the virtuous widow and could not bear to see her grow old and alone? My late father recorded in his *Notes from the Snowy Study*: In the village of Linping, there were four brothers born in the same year—two were born in January as twins, and two in December as twins. Yet there were also two brothers born in the same year and month, but the younger brother's birthday was earlier than the elder's. These two were step-brothers, both born in March: the elder was born in mid-March, while the younger was born in early June (in a leap year), and they were twenty days apart. In years without a leap month, the younger brother would be ten days older than the elder. This matter is not particularly strange, but I record it here for the sake of amusement. Unfortunately, those who spoke of it did not mention their names.

In Hubei, there was a daughter of the Zhang family who was betrothed to a son of the Hu family, with the wedding date set. However, the Hu son fell gravely ill, and his family sent a matchmaker to ask Zhang to call off the engagement. Zhang's daughter refused, saying, "Having already pledged my body to another, I accept life and death with it." She then married into the Hu family. After the Hu son's illness improved somewhat, several years passed, and she gave birth to two sons. He eventually passed away, and Zhang cared for her two sons until the end of her life. This story is similar to the account of Fan's daughter in the fifteenth volume, but while Fan's daughter was lonely and suffering, Zhang not only had integrity but also talent. Her father served as a county magistrate in Fujian, possessed vast wealth, and took only two concubines with him, abandoning his wife at home without concern, leaving her destitute. Members of the community were all outraged and encouraged her to go to Fujian. Her mother declined due to lack of funds, but they raised money for her. She also feared she would not be treated respectfully by her husband. Zhang then said, "Mother, do not fear. I will accompany you. However, we need to prepare the necessary funds. If we are welcomed, we will stay; if not, we will return." Thus, mother and daughter traveled to Fujian, stopping thirty miles short of their destination to send a message.

The father heard that his wife was coming, and he was initially reluctant to accept her. Upon learning that his daughter was coming with her, he finally prepared a grand procession to welcome them. After they arrived, they indeed lived together in harmony. After several months, the daughter requested to return home, and the mother tried to persuade her to stay. The daughter said, "There is a respected elder in our family. How can I stay here and continue to serve you?" The mother wept and said, "As long as you are here, I have no worries. But if you leave, he will treat me like fish and meat. I will follow you back." And so, they returned together. Later, the father was disgraced for corruption, and an imperial decree was issued to his hometown to inventory his property. The local official arrived at the door, and the mother, in fear, hid under the stove, while the others all fled. The daughter, dressed in her best clothes, came out to meet the official and described how her father had neglected her mother in the past. She also said, "When my father was prosperous, my mother did not share in his happiness. Now that he is disgraced, can my mother share in his suffering? Please enter and see for yourself. If there is any hidden wealth, I am willing to accept the punishment for concealing it." Her words were resolute and moving, and the official was deeply moved. Upon entering, he saw only a few dilapidated rooms, a sparse curtain, and a simple bed. He sighed, "Truly as you say, truly as you say." And he left. In Shanxi, there was a wealthy man who was forty years old but had no son. Every time he visited relatives and saw a child, he would always be happy and play with the child.

Once while serving in the Central Plains, I was riding a horse past the outskirts of Zhongmou County. Under the shade of a tree, I saw a little boy, just four or five years old, who was cute and lovable. I dismounted to look at him. The child did not cry at first, so I picked him up and put him on the horse. Fortunately, no one saw. I closely examined this child, noting his beautiful eyebrows and eyes, and his fat and white face like a gourd. I loved him and could not bear to let go, so I finally held him in my arms and tied up the horse and left. By the time I arrived home, I adopted him as a son. After adopting this son, the old man had two more sons, and all three sons grew up, and they were all married. However, the old man's family often whispered among themselves, saying that since the old man already had sons, why did he need this other son? The old man heard it faintly and realized that after his death, they would not accept him. Then he called this son and said to him, "I brought you from Zhongmou, and now you are fortunate to have grown up. But staying here for a long time, it has become less interesting. Leaves fall back to the root, you should return home!" He then prepared a thousand-gold outfit, sent him away with his wife, and warned him, "When you reach Zhongmou, just go to a certain place to visit, and there will be a reunion of flesh and blood. This is where I met you." The son had no choice but to cry and leave.

After arriving in Zhongmou, he left his wife at an inn and went himself to the outskirts of the city to search, asking everyone he met, hoping to find something. After a long time, someone told him, "In my village, there is an old widow from the family of a certain surname who once lost a son. Could this be him?" The man was very happy and quickly asked the villager to guide him there. Upon arrival, an old woman came out and said, "After my husband died, I gave birth to a son by posthumous conception. He went missing at a certain age, and it has been nearly twenty years since then. Even if I see him now, I would not recognize him. However, there are things that can be recognized. This child has marks on his face like those described in the poem '咏豆花' by Táng Chén Àn, which are smallpox scars, and I remember how many there were. They still haven't faded." She then described the number of scars. The crowd looked at the child's face and indeed saw the marks, and the number matched what the old woman had said. Everyone was delighted and said, "This is truly him." The man wept and bowed, recounting all the previous events. He immediately brought his wife from the inn to join them. The old woman's family had been poor since she lost her son, and her grief had made her ill. Now, with the return of her son and his wife, and the sudden wealth, she was overjoyed. "A loss may turn out to be a gain," as the saying goes. Perhaps the Creator pities the chaste widow and helps her in a roundabout way.

In the year of Xin Si during the Guangxu era, the flower farmer, Ni Ruxu, and the monk Benhui from the Solitary Mountain Temple, accompanied by others, visited Yu Lou. They sat in the Xishuang Pavilion on the hill behind the building. After descending the mountain, the monk called out to the flower farmer from behind, saying, "Look, look!" The flower farmer turned to see a frog on a pine tree. It was a light green color, its body smooth and lustrous like jade carved into a precious stone, without any rough or uneven texture, unlike ordinary frogs. Ni Ruxu remarked, "Is this not the General of Jinhua?" The monk nodded and said, "Do not speak much." The next day, the flower farmer went to the Right Platform Pavilion to inform me of the incident. According to the *Xianchun Lin'an Zhi* (Imperial Capital Lin'an Gazetteer), the deity's name is Cao Gao, from Zhending, who served as the magistrate of Jinhua during the Later Tang dynasty. The King of Wu and Yue promoted him to serve in Cangzhou (Dust), and the Qian family entered the imperial court, entrusting him with state affairs. Cao Gao dredged three ponds in the city, naming them "Yongjin." After his death, the people built a shrine on the pond to honor him, as he had made significant contributions to the people. The deity transformed into a frog, which is unusual. However, matters concerning ghosts and deities cannot be measured by ordinary reasoning. For example, the Yellow River deity often transforms into a snake, as recorded in official documents.

A Fellow Examinee, Xue Shixiang, who held the title of Censor, was from Lingbao, Henan. He once told me: "The god of the Yellow River has two forms: the Great King and the General. Whenever the deity appears, officials must present a tray to receive it, offering great sacrifices of animals and staging elaborate theatrical performances. The snake remains motionless in the tray. These snakes vary in size: the smaller one is the Great King, the larger one the General. The Great King snake is no more than a foot long, with its head facing outward and its tail inward, straight as an arrow. Even after several days, if it slightly turns, it never deviates from its original direction. The General snake, by contrast, may be several feet or even over ten feet long, coiled like tangled silk. Its head rises proudly upward, and its tail is not visible. The Great King snake must be placed precisely in the center of the tray, while the General is slightly off to one side. After several days, it vanishes without a trace, and no one knows where it has gone. River engineers, officials, and the people living along the banks of the river all revere and worship it, none daring to treat it with disrespect. There is one known as the Great King Lì, who was Lord Lì Gōngqín of our dynasty, and his spirit is also a snake." Then, what difference is there between the Jinhua General being a frog and this? If we extrapolate this, we can understand that ancient texts record such transformations, such as Bo Gun becoming a yellow bear, the King of Looking becoming a cuckoo, Nüwa becoming the magpie, and Qin Pi becoming a great hawk. This is also in accordance with reason. A narrow-minded scholar, of course, is not capable of understanding this.

In the winter of the tenth year of the Tongzhi era, I returned from West Lake Academy to my temporary residence in Wu, taking a detour through Deqing to visit the graves of my ancestors. I moored my boat in the city and, sitting in a small boat, left the city through the southern gate, arriving at Jinge Mountain to pay respects at the tomb of the late official. I left my servant, Shen Gui, to guard the boat. Suddenly, a man in his forties or fifties appeared, speaking with a local accent from Deqing, and came to the boat to request an audience. Shen questioned his name, and the man replied that it was approximately "Liu." Shen, who had a basic understanding of writing, asked, "Is it the 'Liu' composed of the characters for 'mouse,' 'gold,' and 'knife'?" The man replied, "No, it has a 'wood' radical!" I returned home, and Shen reported this to me. I dismissed it casually. However, considering that the surname has a 'wood' radical and sounds like "Liu," and that it is one of the two surnames that exist, it must be either "Lou" or "Liu." Our town has never had these two surnames, so I do not know who this man is.

Upon returning to Wu, I happened to be in the same county as Jiang Ziping, a scholar of high moral standing, and Cai Yuqing, a talented young scholar. I asked them about the matter, and the two gentlemen replied, "This is quite unusual. Could it be Lord Liu?" Lord Liu is one of our town's guardian deities. Our town has three patron gods: Lord Dai, Lord Liu, and Lord Ye, with Lord Liu being the first among them. During the Tianbao era of the Tang Dynasty, when Deqing County was first established, Lord Liu came to serve as the magistrate. His name was Cha Gong, and he was the grandfather of Liu Zihou. He is the "Degan Jun" mentioned in the *Xian Shiyushi Shendaobiao* (Inscription on the Spirit Road of the Former Magistrate) in Liu Zihou's collected works. He had benevolent policies, and after his death, the people of the town built a shrine for him. Over time, the shrine fell into disuse, and when Lord Dai became active again, the people of the town used the old site of the Liu shrine to build a new one. Thus, they began to worship both Lord Dai and Lord Liu together, and later added Lord Ye to the worship. This is recorded in the county records. I have been living away from home for a long time and have not been able to personally visit the shrine. Moreover, my literary and moral principles are not enough to move the spirits of the living and the dead. What the two gentlemen said is probably just idle talk. This matter is recorded in the fifth volume of *Chunzaitang Suibi* (Spring in the Hall Miscellaneous Notes). Now, because of military affairs in Jinhua, I am recording it again.

In the fifth year of the Guangxu era, on the day YiChou of the tenth month, I buried my wife, Lady Yao, in the original of Right Platform Mountain in Qiantang, and I myself built my own grave to the left of it. Three days after the burial, a butterfly appeared at the tomb, with a black body and yellow patterns. Three days later, it appeared again, just as before. Although it was the small spring of October, it was already the time of great snow, and in the morning, the ground was covered with frost, which should not have been the case, and it was also strange. When I returned to Wu, I told my old friend Wu Pingzai about it, and Pingzai decided it was a divine butterfly from Taichang, but he did not dare to believe it immediately. This matter is recorded in the ninth volume of *Spring in the Hall Poetry Collection*, and it is also recorded here. I wrote a poem called *General of Jinhua*, in which four lines say: "I floated in a boat on the Yubei River, suspecting that a divine being came to accompany me. I built a grave at the foot of Right Platform Mountain, suspecting that a divine butterfly came to accompany the burial." These two events are referred to here. Lu Jinjin, a student from Changzhou, was a high-ranking student, and he was well-known in the school. In the year Xisi of the Guangxu era, he fell seriously ill, and his wife and children prayed to the gods. Lu said: "There is no need to do so, for I recently saw a messenger from the underworld holding a document with my name on it. The character 'jin' below 'Lu' was not originally 'jinjin,' but it was later crossed out and 'jinjin' was written next to it, which is me, how can I escape? Soon after, he died. There were still several people in the Lu family who were named with the character 'jin,' but it is unknown what the original character was. Lu was only twenty-four years old, and that year he had just recently married, and his fellow students were very sorry for him.

Gu, a man from Suzhou, served as an official in Zhejiang and participated in the maritime transport mission. While traveling from Zhejiang to Shanghai, he occasionally drank tea with several friends at a teahouse. Suddenly, he fell ill with a severe and sudden disease. His colleagues sent him back to Suzhou, but his condition worsened. He tried every means to commit suicide, and his family hid all the weapons and ropes. They were so afraid that they even dared not use pottery to serve food and drink. Whenever he got hold of bowls or cups, he would bite and crush them. If they were not prepared in time, he would swallow them whole. Doctors and shamans tried various treatments, but his illness did not improve. One day, someone invited a bamboo basket spirit (箕仙) to inquire about the cause. The spirit wrote with the basket: "The illness is caused by a ghost. The ghost is also named Gu, a commoner from Pinghu, Zhejiang. In his previous life, Gu was the accountant for the ghost's master and embezzled a thousand gold pieces, causing the ghost to die in sorrow. Therefore, the ghost has come to seek retribution. However, Gu only embezzled the assets and did not cause the ghost's death. The ghost died of illness, so it is difficult to argue for compensation. If Gu can perform many good deeds, he can resolve the grudge." Following the advice, Gu's family performed Buddhist rituals, reciting scriptures and offering food for seven days and nights. Gu's illness was cured, and he returned to Shanghai to complete the maritime transport mission. However, he died of another illness within three years. The ghost likely took advantage of his weakened state, as the *Zuo Zhuan* says, "taking advantage of his weakened state."

Mr. A, a native of Yangzhou, resided in Suzhou. At home, he had only his mother and wife. Occasionally, he went out, and his wife closed the door for him. Just returned inside, he suddenly fell ill with madness, shouting and grumbling, making it unbearable. The mother was alarmed and sent someone to urge Mr. A to return. Mr. A noticed the voice and realized it was a man. He asked, "Who are you?" His wife shouted, "Don't you recognize me? I parted from you at a certain place in a certain year, have you forgotten it?" Mr. A suddenly realized it was Mr. B. Previously, when Mr. A was in Yangzhou, he had borrowed ten thousand coins from Mr. B. During the chaos caused by the Cantonese rebels, Mr. A, his mother, and his wife fled to a certain place, and Mr. B also arrived there.

At that time, B had no money and asked A for the borrowed money. A still had forty silver dollars in his bag and wanted to agree, but A's wife said, "The chaos and separation are so great, and life and death are uncertain. Is it a time to repay debts?" A followed her words and stopped, and afterward, they did not see each other again. At this point, A asked about it and said, "After we parted, where did you go?" A's wife spoke in ghostly language and said, "At that time, I was with my mother. You did not return my money. My mother and I were hungry and could not eat. We struggled to the riverbank, and the bandits had already arrived in large numbers. There were ferries in the river, but because I had no money, they would not take us. I carried my mother along the riverbank, exhausting ourselves and falling to the ground, moaning for three days and nights. Both my mother and I died. Every time I think of the suffering at the time of our deaths, I hate you to the core. For over ten years, I have been searching for you in Yangzhou, and for several years in Suzhou. Now that we have met again, I must take your life." A bowed and said, "In the past, I truly acted out of heartlessness, but if my wife and I die, your mother and child would still not survive, which would be of no benefit. And I owe you money, but ultimately, I did not harm your life."

I wish to repay several times the money borrowed in the past, to perform good deeds for you, relying on the power of the Buddha, to be reborn in a good place, isn't that beautiful? "The ghost initially refused, but then said: Your words also make sense, let's do it." Jia then spent money to perform large-scale Buddhist rituals, and his wife's illness was indeed cured. Duke Zhongshan once said: "Whether many or few, they are all in trouble; whether deep or shallow, they all cause heartache." Jia and Yi have always had a relationship of sharing wealth, in a time of crisis, even if there is no debt, one should still share wealth to help, how much more so if there is actual debt? But the wife's words were followed, and not a single hair was pulled out, thus causing mother and child to be displaced and pushed into the ditches, it is natural that they would harbor resentment in the underworld. Even though relying on the power of the Buddha to forgive past sins, I still cannot guarantee the final outcome. Zhao, a person from Suzhou, during times of military turmoil, leaped over the city walls with nowhere to rely on. There was a farmer named Li, out of pity, he kept him, Zhao then had an affair with Li's daughter.

Li Wuhuo was then taken as a son-in-law and lived in his wife's family, and the couple got along well. Zhao had a younger uncle who fled to Ningbo to avoid the war. He sent someone to find Zhao and invited him to come. Zhao told his wife, "My younger uncle has considerable wealth. He has come to invite me, so I will go first. If I succeed, I will come back to get you and my father-in-law." When Zhao arrived in Ningbo, his younger uncle proposed marrying him to a certain family's daughter. Zhao heard that the girl was beautiful and that the dowry was very rich, so he happily agreed. After the war ended, Zhao settled with his younger uncle and did not return to Suzhou. In the spring of the XinSi year during the Guangxu era, Zhao went out to gaze at the scenery and suddenly felt a cold wind on his face, causing him to shiver all over. He then went back inside, covered himself with a blanket, and lay down. In the middle of the night, he suddenly spoke deliriously, saying, "It has been over twenty years since we parted, and I have not thought of our past once. This caused my father to die in anger, and I also died in anger. To be so unfaithful, I have now obtained permission from the gods. Do you still hope to live?" After speaking these words, he died.

In the bustling market area of Yangzhou, there were two people named Wang and Chen who jointly operated a shop. In the shop, they kept a cat whose fur was exceptionally beautiful, and both families loved it very much. The cat gave birth to four kittens, all resembling their mother. After a month, the kittens were leaping and playing on the ground, drawing the attention of everyone who saw them. One day, a young man suddenly entered the shop, grabbed two of the kittens, and ran off. The mother and wife both cried out loudly, and Wang, who had nodded off lightly, woke up and chased after the young man. Someone named Mr. A also helped chase him. Soon, Chen arrived as well. The young man, seeing that three people were chasing him, realized he could not escape, so he abandoned one kitten on the road, and Mr. A took it back. Chen and Wang continued to chase him relentlessly, and the young man, in great distress, ran off with the remaining kitten. The two men returned with the kitten. The young man, not knowing that they had stopped chasing, still thought they were after him, and he ran wildly, accidentally colliding with a pregnant woman and knocking her down. He even stepped on her body as he ran past. The crowd then surrounded and blocked the young man, preventing him from escaping. They saw that the woman was on the verge of death, and her husband was found and brought to the scene. The young man was tied up and taken to the authorities. If the woman died, he would be judged according to the law. Alas! The Duke of Lu was exiled due to a cockfight, and the ruler of Zheng was killed due to a dog chase. A small matter can lead to a major conflict, and a small thing can cause a great disaster. This has been the case since ancient times. This is still a minor example of such incidents.

A certain person from Hubei, residing in Yangzhou as a salt merchant, enjoyed discussing the practices of internal and external alchemy. Once, while drinking with a friend at a teahouse, he saw an old man setting up a tent with cloth, selling medicine underneath. A customer was haggling with him, but the price had not yet been settled. Upon closer inspection, it was a *He Shou Wu* root that resembled a human form. He asked about its price, and it was four thousand coins. He was greatly pleased and paid the amount. His friend said, "This must be fake. How could there be a *He Shou Wu* that has become a human form, and it costs so little?" The person replied, "Not so. Immortals sell medicine, depending on whether there is a karmic connection, not counting the value." He carried it back. That night, he stayed in the garden, not entering the inner room. The next day, he saw it had died. His family was greatly alarmed and thoroughly investigated the cause. His friend informed them. They quickly sent someone to search for the medicine seller, but he had vanished. As the ancient poem says, "Seeking immortality through elixirs often leads to being misled by medicine." Truly, these words are accurate.

In the early years of the Tongzhi era, a major uprising broke out in Yunnan. Wherever the rebels went, they killed people like hemp, leaving fields full of bones. Major cities and towns were all turned into desolate ruins. After the chaos subsided, the few survivors began to return, cleaning up the bones and remains, and rebuilding the ruins. At this time, a major epidemic broke out. Before the epidemic began, rats in the households died without apparent cause, sometimes in the walls and sometimes on the ceilings. People did not see the dead rats, which then rotted over time. Anyone who smelled the rotting rats rarely escaped illness. The disease started with a hard, red, painful lump on the body. Soon after, the patient developed fever and delirium, and some died within a day, while others died within a few days. Doctors were unable to treat the disease. Some people tried to cut off the lumps, but even if one lump was cut off, another would appear elsewhere. Only a few out of many survived. During the height of the epidemic, villagers often saw ghostly lights at night. The lights appeared in groups and moved in formation. When people approached, they heard the sounds of drums, bells, horns, horse hooves, and metal clashing. During moonlit nights, people also saw the appearance of flags. Sometimes, people would suddenly fall to the ground as if sleeping deeply and then wake up after a day. When they woke up, they would say that they had been captured by soldiers and transported goods to a certain place before returning.

Some say that they were ordered to carry and deliver an official edict, which prominently stated, "A certain official is leading a number of troops to a certain place, and it is ordered that they be supplied along the way according to law." After several days, the places they mentioned all suffered great epidemics. The epidemic began in the countryside and spread to the cities. If one family had a sick person, then their neighbors, numbering in the dozens, would move away to avoid it. Countless people fell ill and died on the roads, but ultimately they could not escape it. Even to the point where entire families perished, and every household was left empty, small villages became completely deserted. The *Dao De Jing* says, "Where armies have been, thorns and thistles grow." Indeed! Magistrate Ma Xingwu, from Yunnan, told me this, as he personally witnessed it. The magistrate also said: During the years 1858–1859 of the reign of Emperor Xianfeng, pear trees often did not bear fruit, but instead grew melons; catalpa trees, which normally do not bear fruit, instead produced seeds like beans. Curious people collected and preserved them. Later, an epidemic broke out, and all medicines were ineffective. Someone suggested that these pear melons and catalpa beans, which had never been heard of before, might be useful. Since heaven and earth have produced these strange things, they must have a purpose, perhaps they could cure this strange disease? When tested, it proved effective. But at the time, there were few who collected them, making them very hard to obtain. Those who obtained them valued them more than ginseng or lingzhi mushrooms. In recent years, this disease has not occurred for a long time, and pear trees and catalpa trees no longer produce melons or beans. This shows the benevolence of heaven, even in great disasters, there is always a hint of redemption.

A Man from Wu A man from Wu, serving as an official in the Central Plains, had three sons, all around ten years old, and of similar age. One day, they were playing in the courtyard. The second son, holding a nail in his hand, told the youngest son, "If I shoot this nail, it will become a crossbow bolt and hit your eye." The youngest son replied, "Brother, try it." The second son raised his hand, and the nail flew away. The two younger sons were quite far apart, with another person in between, but the nail actually passed through this person's armpit and stuck in the youngest son's eye. The eyeball immediately popped out, as large as a chicken's egg, brightly colored. Fortunately, the connection was not broken, and it hung down about four or five inches. The youngest son ran back to the inner room and threw himself into his mother's arms. His mother was greatly shocked and held the eyeball with her hand, putting it back into the socket. At first, he didn't feel pain, but then he started feeling intense pain and fell down. After a long time, he regained consciousness, fortunately unharmed afterward, but this eye became blind. While everyone was in a state of confusion, the second son suddenly disappeared. They searched for him thoroughly and found him in a temple, kneeling with his head bowed, praying to protect his younger brother from dying. They helped him back, and he looked ashen, with blood dripping from his forehead. His mother could not bring herself to scold him and comforted him with kind words. This is probably due to the intervention of ghosts and spirits.

Pan was a prominent family in Huizhou. One of their clan members examined the genealogical records and discovered that during the Qianlong era, there was a woman in the family who died in Hangzhou and was buried shallowly outside the city. She had no descendants. However, she had shown great kindness and virtue to her family. Therefore, the entire clan gathered to discuss and decided to bring her remains back and bury them together with her husband's tomb. They collected money and sent one person to Hangzhou. This person arrived in Hangzhou in the fourth month of the seventh year of Emperor Guangxu's reign, followed the records in the genealogy, and successfully found the burial site. When they opened the tomb, they found that the coffin was made of camphor wood, which was still in good condition, but the bottom had rotted. The body was lying in the coffin, with a natural appearance as if it were newly deceased, and her clothes were also undamaged. There was someone who saw that the deceased's right hand held a small box, which was sealed very tightly. This person secretly took the box, not knowing what was inside. Some said, "There must be a treasure inside the box, which is why the body remains uncorrupted." A servant from Wu actually accompanied them and personally witnessed the event, and he told the story to others upon his return.

Li Shi Quan, a scholar-official from Qiantang, passed the provincial examination in the Ding You year of the Daoguang era and was of the same age cohort as I. Initially, he was named Ren Jie. Shortly thereafter, there was a rebel named Zhong Ren Jie in Hubei, and Li Shi Quan did not want to share a name with him, so he changed his name to E. Someone said, "The character 'E' (峨) means 'mountain above me,' which presses down on me. How can this be safe? If you do not change this name, it will surely bring great misfortune!" Li Shi Quan did not heed this advice and died the following year. I consulted *Taiping Guangji*, which cites *Gand Ding Lu* and says: There was an imperial scholar named Li Yue who failed to pass the examinations multiple times. In a dream, someone told him, "With a mountain on your head, how can you achieve the top rank?" Upon waking, he could no longer be named Yue, so he changed his name to Yan and then passed the examination. This is exactly similar to Li Shi Quan's story, and the advice given is not without insight.

According to the *Shuowen Jiezi*, dream speech is referred to as [C204] (引见《一切经音义》), which can also be written as "呓" (yì). The *Shiji* records that Lu Meng's dream speech was related to the *I Ching*. This phenomenon is not uncommon in modern times. In Hubei, there was a woman who was mistreated by her husband. When he went out, he would abandon her. She lived frugally and had no other aspirations. One day, her husband returned home and spent the night with her. He heard her speaking in her sleep, discussing household matters with diligence and care. He was deeply moved and said, "The proverb says, 'Chai and Mi are a couple,' indeed!" Thus, they fell in love and respected each other as before. This is another example of how sincerity can move things. Another story involves a concubine from a noble family who, during the day, pretended to be respectful to her senior wife. Her husband sometimes asked her privately, "How does the senior wife treat you?" She would reply, "Very well!" However, in her dreams, she would cry out as if being scolded by the senior wife, filled with fear and begging for mercy, trembling uncontrollably. When her husband called out to her, she smiled and said, "Nothing, just dream talk!" He persisted in asking, but she never revealed the truth. He sighed, "Has the accumulated power of the senior wife overwhelmed you to this extent?" As a result, he gradually grew distant from his wife. In reality, the concubine's dream speech was all false. This is even more cunning than Lady Li Ji's weeping at midnight. Her senior wife eventually died of sorrow and illness, saying to her attendants, "Even if I die, I will not let this concubine achieve her goals." Not long after, the concubine also died.

In the Xinsi year of the Guangxu era, in April, a household in Ximi Lane, Suzhou, had a sow give birth to a piglet with one head, two bodies, two tails, and eight legs. The family considered this inauspicious and killed the piglet, throwing it into the water. The dogs took it to eat, and there were no other unusual events. According to the *History of Ming*, in the fourth year of the Tianqi era, a pig was born in Yulin with the same unusual characteristics, and the matter is similar to this, which the Five Elements school calls a "pig disaster." Ji Wen Da, in his study of Han Confucianism, is most skeptical of the Five Elements legends, and I agree with him. Confucius, in writing the *Spring and Autumn Annals*, did not fail to record unusual events, but if one follows the sayings of scholars like Liu Xiang and Dong Zhongshu, interpreting a particular unusual event as a response to a particular event, one cannot avoid being speculative and contradictory. The *History of Ming: Five Elements Chapter* records only auspicious and unusual events without recording the corresponding events, which is a very insightful view.

The Son of the Zhang Family The son of the Zhang family was a man from the state of Chu. He was born with a refined and reserved nature, and his parents loved him with unusual affection. At the age of sixteen or seventeen, suddenly a spirit possessed his body, causing his speech to become incoherent and his singing and weeping to be without measure. In his normal state, he was gentle and delicate like a maiden; but when possessed, he would leap and dance wildly, his movements as quick as a monkey. He could climb to the top of a hundred-foot tree, and regarded towering buildings as if they were mere tables. In the customs of Chu, those who were possessed by a spirit would be made into shamans, and the people of the village would all revere and believe in them, earning them a considerable amount. However, his parents genuinely did not wish for their son to become a shaman. They earnestly pleaded with the spirit, saying, "Our son is small and not worthy of serving a great spirit. We wish the spirit to leave." The spirit refused. Then they invited the elders of their family and in-laws, who knelt and bowed before the spirit, pleading even more earnestly. The spirit then possessed their son and said, "Since the people have so earnestly requested, I will leave. However, this child will not live long. While I am here, I can protect him; if I leave, he may not escape." After speaking, the spirit indeed departed. The following year, the boy died. The people believed the spirit’s words, but how can we know that it was not because the boy’s spirit had already been drained, allowing evil to take hold? The boy once said that when the spirit first descended upon him, he saw something like a monkey. They suspected it was a monkey spirit, hence his agility was as such.

A Man from Chu Marries a Bride A man from Chu was about to marry a bride. Just as he was leading her into the bridal chamber to perform the union ritual, the bride suddenly went mad, shouting and making loud noises, rushing and colliding. The entire family was in a state of panic, not knowing what to do. They had no choice but to bring the bride's father to the scene. When the bride saw him, she spoke harshly: "You have come?" The father realized that it was the voice of his older brother. Previously, the father had an older brother who died early, and he entrusted him with two sons. The father embezzled their assets and mistreated the older brother's son, killing one of them. At this point, the spirit of the older brother possessed the bride's body, listing the father's crimes. The father bowed his head and could not say a word. The family of the groom then blamed him, saying: "This woman has already come to our home, so she is part of our family. How can we involve ourselves in your family's affairs? And when this woman was still a maiden, why didn't you possess her and speak through her? Now you are disrupting our family's good ceremony, isn't that unacceptable?" The spirit replied: "When this woman was still a maiden, I possessed her and spoke through her, but who would have heard it? Now both families and relatives are present, there must be a fair judgment. I therefore do not hesitate to be impolite, to vent my anger. I hope the elders will pity me and handle the situation well." The crowd told the bride's father: "You are indeed unreasonable. What can you say now?" They then made him return everything he had embezzled and summoned the older brother's son, choosing an elder in the family to raise him until he grew up. The spirit again possessed the bride, bowed his head, expressed gratitude, and then left. There were no further incidents. However, from then on, the father was no longer respected in the village.

A man from Jiangxia County in Hubei Province, named Zhu, married a woman from Jiangxi Province during a time of war. She was a widow. However, this woman, upon joining the Zhu family, deeply loved and respected her husband, and she treated his parents-in-law with great care, just as if they were her own. Her relationship with her brother-in-laws' wives was harmonious, with no complaints or criticisms. She was also very diligent, and although she had never practiced many of the tasks typically performed by women, she would always do them with effort. The entire family was pleased, considering her a virtuous wife, and they did not look down on her because she had remarried. After living together for several years, she suddenly fell gravely ill. A ghost possessed her and spoke, accusing her of being unrighteous. Upon investigation, it turned out to be her former husband. The people of the Zhu family gathered around and prayed, saying, "In such a time of chaos, you have passed away, leaving this lonely and destitute widow. Who can she rely on? It is not her fault that she could not stay faithful." The ghost replied, "That is not true. When I was alive, I asked her if she could remain faithful to me. She swore she would not remarry and promised to take care of my elderly mother. Before my bones had even grown cold, she broke her promise, causing my mother to wander homeless and die of hunger and cold. I am filled with hatred in the underworld. Now that I have found her again, I will take her life. If your family feels pity for this woman, you can arrange her funeral. Do not waste your money on doctors and sorcerers, as it will be of no use." The woman fell ill for several days and eventually died. This woman was very virtuous in the Zhu family, and it is believed that her former husband had not acted improperly. She was punished for breaking her promise, and thus suffered the retribution of a broken vow. The classics say, "Those who break their word do not suffer," but this serves as a warning to those who would die or live without regard for their promises.

During the reign of Emperor Daoguang, one of the county magistrates in Taihe, Anhui, had a retainer skilled in mediumship with a spirit board. During the long summer days when there was nothing to do, he would often invite his friends to summon a spirit from the board, and they would engage in poetic exchanges for amusement. One day, when the spirit arrived, it composed a poem: "The city walls and people are different from those of old; the battlefield is still covered with withered grass. All my life, I have harbored endless sorrow for my family and country, known only by the night moon at Qingyin Pavilion." The spirit signed itself as "Ququzi," and none of the guests knew who it was. The spirit then wrote on the board: "Do any of you know of Li Xu? I am Xu." The guests asked, "May we inquire about his time and place of birth?" The spirit replied, "Reflecting on these old matters, I still feel pain in my heart. Since you are all interested in such things, let me briefly tell you. I was born at the end of the Wanli era of the Ming dynasty. At that time, Yingzhou belonged to Fuyang Prefecture, and I was from Yingzhou. My father's name was Jingbai, and he served as the governor of Shandong. During that period, Wei Zhongxian held absolute power and instructed the governors and provincial administrators to present auspicious signs. Coincidentally, a strange calf was born in Shandong, and my father, influenced by his colleagues, reported it to the court as the birth of a qilin (a mythical unicorn). The cabinet ministers drafted an edict stating, 'The eunuchs' virtue has led to the birth of a benevolent creature.' The loyal and righteous scholars of the land were outraged, and my father was subsequently disapproved of by the public. After the enthronement of Emperor Siling, the eunuchs were executed, and the court issued a decree to compile the list of those implicated in the scandal, and my father was included in it. I truly feel sorrow for him, and I recall what the *Zhou Book* says: 'You should cover up the sins of your ancestors; only loyalty and filial piety.' Thus, correcting one's father's mistakes can only be done through loyalty and filial piety."

Whenever I read about the deeds of ancient loyal ministers and valiant heroes, I could not help but sigh deeply with emotion. In the eighth year of the Chongzhen era, the rebel forces captured Yingzhou. I thought that dying in vain would be useless, so I jumped and escaped. I fled to Shandong, where I gathered my former subordinates and recruited several hundred loyal warriors. Together, we returned and recaptured Yingzhou. I then fought the rebels at Zihuapu and defeated them decisively. I fought again at Jiaobeiji and captured the rebel leader, Chuantatian, after which the rebels dared not attack Yingzhou again. I guarded Yingzhou for eight years, and my fame spread throughout the Jiang and Huai regions. Later, because the rebel leader Yuan Shimong betrayed me again, I was caught off guard and killed. To die for the country, what more could one regret? I never expected that among the rebels there was a man named Li Xin, formerly a scholar from Qixian. After surrendering to the rebels, he changed his name to Yan and strongly advised the rebel leader Chu to avoid killing too many people in order to win over the people's hearts. He also spread the rumor "Welcome the Prince of Chu, no taxes," to confuse and mislead the people. He is listed in the book of virtuous men but willingly became a rebel—truly not human. He is Li Xin, and I am Li Xu. He is from Qixian, Kaifeng Prefecture, Henan Province, and I am from Yingzhou, Fuyang Prefecture, Zhili Province. We are as different as horses and cattle. I am ashamed of my lack of learning; in my youth, I was a carefree noble, fond of riding horses, practicing swordplay, gambling, and drinking. Sometimes I would compose songs and poems with guests from my office; after drinking, I would draw my sword and sing wildly—that was all I did. I never lowered myself to study, took the imperial examinations, or passed the孝廉 examination. The world has passed on a mistaken belief, saying that I am Li Xin. For hundreds of years, this belief has been unbreakable, with everyone claiming that the Li Xin among the rebels was the son of a certain governor of Shandong named Li.

Alas! My father was already maligned by the world before his death, and my humble intention was truly to sacrifice this frail body to clear his name; yet due to a coincidental similarity in surname, it led to the inability to distinguish between the good and the wicked, leaving a grudge in the netherworld that will never end! "Thus, I composed another poem: 'This grudge will last forever, and no one will discuss it. From ancient times in the historical records, most are the spirits of the wronged.'" This matter, I first heard from Yan Baihua, a talented scholar in Huizhou, who had a record that detailed the battles of Li Xu in great detail, but I have since lost it. According to the *Ming Shi* (History of the Ming Dynasty), during the reign of Emperor Xizong, people competed to flatter Wei Zhongxian, with officials like Yan Mingtai, Liu Zhao, Li Jingbai, and Yao Zongwen vying to praise his virtues and establish shrines in his honor. Thus, Li Jingbai was indeed a member of Wei Zhongxian's faction. It is also recorded that Shandong produced a qilin, and Governor Li Jingbai submitted an illustration of it. Therefore, the appearance of the qilin is a real event. In the second year of the Chongzhen era, the list of traitors was finalized, and Li Jingbai, as a governor and chancellor, was listed in the second tier, sentenced to three years of penal servitude, and redeemed through labor to return to civilian life, as recorded in the *Cui Chengxiu Zhuan* (Biography of Cui Chengxiu). Li Jingbai's character is truly not worth discussing. Furthermore, according to the *Liu Zei Zhuan* (Biography of Bandits), Li Xin, a scholar from Qixian county, was listed in the list of traitors, and his son, who was a governor and chancellor, joined Li Zicheng and changed his name to Yan. Therefore, the history records Li Xin as the son of Li Jingbai. Who, then, is this Li Xu mentioned? Initially, I thought the poems and writings attributed to the spirit of a diviner were likely the work of enthusiasts and not reliable. However, later, upon reading Mr. Zhu Lanpo's compilation *Guo Chao Gu Wen Hui Chao* (Collection of Ancient Prose of the State), I found a biography of Li Xu in Fan Guangyang's *Shuang Xue Tang Ji* (Double Snow Hall Collection), which ultimately matched what the diviner had said.

Fan Guangyang was a jinshi (imperial scholar) during the year of Gengchen in the reign of Emperor Kangxi, not far from the late Ming period. What is heard must have a basis. I therefore have one item of doubt regarding Li Xu, which has already been carved into *Hudong Manlu* (Fourth Volume of *Yulou Zazuan*). I suspect Li Jingbai was involved in the case of rebellion, hated by people across the land. Therefore, when one hears that a son of the Li family followed the rebels, everyone says this must be the son of Li Jingbai. In fact, Li Jingbai's son is Li Xu, not Li Xin. Even those who compiled *Ming Shi* (History of Ming) did not know this. Li Jingbai is only mentioned in the biographies of Wei Zhongxian and Cui Chengxiu. It is unknown where he came from, was he from Qixian or Yingzhou? This matter has not yet been determined. In the year Xinsi of Guangxu, Wang Liumen, the Shidu, was the main lecturer at Xihu Xuehaitang, and he would often visit me.

As the official in front of Liu's door, I was a minor official and asked if there was a stele of the names of the Ming Dynasty's successful candidates. The next day, he showed me several copies of the engraved version, but it was not complete. Initially, I did not know which examination R Li Jingbai passed, so I took one copy and casually flipped through it, and immediately saw the three characters 'Li Jingbai.' He was the 95th candidate in the third class of the Kangxi 22nd year's imperial examination, from the military register of Yingzhou, Henan, and a native of Yingzhou, Zhili. Therefore, Li Xin from Qixian is not his son. Moreover, being from the military register of Yingzhou, Henan, who passed the examination, so the world's belief that Li Xin from Qixian is his son is also not without reason. Several years of doubt were resolved in one day. Li Xu, as a noble and wealthy youth, destroyed his family to relieve difficulties and sacrificed himself for the country. He is also a remarkable person. Observing his words on the joss stick, his resolute spirit and heroic soul have not yet faded. I therefore wrote this account, hoping it will console him in the netherworld.

The Qinghe River Canal Office in Zhili is said to have been the site where thirty-six people met a tragic fate during the late Ming Dynasty. To this day, there is a room in the office where the positions of the thirty-six loyal ministers are set up for worship, though it is not known whether this is true. However, those who have lived there have generally not fared well; they warn each other not to stay, and instead rent civilian houses as public halls, leaving the official residence empty. During the Tongzhi era, Mr. Chen Zuomei, a former Qinghe River Canal Commissioner, did not believe in the sayings about ghosts and gods and resided there calmly. Not long after, he died in office. Those who followed him continued to rent houses to live in. I believe that ghosts of loyalty and righteousness should not cause harm; probably because it has been long abandoned and unoccupied, thus being possessed by evil spirits. A wind comes through a hole, which is what this is about. Mr. Zhao Rongfang from Kunming, who was a contemporary of my late father in the year of Bingzi, died in office as the Grand Judge during the Tongzhi era. At that time, there was an official from Zhili who was returning from Zhengding on a mission. Traveling by carriage at night, he suddenly saw a green-curtained sedan chair coming from the north, led by one horse and followed by two horses. Two lanterns in front of the horse were inscribed with the four characters "Criminal Department Hall." He exclaimed in surprise, "How could there be a high-ranking official passing by so late at night? And there were no attendants along the way, why is that?" Upon reaching the provincial capital, they learned that Zhao Gong had died, and then they realized that what they had encountered was not a person. Traveling south from the capital region, perhaps returning to his original hometown in Yunnan.

The *Liji* (*Book of Rites*) states: "Below the rank of *dàifu* (ministers), communities establish *shè* (local shrines), which are called *zhìshè* (established shrines)." The *Zhengshi* commentary explains: "Communities of over a hundred families jointly establish one shrine, which is what we now call a *lǐshè* (village shrine)." This marks the beginning of the worship of the land god in later generations. As for the *chénghuáng* (city god), the practice began with the sacrifices to the four walls during the Spring and Autumn period, or as some cite the *Liji* passage on "fences and water channels" as evidence. However, Kong Yingda states: "Fences are used to store water, also to block water; water channels are used to receive water, also to release water." Thus, these are merely ditches and canals in the fields, not the *chénghuáng*. The land encompasses a vast area, and the *chénghuáng* is merely one aspect of the land. Hence, the land god is greater than the *chénghuáng*. Yet, the *chénghuáng* is only found in a state or county, while the land god is present even in small villages. This is why the *chénghuáng* is revered more than the land god. Both the *chénghuáng* and the land god are deities of the earth, not human spirits. However, in ancient times, *Jùlóng* was associated with the *shè* (shrine), and Wang Su and others believed that the *shè* is where *Jùlóng* is worshipped. Thus, it is not surprising that in Wu and Yue, *Pang Yu* became the *chénghuáng*. What I have recorded in my books, such as Minister Min Doudyang becoming the *chénghuáng* of Yunan Prefecture, and Magistrate Cai Junfu becoming the *chénghuáng* of Jimo County, those who discuss it all say it is true and not false. Now, I have heard from my disciple Zou Jingtang Baohui about his father-in-law, Mr. Ding Lianfu. Mr. Ding Shaoyou, from Dantu, Jiangsu, passed the imperial examination in the *Gengxu* year of the *Daoguang* era, served as an inspector of education in Zhejiang during the *Tongzhi* era, and died in office. After his death, his wife, Lady Cai, dreamed that Mr. Ding came, with toothed flags and ceremonial umbrellas, still as he was in life. When she asked him, he said he had become the *chénghuáng* of Hangzhou Prefecture.

Her daughter married into the Zou family, and she became the wife of Jingtang. A year before her death, she dreamed of a woman coming to claim her life. She then died due to childbirth difficulties. Jingtang could not stop thinking about it. There was a person skilled in fumigation with sandalwood, who claimed to have the techniques of Li Shaojun. So he asked him to summon her. She indeed attached herself to the writing brush and spoke: "In my past life, I was a doctor who mistakenly injured a pregnant woman. Now I come to demand retribution. I have already repaid her life, and I am not suffering. My father is now the city god of Hangzhou, and after death, I reside with him. I am very happy. If you miss me, you can write a letter and have someone take it to the Wushan City God Temple to burn it. It will definitely reach me." Jingtang followed his instructions, and indeed, he dreamed of his wife coming to him, and she recounted the words in the letter exactly. I have heard that Yu Yuanguī wrote a letter titled "Pursuing a Reply to Kong Tan," and Liu Xiaobiao wrote a letter titled "A Repeated Reply to Liu Zhao." Ancient people did not change their minds because of life and death; writing letters to the deceased is also often the case.

The path to the afterlife is vast and uncertain, who will set up a relay station? I fear that the dream of the Mirror Hall is merely a thought or memory as described in the *Rites of Zhou*. If a single letter could reach the nine original places, then though the realms of the living and the dead are separated, communication would still pass. How could this be in accordance with the ancient principle that the people and the spirits should not mix? As for Lianfu's character, it is pure and that of an ancient gentleman; after his death, he became a spirit, which is fitting. I recall the spring of the year Kuiyou, when I once drank with Lianqu, the Attendant, at Lianfu's place; the three of us were all from the same year of the imperial examination, Gengxu. At that time, Lianfu was still healthy, and after drinking, he gave me a volume of *Sichuan Travels* to edit. Not even a few months later, he died without any illness. By the year Gengchen, Lianqu also returned to the mountain path, leaving only me remaining. Thinking of this, I cannot help but feel the deep sense of loss akin to the story of Ji Kang and his qin. On another day, when I ascend Mount Wu and gaze upon the city god's temple, recalling the words of my life, could I not feel gut-wrenching pain?

In the third year of the Xianfeng era (1853), Wu Youle, a high-ranking official from Shimen with the courtesy name Kangshou, had a dream in which an official held a government document and summoned him. Wu Youle followed the official and arrived at a place that resembled an official's office. He saw an official seated facing south, with many attendants. The official led Wu Youle to the table and made him kneel. Wu Youle secretly looked up and saw a sign reading "威灵公" (Wēilínggōng) hanging on the wall. He realized that he was about to die, but he was not afraid. Instead, the deity seemed very anxious and ordered the official to lead Wu Youle out. Wu Youle spoke loudly, asking, "May I ask what crime has led to my arrest?" The deity replied, "There is no other reason; the official made a mistake in the document." Wu Youle said, "If I have been mistakenly summoned here, I would like to ask something. How many years do I have left?" The deity extended five fingers to indicate the number of years. Wu Youle then woke up. He thought that the five fingers must represent five years. At that time, he was twenty-seven years old. By the age of thirty-two, he privately calculated that he would die. He prepared for his death by wearing a headband. When the new year arrived, he was still alive. Those who knew about the incident told Wu Youle, "The five fingers represent fifty years. Your lifespan will surely reach the age of eighty or ninety." In the Xianyuan year of the Guangxu era, Wu Youle visited my study and told me this story. Wu Youle's sixth-generation ancestor was named Zhi Zhen, with the courtesy name Mengju and the pseudonym Huangye Laoren, a poet from the early Qing dynasty. Wu Youle published a ten-volume poetry collection of his ancestor's work and added poems by other poets, such as Yuyang and Dunong, to the collection. Among the officials, there are few who share such refined and cultured interests.

During the reign of Emperor Guangxu in the year of the Monkey (1890), I served as the magistrate of Qingpu County and came to the countryside on official business, docking my boat in the town of Yuecheng. At that time, the setting sun was behind the trees, and smoke rose from cooking fires, indicating it was mealtime in the village. Leaning out the window to look around, I saw that about several dozen steps away from the boat, along the shore, there was a bamboo fence. Inside and outside the fence, there were several men and women, some sitting and some standing, and there were six or seven children playing among them. Suddenly, one child fell into the water, and the men and women all looked in surprise. The cliff was too steep to climb down. I wanted to move the boat to rescue him, but the boat was tied very securely, and the boatmen and elders had all gone to the tavern, making it not easy to gather them quickly. In a state of shock and staring, I saw a dog jump into the water, biting the child's clothes and swimming across to the other side. This side of the cliff was steep and sheer, while the other side was sloping and could be climbed up. The dog pulled the child onto the shore, and the child's family also hurried over, picking him up. Fortunately, he was unharmed. I found it very strange and sent someone to call the child's family and ask them. They said that the family only had this one child, and if he had drowned, there would be no descendants. The dog was one of the family's pets. Just an ordinary dog, yet it was able to swim and save its young master. There are ancient stories of loyal dogs, this is what they are like. I ordered the attendants to feed the dog with meat and rice. Later, I heard from others that among the men and women in the bamboo fence, there were some of his relatives who greatly benefited from the child's death and gained his property. I said in admiration, "Can humans be less than dogs?"

A man from the Fan family of Xinning, a distant relative of my wife's father, who held the title of "observation," was a troublemaker in his youth. He enjoyed drinking and gambling, did not engage in a steady occupation, and died in his forties. There was a certain person, a close relative of his, who was troubled by the ghost of the Fan family man appearing at his home to cause mischief. The relative said, "I have no grudge against you, why are you causing me trouble?" The ghost replied, "I died without a son, why didn’t you establish an heir for me?" The relative responded, "You were your own downfall, not having a wife or children is not my fault. Moreover, I am far removed from you, aren’t the X’s your close relatives? If you want to establish an heir, why don’t you approach them, why are you troubling me?" The ghost said, "No, I had no property, who would be willing to be my heir? Even if they are close relatives, what can they do? You actually manage the affairs of the clan, your word would make it possible." The Fan family was a prominent clan in Xinning, with a clan leader and several others who managed clan matters, and this relative was one of them.

He then told him, "The matter truly lies with me, but you never cared about this in your lifetime. Why are you so urgent now?" The ghost replied, "I made a big mistake in my lifetime, daily scheming for leisure and pleasure, whether you have children or not doesn't concern me. After death, I am blamed by the ancestral spirits, who believe that the ancestral sacrifices are cut off through me. You do not establish a successor for me, and I have no way to answer the ancestral spirits!" He then spoke to his clan members, choosing one of the close relatives to be the successor, and the ghost finally left. Seeing this, we know that ghosts still seek sustenance, and ancient sayings are not false. Reviving the extinguished and continuing the extinct is the benevolent policy of the ancient kings; respecting the ancestors and gathering the clan is the good law of the ancients. We cannot say that the soul ascends and the spirit descends, and thus completely forget everything.

A Woman of Qiantang, Hui, Wife of Zhang Liangcai Hui, the wife of Zhang Liangcai from Qiantang, was the granddaughter of Peng Nian, a juren (provincial degree holder) in the Jiaqing era and prefectural instructor of Huzhou, and the eldest daughter of Daji, a juren in the Xianfeng era and alternate salt magistrate of Lianghuai. She married Liangcai in the Wuxing era of the Tongzhi reign. The following year, in July, Liangcai fell ill. Hui cut flesh from her thigh to feed him, but it proved ineffective. On the brink of death, Liangcai wept and told Hui, "I have no other regrets, only that my parents are still alive, and I cannot fulfill my filial duties. I wish you would take the place of a son." He then passed away. Hui wanted to die with him, but the others, moved by her husband's final words, encouraged her not to. She served her in-laws with utmost filial piety. When her mother-in-law fell ill, Hui prayed to the gods, asking to take her place, and the illness was cured. In the Jishen year, her father-in-law died; in the Jiyou year of the Guangxu reign, her mother-in-law died. Hui sighed, "My duties are now complete." On the 23rd day of the third month of that year, she took poison and died at the age of thirty-eight. Eighteen days had passed since her mother-in-law's death, and sixteen years had passed since her husband's death. After her death, her relatives found a letter she had written by hand, detailing the arrangements for her mother-in-law's burial. It was clear that her resolve to die had been long-standing. During my time inspecting schools in Henan, I heard of a woman named Niu from Song County, who, after her husband's death, served her in-laws, raised her husband's heir, and only after her in-laws had passed away and the heir had grown up, did she hang herself and die.

The officials prepared the matter and presented it upward, and I personally reviewed the document and wrote: "The woman endured for thirteen years, ultimately achieving her initial resolve, calmly facing death, and can be called someone whose heart is as hard as stone." There is a biography titled "The Biography of Fu Liang, the Loyal Woman," which is preserved in the collection "Collection of Guests and Seeds," and today it is also found in Wei. Zou Qingjiao Baoki from Dantu, when young, encountered someone who was serving in the underworld, and said to him, "In your previous life, you were a胥吏, without fault or evil; in this life, you are also living in peace, but your lifespan is not long." In the year of Guangxu's戊寅, while in Hangzhou, he contracted a cold-damp disease, and his left knee could not bend or straighten. The doctor treated it too aggressively, causing a severe liver wind, and both his eyes became blind. Qingjiao prayed to the gods, wishing to reduce his lifespan by a cycle of twelve years to restore his vision, and indeed, he recovered. By the year of辛巳 in January, his father fell seriously ill, and Qingjiao prayed to the gods again, wishing to lend his own lifespan cycle to his father, and his father's illness was cured. On the nineteenth day of March of that year, Qingjiao suddenly fell ill, knowing he would not recover, and told his family, "My lifespan is indeed not long. In the past, I reduced my lifespan by a cycle of twelve years to treat my eye disease, and now I have reduced it by another cycle of twelve years for my father's illness; my time is up." When asked how he knew this, he said, "The gods have shown me my name and records." On the twenty-fifth day, he passed away with a smile. His brother, Jingtang, is one of my disciples, and he requested that this matter be recorded in my notes. I have included the story of Wang Xiaozhi Jigǔ in the ninth volume, who also reduced his lifespan to benefit his parents' years, and I use such stories to adorn my book, illuminating my declining writing skills.

《右台仙馆笔记》 清 俞樾 ●序余自己卯夏姚夫人卒，精神意兴日就阑衰，著述之事殆将辍笔矣。其年冬，葬夫人于钱唐之右台山，余亦自营生圹于其左。旋于其旁买得隙地一区，筑屋三间，竹篱环之，杂莳花木，颜之曰“右台仙馆”。余至湖上，或居“俞楼”，或居斯馆，谢绝冠盖，昵就松楸，人外之游，其在斯乎！余吴下有“曲园”，即有《曲园杂纂》五十卷；湖上有“俞楼”，即有《俞楼杂纂》五十卷，“右台仙馆”安得无书？而精力衰，不能复有撰述，乃以所著笔记归之。笔记者，杂记平时所见所闻，盖《搜神》、《述异》之类；不足，则又征之于人。嗟乎！不古训之是式，而惟怪之欲闻，余之志荒矣。此其所以为“右台仙馆”之书欤！曲园居士自记。
附录《征求异闻启》并小诗二首：余今岁行年六十矣，学问之道，日就荒芜；著述之事，行将废辍。书生结习，未能尽忘，姑记旧闻，以销暇日。而所闻所见，必由集腋而成；予取予求，窃有乞邻之意。伏望儒林丈人、高斋学士，各举怪怪奇奇之事，为我原原本本而书，寄来“春在草堂”，助作秋灯丛话。约以十事为率，如其多则更佳。先将二绝为媒，幸勿置之不答。

衰不复事丹铅，六十原非亲学年。正似东坡老无事，听人说鬼便欣然。郭冲五事太寥寥，戏学姚崇十事要。不论搜神兼志怪，妄言亦可慰无聊。 ●卷一冯孝子，佚其名，太仓州之老闸镇人。少孤贫，佣耕以养母。乱后无田可耕，乃行乞于市，得钱则市酒肉以进，而歌俚曲以侑之。同治六年，母卒，乞得义冢地，并其父柩合葬之。日则仍出行乞，夕即于墓旁宿焉。每日必携数石以归，环墓成垣，自结草庐，寝处其下。后数年，无病卒。乡人即葬之于其所庐处。知州方公传书立碣表之，曰“冯孝子墓”。《春秋》二百四十年，人材极盛，而开卷即载颍考叔事，表纯孝也。余研经余暇，偶掇拾见闻，成斯笔记，而首以冯孝子事，亦庶几左氏之义夫。

木工陶某，金陵人。年甫四岁，值粤贼陷城，父为所掳，母子相依，幸而无恙。大乱既平，仍设肆于城中。至同治甲戌，陶年二十五矣。忽有老翁携瞽妇至门乞食，与之钱不去，熟视陶曰：“尔非陶姓乳名某者乎？”陶问何以知我，翁曰：“尔乃吾子也。”陶乎母出视，果其父，因扶之入，拜问由来。则始而被掳北行，后又流转至川、陕，今自陕归也。解腰缠出银数锭，皆累年贸易所得，恐途中遇盗贼，伪为窭人耳；瞽妇则其续娶者也。因大欢慰，亲党毕贺。夫兵乱以来，父子夫妻离散者多矣，此家乃得完聚，意其有阴德乎？

吾兄福宁太守，官厦门同知时，署附近有僧寺曰“碧山岩寺”，僧曰碧禅，能诗画，貌亦恂恂，吐属风雅。署中潘少梅、蔡瑜卿诸君，皆喜与之游。一日，忽具牒请还俗。吾兄素不喜僧，骂之曰：“今日既欲还俗，当初何必出家邪？”呵叱而遣之。碧禅故与署中吏魁某甲有隙，疑其谮于官也。是晚甲自署归其家，经由一隘巷，闻碧禅呼其名，回顾之，则已事刂刃于胸出于背。某甲犹能带刃狂奔，至家，告家人曰：“碧禅杀我。”言已，倒地而绝。事闻于官，吾兄命捕碧禅，不得。数月后，有人于署后山上见碧禅，则已有发辫矣。

呼曰：“碧禅在此！”碧禅飞一足，蹴其人仆地，而自从山后跃而下，捷如飞鸟。望之，见其帽坠于地，辫乃缀于帽，非真发也。嗣后署中一幕友王姓者，房中每夜窗户不开，而频有所失。其所失物则皆得之署后山穴中，疑碧禅所为也。阅时既久，捕事益懈，而王姓者失物亦愈稀。已而吾兄调福防同知以去，继之者为龚司马，知此事，命捕之。有金姓者，侦知其在妓楼，纠众以往。碧禅见众至，自楼窗跃至平地，复从平地跃至屋上，顷刻绝迹。而龚司马于内室中得一书云：“吾与汝无仇，汝捕吾何也？然汝亦欲升官耳，吾不汝怨。汝他日还省，当访我于鼓山。”大骇，不知所自来，疑其人盖古剑客之流也。乃不复言捕碧禅。及任满还省垣，竟不敢至鼓山，而碧禅始终不可得。
邢阿金，农家女也。幼随其母往来大家，故有大家风范。修眉纤趾，望之楚楚，烹饪缝纫，并皆精妙。诵唐人小诗，略能上口。年及笄，嫁田舍儿，性粗暴，以其荏弱不任井臼，虐遇之。阿金性柔和，惟背人啜泣而已。其母闻之大戚，以钱赎之归。又嫁一富家子，年少美丰仪。阿金自幸以为得佳婿也，不意其佻达无度，得新弃旧，旋即仳离。乃嫁一官人为妾，又不容于大妇而归。于是阿金年亦二十外矣。有黄大令者年逾周甲，得之为室，甚嬖之。

黄妻久卒，谋以为妻，阿金不可，曰：“妾出身微贱，岂足伉俪君子？不独损折年寿，亦且累君盛德。”黄鉴其诚，益爱重之。黄有子妇，与年相若，阿金待之极有恩礼。子妇承翁意，事之如姑，阿金虽谦不敢当，然子妇执礼不衰。无何黄病，阿金侍汤药惟谨，年余黄卒，竟仰药以殉。黄之子感其殉父，附葬如礼焉。此女四易所天，不为贞，卒殉其夫，不得不谓之烈。使其初适即得良奥，必为善妇。乃所如不合，遂历四姓，卒成大节，是谓质美而未学。君子哀其遇可也，取其晚盖可也。若豫让“众人”、“国士”之言，本非正论，固非女子所得藉口矣。
江西玉山县有水南寺，亦古刹也。有老僧曰月印，年六十余，终日坐一室诵经，足不出户，戒行颇高。畜一狗十许年矣，每月印诵经，狗必往听，一闻木鱼声，辄摇尾而至，僧俗咸叹异焉。后其狗忽病癞，皮毛脱落，且有秽气，然每日听经如故。一日，月印忽谓其徒曰：“此狗可厌，汝曹可牵出扑杀之。”其徒咸骇然，罔测其意。以师素严重，不敢违。姑呼狗出，禁勿使听经，实未忍杀之也。

越三日，狗又乘间来听诵经，月印见之，惊曰：“汝曹未杀此犬乎？殆矣，殆矣！”命其徒至某村某姓家探问，则有一妇坐蓐，三日未产，其势垂危，乳医束手。月印乃曰：“汝曹不忍杀此犬，乃忍杀彼妇乎？此犬不死，彼妇不产。”乃命其徒立时扑杀之。再侦之，某姓家则已产矣，男也。月印曰：“此犬以听经善果，得托生为某姓男子，小有禄位。吾不及见矣，汝曹识之。”及某氏子稍长，辄来寺中，依依不去。月印摩其顶曰：“汝不昧宿根，此意甚善。但尚有小富贵未享，勿遽来此也。”某氏子后果膺微秩，其家亦有中人之产。晚岁家居，恒寄宿寺中。时月印圆寂久矣，为葺治其塔；寺中屋倾圮，亦出赀修理之，并买田供常住费。年七十余而终。

维扬李氏妇，早寡，以纺织自给，有子甫九龄。同邑富人某，艳其色，而无以自通，乃重赂其邻，使为间。会其子将出就傅，邻人以告某曰：“是有机可图也。”乃筑精舍于其旁侧，移子弟于其中，延师课读。宣言曰：“欲入塾者，勿拒。”使邻人以告妇，极言其便，妇乃使子往读。某善遇之，频以饮食馈遗。一日，出十数金相赠，曰：“闻子家徒四壁立，愿以此少佐饣粥。”子持归白母，妇曰：“币重言甘，得毋诱我乎？”乃使其子往谢曰：“极感长者厚意，母当踵门拜谢。”某则大喜。又使其子出金还之曰：“母性多疑，此不敢受。”某又爽然若失。子归备言其状，妇叹曰：“是可得其情矣。”明日戒子勿往。某自来招之，妇使其子谢曰：“子之惠愈厚，子之过愈大矣。绝子，所以报子也。”某惭而退。此妇不特以礼自持，其智识亦足多矣。
明归熙甫著论，极言女未嫁夫死守节之非。然考之礼，婿死，女斩衰往吊。圣人既为制斩衰之服，则已有夫妇之义，其曰既葬而除之者，礼为中人以下设耳。归氏之言，未为定论。乃粤东之俗，有所谓慕清者，则真出乎人情之外，为礼法之所不许矣。粤俗，未婚夫死不嫁，曰守清；原未许嫁而缔婚于已死之男子，往而守节，曰慕清。有许氏女，年逾В梅，言于母，求慕清。母谋之父，父不可。女曰：“姊以遇人不淑，贻父母忧。

倘女亦然，不重有忧乎？且女弱，亦不任中馈事。苟或遁迹空门，是废大伦，诚不可也。若女萝乔木，得托清门，无废大伦而克成素志，父母何病焉？”乃许之。适有陈氏子将婚而夭，所聘之妇不能守清。陈氏寡母止此一子，乃访求慕清者。媒妁以许女告，遂成二姓之好，迎娶如礼。许女既往，每日略循定省虚文，此外无一事。窗明几净，焚香静坐而已。有小姑已许嫁叶氏，与嫂极相得。每至嫂所共话，辄叹曰：“嫂几生修此清福？”许女曰：“止凭此一念之坚耳。”小姑曰：“嫂幸而未许嫁，不然，亦无如何矣。

”许女曰：“未入其门，事犹在我也。”小姑乃日聒其母，亦求慕清。母溺爱，曲从之，言于叶氏。初不可，既而曰：“彼女既绝意于归，强之亦恐非福。”索还聘礼而已。于是二女同居，至于白首，亲族中或颇称焉。真所谓非礼之礼矣。道光间，江南常州龙舟特盛，然多亦不过四五舟。且舟高于屋，运掉不灵，实亦无可观，观者之意不专在龙舟也。自五月起，至六月初旬，或犹未止。城外有地名唐家湾者，尤画舫聚集之所，每日东舷西舫，鳞次栉比，笙歌如沸，粉黛如云，盖亦一销金锅矣。

余时客常州，居停主人邀余同往观之。见邻舟一大腹贾，挟三妓饮酒，拥一幼妓于怀，状甚亵，余命移舟避之。一榜人且解缆且太息曰：“孽报，孽报！”余问其故，榜人曰：“此三妓乃三姊妹也。其父在日为武进县吏魁，豪侈特甚，每年夏日辄乘舟至城外纳凉，招妓侑酒。舟中先携有纱衫裤，妓至则命易之，遍体雪肤，望之了了如无衣者然。屡坐吾舟，吾时年少，乐观之，辄给事于其侧，得寓目焉。不图至今日而其三女子乃有甚于斯也。岂非孽报！”余叹曰：“然则此大腹贾亦可危矣。”

谭某，不知何许人，客于荆州之沙市，年少美丰仪。市有周媪者，卖阿芙蓉为业，生一女，年十八，亦有风致。谭屡过之，遂与目成，使人风示媪，媪居为奇货，未诺也。女乃与谭约，效文君故事，具舟江上，中夜偕亡。媪侦探无踪，痛哭数日，竟服生鸦片而死。谭与女寄居汉口，甚相得。然女每梦母呼与俱返，觉而语谭，谭曰：“此所谓思梦也。”一夕，女倩邻媪买佳酿半壶置床头，女故善饮，亦不异也。夜半，忽自帐中跃出，披发跣足，腹内声如辘轳，十指甲色皆紫黑，始知已和生鸦片而饮之矣。救治不及，竟宛转而死，咸谓母来索命。余谓此女背母而逃，致母于死，死固其分，非必鬼母之为祟也。卫静澜廉访曰：“余披览案牍，见服生鸦片陨命者，几于无日无之。盖此物近来所在皆有，非如砒石等毒物必购求而得也。有一方可救之，宜广为传播。其方用雄黄二钱，鸡蛋青一枚，生桐油一两，河水调匀灌服。”

张永祺，南雄州人。娶何氏，生子甫一岁，挈之如母家。其姨女适某氏者亦至，有女亦一岁，与张子貌酷似，乍视之莫辨也。及何归，则呱呱者已为某氏女矣，盖母家戏易之博一笑也。何命婢往易之，及至家，仍某氏女也。又命媪往易之，至何家，谛视，则实张氏子，而非某氏女。何氏之母曰：“此事本由嬉戏而起，乃已易子而归，仍见为女，及携女来易，又见为子，此必有异矣。”乃自携其女之子至张家还之，且曰：“事诚可异，勿播扬也。”嗣是两家子女时时互易，莫知其所以然。后招姨女来，分宅居之，始已。或曰，此殆狐魅播弄以为戏也。

黄某，不知何许人，父商于羊城，遂家焉。妻何氏，父在时所聘父执女也。黄冗不能自立，父死，遂入于下流，数年尽亡其赀，至无以存活。何曰：“相偕俱死，与君何益。有一策可两全，君意如何？”黄曰：“苟不为门户羞，任卿所为。”何曰：“妾姑母有子妇，早寡，欲得一人为伴侣。君如肯舍妾，彼譬如买婢，数十金不吝也。”黄从之。何商于姑，归告黄曰：“姑始以离人夫妇，执不可，妾力言之乃可。然须郎亲笔一纸书为券，免日后有违言，姑当以五十金润笔也。”黄欣然书券。何持去，遂不归。有知其事者，曰：“何女盖效买臣妻故事，所言[B220]也。”

殷怀乡，江浦人。少孤，有母及诸弟，无恒产，佣力以养。耕作之暇，辄入山刈薪，至夜分始休。明旦入市易甘脆奉母，日以为常。年三十，始娶妇，教之事姑，妇亦婉娩听从。无何岁大荒，无所得食，乃谓妇曰：“俱死无益，不如嫁汝，得银钱可以养吾母及吾弟，汝亦得生路，一举而两利也。”妇不可，殷曰：“此吾意也，非汝负我。且吾母得存活，即汝所以报我也。”妇乃从之，母及弟卒赖以全。后母死，负土成坟。诸弟成立，皆为婚娶，或劝续娶。泣曰：“吾妇归我，无失德，且得母欢心。昔以贫故弃之，今复娶，是负吾贤妇也。

”卒不娶，独庐于墓侧以终。按《陈书》，徐陵弟孝克当侯景之乱，京师大饥，卖妻臧氏与孔景行，以其赀养母。是鬻妻养母，古人有行之者。然郭巨埋儿，论者谓不可为训，然则鬻妻养母，于义似亦未安也。或曰是盖舍其轻且小者，全其重且大者。然此妇既得姑欢心，或母知妇见出以己之故，涕泣不食，则去妇而转不足以养母；又或妇守从一之义，知事难两全，慷慨自杀，则未能养母，而适以杀妇。且岁饥乏食，非如水火盗贼之变起猝然者也，委曲图全，岂遂乏术，而计必出此乎？殷委巷细民，未闻大道，能割爱为此，亦复大难。然以事而论，则徐孝克与郭巨同一不可为训者也。

阿胜，广州人，谈者逸其姓氏。少孤，游于美利加国之旧金山，善贸易。居六载，积赀颇丰，航海而归，将缔婚于中土。有某氏女及笄，因媒合之，女母闻其丰于赀也，许焉。既又惧其仍远游也，曰：“吾女岂能相从于海外哉！”故使媒妁索重聘。阿胜鄙之曰：“卖婚，非礼也。吾何患无妻！”遂已其事，复游金山。女闻之，不直其母，窃附海舶，至金山寻夫。一日，于途中遇之，连呼曰：“阿胜，阿胜！”胜顾之惊曰：“卿闺中弱质，何为至此？”女具告之。胜感其义，与俱归旅舍成礼焉。论者谓女子在室从父母之命，此女不从母命，而从六礼未备之夫，不可为训。然重洋暌隔，万里追寻，亦不可云非奇女子矣。君子姑取其从一之贞，勿责其越礼也。

广东花县有一村聚，距城数十里，河水潆洄，清流如带，有桥甚钜。桥畔一石，形似老翁，村中咸呼为桥头土地神，香火颇盛。后有女子六人，守志不嫁，相约赴桥畔投水死，盖粤俗然也。父老谓神不能保卫，遂废其祀。附近有砖窑，相距里许，窑人舁之归，谋奉为窑神。是夕，窑中有声若霹雳，次日视之，所烧之砖皆成血色，咸谓石为祟，复舁而弃之河干。适有渔舟维岸侧，见此石，异之，载之船唇。俄疾风大作，舟几覆，惧而弃石于河。渔人旋腹痛欲死，乃使人泅水出之，仍置河干。邻邑三山县人闻其事，曰：“神既有灵，曷去彼而就我乎？”夜窃之去，及境，舆从鼓吹，导之以入，灵异大著，祭赛无虚日，为立庙焉。昌黎云：“偶然题作木居士，便有无穷求福人。”况石乎？然以《风俗通义》所书汝南石贤士事观之，则知此亦人心使然，久之当自歇也。
朱了头者，娄县农家子也。家本赤贫，又茕茕无所依，日行乞于市。咸丰辛酉岁，粤贼自嘉善趋枫泾，遇之，劫与俱去。朱曰：“我丐也，既无钱财自赎，又无一艺可供尔用，何劫我为？”贼曰：“汝既丐也，饥寒之困甚矣。从我去，不忧不富贵。”朱怒曰：“我惟甘饥寒，故丐耳；否则为窃为盗，胡不可乎？我不为窃为盗，乃从尔等作贼乎？”抗声大骂，遂见害。呜呼！如朱了头者，可谓有古烈士风矣。

汉阳朱勋臣，侨寓淮城。一日，忽于案头得张禹门书云：“欲借宅同居。”朱初不知张为何人，且罔测书所从来。正疑虑间，倏又得一书，乃知张为狐也。嗣是频有书来，而不以形见。朱惧，谋徙居以避之，徙居而张仍从焉。有请箕仙者，朱往问之，则柳仙，降判曰：“张禹门，吾弟子也，与汝有宿缘，宜为建祠。”朱谢力不及。又判曰：“三日再议。”及期而往，盘中书一“吝”字。朱方欲再问，而家人告火作，奔救乃熄。于是大惧，鸠工庇材，刻日成之。祠成，而张仍往来朱家。

适有为朱女议昏者，朱以问张，张报书曰：“此不可许，三日后当有高人来为媒。”越三日，有一老妪来为徐秀才之子求昏。老妪高姓，始悟高人谓妪也。狐居人家，载籍所记多有其事，此狐自称张禹门，不知其何从得姓，何义命名也。箕仙殆即此狐为之托名。柳仙亦无其人，世传柳仙为吕纯阳弟子，盖本元人杂剧，此岂足为典要？且考宋郑景璧《蒙斋笔谈》，载吕诗“惟有城南老树精，分明知道神仙过”。城南老树，乃古松也，则知元剧已属讹传矣。余雅不信箕仙，窃谓当今之世，而欲绝地天通，宜首禁此术也。

何明达，桂林人，商于岭南，挟赀数千，频年折阅，耗其大半。旅况无聊，偶作狭邪游，至一所曰“巢云别馆”，有妓名翠莲，颇楚楚有致，何流连久之。忽见一妇自帘外过，淡妆素服，丰韵天然，何见而好焉。倩翠莲为之先容，翠莲曰：“是必无成。”问何故，曰：“伊因家难鬻身于此，有能拔出污泥中者，则委身事之；若视作路旁柳，伊不屑也。”何曰：“若然，此女大有志节，宜询其颠末。”乃呼问之。妇自言谭氏，“夫广西人，姓王名基，客游至此。娶妾甫两载，因事被逮于官，非百金不能出狱。妾念夫妇之义，自鬻以拯之。夫出囹圄，妾堕火坑矣。”言已泣下。何曰：“尔夫与吾同乡，吾不忍其家室流离。但不知尔夫已西归否？”曰：“闻犹未也。”何乃辞出，竟访得其夫，助赀使赎妇以归。市井细民，有此高行，虽士大夫或不及矣。
陈明赞，江西南安府人，谈者失记其县。家巨富，止一子，已抱孙矣。陈于生日置酒召族人共饮，语之曰：“某幸藉先业，加以积累，今拥赀三十余万矣。多藏厚亡，非所愿也。愿留奇零之数以赡妻孥，其二十万归始祖祠中，其十万归支祖祠中。岁入其息，仍以三分之一归吾子孙，恐子孙或不能守吾业，仍可藉此为生也。其三分之二以供春秋祭祀之需，族之人冠婚丧葬咸有助，孀者、孤者、废疾者、贫不能存者咸有养。

归始祖祠者，遍及同族；归支祖祠者，惟及同支。以贤且才者一人司其事，虽一陇之地不得私鬻。即以吾今日之言为息壤矣。”闻者咸起拜曰：“盛德事也，但不知翁之子以为何如？”子亦矢言“惟父命是从”。议遂定。夫金穴铜山，终归于尽，陈翁此举，非独高义，抑亦明智也。拥三十万之赀于一家，不三十年必尽矣，今推之于一族，而仍使归其所入三分之一，则是使子孙长有十万也。老子曰：“既已为人，己愈有；既已与人，己愈多。”其是之谓乎！ 贞女王蕙芳，常州人。父遭兵乱，挈家避沪渎，遂家焉。贫不能自存，乃鬻女于王姓者为女，时女甫四岁。及长，丰姿秀丽，娴习女红，王颇爱之。无何王卒，其妻旧居北里，搔头弄姿，非良妇也，携女再嫁。女心非之，而不敢言。其后夫素有登徒子之名，见女美，屡挑之。女惧不免，于同治癸酉岁二月十三日夜缢而死。此事诱与拒两无实迹，女虽死不得以贞女旌，因纪大略，冀不泯耳。

广东潮州乡间多种柿，柿熟制为饼，必先曝之于场，夜则逻守之。有某甲以此为业，场中积柿如山，中设木棚，为守望所。一夕寝棚中，闻有声自东来，近而声益厉，咻咻焉，木棚为之震动。自隙窥之，虎也。急以刀斫之，断其尾，虎咆哮去。甲走归鸣金，集丁壮数十人，荷梃寻之。至一处，见虎屹然立，众莫敢近；久之不动，或以戈扌舂之，应手而仆，则已僵矣，乃裂其肉焉。此人猝见猛虎，从容不乱，可谓胆力俱全矣。孔子曰：“上士捉虎头，下士捉虎尾。”然下士亦正未易为也。

陈元绍，顺德人。家少康，父母俱存。妻某氏，亦故家女，而骄蹇殊甚，不修妇职。陈责之，辄反唇。父母戒陈曰：“和气致祥，慎勿尔也。”陈恐伤亲心，亦曲忍之。会陈父病，汤药皆母子经理，妇若无其事者，陈固心衔之。及父病愈，陈薄责妇，妇怒曰：“谁无父母！小恙非大事，何便作践人家儿女哉！”陈闻言，忿不可遏，挥以老拳，妇亦攘臂与争。母出排解，陈偶一举手，误挤母仆地，急扶母起，伏地请罪，母麾之出。陈惊惧不自安，竟亡去。月余，自广州贻其弟书曰：“兄无状，不可为子，惟有逃罪海外耳。重洋万里，无望生还，书到之日，即死别之年矣。”父见书不骇，自至省访之，不得，托人于外国招工局询问，亦无其子姓名，或云已于上月自澳门出洋矣。陈挤母仆地，事出无心，不孝之小者；长投海外，永绝天伦，不孝之大者。如陈者，其至性颇亦过人，惜其不学无术也。同年应敏斋廉访宝时，尝从金华至杭州，夜行钱唐江中。
其时将二更矣，月色微茫，远树约略可辨。帆风而行，风力甚小，故舟行颇迟，而了不倾侧。长年三老皆偃息篷底，舟中诸客亦俱酣睡，敏斋独携一仆坐船头观江中夜景。所过之地名鲇鱼口，有小沙洲，舟沿洲而行。相距才数武，见洲上有一人与舟并行，迟速适相等。其人长丈余，视常人几三倍之，遍体青绿色，但望之不甚亲切，不知其为肌肉，为皮毛，为鳞甲也。顾问其仆曰：“汝见彼人否？”其仆不应。

敏斋因还入舟中，其仆携坐具从之入，旋即大吐。问其故，曰：“主人所见，奴亦见之，不特形状可畏，且其气甚腥。适闻其气，故吐也。”敏斋后为余述之，云：“不知是何怪，殆传记所称枫人之类欤？”余谓：“沙洲无草木，不应有此。且草木之精，其气亦不腥，此必水怪也。以形论之，其长如此，非鱼即蛇。《搜神记》载孔子厄于陈，弦歌于馆中，夜有一人，长九尺余，皂衣高冠，咤声动左右。子路出与战，仆之于地，乃是大是鱼。君之所见，或亦此类乎？”

聂道人，湖北钟祥县人。乾隆甲子岁，邑大水，聂已五岁矣。有盛契真者，元游宫之道士也，悯其孤露，收之门下。然聂故钝根，一无所能。盛死，聂云游天下，不知历几何年月。嘉庆初，始居鄂之长春观，往往赤双脚，持长柄，行歌于市。至同治甲戌，其年一百三十有五，发丝齿贝，无异曩时，不知其得道引术而不死耶，抑其禀赋独厚也？不可谓非异人矣。 鄂人王慕堂，游于津门，遇盗，丧其所赍，寓居同乡陈姓家。偶出散步，过一曲巷，见一家帘栊低掩，中有丽人，衫痕鬓影，隐约可睹。王意方郁郁，亦不顾也。行数武，有小鬟追及之，曰：“主人相候，请君移玉。”问主人伊谁，曰：“至自识之。”至即帘中人也。然彼此均不相识。女冁然曰：“误矣。”王问故，曰：“妾本秦淮烟柳中人，从买人至此。买人死，遂独居。顷从帘隙窥君，颇似旧识某郎，故遣婢相招，而不知非也。”王因辞去。
女请置酒小饮以谢过，王谢曰：“素昧平生，何烦厚意。”女曰：“相逢何必曾相识，萍水遇合，即是前缘。君即闲游，妾亦枯坐，杯酒小叙，亦足破寂。君何介介欤？”王遂留饮。酒半，言及被盗事，女问行止，曰：“客囊如洗，进退维谷。若小有膏秣资得入都门，则辇下尚有故人，或犹可谋干也。”女曰：“君勿忧。妾虽龌龊，颇具湖海襟怀，必当竭力。”酒罢，竟出百金赠之。王持以入都，所谋果遂。复访之，则门庭如故，人面不知何处矣。

番禺何桐屿，于同治癸亥初冬，有戚某向其借洋钱五百枚。何以其人旧曾借贷两次，皆如期而偿，遂诺之，立券期于岁杪。至期往索，某诧曰：“君梦欤？抑戏也？业已还君，何又索为？”何惊问：“何时还我？”曰：“十二月初三日，我携洋钱至君家交君手，承君留我小饮，犹如昨日事，何遽忘之？谓予不信，君还我借券犹在也。”乃取示何。何曰：“此券尚存我箧，君何伪造此？”某曰：“如君尚存借券，请以示我。”何至家检视，箧中诸券皆在，此券独无，惟诸券中杂有一纸，颇似前所书券，而无一字。其事遂无以自明，偕至神前，沥鸡血为誓，不知究竟若何也。此殆有秘物代墨书，券久而字迹脱落，遂成空纸。段成式《酉阳杂俎》称江东人或取乌贼鱼墨书契，以脱人财物，知古有此事矣。落地开花炮，其炮子虽年久尚能炸裂。乡民拾得者，因其封口处有金皮，贪取其金，每致伤命。

甲戌正月，上海浏河镇东洋子泾桥镊工陈小弟，因新岁负人博，进无以偿。思同治元年七月中官军开放落地开花炮，有炮子落其地，陈小弟埋之土中，久思凿取其金，未敢措手。至是已历十三年，久在土中，必受潮湿，计必无碍矣。乃掘出，用斧凿取金皮，忽然炸裂，陈头面俱焚，左手打在数里外杨树上。室中一老妇、一童子，均轰死。军兴以来，官军用落地开花炮，无往不利。其炮子之遗落者，所在有之，愿得者以此为鉴，勿贪小利而致大害也。上海某甲，商于津门，十年不归。其家止一妾二女，妾故冶荡，不能独守空帏也。长女颇有母风，母倚之为钱树子。无何从人亡去，乃使次女继其业，次女不可，母憾焉。会有六十翁欲买妾，母贪重值，以次女妾之。甫一载，翁死，无子，家业尽为族人瓜分，女惟以针黹自给。母谓之曰：“人生世上，如轻尘栖弱草，何自苦为？当为汝别觅佳婿。”女断发自誓，竟不可夺。梅禹金有《青泥莲花记》，殆此女之谓乎？

李广元，不知其乡里。妻早卒，遗二子，长曰上朋，年七岁；次日上俊，三岁。李续娶郑氏，性阴险，善蛊惑，视前妻子如仇，日加楚毒。会郑所生子夭，谓由朋惊之使然，益虐遇之，朋竟死。俊忽得颠痫疾，忽歌忽泣，殊无人理。郑以为伪，试以秽物予之，食尽无难色，乃视为废物，亦不顾问。如是者数年，郑死，俊忽自愈。父怪之，俊泣曰：“儿病伪也。非伪为痫疾，则死继母手久矣。今继母死，又察父复有怜儿意，故敢以情告也。”此子在童年即能韬晦自全，其德慧术智过人远矣。

黄冈县易家坂有易翁者，夫妇二人，老而无子。止生三女，长次皆适人，惟幼女在室。翁死，母女相依为命，每抚女叹曰：“恨汝非男子也。”年十九而嫁，夫家甚贫，故恒居母家。癸酉十一月初十日，风雨大作，女偶立门前，为狂风卷去，母侦探无踪。三日后，忽自返，问所往，茫然也，而下体已化为男。母遍告族人验之，信，乃出钱七十二千与夫，令别娶妻。女易钗而弁，已聘王氏女为妇矣。晋定公二十五年，西山女子化为丈夫，与之妻，能生子，事见《开元占经》引《汲冢纪年》。然则此事自古有之，不足异也。
戴琴庄先生名福谦，先祖母戴太夫人之侄孙，与余兄弟行。而余少时曾请其授读，故始终以先生称之。其馆临平孙氏时，一日行于途，有娶新妇者，彩舆经过，先生避立道旁。俄风揭轿帘，瞥见轿中白如霜雪，一瞬之间，未能谛视，而心颇异之。偶言于先大夫，先大夫曰：“此必是日犯白虎凶星，得君文星解释之，传记中多有此事。君异时必大贵也。”众皆以为然。然先生于道光丁酉举于乡，越二岁客死京师，意不显。

安徽玉溪口，泊有炮船二。有某广文挈眷属坐小船过其地，日暮泊焉。夜鼓再严，管带炮船之营官乘他船出巡，船中兵勇起意至小船行劫，持刀登其舻。广文见盗至，大呼炮船救援。即杀之，并及其一妻一子一女一仆；长年三老，均投于水。因断其缆，使顺流而去。及营官回见邻舟不在，问之，众曰：“已解维行矣。”营官惊曰：“黑夜江行，必有失事，汝曹胡不阻止？”众曰：“阻而不听也。”营官见几上有翡翠条脱一事，审视即邻舟女子腕上物也，因亦不言。及旦鼓发旬，乃告于统带之官，捕船中兵勇，严鞫得实，枭其首于竿。苜蓿中，度无长物，扁舟江上，竟占无妄之灾。信乎江湖之险，不独风波也。
范某年四十无子，止一女，名婉如，颇婉娈可爱。有甥李仙槎，五岁而孤，偕其母以归，与婉如年相若，交相怜也。仙槎性柔和，范妻亦喜之，呼为李郎。仙槎母将死，泣谓范曰：“吾惟此子，恨不见其娶妇。婉如我所爱也，异日无令归他族，吾死目瞑矣。”仙槎既长，范予千金，使营什一利，而仙槎不善经营，数年折阅殆尽。范因憾之，寝前议。仙槎闻之，仰药死，婉如亦缢以殉。范大悔恨，合葬之。

扬州某甲，生一女，年破瓜矣，颇有姿色。其东邻为某氏别业，某子为邑诸生，读书其中，翩翩少年也。女屡入园采花，与生有私，女父母知而防闲之，遂绝迹。生思慕綦初。一日女至，曰：“殆矣，父母将为我择配矣。君急以媒妁来，或犹可及也。”言已即去。生告父母，初不可，强而后可。媒者致命，女父母曰：“齐大非吾耦也。”且知女私于生，恐异日不为舅姑所礼，竟谢绝之，而许女于他族。女知事不谐，服阿芙蓉膏死，生闻之亦自经死。两家父母皆大悔，卒合葬焉。痴儿荡妇，颇乖风化，其情可怜耳。

此与前所记李仙槎、范婉如事相类，然彼则始有成言，谓之因情而死可也，谓之守义而死亦可也，固非此事所能拟矣。上海北乡有黄某者，妻李氏颇有姿。而黄贫不能自存，谋于李。李曰：“君为男子，而谋及妇人；无已，请鬻我乎？妾我可百金，妓我可千金也。”黄不可，李曰：“然则放鹁鸽乎？”沪俗伪鬻妻妾于人，伺间亡归，谓之放鹁鸽。黄从之，伪为兄妹，鬻于浦东曹氏为妾。不三日，黄往访之，李出见，颇落寞。曹留黄宿于家，翌日将告归，促李出言别。李始不出，久之乃出，不数语，遽厉声曰：“汝鬻我于此，乃谋与我偕遁乎！我至此无返理，汝不速去，我言于主人，缚送官矣。”黄大窘，踉跄而归。鹁鸽化为黄鹤，一去而不复返矣。此亦为鬼为蜮者之所不自料也。

韶州李弼廷有女曰松姑，年十五矣。自幼佞佛，奉一檀木观音像，已历六寒暑。父母欲议婚，女不可。父曰：“此皆奉佛之故也。”欲毁其像。女急白母，言像不可毁，请从父命。乃缔姻于程氏。女自是清癯异昔，医者诊之，言脾肺俱弱矣。一夕，女午夜欲起礼佛，觉有物触臂，方惊诧而腕已受伤。呼婢烛之，则有一蛇长二尺许，色如墨，蜿蜓下榻去。父母闻声趋视，蛇已不知所住，视女伤处，蛇齿宛然，黑气缕缕直达臂上。医至，谢不治，黎明竟卒。或言女本天仙化身，不合缔人间姻眷，故菩萨使以洁身归净土。果尔，何不使以无疾化去，而必死于蛇口乎？是殆有夙冤也。
